definitions.definition: rather; to some extent
examples: These trousers are a bit tight.
‘Are you tired?’ ‘Yes, I am a bit.’
I was a bit disappointed by the film.
It costsa bit morethan I wanted to spend.
The future looksa little bitbrighter this morning.
I felta wee bitguilty about it.
I can lend you fifty pounds, if you want. That should help a bit.

definitions.definition: a short time or distance
examples: Wait a bit!
Can you move up a bit?
for a bitGreg thought for a bit before answering.
in a bitSee you in a bit.

definitions.definition: a small amount or piece of something
examples: Here are some usefulbits of information.
I have abit ofgoodnewsfor you.
Let me give youa little bitof advice.
Witha bit of luck, we'll be there by 12.
I've got a bit of shopping to do.
bits of paper/wood/plastic

definitions.definition: a part of something larger
examples: The best bit of the holiday was seeing the Grand Canyon.
I read it, but I missed out the boring bits.
bit about somethingI like the bit about owls in the first chapter.

definitions.definition: a large amount
examples: ‘How much does he earn?’‘Quite a bit!’
It raineda fair bitduring the night.
The new system willtake a bit ofgetting used to(= it will take a long time to get used to).

definitions.definition: the smallest unit of information used by a computer
a metal bar that is put in a horse’s mouth so that the rider can control it
a tool or part of a tool fordrilling(= making)holes
an amount of money equal to 12½ cents
a person’s sexual organs
to be very sad or worried
examples: Inside I'm in bits because I miss him so much.
I’m in bits about the problems I’ve had.
She was in bits over her decision to quit.

definitions.definition: behaviour or ideas that are typical of a particular group, type of person or activity
examples: She couldn't accept the whole drug-culture bit.

definitions.definition: a piece at a time; gradually
examples: He assembled the model aircraft bit by bit.
Bit by bit memories of the night came back to me.

definitions.definition: not fair or not reasonable
examples: It's a bit much calling me at three in the morning.
The noise from next door is getting a bit much.

definitions.definition: used when talking about unpleasant or negative things or ideas, to mean ‘rather a…’
examples: We may have a bit of a problem on our hands.
The rail strike is a bit of a pain.

definitions.definition: a person that you think is sexually attractive
a person of a low social class who has a sexual relationship with somebody of a higher class
the boyfriend or girlfriend of somebody who is already married or in a steady sexual relationship with somebody else
examples: Is he your bit on the side?

definitions.definition: small objects or items of various kinds
examples: She stuffed all her bits and pieces into a bag and left.
I just need to get a few bits and pieces at the supermarket.

definitions.definition: to use bombs or other weapons to destroy somebody/something completely
examples: The plane was blown to pieces when the bomb exploded.

definitions.definition: impatient to do or start doing something
examples: Gerry’s champing at the bit to go to college.

definitions.definition: to do your share of a task
examples: We can finish this job on time if everyone does their bit.

definitions.definition: just as good, bad, etc.; equally good, bad, etc.
examples: Rome is every bit as beautiful as Paris.
He’s every bit as clever as she is.

definitions.definition: to become very enthusiastic about something that you have started to do so that you are unlikely to stop until you have finished
not at all; not in any way
examples: ‘Are you cold?’ ‘Not a bit.’
It's not a bit of use(= there's no point in)complaining.
I'm not the least bit interested in football.
I don't like that idea one bit.

definitions.definition: used for saying that something that you had expected to happen did not happen
examples: You'd think she'd be tired after the journey but not a bit of it!

definitions.definition: not any
examples: He didn'ttake a blind bit of noticeof me(= he ignored me).
It won'tmake the blindest bit of difference(= it will make no difference at all).

definitions.definition: not at all
examples: Normally I'm not the least bit shy.
He wasn't the slightest bit interested in answering our questions.

definitions.definition: into small pieces
examples: The book fell to bits in my hands.
All the crockery had been smashed to bits.
She took the engine to bits, then carefully put it together again.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: I love my kids to bits.
She wasthrilled to bitswhen I said I'd come.

origin: senses 1 to 5 and senses 9 to 10 Old Englishbita‘bite, mouthful’, of Germanic origin; related to GermanBissen, also tobite.senses 7 to 8 Old Englishbite‘biting, a bite’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchbeetand GermanBiss, also tobite.sense 6 1940s: blend ofbinaryanddigit.

definitions.definition: used with plural nouns and a plural verb to mean ‘a small number’, ‘some’
examples: We've had a few replies.
I need a few things from the store.

I try to visit my parentsevery few weeks.

definitions.definition: used with plural nouns and a plural verb to mean ‘not many’
examples: Few people understand the difference.
There seem to be fewer tourists around this year.
Very few students learn Latin now.

definitions.definition: not happening often

origin: Old Englishfēawe,fēawa, of Germanic origin; related to Old High Germanfao, from an Indo-European root shared by Latinpaucusand Greekpauros‘small’.

definitions.definition: two people or things
examples: I saw a couple of men get out.

definitions.definition: a small number of people or things
examples: We went therea couple of yearsago.
a couple of minutes/hours/days/weeks/months
a couple of people/friends/guys

definitions.definition: two people who are seen together, especially if they are married or in a romantic or sexual relationship
examples: married couples
same-sex/gay/lesbian couples
ayoung couple
anelderly couple

Several couples were on the dance floor.
Thecouple was/were marriedin 2022.
couple from…We met a couple from Chicago, who were travelling with their three children.

definitions.definition: very soon
examples: We’ll be there in a couple of shakes.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchcople(noun),copler(verb), from Latincopula(noun),copulare(verb), fromco-‘together’ +apere‘fasten’. Compare withcopulaandcopulate.

definitions.definition: a group of people or things that are the same in some way; a particular variety or type
examples: kind of somebody/somethingthree kinds of cakes/cake

I need to buy paper and pencils, thatkind of thing.

that kind of somethingI'll never have that kind of money(= as much money as that).

this kind of somethingThis kind of exercise is very popular.
of a kindExercises of this kind are very popular.
music ofall/various/different kinds

definitions.definition: consisting of goods or services, not money
examples: As well as his salary, he gets benefits in kind.

definitions.definition: with the same thing
examples: She insulted him and he responded in kind.

definitions.definition: used to show that something you are saying is not exact
examples: I had a kind of feeling this might happen.

definitions.definition: slightly; in some ways
examples: That made me feel kind of stupid.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that the situation is very different from what has been said
examples: ‘I was terrible!’ ‘You were nothing of the kind.’

definitions.definition: not as good as it could be
examples: You're making progress of a kind.

definitions.definition: very similar
examples: They're two of a kind—both workaholics!

definitions.definition: the only one like this
examples: My father was one of a kind—I'll never be like him.

definitions.definition: something like what has been said
examples: ‘He's resigning.’ ‘I'd suspected something of the kind.’

origin: nounOld Englishcynd(e),gecynd(e), of Germanic origin; related tokin. The original sense was ‘nature, the natural order’, also ‘innate character, form, or condition’ (compare with the adjectivekind); hence ‘a class ’or‘ race distinguished by innate characteristics’.

definitions.definition: a particular length of time
examples: a long/an extended period
a short/brief period
period of somethinga period of transition/uncertainty/expansion
a period of two years/six months/four weeks
a two-year/six-month/four-week period
All these changes happened overa period of time.
A year-to-year lease has no fixedtime period.
for a periodThe offer is available for a limited period only.
This compares with a 4% increase for thesame periodlast year.

over a periodThe hall will be closed over a 2-year period.
during a periodWe know little of her life during that period.

Tomorrow will be cold with sunny periods.

definitions.definition: a length of time in the life of a particular person or in the history of a particular country
examples: Whichperiod of historywould you most like to have lived in?
This textbook covers the post-war period.
The church dates from the Norman period.
We are currently studying the early medieval period.

Most teenagers go through a period of rebelling.

definitions.definition: a length of time which is a division of anera. Aperiodis divided intoepochs.
examples: the Jurassic period

definitions.definition: any of the parts that a day is divided into at a school, college, etc. for study
examples: ‘What do you have next period?’ ‘French.’
afree/study period(= for private study)

definitions.definition: the flow of blood each month from the body of a woman who is not pregnant
examples: period pains
monthly periods
When did you lasthave a period?

definitions.definition: the mark ( . ) used at the end of a sentence and in some abbreviations, for examplee.g.

origin: late Middle English (denoting the time during which something, especially a disease, runs its course): from Old Frenchperiode, via Latin from Greekperiodos‘orbit, recurrence, course’, fromperi-‘around’ +hodos‘way, course’. The sense ‘portion of time’ dates from the early 17th cent.

definitions.definition: used with uncountable nouns to mean ‘a small amount’, ‘some’
examples: a little milk/sugar/tea

examples: There was little doubt in my mind.

definitions.definition: slowly; gradually
examples: Little by little the snow disappeared.

origin: Old Englishlȳtel, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchluttel, German dialectlützel.

definitions.definition: the action of giving out cards to the players
examples: It's your deal.

definitions.definition: the soft pale wood offirorpinetrees, especially when it is cut into boards for making things
examples: a deal table

definitions.definition: used to say that you are not impressed by something

definitions.definition: much; a lot
examples: She's feeling a good deal better.

definitions.definition: used to say that you agree to somebody's terms
examples: ‘I'll cook if you wash the dishes.’ ‘It's a deal!’

definitions.definition: used to say that something is not important or not a problem
examples: If I don't win it's no big deal.

definitions.definition: to make an agreement with somebody in which both sides have an advantage
examples: Perhaps we could strike a bargain.

definitions.definition: what is happening in the present situation?
examples: What's the deal? Do you want to go out or not?

origin: nounsenses 1 to 3 Old Englishdǣlan‘divide’, ‘participate’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchdeeland GermanTeil‘part’ (noun), also todole. The sense ‘divide’ gave rise to ‘distribute’, hence sense 1 of the verb and 'deal somebody/​something a blow'; the sense ‘participate’ gave rise to ‘have dealings with’, hence senses 2 of the verb and 'deal with'/'deal in'.nounsense 4 Middle English: from Middle Low German and Middle Dutchdele‘plank’.

definitions.definition: used with verbs to mean ‘a great amount’
examples: I care a lot about you.

definitions.definition: used with adjectives and adverbs to mean ‘much’
examples: I'm feeling a lot better today.

origin: Old Englishhlot(noun), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlot, GermanLos. The original meaning was ‘by lot’ and (by extension) the sense ‘a portion assigned to someone’; this gave rise to the other noun senses. The pronoun and adverb uses date from the early 19th cent.

definitions.definition: an action, an object or a system by which a result is achieved; a way of achieving or doing something

means of doing somethingIs there any means of contacting him?
as a means of (doing) somethingInterest rates are used as a means of controlling borrowing.
means to do somethingThey are using peaceful means to achieve their aims.

means to somethingEquality is an end in itself, not just a means to a goal.
by… meansThis weed can be controlled by various means.

definitions.definition: the money that a person has
examples: aman of means(= a rich man)
according to somebody's meansPeople should pay according to their means.
within somebody's meansAre the monthly repayments within your means(= can you afford them)?

beyond somebody's meansPrivate school fees are beyond the means of most people(= more than they can afford).
means to do somethingHe doesn't have the means to hire a lawyer.

definitions.definition: a thing or an action that is not interesting or important in itself but is a way of achieving something else

examples: ways and means of raising money

origin: late Middle English: plural ofmean(noun), the early sense being ‘intermediary’.

definitions.definition: a large number or amount

a lot to doI have a lot to do today.

origin: Old Englishhlot(noun), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlot, GermanLos. The original meaning was ‘by lot’ and (by extension) the sense ‘a portion assigned to someone’; this gave rise to the other noun senses. The pronoun and adverb uses date from the early 19th cent.

definitions.definition: the opportunity or right to use something or to see somebody/something

access to something/somebodyYou need a password toget accessto the computer system.
access to information/services/resources

definitions.definition: a way of entering or reaching a place

access to somethingWe welcome disabled visitors and there is wheelchair access to all parts of the park.

origin: early 17th cent.: from Latinaccessus, from the verbaccedere‘to approach’, fromad-‘to’ +cedere‘give way, yield’

definitions.definition: at or to a higher place or position than something/somebody

definitions.definition: of greater importance or of higher quality than somebody/something

examples: Above all, keep in touch.

origin: Old Englishabufan(as an adverb), froma-‘on’ +bufan(frombi‘by’ +ufan‘above’).

definitions.definition: to a small degree

origin: Old Englishlȳtel, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchluttel, German dialectlützel.

even numbers(= 2, 4, 6, etc.)
odd numbers(= 1, 3, 5, etc.)

staff/student numbers

examples: painting by numbers

definitions.definition: following closely the accepted rules for doing something
an easy job; a pleasant situation that other people would like
examples: It’s all right for him—he’s got a very cushy number.

definitions.definition: to do something that will harm somebody/something, especially when it feels like it is done deliberately, from a desire to hurt (even if this is not actually the case)
examples: Throat cancer did a number on me a few years ago.

definitions.definition: to know what somebody is really like and what they plan to do

definitions.definition: the time has come when you will die or lose everything
a way of considering an activity, etc. that considers only the number of people doing something, things achieved, etc., not with who or what they are
examples: MPs were playing the numbers game as the crucial vote drew closer.

definitions.definition: a person who does the same job as you in another organization

definitions.definition: being in a group makes you safer and makes you feel more confident
being in a group gives you more power

definitions.definition: the combined power, strength or influence of a group
examples: They won the argument bysheer weight of numbers.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchnombre(noun),nombrer(verb), from Latinnumerus.

outside a rangeThis wasoutside the range of his experience.

definitions.definition: the extent of somebody's knowledge or abilities

a line or group of mountains or hills

from only a short distance away

definitions.definition: near enough to be reached, seen or heard

origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘line of people or animals’): from Old Frenchrange‘row, rank’, fromrangier‘put in order’, fromrang‘rank’. Early usage also included the notion of ‘movement over an area’.

definitions.definition: rather; to some extent
examples: These trousers are a bit tight.

examples: Wait a bit!

definitions.definition: a large amount
examples: ‘How much does he earn?’‘Quite a bit!’

definitions.definition: the smallest unit of information used by a computer
a metal bar that is put in a horse’s mouth so that the rider can control it
a tool or part of a tool fordrilling(= making)holes
an amount of money equal to 12½ cents
a person’s sexual organs
to be very sad or worried
examples: Inside I'm in bits because I miss him so much.
I’m in bits about the problems I’ve had.
She was in bits over her decision to quit.

definitions.definition: behaviour or ideas that are typical of a particular group, type of person or activity
examples: She couldn't accept the whole drug-culture bit.

definitions.definition: a piece at a time; gradually
examples: He assembled the model aircraft bit by bit.
Bit by bit memories of the night came back to me.

definitions.definition: not fair or not reasonable
examples: It's a bit much calling me at three in the morning.
The noise from next door is getting a bit much.

definitions.definition: used when talking about unpleasant or negative things or ideas, to mean ‘rather a…’
examples: We may have a bit of a problem on our hands.
The rail strike is a bit of a pain.

definitions.definition: a person that you think is sexually attractive
a person of a low social class who has a sexual relationship with somebody of a higher class
the boyfriend or girlfriend of somebody who is already married or in a steady sexual relationship with somebody else
examples: Is he your bit on the side?

definitions.definition: small objects or items of various kinds
examples: She stuffed all her bits and pieces into a bag and left.
I just need to get a few bits and pieces at the supermarket.

definitions.definition: to use bombs or other weapons to destroy somebody/something completely
examples: The plane was blown to pieces when the bomb exploded.

definitions.definition: impatient to do or start doing something
examples: Gerry’s champing at the bit to go to college.

definitions.definition: to do your share of a task
examples: We can finish this job on time if everyone does their bit.

definitions.definition: just as good, bad, etc.; equally good, bad, etc.
examples: Rome is every bit as beautiful as Paris.
He’s every bit as clever as she is.

definitions.definition: to become very enthusiastic about something that you have started to do so that you are unlikely to stop until you have finished
not at all; not in any way
examples: ‘Are you cold?’ ‘Not a bit.’
It's not a bit of use(= there's no point in)complaining.
I'm not the least bit interested in football.
I don't like that idea one bit.

definitions.definition: used for saying that something that you had expected to happen did not happen
examples: You'd think she'd be tired after the journey but not a bit of it!

definitions.definition: not any
examples: He didn'ttake a blind bit of noticeof me(= he ignored me).
It won'tmake the blindest bit of difference(= it will make no difference at all).

definitions.definition: not at all
examples: Normally I'm not the least bit shy.
He wasn't the slightest bit interested in answering our questions.

definitions.definition: into small pieces
examples: The book fell to bits in my hands.
All the crockery had been smashed to bits.
She took the engine to bits, then carefully put it together again.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: I love my kids to bits.
She wasthrilled to bitswhen I said I'd come.

origin: senses 1 to 5 and senses 9 to 10 Old Englishbita‘bite, mouthful’, of Germanic origin; related to GermanBissen, also tobite.senses 7 to 8 Old Englishbite‘biting, a bite’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchbeetand GermanBiss, also tobite.sense 6 1940s: blend ofbinaryanddigit.

definitions.definition: to increase something in size, number, amount, etc.
examples: The bad weather only added to our difficulties.
The findingsadd greatly toour understanding of this period.
toadd significantly/considerably tosomething
The house has been added to(= new rooms, etc. have been built on to it)from time to time.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: measuring or covering a great length or distance, or a greater length or distance than usual
examples: She had long dark hair.
He walked down the long corridor.
It was the world's longest bridge.
along journey/walk/drive/flight
We'rea long wayfrom anywhere here.
It's along way away.
He had long, thin fingers.
a lush meadow of long grass

definitions.definition: used for asking or talking about particular lengths or distances
examples: How longis the River Nile?
The table is six feet long.
The report is only three pages long.

definitions.definition: lasting or taking a great amount of time or more time than usual
examples: He’s been ill(for) a long time.
There was a long silence before she spoke.
I like it now the days are getting longer(= it stays light for more time each day).
along book/film/list(= taking a lot of time to read/watch/deal with)
Nurses have to worklong hours(= for more hours in the day than is usual).
(North American English)He stared at themfor the longest time(= for a very long time)before answering.
Chicago hasa long history ofcountry music.
to have a long life/career

definitions.definition: used for asking or talking about particular periods of time
examples: How longis the course?
I think it's only three weeks long.
How long a stay did you have in mind?

definitions.definition: seeming to last or take more time than it really does because, for example, you are very busy or not happy
examples: I'm tired. It's been a long day.
We were married for ten long years.

definitions.definition: covering all or most of your legs or arms
examples: She usually wears long skirts.
a long-sleeved shirt

definitions.definition: travelling a great distance
examples: The full-back intercepted an attempted long pass and ran through to score.
A hopeful long ball from out of defence led to a goal.
She's good with the driver and hits a long ball.

definitions.definition: travelling too far so that it goes out of play
examples: Her return of serve was long.

definitions.definition: having a lot of a particular quality
examples: The government is long on ideas but short on performance.

definitions.definition: alongvowel is pronounced for a longer time than other vowels
examples: Compare the long vowel in ‘beat’ and the short vowel in ‘bit’.

definitions.definition: very long
examples: There's a list of repairs as long as your arm.

definitions.definition: not longer than the particular time given
examples: It will take an hour at the longest.

definitions.definition: after a long time
examples: At long last his prayers had been answered.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: to have known each other for a long time
examples: We go back a long way, he and I.

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.
A small amount of this paint goes a long way(= covers a large area).
(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something
examples: The new law goes a long way towards solving the problem.

definitions.definition: to have made a lot of progress
examples: We've come a long way since the early days of the project.
The study of genes has come a long way in recent years.

definitions.definition: to need to make a lot of progress before you can achieve something
examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no definite answer to a question
examples: ‘How long will it take?’ ‘How long's a piece of string?’

definitions.definition: relating to a longer period in the future
examples: This measure inevitably means higher taxes in the long run.

definitions.definition: used to describe what will happen a long, short, etc. time in the future
examples: Such a development seems unlikely, at least in the short term(= it will not happen for quite a long time).
In the longer term, children of depressed mothers are more likely to suffer from childhood depression.
In the long term, our efforts will pay off.

definitions.definition: it makes no real difference which of two possible choices you make
used to say that the reasons for something are complicated and you would prefer not to give all the details
to reject something or treat it as not important; to stop dealing with a problem
examples: He tends to deal with disputes by kicking them into the long grass.

definitions.definition: used when you are telling somebody the essential facts about something or what effect it will have, without explaining all the details
the power and/or authority of something
examples: There is no escape from the long arm of the law.

definitions.definition: (to have) an unhappy or disappointed expression
examples: He took one look at her long face and said ‘What’s wrong?’
The news for the company isn't good, to judge from the long faces in the boardroom.

definitions.definition: old or too old
an attempt or a guess that is not likely to be successful but is worth trying
examples: It's a long shot, but it just might work.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that you will get to the point of what you are saying quickly, without including all the details
examples: To cut a long story short, we didn’t get home until 3 in the morning!
Anyway, long story short: we had this argument and I haven't seen him since.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
not nearly; not at all
examples: It's not over yet—not by a long chalk.

definitions.definition: to consider a problem or possibility very carefully and without hurrying
examples: We need to take a long, hard look at all the options.

definitions.definition: to consider what is likely to happen or be important over a long period of time rather than only considering the present situation
examples: As pension funds are investing for members’ retirements, they can take the long view.

origin: adjectiveOld Englishlang,long(adjective),lange,longe(adverb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanlang.

definitions.definition: a plan, thought or suggestion, especially about what to do in a particular situation
examples: It would bea good ideato call before we leave.
That's agreat idea!
I’ve had a brilliant idea!
I knew this was abad idea.
The party's latestbig ideais to create a Centre for Teaching Excellence.
The surprise party was Jane's idea.
idea for somethingHe alreadyhad an ideafor his next novel.
We’re having a meeting to try to come up with ideas for fundraising.
idea of somethingThe idea of a new museum was first discussed two years ago.
idea of doing somethingIlike the ideaof living on a boat.
We've beentoying with the idea of(= thinking about)getting a dog.
Her family expected her to go to college, but she hadother ideas.
I don't know what to do, but I'mopen to ideas.
It might be an idea(= it would be sensible)to try again later.
It seemed like a good idea at the time, and then it all went horribly wrong.
Ben is theideas manand the others do the day-to-day work.
(North American English usually)Ben is theidea man.

definitions.definition: a picture or an impression in your mind of what somebody/something is like
examples: The brochure should give youa good idea ofthe hotel.
I hadsome idea ofwhat the job would be like.
This film changed thevery ideaof what an outer space movie could be.
An evening at home watching TV is not my idea of a good time.
If this is your idea of a joke, then I don’t find it very funny.
I don't want anyonegetting the wrong idea(= getting the wrong impression about something).

definitions.definition: an opinion or a belief about something
examples: the experiences that shaped her ideas
ideas about somethingHe has some very strange ideas about education.
ideas on somethingHer ideas on the family are pretty old-fashioned.
ideas of somethingThese photographs challenge conventional ideas of beauty.
idea that…She rejects the idea that product quality has suffered.

definitions.definition: a feeling that something is possible or is true
examples: Where on earth did youget that idea?
idea that…Whatgave you the ideathat he'd be here?
idea where, who, etc…I have a prettygood ideawhere I left it—I hope I'm right.

definitions.definition: the aim or purpose of something
examples: You'll soonget the idea(= understand).
idea of somethingWhat's the ideaof the game?
idea of doing somethingThewhole ideaof going was so that we could meet her new boyfriend.
with the idea of doing somethingThey buy other people's tickets with the idea of reselling them.

definitions.definition: to start behaving in a more acceptable way, so that work gets done better, etc.
to give somebody hopes about something that may not be possible or likely; to make somebody act or think in an unreasonable way
examples: Who's been putting ideas into his head?

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you do not know something
examples: ‘What's she talking about?’ ‘I've no idea.’
He hasn't the faintest idea how to manage people.
I had no idea she’d had such a difficult life.
I don’t have any idea where he is.
She doesn't seem to have any idea of what I'm talking about.
I haven't got the faintest idea what she meant.
He hadn't had the slightest idea about what had been going on.

definitions.definition: to have found a very good or successful way of living, doing something, etc.
examples: He's certainly got the right idea—retiring at 55.
The party had the right idea, but failed to win over the voters.

definitions.definition: used to reply in a positive way to a suggestion that somebody has made
examples: Hey, that's an idea! And we could get a band, as well.

definitions.definition: used to encourage people and to tell them that they are doing something right
examples: That's the idea! You're doing fine.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is hard for somebody else to imagine
examples: You've no idea how much traffic there was tonight.

origin: late Middle English (as a term in Platonic philosophy): via Latin from Greekidea‘form, pattern’, from the base ofidein‘to see’.

definitions.definition: to do something for a particular purpose or in order to deal with a situation
examples: act to do somethingIt is vital that we act to stop the destruction of the rainforests.
+ adv./prep.Byacting quicklydoctors saved his life.
The government was criticized for failing to act decisively.
Initial reports indicated sheacted alone.
He claims heacted in self-defence.
Weacted in good faith(= believing that we were doing the right thing).
They are acting out of self-interest.
She was not acting for personal gain.

definitions.definition: to behave in a particular way
examples: + adv.The man was seenacting suspiciously.
I think that most employersact responsiblyin these situations.
Decide what your priorities are andact accordingly(= in an appropriate way).
act like somebody/somethingStop acting like spoilt children!
act as if/though…She was acting as if she'd seen a ghost.

definitions.definition: to perform a part in a play or film
examples: Have you ever acted?
He just can't act.
Most of the cast act well.
The play was well acted.
Who's acting the part of Hamlet?
She is acting the role of Juliet.

definitions.definition: to pretend by your behaviour to be a particular type of person
examples: + nounHe's been acting the devoted husband all day.
+ adj.I decided to act dumb.
to act weird/strange/normal/stupid
He acts all macho, but he’s a real softie underneath.

definitions.definition: to perform a particular role or function
examples: act as somethingSheacted as an adviserto the committee.
Can you act as interpreter?
We hope this sentence willact as a deterrentto others.
Culture canact as apowerfulcatalystfor urban regeneration.
The council is only really acting as an agent.
act like somethinghormones in the brain that act like natural painkillers

definitions.definition: to have an effect on something
examples: Alcohol acts quickly on the brain.
It took a few minutes for the drug to act.

definitions.definition: to behave in a stupid way in order to make people laugh, especially in a way that may also annoy them
examples: Quit playing the fool and get some work done!

definitions.definition: to behave in a way that is suitable for somebody of your age and not as though you were much younger
examples: Isn’t it time you started acting your age?

definitions.definition: to behave in a very confident way that annoys other people, for example by telling them what to do
examples: She was acting as if she owned the place.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: rather; to some extent
examples: These trousers are a bit tight.
‘Are you tired?’ ‘Yes, I am a bit.’
I was a bit disappointed by the film.
It costsa bit morethan I wanted to spend.
The future looksa little bitbrighter this morning.
I felta wee bitguilty about it.
I can lend you fifty pounds, if you want. That should help a bit.

definitions.definition: a short time or distance
examples: Wait a bit!
Can you move up a bit?
for a bitGreg thought for a bit before answering.
in a bitSee you in a bit.

definitions.definition: a small amount or piece of something
examples: Here are some usefulbits of information.
I have abit ofgoodnewsfor you.
Let me give youa little bitof advice.
Witha bit of luck, we'll be there by 12.
I've got a bit of shopping to do.
bits of paper/wood/plastic

definitions.definition: a part of something larger
examples: The best bit of the holiday was seeing the Grand Canyon.
I read it, but I missed out the boring bits.
bit about somethingI like the bit about owls in the first chapter.

definitions.definition: a large amount
examples: ‘How much does he earn?’‘Quite a bit!’
It raineda fair bitduring the night.
The new system willtake a bit ofgetting used to(= it will take a long time to get used to).

examples: We can finish this job on time if everyone does their bit.

examples: Rome is every bit as beautiful as Paris.

not at all; not in any way
examples: ‘Are you cold?’ ‘Not a bit.’

I'm not the least bit interested in football.

examples: He didn'ttake a blind bit of noticeof me(= he ignored me).

definitions.definition: not at all
examples: Normally I'm not the least bit shy.

examples: I love my kids to bits.

definitions.definition: having the skill, intelligence, opportunity, etc. needed to do something
examples: You must be able to speak French for this job.
A viral illness left herbarely ableto walk.
We're stillable to getvisas to come and go from Thailand.
I didn't feel able to disagree with him.
Will you be able to come?
Are you reallywilling and ableto do what is necessary?
They don't even seemable to seewhat's good about their ideas.
These families are less able to afford a balanced, healthy diet.

definitions.definition: intelligent; good at something
examples: She's the ablest student in the class.

origin: late Middle English (also in the sense ‘easy to use, suitable’): from Old Frenchhable, from Latinhabilis‘handy’, fromhabere‘to hold’.

definitions.definition: to be the explanation or cause of something
examples: The poor weather may have accounted for the small crowd.
Oh well, that accounts for it(= I understand now why it happened).
The differences in achievement between the pupils are partly accounted for by differences in age.

definitions.definition: to give an explanation of something
examples: How do you account for the show's success?
He was unable to account for the error.
The increase can be fully accounted for.

definitions.definition: to be a particular amount or part of something
examples: The Japanese market accounts for 35 per cent of the company's revenue.

definitions.definition: to know where somebody/something is or what has happened to them, especially after an accident
examples: All passengers have now been accounted for.
Three files cannot be accounted for.

definitions.definition: to defeat or destroy somebody/something
examples: Their anti-aircraft guns accounted for five enemy bombers.

definitions.definition: to give a record of how the money in your care has been spent
examples: We have to account for every penny we spend on business trips.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a large number or amount of somebody/something
examples: What a lot of presents!
A lot of people are coming to the meeting.
black coffee with lots of sugar
I saw a lot of her(= I saw her often)last summer.

origin: Old Englishhlot(noun), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlot, GermanLos. The original meaning was ‘by lot’ and (by extension) the sense ‘a portion assigned to someone’; this gave rise to the other noun senses. The pronoun and adverb uses date from the early 19th cent.

definitions.definition: several different sorts of the same thing
examples: There is awide varietyof patterns to choose from.
He resignedfor a variety of reasons.
This tool can be usedin a variety of ways.
The text consists of extractsfrom a variety of sources.
articles and essays on avariety of topics
I don't eat meat, only fish, but there's a good variety available here.
The day-long excursion is one of a variety on offer for tourists.
I was impressed by the variety of dishes on offer.

definitions.definition: the quality of not being the same or not doing the same thing all the time
examples: It's the variety that makes my job so enjoyable.
We all need variety in our diet.
We want more variety in our work.
a constant craving for variety and adventure
The festival demonstrates the variety of the UK music scene.

definitions.definition: a type of a thing, for example a plant or language, that is different from the others in the same general group
examples: Apples come in a great many varieties.
variety of somethingdifferent varietiesof English
This is a very rare variety of orchid.
A typical estate in the region will grow at least threegrape varieties.
My cooking is of the ‘quick and simple’ variety.

definitions.definition: a form of theatre or television entertainment that consists of a series of short performances, such as singing, dancing and funny acts
examples: avariety show/theatre

definitions.definition: having a range of different experiences makes life more interesting

origin: late 15th cent.: from Frenchvariétéor Latinvarietas, fromvarius‘changing, diverse’.

definitions.definition: to say that somebody has done something wrong or is guilty of something
examples: accuse somebody of somethingtoaccuse somebody of murder/a crime
accuse somebody of doing somethingShe accused him of lying.
be accused of somethingThe government was accused of incompetence.
to befalsely/wrongly/unjustly accusedof something
(formal)Theystand accusedof crimes against humanity.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchacuser, from Latinaccusare‘call to account’, fromad-‘towards’ +causa‘reason, motive, lawsuit’.

definitions.definition: later than something; following something in time
examples: We'll leave after lunch.
They arrivedshortly after5.
Not long after thathe resigned.
Let’s meetthe day after tomorrow/the week after next.
After winning the prize she became famous overnight.
After an hour I went home(= when an hour had passed).
(North American English)It’s ten after seven in the morning(= 7.10 a.m.).

definitions.definition: next to and following somebody/something in order or importance
examples: Your name comes after mine in the list.
He's the tallest, after Richard.
After you(= Please go first).
After you withthe charger(= Can I have it next?).

definitions.definition: behind somebody when they have left; following somebody
examples: Shut the door after you.
I'm always having to clean up after the children(= clean the place after they have left it dirty and untidy).
He ran after her with the book.
She was left staring after him.

definitions.definition: in contrast to something
examples: It was pleasantly cool in the house after the sticky heat outside.

definitions.definition: as a result of or because of something that has happened
examples: I'll never forgive them after what they did.

definitions.definition: despite something; although something has happened
examples: I can't believe she'd do that, not after all I've done for her.

definitions.definition: used to show that something happens many times or continuously
examples: day after day of hot weather
I've told you time after time not to do that.

definitions.definition: trying to find or catch somebody/something
examples: The police are after them.
He's after a job at our place.

definitions.definition: about somebody/something
examples: She asked after you(= how you were).

definitions.definition: in the style of somebody/something; following the example of somebody/something
examples: a painting after Goya
We named the baby Ena after her grandmother.

definitions.definition: happening or done later than the time or event mentioned
examples: after-hours drinking(= after closing time)
an after-school club
after-dinner mints

definitions.definition: despite what has been said or expected
examples: So you made it after all!

definitions.definition: used when you are explaining something, or giving a reason
examples: He should have paid. He suggested it, after all.

definitions.definition: to be going to do something soon; to be intending to do something soon
to have just done something

origin: Old Englishæfter, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchachter.

definitions.definition: one more time; on another occasion
examples: This must neverhappen again.
Try again—it takes practice.
Can westart again, please?
Could you say it again, please?
When will I see you again?
This author again proves to be a superb storyteller.
I've told youagain and again(= many times)not to do that.
I'll have to write itall over again(= again from the beginning).
She triedover and over again(= many times)to get it right.
He hasyet again(= as has happened many times before)shown that he cannot be trusted.
Once again(= as had happened several times before), the train was late.

definitions.definition: showing that somebody/something is in the same place or state that they were in originally
examples: We're very happy to be here together again.
He was glad to be home again.
You'll soon feel well again.
She spends two hours a day getting to work andback again.

definitions.definition: added to an amount that is already there
examples: The cost is abouthalf as much againas it was two years ago.
I'd likethe same again(= the same amount or the same thing).

definitions.definition: used to show that a comment or fact is connected with what you have just said
examples: And again, we must think of the cost.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact or an opinion that contrasts with what you have just said
examples: We might buy it but then again we might not.

definitions.definition: used when you ask somebody to tell you something or repeat something that you think they have told you already
examples: What was the name again?

definitions.definition: from time to time; occasionally
examples: Every now and again she checked to see if he was still asleep.

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to serve you the same drink as before
examples: Same again, please!

definitions.definition: often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

origin: Old Englishongēan,ongægn, etc., of Germanic origin; related to Germanentgegen‘opposite’.

definitions.definition: to have the same opinion as somebody; to say that you have the same opinion
examples: When he said that, I had to agree.
+ speech‘That's true’, she agreed.
agree with somebodyItotally agreewith you!
agree about/on somethingYou can't expect them to agree on everything.
agree with somebody about/on somethingHe agreed with them about the need for change.
agree with somethingMany expertsagree wholeheartedlywith this statement.
Itend to agreewith her political views.
agree (that)…All parties agree (that) urgent action is required.
We agreed (that) the proposal was a good one.
‘It's terrible.’ ‘Icouldn't agree more!’(= I completely agree)

definitions.definition: to approve of something because you think it is morally right
examples: agree with doing somethingI don't agree with hitting children as a punishment.
agree with somethingI'm not sure I agree with the government's policy on this.

definitions.definition: to say ‘yes’; to say that you will do what somebody wants or that you will allow something to happen
examples: I asked for a pay rise and she agreed.
agree to somethingThe government has finallyagreed in principle(= agreed in general but not in detail)to the terms of the deal.
Do you think he'll agree to their proposal?
agree (that)…She agreed (that) I could go early.
agree to do somethingHereluctantly agreedto pay for the damage.
She agreed to let me go early.

definitions.definition: to decide with somebody else to do something or to have something
examples: agree on/upon somethingCan we agree on a date?
The new plan was agreed upon between the two sides.
agree somethingThey met at the agreed time.
Can we agree a price?
agree to do somethingWe agreed to meet on Thursday.
agree what, where, etc…We couldn't agree what to do.
as agreedThey left at ten, as agreed.

definitions.definition: to officially accept a plan, request, etc.
examples: The companyagreed a dealworth $100 million.
Next year's budget has been agreed.
We arranged to meet a few days later and agree terms.

definitions.definition: to be the same as something
examples: The figures do not agree.
agree with somethingYour account of the accident does not agree with hers.

definitions.definition: to match a word or phrase innumber,genderorperson
examples: In ‘Tom likes jazz’, the singular verb ‘likes’ agrees with the subject ‘Tom’.

definitions.definition: if two peopleagree to differ/disagree, they accept that they have different opinions about something, but they decide not to discuss it any longer
examples: We must just agree to differ on this.
In the end we agreed to disagree.
I think you're wrong. Let's just agree to differ.

origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchagreer, based on Latinad-‘to’ +gratus‘pleasing’.

definitions.definition: to try or plan to achieve something
examples: He has alwaysaimed high(= tried to achieve a lot).
aim for somethingWe should aim for a bigger share of the market.
The team is aiming for the top this season.
Don't try to aim for perfection.
aim at somethingThe government is aiming at a 50% reduction in unemployment.
aim to do somethingThey are aiming to reduce unemployment by 50%.
The project aims to provide employment for people with learning difficulties.
We aim to be there around six.
aim at doing somethingThe training programme aims at raising employees' awareness about human rights.

definitions.definition: to have something as an aim
examples: The initiative isspecifically aimedat helping young people.
These measures are aimed at preventing violent crime.

definitions.definition: to say or do something that is intended to influence or affect a particular person or group
examples: be aimed at somebodyThe courses areaimed primarilyat older people.
My criticism wasn't aimed at you.
The book is aimed at very young children.

definitions.definition: to point or direct a weapon, camera, shot, kick, etc. at somebody/something
examples: aim at somebody/somethingI was aiming at the tree but hit the car by mistake.
aim for somebody/somethingAim for the middle of the target.
aim something at somebody/somethingShe raised one leg and aimed an almighty kick at the door.
aim somethingI aimed my camera and got one shot.
aim something + adv./prep.He aimed the rifle in the direction of the target and fired.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: rather; to some extent
examples: These trousers are a bit tight.
‘Are you tired?’ ‘Yes, I am a bit.’
I was a bit disappointed by the film.
It costsa bit morethan I wanted to spend.
The future looksa little bitbrighter this morning.
I felta wee bitguilty about it.
I can lend you fifty pounds, if you want. That should help a bit.

definitions.definition: a short time or distance
examples: Wait a bit!
Can you move up a bit?
for a bitGreg thought for a bit before answering.
in a bitSee you in a bit.

definitions.definition: a small amount or piece of something
examples: Here are some usefulbits of information.
I have abit ofgoodnewsfor you.
Let me give youa little bitof advice.
Witha bit of luck, we'll be there by 12.
I've got a bit of shopping to do.
bits of paper/wood/plastic

definitions.definition: a part of something larger
examples: The best bit of the holiday was seeing the Grand Canyon.
I read it, but I missed out the boring bits.
bit about somethingI like the bit about owls in the first chapter.

definitions.definition: a large amount
examples: ‘How much does he earn?’‘Quite a bit!’
It raineda fair bitduring the night.
The new system willtake a bit ofgetting used to(= it will take a long time to get used to).

definitions.definition: the smallest unit of information used by a computer
a metal bar that is put in a horse’s mouth so that the rider can control it
a tool or part of a tool fordrilling(= making)holes
an amount of money equal to 12½ cents
a person’s sexual organs
to be very sad or worried
examples: Inside I'm in bits because I miss him so much.
I’m in bits about the problems I’ve had.
She was in bits over her decision to quit.

definitions.definition: behaviour or ideas that are typical of a particular group, type of person or activity
examples: She couldn't accept the whole drug-culture bit.

definitions.definition: a piece at a time; gradually
examples: He assembled the model aircraft bit by bit.
Bit by bit memories of the night came back to me.

definitions.definition: not fair or not reasonable
examples: It's a bit much calling me at three in the morning.
The noise from next door is getting a bit much.

definitions.definition: used when talking about unpleasant or negative things or ideas, to mean ‘rather a…’
examples: We may have a bit of a problem on our hands.
The rail strike is a bit of a pain.

definitions.definition: a person that you think is sexually attractive
a person of a low social class who has a sexual relationship with somebody of a higher class
the boyfriend or girlfriend of somebody who is already married or in a steady sexual relationship with somebody else
examples: Is he your bit on the side?

definitions.definition: small objects or items of various kinds
examples: She stuffed all her bits and pieces into a bag and left.
I just need to get a few bits and pieces at the supermarket.

definitions.definition: to use bombs or other weapons to destroy somebody/something completely
examples: The plane was blown to pieces when the bomb exploded.

definitions.definition: impatient to do or start doing something
examples: Gerry’s champing at the bit to go to college.

definitions.definition: to do your share of a task
examples: We can finish this job on time if everyone does their bit.

definitions.definition: just as good, bad, etc.; equally good, bad, etc.
examples: Rome is every bit as beautiful as Paris.
He’s every bit as clever as she is.

definitions.definition: to become very enthusiastic about something that you have started to do so that you are unlikely to stop until you have finished
not at all; not in any way
examples: ‘Are you cold?’ ‘Not a bit.’
It's not a bit of use(= there's no point in)complaining.
I'm not the least bit interested in football.
I don't like that idea one bit.

definitions.definition: used for saying that something that you had expected to happen did not happen
examples: You'd think she'd be tired after the journey but not a bit of it!

definitions.definition: not any
examples: He didn'ttake a blind bit of noticeof me(= he ignored me).
It won'tmake the blindest bit of difference(= it will make no difference at all).

definitions.definition: not at all
examples: Normally I'm not the least bit shy.
He wasn't the slightest bit interested in answering our questions.

definitions.definition: into small pieces
examples: The book fell to bits in my hands.
All the crockery had been smashed to bits.
She took the engine to bits, then carefully put it together again.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: I love my kids to bits.
She wasthrilled to bitswhen I said I'd come.

origin: senses 1 to 5 and senses 9 to 10 Old Englishbita‘bite, mouthful’, of Germanic origin; related to GermanBissen, also tobite.senses 7 to 8 Old Englishbite‘biting, a bite’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchbeetand GermanBiss, also tobite.sense 6 1940s: blend ofbinaryanddigit.

definitions.definition: a group of people or things that are the same in some way; a particular variety or type
examples: kind of somebody/somethingthree kinds of cakes/cake
What kind of house do you live in?
They sellall kinds ofthings.
I need to buy paper and pencils, thatkind of thing.
His diet's terrible—burgers, chips, pizzas, thatkind of stuff.
I'm thekind of personwho hates to do anything wrong.
We have thesame kind ofcar.
that kind of somethingI'll never have that kind of money(= as much money as that).

this kind of somethingThis kind of exercise is very popular.
of a kindExercises of this kind are very popular.
music ofall/various/different kinds

definitions.definition: consisting of goods or services, not money
examples: As well as his salary, he gets benefits in kind.

definitions.definition: with the same thing
examples: She insulted him and he responded in kind.

definitions.definition: used to show that something you are saying is not exact
examples: I had a kind of feeling this might happen.

definitions.definition: slightly; in some ways
examples: That made me feel kind of stupid.
I like him, kind of.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that the situation is very different from what has been said
examples: ‘I was terrible!’ ‘You were nothing of the kind.’

definitions.definition: not as good as it could be
examples: You're making progress of a kind.

definitions.definition: very similar
examples: They're two of a kind—both workaholics!

definitions.definition: the only one like this
examples: My father was one of a kind—I'll never be like him.

definitions.definition: something like what has been said
examples: ‘He's resigning.’ ‘I'd suspected something of the kind.’

origin: nounOld Englishcynd(e),gecynd(e), of Germanic origin; related tokin. The original sense was ‘nature, the natural order’, also ‘innate character, form, or condition’ (compare with the adjectivekind); hence ‘a class ’or‘ race distinguished by innate characteristics’.

definitions.definition: forward
examples: I was just walking along singing to myself.
He pointed out various landmarks as we drove along.

definitions.definition: with somebody
examples: We're going for a swim. Why don't you come along?
I'll be along(= I'll join you)in a few minutes.

definitions.definition: towards a better state or position
examples: The book's coming along nicely.

definitions.definition: in addition to somebody/something; in the same way as somebody/something
examples: She lost her job when the factory closed, along with hundreds of others.

origin: Old Englishandlang, of West Germanic origin; related to the adjectivelong.

definitions.definition: what is measured in minutes, hours, days, etc.
examples: The world exists in time and space.
The changing seasons mark the passage of time.
in timeA visit to the museum will take youback in timeto the 1930s.

over timePerceptions change over time(= as time passes).

As time went bywe saw less and less of each other.
The main character is able to travel through time.

definitions.definition: the time shown on a clock in minutes and hours
examples: What time is it/What’s the time?
Do you have the time?
(British English)What time do you make it?
(North American English)What time do you have?
Let me just check the time.
The time is now half past ten.
(British English)Can shetell the timeyet(= say what time it is by looking at a clock)?
(North American English)Can shetell timeyet?
My watchkeeps perfect time(= always shows the correct time).
Look at the time! We'll be late.
This time tomorrowI'll be in Canada.

definitions.definition: the time measured in a particular part of the world
examples: 6 o’clock local time

definitions.definition: the time when something happens or when something should happen
examples: What time do you finish work?

A computer screen shows arrival and departure times.
time to do somethingI think it's time to go to bed.

time for somethingIt's time for lunch.
time (that)…It's time the kids were in bed.

by the time (that)…By the time you get there the meeting will be over.
at a… timeHave I called at a bad time? Shall I call back later?

at the timeI didn't realize it at the time, but that was the best day of my life.
The promotion came at just theright timefor me.
The new product will launchwhen the time is right.
You'll feel differently about itwhen the time comes(= when it happens).
around the time of somethingSeveral articles were published around the time of his death in 2018.

definitions.definition: an amount of time; the amount of time available to work, rest, etc.
examples: I can't go with you—I don'thave time.
Hespendsa lot of histimeworking.
time for somethingDo you have time for a chat?
time to do somethingAllowplenty of timeto get to the airport.
I can probablymake the timeto see them.
Ittakes timeto make changes in the law.
We haveno time to lose(= we must hurry).
We got to the airport with time to spare.
She plans to study overseas for an extendedperiod of time.
What do you like to do in yourfree time?
She doesn’t have muchspare time.
Sorry, I can’t spare the time.
Don'twasteany moretime!
What awaste of time!

I didn't finish the test—I ran out of time.
Time's up—have you worked out the answer yet?

Jane's worked herefor some time(= for a fairly long period of time).

The journey time is two hours.
I don’t want to take up too much of your precious time.
What’s the hurry? We have all the time in the world.

definitions.definition: a period of time, either long or short, during which you do something or something happens
examples: His injuries willtake a long timeto heal.
It took them a long time to get home.
Did you have to wait a long time to see the doctor?
for a timeI lived in Egypt for a time.
I was only there fora short time.
The early morning is the besttime of day.
Her parents dieda long time ago.
At one time(= at a period of time in the past)Emily was my best friend.
Mr Curtis was the managerin my time(= when I was working there).

definitions.definition: a period of history connected with particular events or experiences in people’s lives
examples: at the time of somethingThe movie is set at the time of the Russian Revolution.
in… time(s)the politician who promised ‘peace in our time’
in ancient times
The Industrial Revolution took place in Victorian times.
the violent times we live in(= the present period of history)
in times of somethingin times of trouble

Times are hard for the unemployed.
Times have changedsince Grandma was young.

definitions.definition: an occasion when you do something or when something happens
examples: Every timeI hear that song I feel happy.
It doesn't matter if you don't win every time(= every time you play).
Next timeyou're here let's have lunch together.

He's determined to passthis time.

I see her two or threetimes a week.
How many times(= how often)do I have to tell you not to do that?
(especially North American English)I rememberone time(= once)we had to abandon our car in the snow.
(formal)At no time didI give my consent to the plan.

definitions.definition: an event or occasion that you experience in a particular way
examples: Did youhave a good timein Spain?
They'rehaving a hard timedealing with her illness.
I had an awful time in the hospital.
Mike and I shared some really good times.

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event
examples: The winner's time was 11.6 seconds.

one of the fastest times ever

definitions.definition: the number ofbeats(= units of rhythm)in abarof music
examples: This piece is in four-four time.
a slow waltz time
