definitions.definition: the correct speed and rhythm of a piece of music
examples: in time to somethingTry and dance in time to the music(= with the same speed and rhythm).
Clap your hands tokeep time(= sing or move with the correct speed and rhythm).
in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)
He always plays in perfect time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.
The plane took off an hour behind time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.
Hot water is available at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.
She ran up the stairs two at a time.
He surfs the internet for hours at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.
The rain will become heavy at times in the afternoon.
He can be rather moody at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.
If you didn't win a prize, better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.
to deliberately make a situation difficult and unpleasant for somebody
examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.
You'll get used to the work in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?
The ambulance got there just in time(= to save somebody's life).

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
Time has flown since the holiday began.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.
Time was when we never needed to lock our house at night.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: to try or plan to achieve something
examples: He has alwaysaimed high(= tried to achieve a lot).
aim for somethingWe should aim for a bigger share of the market.
The team is aiming for the top this season.
Don't try to aim for perfection.
aim at somethingThe government is aiming at a 50% reduction in unemployment.
aim to do somethingThey are aiming to reduce unemployment by 50%.
The project aims to provide employment for people with learning difficulties.
We aim to be there around six.
aim at doing somethingThe training programme aims at raising employees' awareness about human rights.

definitions.definition: to have something as an aim
examples: The initiative isspecifically aimedat helping young people.
These measures are aimed at preventing violent crime.

definitions.definition: to say or do something that is intended to influence or affect a particular person or group
examples: be aimed at somebodyThe courses areaimed primarilyat older people.
My criticism wasn't aimed at you.
The book is aimed at very young children.

definitions.definition: to point or direct a weapon, camera, shot, kick, etc. at somebody/something
examples: aim at somebody/somethingI was aiming at the tree but hit the car by mistake.
aim for somebody/somethingAim for the middle of the target.
aim something at somebody/somethingShe raised one leg and aimed an almighty kick at the door.
aim somethingI aimed my camera and got one shot.
aim something + adv./prep.He aimed the rifle in the direction of the target and fired.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a group or type of people or things that are similar in a particular way
examples: sort of somebody/something‘What sort ofmusic do you like?’ ‘Oh,all sorts.’
This sort ofproblem is quite common./These sorts ofproblems are quite common.
He'sthe sort of personwho only cares about money.
The sort/sorts of peoplewho are having large families in the US and Ireland are very similar.
For dessert there's a fruit pieof some sort(= you are not sure what kind).
Most people went on training coursesof one sort or another(= of various types)last year.
(informal)There were snacks—peanuts, olives,that sort of thing.
(informal)There areall sorts ofactivities(= many different ones)for kids at the campsite.
(informal)What sort ofprice did you want to pay?(= approximately how much)
(informal)What sort oftime do you call this?(= I'm very angry that you have come so late.)
Statements like these are notthe sort of stuffyou'd expect from one of Australia's most powerful women.
(informal)Tracey has been causing her motherall sorts of problems(= many different ones).

definitions.definition: a particular type of person
examples: My brother is not the sort to lie about something like that.

definitions.definition: the process of putting data in a particular order
examples: to do a sort

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody’s behaviour is very strange or unusual but that everyone is different and likes different things
examples: ‘They spent their honeymoon mountain climbing.’ ‘Oh well, it takes all sorts!’

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that the situation is very different from what has been said
examples: ‘I was terrible!’ ‘You were nothing of the sort.’

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that something is not a good example of a particular type of thing
examples: He offered us an apology of sorts.

definitions.definition: ill or upset
examples: She was tired and out of sorts by the time she arrived home.
Are you feeling all right? You look a bit out of sorts.

definitions.definition: to some extent but in a way that you cannot easily describe
examples: She sort of pretends that she doesn't really care.
‘Do you understand?’ ‘Sort of.’

definitions.definition: used for describing something in a not very exact way
examples: I had a sort of feeling that he wouldn't come.
She was jumping around like some sort of kangaroo.
They're a sort of greenish-blue colour.
I was glad about it afterwards, in a funny sort of way.

origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchsorte, from an alteration of Latinsors,sort-‘lot, condition’.

definitions.definition: on the subject of somebody/something; in connection with somebody/something
examples: a book about flowers
Tell meall aboutit.
What's she so angry about?
There's something strange about him.
There's something about this business that is not quite right.
I don't know what you'reon about(= talking about).
There's nothing you can do about it now.

definitions.definition: used to describe the purpose or an aspect of something
examples: Movies areall aboutmaking money these days.
What was all that about?(= what was the reason for what has just happened?)

definitions.definition: busy with something; doing something
examples: Everywhere people were going about their daily business.
Andwhile you’re about it…(= while you’re doing that)

definitions.definition: in many directions in a place; here and there
examples: We wandered about the town for an hour or so.
He looked about the room.

definitions.definition: in various parts of a place; here and there
examples: The papers were strewn about the room.

definitions.definition: next to a place or person; in the area mentioned
examples: She's somewhere about the office.

definitions.definition: surrounding somebody/something
examples: She wore a shawl about her shoulders.

definitions.definition: used when asking for information about somebody/something
examples: How about Ruth? Have you heard from her?
I'm having fish. What about you?

definitions.definition: used to make a suggestion
examples: How about going for a walk?
(especially North American English)How about we go for a walk?
What about a break?

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: away from a place; out
examples: They set forth at dawn.
Huge chimneys belched forth smoke and grime.

definitions.definition: towards a place; forwards
examples: Water gushed forth from a hole in the rock.

definitions.definition: used at the end of a list to show that it continues in the same way
examples: We discussed everything—when to go, what to see and so on.

definitions.definition: from one place to another and back again repeatedly
examples: ferries sailing back and forth between the islands

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that day forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

origin: Old English, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvoortand Germanfort, from an Indo-European root shared byfore-.

definitions.definition: a thing that you can choose to do or have out of two or more possibilities
examples: The car is too expensive so we're trying to find a cheaper alternative.
There is a vegetarian alternative on the menu every day.
Wehad no alternative but tofire Gibson.
tooffer/provide an alternative
alternative to somethingDoes this offer aviable alternativeto the existing system?
as an alternative to somethingFruit juice is provided as an alternative to alcoholic drinks.
alternative for somebodyThis treatment is theonly alternativefor some patients.

origin: mid 16th cent. (in the sense ‘alternating, alternate’): from Frenchalternatif,-iveor medieval Latinalternativus, from Latinalternare‘interchange’, fromalternus‘every other’, fromalter‘other’.

definitions.definition: to make a deeply felt request, especially for something that is needed immediately
examples: We are appealing(= asking for money)on behalf of all the victims of this terrible disaster.
appeal for somethingCommunity leaders appealed for calm(= urged people to remain calm).
appeal to somebodyAppeal directly to the public, answer their questions.
appeal to somebody for somethingThe government appealed to the British people for help.
appeal for somebody to do somethingPolice have appealed for witnesses to come forward.
appeal to somebody to do somethingOrganizers appealed to the crowd not to panic.

definitions.definition: to attract or interest somebody
examples: The prospect of a long wait in the rain did not appeal.
appeal to somebodyThe design has to appeal to all ages and social groups.
His movies appeal to a broad audience.
The ad is designed to appeal to female voters.

definitions.definition: to make a formal request to a court or to somebody in authority for a judgment or a decision to be changed
examples: He said he would appeal after being found guilty on four counts of murder.
appeal against somethingThe company is appealing against the ruling.
appeal to somebody/something against somethingHe is seekingleave to appealto the High Court against the decision.
appeal to somebody/somethingPermission to appeal to the House of Lords was refused.

definitions.definition: to try to persuade somebody to do something by suggesting that it is a fair, reasonable or honest thing to do
examples: They needed to appeal to his sense of justice.

origin: Middle English (in legal contexts): from Old Frenchapel(noun),apeler(verb), from Latinappellare‘to address’, based onad-‘to’ +pellere‘to drive’.

definitions.definition: to try or plan to achieve something
examples: He has alwaysaimed high(= tried to achieve a lot).
aim for somethingWe should aim for a bigger share of the market.
The team is aiming for the top this season.
Don't try to aim for perfection.
aim at somethingThe government is aiming at a 50% reduction in unemployment.
aim to do somethingThey are aiming to reduce unemployment by 50%.
The project aims to provide employment for people with learning difficulties.
We aim to be there around six.
aim at doing somethingThe training programme aims at raising employees' awareness about human rights.

definitions.definition: to have something as an aim
examples: The initiative isspecifically aimedat helping young people.
These measures are aimed at preventing violent crime.

definitions.definition: to say or do something that is intended to influence or affect a particular person or group
examples: be aimed at somebodyThe courses areaimed primarilyat older people.
My criticism wasn't aimed at you.
The book is aimed at very young children.

definitions.definition: to point or direct a weapon, camera, shot, kick, etc. at somebody/something
examples: aim at somebody/somethingI was aiming at the tree but hit the car by mistake.
aim for somebody/somethingAim for the middle of the target.
aim something at somebody/somethingShe raised one leg and aimed an almighty kick at the door.
aim somethingI aimed my camera and got one shot.
aim something + adv./prep.He aimed the rifle in the direction of the target and fired.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a building where paintings and other works of art are shown to the public

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: completely
examples: She was dressed all in white.
He lives all alone.
The coffee went all over my skirt.

definitions.definition: very
examples: She was all excited.
Now don't get all upset about it.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is more than you would like
examples: I'm all too aware of the problems.
The end of the trip came all too soon.

definitions.definition: to each side
examples: The score was four all.

definitions.definition: all the time; from the beginning
examples: I realized it was in my pocket all along.

definitions.definition: in every way; in all respects
examples: a good performance all around

definitions.definition: for each person
examples: She bought drinks all around.

definitions.definition: so much better, harder, etc.
examples: We'll have to work all the harder with two people off sick.
Her difficult childhood makes her achievements all the more remarkable.

definitions.definition: almost
examples: The party was all but over when we arrived.
It was all but impossible to read his writing.

definitions.definition: everything or everyone except something/somebody
examples: All but one of the plates were damaged.

definitions.definition: physically tired
examples: At the end of the race he felt all in.

definitions.definition: including everything
examples: The trip cost £750 all in.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an amount, a size, etc. usually when it is very small
examples: It must be all of 100 metres to the car!

definitions.definition: everywhere
examples: We looked all over for the ring.

definitions.definition: what you would expect of the person mentioned
examples: That sounds like my sister all over.

definitions.definition: in every way; in all respects
examples: a good performance all round

definitions.definition: for each person
examples: She bought drinks all round.

definitions.definition: (not) having a healthy mind; (not) thinking clearly
examples: My dog behaves very oddly at times—I don't think he's quite all there.

definitions.definition: used to say what the most important aspect of something is
examples: It's all about money these days.

definitions.definition: to believe strongly that something should be done
examples: They're all for saving money where they can.

definitions.definition: to show a lot of interest in or enthusiasm for somebody
examples: He was all over her at the party.

definitions.definition: to be very attractive or impressive
examples: He thinks he's all that.

definitions.definition: to be the end for somebody
examples: It looks as though it's all up with us now(= we are ruined, have no further chances, etc.).

origin: Old Englishall,eall, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchaland Germanall.

definitions.definition: except for
examples: I've finished apart from the last question.

definitions.definition: in addition to; as well as
examples: Apart from their house in London, they also have a villa in Spain.
It was a difficult time. Apart from everything else, we had financial problems.
You've got to help. Apart from anything else you're my brother.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to get to a place, especially at the end of a journey
examples: I'll wait until they arrive.
I was pleased to hear youarrived homesafely.
toarrive early/latefor a meeting
arrive in…She'll arrive in New York at noon.
arrive at…The train arrived at the station 20 minutes late.
We didn't arrive back at the hotel until very late.
By the time Iarrived on the scene, it was all over.
Pupils may benewly arrivedin Britain, with little or no English.
A police officerarrivedjustin timeto see the burglars walking out of the building.
Theyarrived too lateto take any action.
The police arrived to arrest him.

definitions.definition: to be brought to somebody
examples: A letter arrived for you this morning.
Send your application to arrive by 31 October.
We waited an hour for our lunch to arrive.
The new product will arrive on supermarket shelves(= be available)early next year.

definitions.definition: to happen or to come, especially when you have been waiting for it
examples: The wedding day finally arrived.
The baby arrived(= was born)early.

definitions.definition: somebody has become successful
examples: He knew he had arrived when he was shortlisted for the Man Booker prize.
The lad has arrived as a Premiership goalkeeper.

origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘reach the shore after a voyage’): from Old Frenchariver, based on Latinad-‘to’ +ripa‘shore’.

definitions.definition: used instead ofsomethingin negative sentences and in questions; afterif/whether; and after verbs such asprevent,ban,avoid, etc.
examples: Would you likeanything else?
There's never anything worth watching on TV.
If you remember anything at all, please let us know.
We hope to prevent anything unpleasant from happening.

definitions.definition: any thing at all, when it does not matter which
examples: I'm so hungry, I'll eat anything.

definitions.definition: any thing of importance
examples: Is there anything(= any truth)in these rumours?

definitions.definition: definitely not
examples: The hotel was anything but cheap.
It wasn't cheap. Anything but.

definitions.definition: similar to somebody/something
examples: He isn't anything like my first boss.

definitions.definition: very happy, quick, etc.
examples: I felt as pleased as anything.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: They're always slagging me off like anything.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that something is not as good, not enough, etc.
examples: The book wasn't anything like as good as her first one.

definitions.definition: definitely not
examples: I wouldn't give it up for anything.

definitions.definition: or another thing of a similar type
examples: If you want to call a meeting or anything, just let me know.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to make a formal request, usually in writing, for something such as a job, a loan, permission for something, a place at a university, etc.
examples: You should apply in person/by letter.
apply for somethingI have decided toapply forthis newjob.
They have successfully applied for political asylum.
toapply for a visa/passport/grant/licence
The developersapplied forplanningpermissionto build over 200 houses.
apply to somebody/somethingto apply to a company/university
apply to somebody/something for somethingThey may apply to the court for the decision to be reversed.
apply to do somethingHe has applied to join the army.

definitions.definition: to affect or be relevant to somebody/something
examples: Special conditions apply if you are under 18.
apply to somebody/somethingTherules appliedto employees and their behaviour at work.
What I am saying applies only to some of you.
The policy willapply equallyto everyone.
Thelaw appliedonly to workers in member states.
apply something to somebody/somethingThe word ‘unexciting’ could never be applied to her novels.

definitions.definition: to use something or make something work in a particular situation
examples: apply somethingtoapplypoliticalpressure
The challenge allows students toapplyeconomicprinciplesthey learn in class.
to apply economic sanctions
to apply a test/method/technique/approach/rule
We need toapplythe samestandardsto ourselves as to others.
apply something to somethingThe new technology was applied to farming.
Now is the time to apply the insights you have gained from your studies.

definitions.definition: to put or spread something such as paint, cream, etc. onto a surface
examples: apply something to somethingApply the cream sparingly to your face and neck.
The glue should be applied to both surfaces.
apply somethingShe combed her hair and carefully applied her make-up.
The paint was applied with a fine brush.

definitions.definition: to work at something or study something very hard
examples: apply yourselfYou would pass your exams if you applied yourself.
apply yourself/something to (doing) somethingWe applied our minds to finding a solution to our problem.

definitions.definition: to press on something hard with your hand, foot, etc. to make something work or have an effect on something
examples: apply somethingto apply the brakes (of a vehicle)
apply something to somethingPressure applied to the wound will stop the bleeding.

origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchaplier, from Latinapplicare‘fold, fasten to’, fromad-‘to’ +plicare‘to fold’.

definitions.definition: a thing that is caused or produced because of something else
examples: And did your interventionproduce thedesiredresult?
toyield/achieve a result
result of somethingThe company's failure was adirect resultof bad management.
This book is the result of 25 years of research.
Theend result(= the final one)of her hard work was a place at medical school.
as a resultHe made one big mistake, and, as a result, lost his job.
as a result of somethingShe died as a result of her injuries.
with the result that…The farm was flooded, with the result that the crop was lost.
with… resultsHe decided to get involved, with disastrous results.

definitions.definition: the final score or the name of the winner in a sports event, competition, election, etc.
examples: theelection results
The announcer was reading out the football results.
result of somethingThey willannounce the resultof the vote tonight.

definitions.definition: a victory or a success
examples: We badly need toget a resultfrom this match.
This was a great result for us.
He left. Result!(= used when you are happy something has happened)

definitions.definition: the mark or grade you get in an exam or in a number of exams
examples: Have you had your results yet?
When do you get yourexam results?

definitions.definition: the information that you get from a scientific test or piece of research
examples: Other research hasyieldedsimilarresults.
The doctor will explain your bloodtest results.
apositive/negativetestresult
result of somethingtheresults of a study
Theresultsof our experimentindicatethat environmental factors play a part.
Theresults obtainedwere compared with previous results.

definitions.definition: the information that you get from a computer search
examples: My search produced several thousand results.
The user can control how thesearch resultsare displayed.
the first page of search results

definitions.definition: things that are achieved successfully
examples: The project is beginning toshow results.
a coach who knows how toget resultsfrom his players
For best results, defrost fully before use.

definitions.definition: the amount of profit or loss made by a business over a particular period of time
examples: Companies are required by law toreport theirfinancialresultson a quarterly basis.
The company willpublish itsinterimresults(= before the final results are known)on Monday.
The corporation is expected toannounceimprovedresultsfor the last quarter.

origin: late Middle English (as a verb): from medieval Latinresultare‘to result’, earlier in the sense ‘spring back’, fromre-(expressing intensive force) +saltare(frequentative ofsalire‘to jump’). The noun dates from the early 17th cent.

definitions.definition: all that there is of something
examples: The scheme would cover the whole of the UK.
The effects will last for the whole of his life.

definitions.definition: a thing that is complete in itself
examples: Four quarters make a whole.
Taken as a whole, the image is slightly disappointing.
The subjects of the curriculum form a coherent whole.
Harmony in music is essentially about the right relationship of the parts to the whole.

definitions.definition: as one thing or piece and not as separate parts
examples: Unemployment is higher in the north than in thecountry as a whole.
The festival will be great for our city and for the country as a whole.
for thecommunity/economy/industry as a whole

definitions.definition: considering everything; in general
examples: On the whole, I'm in favour of the idea.

origin: Old Englishhāl, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchheeland Germanheil, also to the verbhail(senses 1-3). The spelling withwh-(reflecting a dialect pronunciation withw-) first appeared in the 15th cent.

definitions.definition: for a long time
examples: Have you been here long?
Stay as long as you like.
The party went on long into the night.
You shouldn't have to waittoo long.
Now we'reliving longerand retiring later.
This maytake longerthan we thought.
Shows don't usuallylastthislong.
I won'tbe long(= I'll return, be ready, etc. soon).

examples: He retired long before the war.

before longWe'll be home before long(= soon).
long agoThe house was pulled down long ago.

long past somethingIt was long past midnight.
long sinceThey had long since(= a long time before the present time)moved away.

definitions.definition: used after a noun to emphasize that something happens for the whole of a particular period of time
examples: We had to waitall day long.
The baby was cryingall night long.

definitions.definition: only if
examples: We'll go as long as the weather is good.

definitions.definition: since; to the extent that
examples: Most experts agree that so long as there is money to be made, ransomware attacks will not stop.

definitions.definition: for (such) a long time
examples: Will you be away for long?

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody not to be too proud of their present success; in the end another person may be more successful
used to say that something is going to take a long time to explain
examples: What do I think about it? How long have you got?

definitions.definition: used to say that you hope somebody/something will live or last for a long time

definitions.definition: used to say that something that was possible or true before, is not now
examples: I can't wait any longer.

definitions.definition: goodbye

origin: adverbOld Englishlang,long(adjective),lange,longe(adverb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanlang.

concerned parents/residents/parents/locals

examples: She was concerned to write about situations that everybody could identify with.

examples: As far as I am concerned, you can do what you like.

definitions.definition: used to give facts or an opinion about a particular aspect of something
examples: She's doing fine as far as French is concerned, it's her German I'm worried about.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: while something else is happening
examples: He sat watching her as she got ready.

definitions.definition: in the way in which
examples: They did as I had asked.

She lost it, just as I said she would.

definitions.definition: used to state the reason for something
examples: As you were out, I left a message.

definitions.definition: used to make a comment or to add information about what you have just said
examples: As you know, Julia is leaving soon.
She's very tall, as is her mother.

definitions.definition: used to say that although something is true, what follows is also true
examples: Happy as they were, there was something missing.
Try as he might(= however hard he tried), he couldn't open the door.

definitions.definition: in contrast with something
examples: They got 27 per cent of the vote as against 32 per cent at the last election.

definitions.definition: used to say that something may happen at some time in the future, but only when something else has happened
examples: We'll decide on the team as and when we qualify.

definitions.definition: used to start talking about somebody/something
examples: As for Jo, she's doing fine.
As for food for the party, that's all being taken care of.

definitions.definition: used to show the time or date from which something starts
examples: Our phone number is changing as from May 12.

examples: He behaved as if nothing had happened.
It sounds as though you had a good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that you doubt something very much
examples: ‘He says he'll be on time tomorrow.’ ‘As if! He's always late!’

definitions.definition: considering the present situation; as things are
examples: We were hoping to finish it by next week—as it is, it may be the week after.
I can't help—I've got too much to do as it is(= already).

definitions.definition: used when a speaker is giving his or her own impression of a situation or expressing something in a particular way
examples: Teachers must put the brakes on, as it were, when they notice students looking puzzled.

definitions.definition: used when you are referring to something
examples: As to tax, that will be deducted from your salary.

There is a serious issue as to whether or not we give tutors enough guidance.

definitions.definition: until now or until a particular time in the past
examples: an as yet unpublished report

definitions.definition: used as a comment on something that you have just said
examples: He smiled and I smiled back. As you do.
(ironic)A little boy finds a gigantic robot in his backyard—as you do—and has to keep it secret.

definitions.definition: if you are going to be punished for doing something wrong, whether it is a big or small thing, you may as well do the big thing

origin: Middle English: reduced form of Old Englishalswā‘similarly’ (seealso).

definitions.definition: in a short time from now; a short time after something else has happened
examples: She sold the housesoon afterher husband died.
Soon afterwards, he joined a youth theatre group.

Isoon realizedthe mistake.
See you soon!
More successsoon followed.
We’ll be home soon./We’ll soon be home.
I'll beback soon.
Itsoon becameclear that the programme was a failure.
He promises to visit again soon.
Coming soonto a cinema/theater near you: 'War of the Wizards II'.
He moved in with a friend andsoon foundwork.
The two mensoon discoveredthey had much in common.
The trial is expected to start soon.
Soon thereafter(= soon after that), she returned to Tanzania.

definitions.definition: early; quickly
examples: How sooncan you get here?
We'll deliver the goodsas soon aswe can.
Please send itas soon as possible.
Do you really have to goso soon?

Next Monday isthe soonestwe can deliver.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences and questions to refer to the near future
examples: Will she be back anytime soon?

definitions.definition: used to say that you would equally well like to do something as do something else that has been suggested
examples: I'd just as soon stay at home as go out tonight.

definitions.definition: to prefer to do something (than do something else)
examples: She'd sooner share a house with other students than live at home with her parents.

definitions.definition: a bad situation will pass or be forgotten most quickly if nothing more is said about it
used to say that something was, or will be, done immediately
used to say that something happens immediately after something else
examples: No sooner had she said it than she burst into tears.

definitions.definition: very soon; as soon as possible
examples: ‘When shall I tell him?’ ‘The sooner the better.’

definitions.definition: at some time in the future, even if you are not sure exactly when
examples: Sooner or later you will have to make a decision.

definitions.definition: after a short time rather than after a long time
examples: We urged them to sort out the problem sooner rather than later.

origin: Old Englishsōna‘immediately’, of West Germanic origin.

definitions.definition: to come or go after or behind somebody/something
examples: follow somebody/somethingFollow me please.
follow somebody + adv./prep.He followed her into the house.
They were followed down the street by a bunch of photographers.
Wherever she led, they followed.
Sam walked in, with the rest of the boysfollowing closelybehind.

definitions.definition: to come or go after or behind somebody in order to watch where they go and what they do
examples: I think we're being followed.
Police followed the car along Station Road where it mounted the kerb again.
As she walked home, she had the feeling she was being followed.

definitions.definition: to come after something/somebody else in time or order; to happen as a result of something else
examples: follow (something/somebody)I remember little of the days that followed the accident.
A period of unrest followed the president's resignation.
The first two classes are followed by a break of ten minutes.
The main course was followed by fresh fruit.
to followI'll have soup and fish to follow.

There follows…There followed a short silence.
as follows…The opening hours are as follows…
follow on from somethingA new proposal followed on from the discussions.
the workers' revolts thatfollowed in the wake ofthe student uprising

definitions.definition: to do something after something else
examples: follow something with somethingGive your contact details and follow this with a brief profile of yourself.
follow something up with somethingThey follow up their March show with four UK dates next month.

definitions.definition: to accept advice, instructions, etc. and do what you have been told or shown to do

definitions.definition: to accept somebody/something as a guide, a leader or an example; to copy somebody/something
examples: follow (somebody/something)I encourage others tofollow our example.

definitions.definition: to click on a link in a website
examples: Follow the linksto sign up for the webinar.

definitions.definition: to go along a road, path, etc.
examples: Follow this road until you get to the school, then turn left.

definitions.definition: to go in the same direction as something orparallelto something
examples: The lane follows the edge of a wood for about a mile.

definitions.definition: to be the logical result of something
examples: follow from somethingI don't see how that follows from what you've just said.
follow on from somethingSeveral conclusions follow on from his statement.
it follows that…If a = b and b = c it follows that a = c.
It does not necessarily follow that sleep loss would cause these symptoms.

definitions.definition: to understand an explanation or the meaning of something
examples: Sorry, I don't follow.
follow somebodyDo you follow me?
follow somethingI couldn't really follow his argument.

to be easy/difficult/hard to follow

definitions.definition: to watch or listen to somebody/something very carefully
examples: The children were following every word of the story intently.

definitions.definition: to take an active interest in something and be aware of what is happening; to support a sports team
examples: We've beenfollowing this storyfor months.

definitions.definition: to choose to regularly see content from a person, company, etc. using asocial mediaservice
examples: I don’t follow many celebrities on Instagram any more.

definitions.definition: to be about the life or development of somebody/something
examples: The novel follows the fortunes of a village community in Scotland.

definitions.definition: to develop or happen in a particular way
examples: The day followed the usual pattern.

definitions.definition: to do the same job, have the same style of life, etc. as somebody else, especially somebody in your family
examples: She works in television, following in her father's footsteps.

definitions.definition: to be guided by your sense of smell
to go straight forward
examples: The garage is a mile ahead up the hill—just follow your nose.

definitions.definition: to act according to what seems right or reasonable, rather than following any particular rules
to play a card of the samesuitthat has just been played
to act or behave in the way that somebody else has just done
a person or event that is so good or successful at something that it will be difficult for anyone/anything else coming after them to be as good or successful
examples: She has been an excellent principal and will be a hard act to follow.
Their contribution will prove a tough act to follow.

origin: Old Englishfolgian, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvolgenand Germanfolgen.

definitions.definition: disagreeing strongly with something and trying to stop it
examples: opposed to somethingHe wasstrongly opposedto modernism in art.

definitions.definition: used to make a contrast between two things
examples: 200 attended, as opposed to 300 the previous year.

origin: N/A

such a/an…Why are you in such a hurry?

definitions.definition: of the type already mentioned

examples: Wild flowers such as primroses are becoming rare.

definitions.definition: of a kind that; like
examples: Opportunities such as this did not come every day.

origin: Old Englishswilc,swylc; related to Dutchzulk, Germansolch, from the Germanic bases ofsoandalike.

definitions.definition: the earth, with all its countries, peoples and natural features

in the worldWhich is the largest city in the world?
around/round the worldto sail around the world

a meeting ofworld leaders
campaigning forworld peace
theworld economy
world markets/trade
theworld premiereof the movie

the major world religions

examples: Parents are the most important people in a child's world.

definitions.definition: a planet like the earth

definitions.definition: the state of human existence
examples: this world and the next(= life on earth and existence after death)

definitions.definition: to be loved by and very important to somebody
to be giving all your attention to something so that you do not notice what is happening around you
examples: When he’s listening to music he’s lost to the world.

examples: If you enjoy the coast and the country, you'll get the best of both worlds on this walk.

examples: Although they are twins, they are worlds apart in their attitude to life.

examples: the brave new world of technology
the architects' vision of a brave new world of pristine concrete

definitions.definition: to become less/more important or successful in society
to be born
in a deep sleep
to make somebody feel much better; to improve something
examples: A change of job would do you the world of good.

definitions.definition: exactly as if…; exactly like somebody/something
examples: She behaved for all the world as if nothing unusual had happened.

definitions.definition: to be very successful and admired
used to emphasize something and to show that you are surprised or annoyed
examples: What in the world did they think they were doing?

definitions.definition: used to say that something is what you would like to happen or what should happen, but you know it cannot
examples: In an ideal world we would be recycling and reusing everything.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying
examples: There's nothing in the world I'd like more than to visit New York.
Don't rush—we've gotall the time in the world.
You look as if youhaven't got a care in the world!

examples: I tapped on the window to get her attention but she was in a world of her own.

a person with a lot of experience of life, who is not easily surprised or shocked
not the worst thing that could happen to somebody
examples: Failing one exam is not the end of the world.

definitions.definition: used to say that you would never do something
examples: I wouldn't hurt you for the world.

examples: We all envy the Bill Gateses of this world(= the people who are as rich and successful as Bill Gates).

examples: The meal was out of this world.

examples: Politicians promise the earth before an election, but things are different afterwards.

definitions.definition: to travel widely and gain wide experience
examples: As a young man, he wanted to see the world before he settled down.

examples: He's never going to set the world on fire with his paintings.

definitions.definition: to talk about how the world could be changed to be a better place
examples: We stayed up all night, setting the world to rights.

the twilight zone between living and merely existing

definitions.definition: to relax and watch people in a public place
examples: We sat outside a cafe, watching the world go by.

examples: People are basically the same the world over.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have all the possible disadvantages at the same time

definitions.definition: in the way in which
examples: They did as I had asked.
Leave the papers as they are.
She lost it, just as I said she would.

definitions.definition: used to state the reason for something
examples: As you were out, I left a message.
She may need some help as she's new.

definitions.definition: used to make a comment or to add information about what you have just said
examples: As you know, Julia is leaving soon.
She's very tall, as is her mother.

definitions.definition: used to say that although something is true, what follows is also true
examples: Happy as they were, there was something missing.
Try as he might(= however hard he tried), he couldn't open the door.

definitions.definition: in contrast with something
examples: They got 27 per cent of the vote as against 32 per cent at the last election.

definitions.definition: used to say that something may happen at some time in the future, but only when something else has happened
examples: We'll decide on the team as and when we qualify.
I'll tell you more as and when(= as soon as I can).

definitions.definition: used to start talking about somebody/something
examples: As for Jo, she's doing fine.
As for food for the party, that's all being taken care of.

definitions.definition: used to show the time or date from which something starts
examples: Our phone number is changing as from May 12.
Lee and I are now officially engaged, as of yesterday.

definitions.definition: in a way that suggests something
examples: He behaved as if nothing had happened.
It sounds as though you had a good time.
It's my birthday. As if you didn't know!
‘Don't say anything.’ ‘As if I would!’(= surely you do not expect me to)

definitions.definition: used to say that you doubt something very much
examples: ‘He says he'll be on time tomorrow.’ ‘As if! He's always late!’

definitions.definition: considering the present situation; as things are
examples: We were hoping to finish it by next week—as it is, it may be the week after.
I can't help—I've got too much to do as it is(= already).

definitions.definition: used when a speaker is giving his or her own impression of a situation or expressing something in a particular way
examples: Teachers must put the brakes on, as it were, when they notice students looking puzzled.

definitions.definition: used when you are referring to something
examples: As to tax, that will be deducted from your salary.
I have a few ideas as to how we might do this.
There is a serious issue as to whether or not we give tutors enough guidance.

definitions.definition: until now or until a particular time in the past
examples: an as yet unpublished report
As yet little was known of the causes of the disease.

definitions.definition: used as a comment on something that you have just said
examples: He smiled and I smiled back. As you do.
(ironic)A little boy finds a gigantic robot in his backyard—as you do—and has to keep it secret.

definitions.definition: if you are going to be punished for doing something wrong, whether it is a big or small thing, you may as well do the big thing

origin: Middle English: reduced form of Old Englishalswā‘similarly’ (seealso).

definitions.definition: used when you are comparing two people or things, or two situations
examples: You're as tall as your father.
He was as white as a sheet.
She doesn't play as well as her sister.
I haven't known him as long as you(= as you have known him).
He doesn't earnas much asme.
He doesn't earn as much as I do.
It's not as hard as I thought.
Run as fast as you can.
We'd like it as soon as possible.

definitions.definition: used to say that something happens in the same way
examples: As always, he said little.
The ‘h’ in honest is silent, as in ‘hour’.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an amount
examples: As many as2 million people could be affected.
The chimp is an endangered species, withas few as170 000 chimps left in the wild.

origin: Middle English: reduced form of Old Englishalswā‘similarly’ (seealso).

examples: He sat watching her as she got ready.
As she grew older she gained in confidence.

examples: He smiled and I smiled back. As you do.
