definitions.definition: before anyone or anything else; at the beginning
examples: ‘Do you want a drink?’ ‘I'll finish my work first.’
First I had to decide what to wear.
Whocame firstin the race(= who won)?
It plunged nose first into the river.
After the war, Erica went on to marry first one American millionaire and then another.

definitions.definition: for the first time
examples: When did you first meet him?
The book was first published in 1971.
The image has haunted me ever since I first saw it.
I first discovered the cello when I was four and a half.
If there's anything really exciting, you'll hear it here first.

definitions.definition: used to introduce the first of a list of points you want to make in a speech or piece of writing
examples: This method has two advantages: first it is cheaper and second it is quicker.
First, however, I would like to thank the folks at the Center for Financial Education for inviting me to speak today.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you are determined not to do something
examples: She swore that she wouldn't apologize—she'd die first!

definitions.definition: at or in the beginning
examples: I didn't like the job much at first.
At first I thought he was shy, but then I discovered he was just not interested in other people.
(saying)If at first you don't succeed, try, try again.

definitions.definition: to be considered more important than anything else
examples: In any decision she makes, her family always comes first.

definitions.definition: with your feet touching the ground before any other part of your body
examples: He landed feet first.

definitions.definition: if you leave a placefeet first, you are carried out after you are dead
examples: You'll have to carry me out feet first!

definitions.definition: more than anything else
examples: He does a little teaching, but first and foremost he's a writer.

definitions.definition: in every way that is important; completely
examples: She regarded herself, first and last, as a musician.

definitions.definition: people will be dealt with, seen, etc. strictly in the order in which they arrive
examples: Tickets are available on a first come, first served basis.

definitions.definition: before doing anything else; at the beginning
examples: First of all, let me ask you something.

definitions.definition: as the most important thing
examples: The content of any article needs, first of all, to be relevant to the reader.

definitions.definition: before anything else
examples: First off, let's see how much it'll cost.

definitions.definition: to start with; before anything else
examples: First up is a report on the situation in Australia.

definitions.definition: moving forwards or downwards with your head in front of the rest of your body
examples: He fell head first down the stairs.

definitions.definition: without thinking carefully about something before acting
examples: She got divorced and rushed head first into another marriage.

definitions.definition: to consider somebody/something to be more important than anyone/anything else
examples: She always puts her children first.

definitions.definition: safety is the most important thing
examples: When cycling on the roads, remember: safety first.

origin: Old Englishfyr(e)st; of Germanic origin, related to Old Norsefyrstrand GermanFürst‘prince’, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritprathama, Latinprimus, and Greekprōtos.

definitions.definition: the earth, with all its countries, peoples and natural features
examples: a map of the world
French is spoken in manyparts of the world.
in the worldWhich is the largest city in the world?
around/round the worldto sail around the world
all over the worldShe has been all over the world.
throughout the worldTheir products are sold throughout the world.
Theytravelled the worldfor pleasure.
He's the world's highest paid entertainer.
a meeting ofworld leaders
campaigning forworld peace
theworld economy
world markets/trade
theworld premiereof the movie

definitions.definition: used before another noun to describe one of the most important people or things of their type in the world
examples: theworld champion/championship/record/title
As of October, herworld rankingwas No.1.
the major world religions

definitions.definition: a particular part of the earth; a particular group of countries or people; a particular period of history and the people of that period
examples: theArab world
In theWestern world, there is a different attitude to marriage.
in the … worldfarmers in thedeveloping world
The book has sold 12 million copies in the English-speaking world and has been translated into six languages.
in themodern/ancient world

definitions.definition: the people or things belonging to a particular group or connected with a particular interest, job, etc.
examples: the animal/plant/insect world
thebusiness/corporate world
She is a popular figure in theart world.
the world of somethingtheworld of politics/business/sport
the world of music/art/fashion
stars from the sporting and artistic worlds

definitions.definition: everything that exists of a particular kind; a particular kind of life or existence
examples: the natural world(= animals, plants, minerals, etc.)
the physical/material world
They are a couple inthe real worldas well as in the movie.
The island is a world of brilliant colours and dramatic sunsets.
They had little contact with theoutside world(= people and places that were not part of their normal life).

definitions.definition: our society and the way people live and behave; the people in the world
examples: We live in a rapidly changing world.
Young people always think they are going tochange the world.
It's not your job tosave the world.
He's too young to understand the ways of the world(= how the world works).
Thewhole worldwas waiting for news of the astronauts.
She felt that the world was against her.
Theeyes of the worldare on the President(= many people around the world are waiting to see what the President will do).
News of the incident was slow to reach thewider world.

definitions.definition: a way of life where possessions and physical pleasures are important, rather than spiritual values
examples: monks and nuns renouncing the world

definitions.definition: a person’s environment, experiences, friends and family, etc.
examples: Parents are the most important people in a child's world.
When his wife died, his entire world was turned upside down.

definitions.definition: an environment that is imagined or constructed, for example in a computer game
examples: Computer games create wholevirtual worlds.
The game comprises four separate worlds, each split into six levels.
You're living in a fantasy world!

definitions.definition: a planet like the earth
examples: There may be other worlds out there.

definitions.definition: the state of human existence
examples: this world and the next(= life on earth and existence after death)

definitions.definition: to be loved by and very important to somebody
to be giving all your attention to something so that you do not notice what is happening around you
examples: When he’s listening to music he’s lost to the world.

definitions.definition: the benefits of two or more completely different situations that you can enjoy at the same time
examples: If you enjoy the coast and the country, you'll get the best of both worlds on this walk.
Maybe in the best of all possible worlds things would be different.

definitions.definition: to be completely different in attitudes, opinions, etc.
examples: Although they are twins, they are worlds apart in their attitude to life.

definitions.definition: a situation or society that changes in a way that is meant to improve people’s lives but is often a source of extra problems
examples: the brave new world of technology
the architects' vision of a brave new world of pristine concrete

definitions.definition: to become less/more important or successful in society
to be born
in a deep sleep
to make somebody feel much better; to improve something
examples: A change of job would do you the world of good.

definitions.definition: exactly as if…; exactly like somebody/something
examples: She behaved for all the world as if nothing unusual had happened.
He looked for all the world like a schoolboy caught stealing apples.

definitions.definition: to be very successful and admired
used to emphasize something and to show that you are surprised or annoyed
examples: What in the world did they think they were doing?

definitions.definition: used to say that something is what you would like to happen or what should happen, but you know it cannot
examples: In an ideal world we would be recycling and reusing everything.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying
examples: There's nothing in the world I'd like more than to visit New York.
Don't rush—we've gotall the time in the world.
You look as if youhaven't got a care in the world!

definitions.definition: if you arein a world of your own, you are so concerned with your own thoughts that you do not notice what is happening around you
examples: I tapped on the window to get her attention but she was in a world of her own.

definitions.definition: used to express your surprise when you meet somebody you know in an unexpected place, or when you are talking to somebody and find out that you both know the same person
a person with a lot of experience of life, who is not easily surprised or shocked
not the worst thing that could happen to somebody
examples: Failing one exam is not the end of the world.
It won't be the end of the world if you don't get the job.

definitions.definition: used to say that you would never do something
examples: I wouldn't hurt you for the world.

definitions.definition: used to refer to people of a particular type
examples: We all envy the Bill Gateses of this world(= the people who are as rich and successful as Bill Gates).

definitions.definition: very happy or proud
used to emphasize how good, beautiful, etc. something is
examples: The meal was out of this world.

definitions.definition: to make promises that will be impossible to keep
examples: Politicians promise the earth before an election, but things are different afterwards.

definitions.definition: to travel widely and gain wide experience
examples: As a young man, he wanted to see the world before he settled down.

definitions.definition: to be very successful and admired by other people
examples: He's never going to set the world on fire with his paintings.

definitions.definition: to talk about how the world could be changed to be a better place
examples: We stayed up all night, setting the world to rights.

definitions.definition: a state in which things are strange, mysterious or secret; a state that exists on the dividing line between two things
examples: the twilight world of the occult
The dissidents lived in a twilight world of hushed voices and secret meetings.
the twilight zone between living and merely existing
They lived in the twilight zone on the fringes of society.

definitions.definition: to relax and watch people in a public place
examples: We sat outside a cafe, watching the world go by.
I love just watching the world go by.

definitions.definition: the way that most people behave; the way that things happen, which you cannot change
examples: The rich and powerful make the decisions—that's the way of the world.

definitions.definition: used to express dislike, surprise or shock, especially at changes in people’s attitudes or behaviour
examples: When I listen to the news these days, I sometimes wonder what the world is coming to.

definitions.definition: used to say that you cannot do something, even though you really want to
examples: With the best will in the world I could not describe him as a good father.

definitions.definition: everyone; a large number of people
used to emphasize how different two things are
examples: His new luxury mansion was a world away from the tiny house where he was born.

definitions.definition: there is no limit to the opportunities open to you
examples: With talent like that, the world is her oyster.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize how much difference there is between two things
examples: There's a world of difference between liking someone and loving them.

definitions.definition: everywhere in the world
examples: People are basically the same the world over.
Scientists the world over have been waiting for this breakthrough.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have all the possible disadvantages at the same time

origin: Old Englishw(e)oruld, from a Germanic compound meaning ‘age of man’; related to Dutchwereldand GermanWelt.

definitions.definition: that can be done or achieved
examples: possible to do somethingNew technologyhas made it possibleto communicate more easily.
it is possible to do somethingIt is possible to get there by bus.
It's just not physically possible to finish all this by the end of the week.
it is possible for somebody to do somethingWould it be possiblefor me to leave a message for her?
This wouldn't have been possible without you.
I'd like the money back by next weekif possible.
Try to avoid losing your temperif at all possible(= if you can).
Use public transportwhenever possible(= when you can).
The doctors did all that was humanly possible to save him.
We spent every possible moment on the beach.
Expansion was made possible by the investment of government money.
This film was better than Ithought possible.

definitions.definition: that might exist or happen but is not certain to
examples: the possible side effects of the drug
a possible future president
Frost is possible, although unlikely, at this time of year.
it is possible (that)…It is quite possiblethat the river will flood again.
It's just possible that I gave them the wrong directions.
‘You might be wrong, of course!’ ‘It’s possible, but I doubt it.’
With thepossible exceptionof the Beatles, no other band has become so successful so quickly.
‘Do you think he’ll resign?’ ‘Anything’s possiblewhere he’s concerned.’
a possible outcome/effect/consequence
We have to look at every possible scenario.

definitions.definition: reasonable or acceptable in a particular situation
examples: There are several possible explanations.
Please suggest possible ways in which you would improve the existing program.
a possible solution
One possible cause/reason is…

definitions.definition: used after adjectives to emphasize that something is the best, worst, etc. of its type
examples: It was thebest possiblesurprise anyone could have given me.
Don't leave your packing until thelast possiblemoment.
Jacobs faces themaximum possiblesentence of five years imprisonment.

definitions.definition: as quickly, much, soon, etc. as you can
examples: We will get your order to you as soon as possible.
Try to give as much detail as possible in your answer.
She did as much as possible to help him.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have all the possible advantages/disadvantages together
examples: In the best of all possible worlds, you should be able to develop your career and enjoy a rewarding family life.

definitions.definition: the benefits of two or more completely different situations that you can enjoy at the same time
examples: If you enjoy the coast and the country, you'll get the best of both worlds on this walk.
Maybe in the best of all possible worlds things would be different.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have all the possible disadvantages at the same time

origin: late Middle English: from Old French, or from Latinpossibilis, fromposse‘be able’.

definitions.definition: in a good, right or acceptable way
examples: Theyplayed wellin the tournament.
The teamwork welltogether.
The kids all behaved well.
The conference was very well organized.
Well done!(= expressing admiration for what somebody has done)
His campaign was notgoing well.
These animals make very good pets if treated well(= with kindness).
Peoplespoke well of(= spoke with approval of)him.
Shetook itverywell(= did not react too badly), all things considered.
They lived well(= in comfort and spending a lot of money)and were generous with their money.
She was determined to marry well(= marry somebody rich and/or with a high social position).

definitions.definition: completely and properly
examples: Add the lemon juice and mix well.
The surface must be well prepared before you start to paint.
How well do you know Carla?
He's well able to take care of himself.
a well trained dog

definitions.definition: to a great extent or degree
examples: He was driving at well over the speed limit.
She iswell awareof the dangers.
They are verywell suitedto each other.
a well-loved tale
The castle iswell wortha visit.
He liked herwell enough(= to a reasonable degree)but he wasn't going to make a close friend of her.
(British English, informal)I was well annoyed, I can tell you.

definitions.definition: easily
examples: She could well afford to pay for it herself.

definitions.definition: probably
examples: You may well be right.
It may well be thatthe train is delayed.

definitions.definition: with good reason
examples: Ican't very wellleave now.
Icouldn't very wellrefuse to help them, could I?
‘What are we doing here?’ ‘You may well ask(= I don't really know either).’

definitions.definition: in addition to somebody/something; too
examples: Are they coming as well?
They sell books as well as newspapers.
She is a talented musician as well as being a photographer.

definitions.definition: to be getting healthier after an illness; to be in good health after a birth
examples: Mother and baby are doing well.

definitions.definition: to have nearly achieved something and be going to achieve it soon
examples: She is well on the way to recovery.
He is well on the way to establishing himself among the top ten players in the world.

definitions.definition: to be lucky that you are not involved in something unpleasant or difficult to deal with
examples: I don’t know exactly what’s been going on. I only know that you’re well out of it.

definitions.definition: to know a lot about something
examples: He's well up in all the latest developments.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an angry statement or an order
examples: You can bloody well keep your job—I don't want it!
‘I’m not coming.’ ‘Yes, you bloody well are!’

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an angry statement or an order
examples: You can bloody well keep your job—I don't want it!
‘I’m not coming.’ ‘Yes, you bloody well are!’

definitions.definition: to be successful
examples: Jack is doing very well at school.

definitions.definition: to treat somebody generously
to become successful or rich
to make a profit or get money from somebody/something
to be sensible or wise to do something
examples: Hewould do well toconcentrate more on his work.
You did well to sell when the price was high.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an angry statement or an order
examples: You're fucking well coming whether you want to or not.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize a statement when you are annoyed about something
examples: If you don't come now, you can jolly well walk home!

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody by saying that they should realize or understand something
examples: You know as well as I do that you're being unreasonable.

definitions.definition: to be very aware of a fact and unable to deny or ignore it
examples: He knew full well what she thought of it.

definitions.definition: to not get involved in something that you have no connection with
examples: When it comes to other people's arguments, it's better to leave well alone.

definitions.definition: if you are going to be punished for doing something wrong, whether it is a big or small thing, you may as well do the big thing
used to say that something seems the best thing to do in the situation that you are in, although you may not really want to do it
examples: If no one else wants it, we might as well give it to him.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no real difference between two things that you might do
examples: If you are going to have sugar, you may as well have a bar of chocolate.

definitions.definition: to have good intentions, although their effect may not be good
almost; almost completely
examples: One dog looks pretty much like another to me.
He goes out pretty well every night.
The first stage is pretty near finished.

definitions.definition: completely
examples: By that time we were well and truly lost.

definitions.definition: having made good progress
examples: If we got Terry to do that, we'd be well away.

definitions.definition: drunk or in a deep sleep
to be good friends with somebody, especially somebody important
examples: She seems to be well in with all the right people.

origin: adverbOld Englishwel(l), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchweland Germanwohl; probably also to the modal verbwill.

definitions.definition: while something else is happening
examples: He sat watching her as she got ready.
As she grew older she gained in confidence.

definitions.definition: in the way in which
examples: They did as I had asked.
Leave the papers as they are.
She lost it, just as I said she would.

definitions.definition: used to state the reason for something
examples: As you were out, I left a message.
She may need some help as she's new.

definitions.definition: used to make a comment or to add information about what you have just said
examples: As you know, Julia is leaving soon.
She's very tall, as is her mother.

definitions.definition: used to say that although something is true, what follows is also true
examples: Happy as they were, there was something missing.
Try as he might(= however hard he tried), he couldn't open the door.

definitions.definition: in contrast with something
examples: They got 27 per cent of the vote as against 32 per cent at the last election.

definitions.definition: used to say that something may happen at some time in the future, but only when something else has happened
examples: We'll decide on the team as and when we qualify.
I'll tell you more as and when(= as soon as I can).

definitions.definition: used to start talking about somebody/something
examples: As for Jo, she's doing fine.
As for food for the party, that's all being taken care of.

definitions.definition: used to show the time or date from which something starts
examples: Our phone number is changing as from May 12.
Lee and I are now officially engaged, as of yesterday.

definitions.definition: in a way that suggests something
examples: He behaved as if nothing had happened.
It sounds as though you had a good time.
It's my birthday. As if you didn't know!
‘Don't say anything.’ ‘As if I would!’(= surely you do not expect me to)

definitions.definition: used to say that you doubt something very much
examples: ‘He says he'll be on time tomorrow.’ ‘As if! He's always late!’

definitions.definition: considering the present situation; as things are
examples: We were hoping to finish it by next week—as it is, it may be the week after.
I can't help—I've got too much to do as it is(= already).

definitions.definition: used when a speaker is giving his or her own impression of a situation or expressing something in a particular way
examples: Teachers must put the brakes on, as it were, when they notice students looking puzzled.

definitions.definition: used when you are referring to something
examples: As to tax, that will be deducted from your salary.
I have a few ideas as to how we might do this.
There is a serious issue as to whether or not we give tutors enough guidance.

definitions.definition: until now or until a particular time in the past
examples: an as yet unpublished report

definitions.definition: used as a comment on something that you have just said
examples: He smiled and I smiled back. As you do.
(ironic)A little boy finds a gigantic robot in his backyard—as you do—and has to keep it secret.

definitions.definition: if you are going to be punished for doing something wrong, whether it is a big or small thing, you may as well do the big thing

origin: Middle English: reduced form of Old Englishalswā‘similarly’ (seealso).

definitions.definition: on one occasion only; one time
examples: I've only been there once.
He cleans his caronce aweek.
She only sees her parentsonce everysix months.
For optimum effect, these drops are usedonce daily.
I had met Louonce before.
Last year the region received rainonly once.
(informal)He only did itthe once.
Rome is a city you should visitat least oncein your life.
They meet once a week at a Paris café.

definitions.definition: at some time in the past
examples: I once met your mother.
He once lived in Zambia.
This book was famous once, but nobody reads it today.
She remembers once travelling with her father to Kilkenny.
I famously once said that I was a fighter and not a quitter.
He once told an interviewer that his biggest regret was missing his father's funeral.
It was once thought to be the best hotel in Europe.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences and questions, and afterifto mean ‘ever’ or ‘at all’
examples: Henever onceoffered to help.
If she once decides to do something, you won't change her mind.

definitions.definition: used with a preposition or another adverb to mean‘as soon as somebody/something is/was’
examples: Once insidethe gate, Sam hurried up the path.

definitions.definition: suddenly
examples: All at once she lost her temper.

definitions.definition: all together; at the same time
examples: I can't do everything all at once—you'll have to be patient.

definitions.definition: immediately; without delay
examples: Come here at once!

definitions.definition: at the same time
examples: Don't all speak at once!
I can't do two things at once.

definitions.definition: on this occasion (which is in contrast to what happens usually)
examples: Just for once he arrived on time.
Can't you be nice to each other just this once?

definitions.definition: said by anauctioneerto show that an item has been sold
one more time; another time
examples: Once again the train was late.
Let me hear it just once more.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody cannot change
examples: Once an actor, always an actor.

definitions.definition: after an unpleasant experience you are careful to avoid something similar
now and for the last time; finally or completely
examples: We need to settle this once and for all.

definitions.definition: very rarely
occasionally
examples: Everybody makes a mistake once in a while.

definitions.definition: a few times
examples: I don't know her well, I've only met her once or twice.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody has done something wrong or stupid again, and this time they will suffer because of it
examples: You've tried that trick once too often.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

origin: Middle Englishones, genitive ofone. The spelling change in the 16th cent. was in order to retain the unvoiced sound of the final consonant.

definitions.definition: some but not all of a thing
examples: We spent part of the time in the museum.
Part of the building was destroyed in the fire.

the latter part of the 19th century

animportant/essential partof the project

in partsThe novel is good in parts.

definitions.definition: an element or member of something; a person or thing that helps to make up a whole or belongs in it
examples: Snacks can be part of a healthy eating plan.

definitions.definition: a separate piece or area of a human or animal body or of a plant
examples: The cancer could spread to otherparts of the body.

definitions.definition: a piece of a machine or structure
examples: The factory manufactures aircraft parts.
car/machine/engine parts
the working parts of the machinery

definitions.definition: an area or a region of the world, a country, a town, etc.
examples: The island is mostly flat, but there are some hilly parts.
part of somethingthe northern part of town
differentparts of the world/country
Which part of Japan do you come from?

definitions.definition: a region or an area
examples: She's not from these parts.

definitions.definition: a section of a book, television series, etc., especially one that is published or broadcast separately

definitions.definition: a role played by an actor in a play, film, etc.; the words spoken by an actor in a particular role

(figurative)He's alwaysplaying a part(= pretending to be something that he is not).

part in somethingHe got a small part in a West End play.
in a partShe was very good in the part.

definitions.definition: the way in which somebody/something is involved in an action or situation
examples: Luckplayed abigpartin their success.

definitions.definition: music for a particular voice or instrument in a group singing or playing together
examples: the clarinet part

definitions.definition: a unit of measurement that allows you to compare the different amounts of substances in something
examples: Add three parts wine to one part water.
fluoride levels of 0.2 parts per million

definitions.definition: a line on a person’s head where the hair is divided with acomb
to be better or more effective as a group than you would think just by looking at the individual members of the group
examples: The team is greater than the sum of its parts.

definitions.definition: most of something, especially a period of time; more than half of something
examples: He drank the best part of a bottle of Scotch waiting for her to get home.

definitions.definition: you should avoid danger and not take unnecessary risks
mostly; usually
examples: The contributors are, for the most part, professional scientists.

definitions.definition: used to express your own, his, their, etc. opinion or feelings about something
examples: For my part, I would reject all these arguments.

definitions.definition: to a great extent
examples: Their success is due in large part to their determination.

definitions.definition: partly; to some extent
examples: Her success was due in part to luck.

definitions.definition: to have an appearance or wear clothes suitable for a particular job, role or position
examples: He acts and dresses the part of a gentleman.

definitions.definition: a person with many skills
made or done by somebody
examples: It was an error on my part.

definitions.definition: a normal part of something
examples: Keeping the accounts is part and parcel of my job.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that you are so used to seeing that you no longer notice them
examples: I worked there so long that I became part of the furniture.

definitions.definition: to accept something slightly unpleasant without complaining or being offended
examples: He took the teasing in good part.

definitions.definition: to be involved in something
examples: to take part in a discussion/demonstration/fight/celebration

definitions.definition: to support somebody, for example in an argument
examples: His mother always takes his part.

origin: Old English (denoting a part of speech), from Latinpars,part-. The verb (originally in Middle English in the sense ‘divide into parts’) is from Old Frenchpartir, from Latinpartire,partiri‘divide, share’.

definitions.definition: a period of 24 hours
examples: ‘What day is it today?’ ‘Monday.’

within two, 30, etc. daysFull payment must be made within 21 days.

a/per dayTake the medicine three times a day.

definitions.definition: the time between when it becomes light in the morning and when it becomes dark in the evening
examples: What a beautiful day!

during the dayHe works at night and sleeps during the day.
by dayNocturnal animals sleep by day and hunt by night.
on a… dayOn a sunny day in June…

definitions.definition: the hours of the day when you are active, working, etc., not sleeping
examples: a seven-hourworking day
(especially North American English)Have a nice day!

definitions.definition: a particular period of time or history
examples: in somebody's dayin Queen Victoria’s day

back in theearly daysof computers
in those daysHe was the biggest star in Hollywood in those days.
(informal)in theold days(= in the past)

in his younger days

definitions.definition: part of your normal working life and not unusual

examples: The letter should arrive any day now.

examples: My dad's always talking about how great everything was back in the day.

definitions.definition: at a particular time in the past
examples: I was a fan back in the days when the band wasn't yet famous.

definitions.definition: the moment in the early hours of the morning when it begins to get light
to decide or agree to stop doing something
examples: After forty years in politics I think it's time for me to call it a day(= to retire).

definitions.definition: to be successful against somebody/something
examples: Despite strong opposition, the ruling party carried the day.

definitions.definition: easy to see or understand
each day repeatedly (used especially when something is boring or annoying)
examples: She hates doing the same work day after day.

definitions.definition: all the time; continuously
examples: The store is open day and night.

definitions.definition: all the time; a little at a time and gradually
examples: Day by day his condition improved.

examples: Living on junk food day in day out is not good for you.

a person or thing will not continue to live, exist or be successful for much longer
examples: His days as leader of the party are numbered.

definitions.definition: to spend the last part of your life in a particular state or place
examples: He ended his days in poverty.

definitions.definition: everyone has good luck or success at some point in their life
the time when you have to do something difficult or unpleasant
examples: I’d better go and see the dentist—I can’t put off the evil hour any longer.

definitions.definition: the amount of fruit or vegetables that some organizations say you should eat in order to be healthy

definitions.definition: from the beginning
examples: It's never worked from day one.

definitions.definition: with no thoughts or plans for the future
examples: They live from day to day, looking after their sick daughter.

definitions.definition: if a situation changesfrom day to day, it changes often
examples: A baby's need for food can vary from day to day.

definitions.definition: if a situation changesfrom one day to the next, it is uncertain and not likely to stay the same each day
examples: I never know what to expect from one day to the next.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that day forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

examples: I took Sarah’s children to school so I’ve done my good deed for the day.

definitions.definition: an earlier period of time in your life or in history that is seen as better/worse than the present

definitions.definition: to no longer be successful, powerful, etc.
examples: She's had her day as a supermodel.

definitions.definition: a friendly way of saying goodbye, especially to customers

examples: Our car has seen better days!

definitions.definition: (used when talking about somebody’s age) at least
examples: He must be 70 if he's a day!

definitions.definition: used when you are very surprised at something you have never heard or seen before
examples: I've never heard such nonsense in all my born days.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: What was life like in the olden days, Gran?

examples: Slavery continues to exist, even in this day and age.

definitions.definition: after the time when an action could be successful
examples: He started working hard much too late in the day—he couldn't possibly catch up.

definitions.definition: the whole length of the day
used to say that although you have failed or had a bad experience, you will continue

examples: The phone call from Mike really made my day.

definitions.definition: all the time; continuously
examples: The machines are kept running night and day.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that makes people excited for a short time but does not last very long
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: to not have much time
examples: Come on! We don't have all day!

definitions.definition: during a particular period of time when somebody lived
examples: the best player of his day

definitions.definition: of the present time
examples: the political issues of the day

definitions.definition: that is served on a particular day in a restaurant
examples: soup of the day

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying when you are surprised, shocked or annoyed
examples: Oh my days! Is that true?

definitions.definition: at some time in the future, or on a particular day in the past
examples: One day, I want to leave the city and move to the country.

definitions.definition: before a long time has passed
examples: One of these days you'll come back and ask me to forgive you.

definitions.definition: common, popular or suitable at a particular time or for a particular occasion
examples: Pessimism seems to be the order of the day.

definitions.definition: recently
examples: I saw Jack the other day.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
very obvious
used to say that a complicated task will take a long time and should not be rushed
the time when you are young and do not have much experience of life
to prevent failure or defeat, when this seems certain to happen
examples: Salah's late goal saved the day for Liverpool.

definitions.definition: to save something, especially money, for a time when you will really need it
at an unknown time in the future
examples: Some day I'll be famous.
I hope we meet again some day.

definitions.definition: to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

examples: Paul? Apologize? That'll be the day!

definitions.definition: used to talk about the present, especially when you are comparing it with the past

definitions.definition: used to suggest that a time in the past was happier or better than now
exactly
examples: It's been three years to the day since we met.

definitions.definition: even now, when a lot of time has passed
examples: To this day, I still don't understand why he did it.

origin: Old Englishdæg, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchdagand GermanTag.

definitions.definition: the time when something starts; the first part of an event, a story, etc.

beginning of somethingWe missed the beginning of the movie.

at the beginning of somethingWe're going to Japan at the beginning of July.

from the beginningLet's start again from the beginning.

examples: The scandal was the beginning of the end of his career as a politician.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to say or write something in the form of a question, in order to get information
examples: How old are you—if you don’tmind me/my asking?
ask about somebody/somethingHe asked about her family.
ask somethingCan Iask a question?

You do what I tell you to,no questions asked.

frequently asked questions
+ speech‘Where are you going?’ she asked.
ask somebody + speech‘Are you sure?’ he asked her.
ask somebody somethingShe asked the students their names.
I oftenget askedthat!

ask where, what, etc…He asked where I lived.
ask somebody where, what, etc…I had to ask the teacher what to do next.

to ask for a job/a drink/an explanation

ask somethingWhy don't you ask his advice?
ask somebody for somethingWhy don't you ask him for his advice?

ask somebody somethingCan I ask you a favour?

ask if, whether, etc…I'll ask if it's all right to park here.

ask somebodyThank you so much for asking me.

examples: Their marriage was a mistake, if you ask me.

origin: N/A

especially

definitions.definition: the person or thing that comes or happens after all other similar people or things

definitions.definition: the only part or items that remain of something
examples: These are the last of our apples.
We finished the last of the milk at breakfast.

definitions.definition: the latest
examples: That was the last I heard of him.

definitions.definition: the least likely
examples: Known for their self-sufficiency, fishermen would bethe last to admitto mental strain.

definitions.definition: after much delay, effort, etc.; in the end
examples: At last we're home!
At long last the package arrived.

definitions.definition: to die
from beginning to end; during the whole time
examples: It's a fine performance that commands attention from first to last.

definitions.definition: to hear/see somebody/something for the last time
examples: That was the last I ever saw of her.
Unfortunately, I don't think we've heard the last of this affair.

definitions.definition: used to give the most recent news you have about somebody/something
examples: The last I heard he was still working at the garage.

definitions.definition: the one before the last one
examples: She finished second to last.

definitions.definition: until the last possible moment, especially until death
examples: He died protesting his innocence to the last.

origin: nounOld Englishlatost(adverb) ‘after all others in a series’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlaatst,lestand Germanletzt, also tolate.

definitions.definition: a thing that you give to somebody as a gift
examples: What can I get him for abirthday present?
Christmas/wedding presents
I want to give you a little present.
as a presentHe gave her the painting as a present.
present for somebodyWe have a present for you.
I still need to buy a present for my boyfriend.
present from somebodyThat dress was a present from my sister.

definitions.definition: the time now
examples: in the presentYou've got to forget the past and start living in the present.
at presentI'm sorry he's out at present(= now).
to the presentThe book covers the period from the early 1990s to the present.

definitions.definition: the form of a verb that expresses an action that is happening now or at the time of speaking
for now; for a short time
examples: This house is big enough for the moment, but we'll have to move if we have children.

definitions.definition: for now; for a short time
examples: This house is big enough for the present, but we'll have to move if we have children.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future

origin: nounsense 1 Middle English: from Old French, originally in the phrasemettre une chose en present à quelqu'un‘put a thing into the presence of a person’.nounsenses 2 to 3 Middle English: via Old French from Latinpraesent-‘being at hand’, present participle ofpraeesse, fromprae‘before’ +esse‘be’.

definitions.definition: a thing that somebody says or writes giving their opinion or stating a fact
examples: Shemadeseveral interestingpointsin the article.
Itake your point(= understand and accept what you are saying).
He's just saying that toprove a point(= to show his idea is right).
OK, you'vemade your point!

definitions.definition: the main or most important idea in something that is said or done
examples: The point isyou shouldn't have to wait so long to see a doctor.
I wish he wouldget to the point(= say it quickly).
I'llcome straight to the point: we need more money.
Do yousee my point(= understand)?
I think Imissed the point(= did not understand).
Youhave a point(= your idea is right)—it would be better to wait till this evening.
‘There won’t be anywhere to park.’ ‘Oh,that’s a (good) point.’(= I had not thought of that)
It just isn't true. That'sthe whole point(= the only important fact).
I thought the whole point was getting the same amount done with less effort.
‘He's been married before.’ ‘That's beside the point(= not important).’
I know it won't cost very much butthat's not the point(= not the important thing).
point behind somethingThe point behind the film was to help people understand the motivations of the criminals.

definitions.definition: the purpose or aim of something
examples: What's the point of all this violence?
‘Why don't you try again?’ ‘What's the point?’
The point of the lesson is to compare the two countries.
point of doing somethingIdon't see the point ofdoing it all again.
point in doing somethingThere'sno pointin getting angry.

definitions.definition: a particular detail or fact
examples: Can you explain that point again?
Summarize themain pointsof the argument.
I should like to highlight thekey points.
Animportant pointhas been missed out.
Youraisean interestingpoint.

definitions.definition: a particular quality or feature that somebody/something has
examples: Tact is not one of herstrong points.
Read the manual to learn the program'sfiner points(= small details).
Living in Scotlandhas its good pointsbut the weather is not one of them.
One of the hotel'splus points(= good features)is that it is very central.
There is not a single weak point in his essay.

definitions.definition: a particular time or stage of development
examples: We hadreached the pointwhen there was no money left.
at/on the point of somethingThe climber was at the point of death when they found him.
at one, some, this, etc. pointAt one pointhe looked like winning.
Many people experience anxietyat some pointin their lives.
At this point in timewe just have to wait.
At this pointI don't care what you decide to do.
up to/until that pointUp to that point we were living in London.

definitions.definition: a particular place or area
examples: I'll wait for you at themeeting pointin the arrivals hall.
the point at which the river divides
Draw a line from point A to point B.
No parking beyond this point.

definitions.definition: one of the marks of direction around acompass
examples: the points of the compass(= N, S, E, W, etc.)

definitions.definition: an individual unit that adds to a score in a game or sports competition
examples: towin/lose a point
Australia finished 20 points ahead.
point for (doing) somethingPlayersscore pointsfor finding words not found by any other player.
You getbonus pointsfor answering the mystery question.
on pointsThey won on points(= by scoring more points rather than by completely defeating their opponents).

definitions.definition: a mark or unit on a scale of measurement
examples: The party's share of the vote fell by tenpercentage points.

definitions.definition: a unit of credit towards an award or benefit
examples: Applicants are awarded points on criteria such as education, skills and knowledge of English.
Let children trade their earned points for privileges, like extra playtime.

definitions.definition: the sharp, thin end of something
examples: the point of a pencil/knife/pin

definitions.definition: a narrow piece of land that stretches into the sea
examples: The ship sailed around the point.
Pagoda Point

definitions.definition: a small round mark used in writing, especially the mark that separates a whole number from the part that comes after it
examples: two point six (2.6)
a decimal point
We broadcast on ninety-five point nine (95.9) FM.

definitions.definition: a very smalldotof light or colour
examples: The stars were points of light in the sky.

definitions.definition: a place in a wall, etc. where a piece of equipment can be connected to electricity
examples: a power/shaver/telephone point

definitions.definition: the hard tops of the toes of a type of shoe that aballetdancer balances on
a piece of track at a place where a railway line divides that can be moved to allow a train to change tracks
a unit of measurement for the size of letters in printing or on a computer screen, etc.
examples: Change the text to 10 point.

definitions.definition: to repeat an idea, argument, etc. many times to emphasize it, especially when it has already been mentioned or understood
examples: I don’t want to belabour the point, but it’s vital you understand how important this is.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: the more complicated or detailed aspects of something
examples: She seems at ease discussing the finer points of foreign policy.
He learnt the finer points of cricket during a trip to London.

definitions.definition: used when you have to decide something or say what you really think
examples: When it comes to the point, he always changes his mind.

definitions.definition: used to say what is true in a situation
examples: In point of fact, she is their adopted daughter.

definitions.definition: to continue to repeat or explain something that has already been said and understood
examples: I understand what you’re saying—there’s no need to labour the point.

definitions.definition: to be or make sure you do something because it is important or necessary
examples: I made a point of closing all the windows before leaving the house.

definitions.definition: a matter about which there may be difference of opinion or a lack of understanding
examples: Whether this should be enforced by law or not is a moot point.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is more important than something else
examples: I couldn't do the job—I've never been to Spain and, more to the point, I don't speak Spanish.
More to the point, did they get away?

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something that is expressed clearly and directly, especially a criticism
examples: Not to put too fine a point on it, I think you are lying.

definitions.definition: appropriate or relevant to the situation
examples: The quotation was directly on point.
Let's stay on point.
None of the replies was on point.

definitions.definition: perfect; exactly right for the occasion
examples: The music here is totally on point.

definitions.definition: to be close to doing something; to be going to do something very soon
examples: I was just on the point of calling you.
We were on the point of giving up.

definitions.definition: a place where you go or a person that you speak to when you are dealing with an organization
examples: The receptionist is the first point of contact most people have with the clinic.

definitions.definition: a place where a journey starts
an idea, a theory or an event that is used to start a discussion, an activity, etc.
examples: The author takes Freud's dream theories as the point of departure for his essay.

definitions.definition: a thing that somebody considers to be very important for their honour or reputation
the time when you must continue with what you have decided to do, because it is not possible to get back to an earlier situation
examples: We have passed the point of no return.

definitions.definition: used to say that you accept that somebody else is right when they have disagreed with you or criticized you
examples: Point taken. Let's drop the subject.

definitions.definition: to show that you are better than somebody, especially by making clever remarks, for example in an argument
examples: He was always trying to score points off his teachers.

definitions.definition: a subject that makes you feel angry or upset when it is mentioned
examples: It's a sore point with Sue's parents that the children have not been baptized yet.

definitions.definition: to allow or do something that is not usually acceptable, especially because of a particular situation
examples: I was hoping they would stretch a point and let me stay on for a while.

definitions.definition: expressed in a simple, clear way without any extra information or feelings
examples: The letter was short and to the point.

definitions.definition: to a degree that can be described as something
examples: He was rude to the point of being aggressive.

definitions.definition: to some extent; to some degree but not completely
examples: I agree with you up to a point.

origin: Middle English: the noun partly from Old Frenchpoint, from Latinpunctum‘something that is pricked’, giving rise to the senses ‘unit, mark, point in space or time’; partly from Old Frenchpointe, from Latinpuncta‘pricking’, giving rise to the senses ‘sharp tip, promontory’. The verb is from Old Frenchpointer, and in some senses from the English noun.

definitions.definition: a place where children go to be educated
examples: My brother and Iwent tothe sameschool.
(formal)Whichschooldo theyattend?

a girls'/boys' school
school for somebodya school for girls aged 11–16
school buildings
The kids get theschool busevery morning.

definitions.definition: the process of learning in aschool; the time during your life when you go to aschool
examples: (British English)tostart/leave school
(North American English)tostart/quit school
Where did yougo to school?
(British English)at schoolAll my kids are still at school.

(North American English)in schoolAll my kids are still in school.
(North American English)toteach school(= teach in a school)

Theschool yearwas nearly over.

definitions.definition: the time during the day when children are working in a school
examples: School begins at 9.
after schoolShall I meet you after school today?
at/in schoolThe kids are at/in school until 3.30.
off schoolI'm off school this week.
(especially British English)His mum kept him off school for two weeks when he was ill.
out of school(North American English)His mom kept him out of school for two weeks when he was sick.

after-schoolactivities
The next day was Monday, aschool day.

definitions.definition: all the children or students and the teachers in a school
examples: I had to stand up in front of the whole school.

definitions.definition: a place where people go to learn a particular subject or skill
examples: a drama/language/riding school

definitions.definition: a college or university; the time that you spend there
examples: famous schools like Yale and Harvard
Where did yougo to school?

definitions.definition: a department of a college or university that teaches a particular subject
examples: the business/law/medical school
school of somethingthe School of Dentistry
He was determined toget intomedicalschool.

definitions.definition: a group of writers, artists, etc. whose style of work or opinions have been influenced by the same person or ideas
examples: the Dutch school of painting

definitions.definition: a large number of fish or other sea animals, swimming together
examples: a school of dolphins

definitions.definition: an old-fashioned person who likes to do things as they were done in the past
a way of thinking that a number of people share
examples: There are two schools of thought about how this illness should be treated.
He belongs to the school of thought that says that competition can be very stimulating for children.

definitions.definition: very fashionable
examples: The assistants look like they're too cool for school.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 8 Old Englishscōl,scolu, via Latin from Greekskholē‘leisure, philosophy, lecture-place’, reinforced in Middle English by Old Frenchescole.nounsense 9 late Middle English: from Middle Low German, Middle Dutchschōle, of West Germanic origin; related to Old Englishscolu‘troop’. Compare withshoalsense (1).

definitions.definition: the final part of a period of time, an event, an activity or a story
examples: It's the end of an era.
the end of the book
at the end of somethingat the end of the week
at the endThere’ll be a chance to ask questions at the end.
by the end of somethingHe wants the reports by the end of the month.
to the end of somethingShe remained active as an artist to the end of her life.
towards the end (of something)I was getting bored towards the end of the talk.
until the endWe didn't leave until thevery end.
until the end of somethingI'm staying until the end of this week.

Our end-of-season sale starts next week.

definitions.definition: a situation in which something does not exist any more
examples: It was the end of all his dreams.
That was by no means the end of the matter.
at an endThe war was finally at an end.
The meetingcame to an end(= finished).

an end to somethingThey havecalled for an end toviolence.
an end to the conflict/war

definitions.definition: the part of an object or a place that is the furthest away from its centre
examples: I joined the end of the queue.
Go to the end of the line!
at the end of somethingTurn right at the end of the road.
His office is the room atthe other endof the corridor.

on the end of somethingYou've got something on the end of your nose.

These two products are fromopposite endsof the price range.

They live in the end house.

definitions.definition: an aim or a purpose
examples: They are prepared to use violence in pursuit of their ends.

We are willing to make any concessions necessaryto this end(= in order to achieve this).

definitions.definition: a part of an activity with which somebody is involved, especially in business
examples: We need somebody to handle the marketing end of the business.

I have kept my end of the bargain.

definitions.definition: either of two places connected by a phone call, journey, etc.
examples: I answered the phone but there was no oneat the other end.
Jean is going to meet me atthe other end.

definitions.definition: one of the two halves of a sports field
examples: The teams changed ends at half-time.

definitions.definition: a small piece that is left after something has been used
examples: a cigarette end

definitions.definition: a person’s death. People say‘end’to avoid saying‘death’.
examples: She came toan untimely end(= died young).
I was with him at the end(= when he died).
(literary)Hemet his end(= died)at the Battle of Waterloo.

definitions.definition: alinemanwho is nearest to the side of the field
examples: a defensive end
He does as good a job as any end in football at helping his teammates.

definitions.definition: used to introduce the most important fact after everything has been considered
examples: At the end of the day, he'll still have to make his own decision.

definitions.definition: having nothing to do and not knowing what you want to do
examples: Come and see us, if you're at a loose end.

definitions.definition: something unpleasant that happens to somebody, for example punishment or a violent death, usually because of their own actions
examples: He'llcome to a sticky endone of these days if he carries on like that.

definitions.definition: to have almost nothing left of something
examples: I'm at the end of my patience.
They are at the end of their food supply.

definitions.definition: to feel that you cannot deal with a difficult situation any more because you are too tired, worried, etc.
examples: You’d better let her know you’re safe. She’s at the end of her tether.

definitions.definition: to be the person that an action, etc. is directed at, especially an unpleasant one
examples: She found herself on the receiving end of a great deal of criticism.

definitions.definition: to be so worried by a problem that you do not know what to do next
examples: She was at her wits’ end wondering how she’d manage it all in the time.

definitions.definition: when you say that people or situations arethe end, you mean that you are annoyed with them
the first sign of something ending
examples: The scandal was the beginning of the end of his career as a politician.

definitions.definition: to become very tired by trying to do too many things and going to bed late and getting up early
a thing that is itself important and not just a part of something more important
examples: For her, shopping had become an end in itself.
For him, travelling had become an end in itself rather than a means of seeing new places.

definitions.definition: bad or unfair methods of doing something are acceptable if the result of that action is good or positive
examples: He defended a morality in which the end justifies the means.
That's only OK if you believe that the end justifies the means.

definitions.definition: (to reach) the point at which something can no longer continue in the same way
examples: A defeat in the second round marked the end of the line for last year's champion.
The loss of this contract could signal the end of the line for the shipyard.
This latest disagreement could mean the end of the road for the band.

definitions.definition: used when you are stating that there is nothing more that can be said or done about something
examples: If she doesn’t agree, I won’t go ahead. End of story.

definitions.definition: in a line, with theendstouching
examples: They arranged the tables end to end.

definitions.definition: to have sex
to be the person in a group who is chosen or forced to perform an unpleasant duty or task
to understand something in the wrong way
to suddenly become very angry or emotional
to do everything possible, even if it is difficult, in order to get or achieve something
examples: I'd go to the ends of the earth to see her again.

definitions.definition: after a long period of time or series of events
examples: He tried various jobs and in the end became an accountant.
In the end, they decided to spend Christmas at home.

definitions.definition: after everything has been considered
examples: You can try your best to impress the interviewers but in the end it's often just a question of luck.

definitions.definition: to start or be made to start a new and difficult activity that you are not prepared for
examples: Junior hospital doctors are thrown in at the deep end in their first jobs.
The company believes in throwing new employees in at the deep end with no training.

definitions.definition: to continue to be cheerful in a difficult situation
something that shows you are nearly at the end of a long and difficult time or situation
examples: For the first time since the start of his treatment, we can now see light at the end of the tunnel.

definitions.definition: to earn just enough money to be able to buy the things you need
examples: Many families struggle to make ends meet.

definitions.definition: to shock or frighten somebody
examples: a chilling tale that will make your hair stand on end

definitions.definition: a thing or an action that is not interesting or important in itself but is a way of achieving something else
examples: He doesn't particularly like the work but he sees it as a means to an end.
I don't enjoy studying computing—it's just a means to an end.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: It upset me no end to hear they'd split up.

definitions.definition: a lot of something
examples: We had no end of trouble getting them to agree.

definitions.definition: not the worst thing that could happen to somebody
examples: Failing one exam is not the end of the world.
It won't be the end of the world if you don't get the job.

definitions.definition: to keep being reminded of something because somebody is always talking to you about it
examples: If we don't get her a dog we'll never hear the end of it.

definitions.definition: in a position standinguprightrather than lying flat
examples: It'll fit if you stand it on end.
Stand the box on end.
Stand it on end.

definitions.definition: for the stated length of time, without stopping
examples: He would disappear forweeks on end.

definitions.definition: to kill yourself
a situation in which somebody is treated badly or unfairly
the place or position of greatest difficulty or responsibility
examples: He started work at the sharp end of the business, as a salesman.

definitions.definition: an event or action that is the beginning of something more serious and/or unpleasant
examples: The introduction of a tax on workplace parking is seen by many as the thin end of the wedge.

definitions.definition: continuing until you have done everything you can, or until something is completely finished, despite difficulties and problems
examples: They were prepared to fight to the bitter end for their rights.
We will fight this case to the bitter end.

origin: Old Englishende(noun),endian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutcheinde(noun),einden(verb) and GermanEnde(noun),enden(verb).

definitions.definition: the house or flat that you live in, especially with your family
examples: Most older people prefer to stay in their own homes.

While travelling she missed thecomforts of home.

from homeI'll call you from home later.
stray dogs needing new homes

definitions.definition: a house or flat, etc., when you think of it as property that can be bought and sold
examples: aholiday/summer home

Privatehome ownershipis increasing faster than ever.
They applied for a home improvement loan.

definitions.definition: the town, district, country, etc. that you come from, or where you are living and that you feel you belong to
examples: I often think about my friendsback home.
Jane left England and made Greece her home.
be home to somebodyJamaica is home to over two million people.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a family living together, and the way it behaves
examples: She came from a violent home.

He had always wanted a real home with a wife and children.

definitions.definition: a place where people who cannot care for themselves live and are cared for by others
examples: achildren’s home
a residential home for young people with learning disabilities
in a homeShe has lived in a home since she was six.

definitions.definition: a place where pets with no owner are taken care of
examples: adogs’/cats’ home

definitions.definition: the place where a plant or animal usually lives; the place where somebody/something can be found
examples: This region is the home of many species of wild flower.

Beverly Hills is the home of the stars.
home to somebody/somethingThe Rockies are home to bears and mountain lions.

definitions.definition: a place where an object is kept
examples: We haven'tfound a homefor all our books yet.

definitions.definition: the place where something was first discovered, made or invented
examples: New Orleans, the home of jazz
Greece, the home of democracy
Andalusia, the home of flamenco

definitions.definition: in a person’s own house, flat, etc.
examples: I called round last night, but you weren't at home.

definitions.definition: comfortable and relaxed
examples: Sit down andmake yourself at home.

definitions.definition: in somebody’s own country, not in a foreign country
examples: The president is not as popular at home as he is abroad.

examples: Leeds are playing at home this weekend.

definitions.definition: away from a person’s own house, flat, etc.
examples: He works away from home during the week.

definitions.definition: if a sports team playsaway from home, it plays in the town, etc. that its opponent comes from
examples: The side has scored 24 goals away from home this season.

definitions.definition: you should help and care for your own family, etc. before you start helping other people
if a remark or topic of discussion isclose to home, it is accurate or connected with you in a way that makes you uncomfortable or embarrassed
examples: Her remarks about me were embarrassingly close to home.

definitions.definition: that involves somebody directly
examples: The next year tragedy struck much closer to home.

definitions.definition: to eat a lot of somebody else’s food
examples: How much longer is he staying? He’s eating us out of house and home.

definitions.definition: a place where you feel relaxed and comfortable as if you were in your own home
examples: The guests are made to feel that the hotel is a home from home.

definitions.definition: a home is where the people you love are
used to say how pleasant your home is (especially when you really mean that it is not pleasant at all)
a person’s home is a place where they can be private and safe and do as they like
(used especially about a couple) to start living in a new place
examples: They got married and set up home together in Hull.

definitions.definition: the place where you are happiest, especially a country where you feel you belong more than in your own country because you share the ideas and attitudes of the people who live there
examples: When she moved to Italy, she knew she'd found her true spiritual home.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize a question about somebody/something
examples: Who's she when she's at home?(= I don't know her)

definitions.definition: to do your job in your own home, especially a job that is usually done in an office
examples: I usually work from home on Fridays.

origin: Old Englishhām, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchheemand GermanHeim.

definitions.definition: a very long time
examples: It'll probablytake agesto find a parking space.
for agesI waited for ages.
ages agoCarlos left ages ago.
