definitions.definition: a particular period of a person’s life
examples: inmiddle/old age
15 is an awkward age.

definitions.definition: a particular period of history
examples: the nuclear age
We live in an age of globalization.
through the agesa study of fashion through the ages

definitions.definition: the state of being old
examples: The jacket was showing signs of age.
White hair is a sign of great age.
with ageWine improves with age.
the wisdom that comes with age

definitions.definition: a length of time that is a division of anepoch
to behave in a way that is suitable for somebody of your age and not as though you were much younger
examples: Isn’t it time you started acting your age?

definitions.definition: when a personcomes of age, they reach the age when they have an adult’s legal rights and responsibilities
examples: The money will go to the children when they come of age.

definitions.definition: if somethingcomes of age, it reaches the stage of development at which people accept and value it
examples: It was the year that concern for the environment really came of age.

definitions.definition: to realize that you are getting old, especially compared with people you are with who are younger than you
a great age
examples: She finally learned to drive at the grand old age of 70.

definitions.definition: now, in the modern world
examples: Slavery continues to exist, even in this day and age.
Why dress so formally in this day and age?

definitions.definition: to seem as old as you really are and not younger or older
examples: She doesn’t look her age; I thought she was ten years younger.

definitions.definition: used in polite expressions to describe somebody as ‘very old’
examples: He was a man of advanced years.
(humorous)Even at my advanced age I still know how to enjoy myself!

definitions.definition: if you talk about a person beingof a certain age, you mean that they are no longer young but not yet old
examples: The show appeals to an audience of a certain age.

definitions.definition: an age that is considered to be very old
examples: He lived to the ripe old age of 91.

definitions.definition: not legally old enough to do a particular thing
examples: It is illegal to sell vapes to children who are under age.

origin: Middle English: from Old French, based on Latinaetas,aetat-, fromaevum‘age, era’.

definitions.definition: Saturday and Sunday
examples: this/next/last weekend
at the weekend(British English)The office is closed at the weekend.
on the weekend(especially North American English)The office is closed on the weekend.
on the weekend of…It happened on the weekend of 24 and 25 April.
on a weekend(British English, informal)I like to go out on a weekend.
over the weekendAre you doing anything over the weekend?
Have a good weekend!
We go skiing most weekends in winter.
Wespent the weekendat the beach.
Her birthday isthis coming weekend.
There was a meeting in Brusselsthis past weekend.
Box office receipts for the film'sopening weekendwere disappointing.
She's away for the weekend.

definitions.definition: Saturday and Sunday, or a slightly longer period, as a holiday
examples: He won a weekend for two in Rome.
Michael recently enjoyed a weekend in Dublin.
a weekend break
(British English)a bank holiday weekend

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a very short period of time
examples: Could youwait a moment, please?
One moment, please(= Please wait a short time).
for a momentHe thought for a moment before replying.
For one brief moment I thought, ‘I can handle this.’
in a momentI'll be back in a moment.
Have you got a moment?
This won't take a moment.
We arrivednot a moment too soon(= almost too late).
Moments later(= a very short time later), I heard a terrible crash.

definitions.definition: an exact point in time
examples: at the momentWe're busy at the moment(= now).
At that very moment, the phone rang.
You need to be aware of what you are doingat any given moment.
in a moment of somethingI agreed in a moment of weakness.
From that moment onFrom that moment on, she never felt really well again.

definitions.definition: a good time for doing something; an opportunity
examples: I'm waiting forthe right momentto tell him the bad news.
Have I caught you at a bad moment?

definitions.definition: a particular time in somebody's life, or in the course of an event or the development of something
examples: That was one of the happiest moments of my life.
Her finest moment came when she won Wimbledon.
The film has many memorable moments.
a great moment in the country's history
This referendum is adefining momentfor our nation.
I managed tocapture the momenton film.
Enjoy your moment of fame!

definitions.definition: very soon
examples: Hurry up! He'll be back any moment now.
He might wake up at any moment.

definitions.definition: not long in advance; without warning or time for preparation
examples: This was the best room we could get at such short notice.
You must be ready to leave at a moment's notice.

definitions.definition: now, at the present time
examples: At this moment in time, I don't know what my decision will be.

definitions.definition: to be popular for a short time (used about something that is not usually popular)
examples: The previously unfashionable Tyrol is currently having a moment.
Classic '70s hairstyles are having a moment once more.

definitions.definition: to not act normally for a short time, for example when you forget where you are or what you are doing
examples: Sorry, I was just having a moment. What time is it?

definitions.definition: to have a short argument or romantic exchange
examples: Oh, sorry—were you two having a moment?

definitions.definition: for now; for a short time
examples: This house is big enough for the moment, but we'll have to move if we have children.

definitions.definition: to have short times that are better, more interesting, etc. than others
examples: The job isn't exciting all the time, but it has its moments.
I found the play rather boring, but it had its moments.

definitions.definition: completely mentally involved in what you are doing or experiencing
examples: The time goes so quickly—you just have to remember to stay in the moment.

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to wait for a short time
examples: ‘Is Mr Burns available?’ ‘Just a second, please—I'll check.’

definitions.definition: the latest possible time before an important event
examples: They changed the plans at the last minute.
Don't leave your decision to the last moment.

definitions.definition: as soon as…
examples: I want to see him the moment he arrives.
The moment she walked through the door I thought she looked different.
I felt at home here from the very moment I arrived.

definitions.definition: a time when somebody/something is tested, or when important decisions are made
examples: The moment of truth is when the trainee pilots take over the controls of the plane.

definitions.definition: certainly not; not at all
examples: I don't think for a moment that she'll accept but you can ask her.

definitions.definition: very important
examples: matters of great moment

definitions.definition: famous, important and talked about a lot now
examples: She's the fashion designer of the moment.

definitions.definition: suddenly, without planning in advance
examples: I phoned him up on the spur of the moment.
a spur-of-the-moment decision

definitions.definition: the best time to do something in order for it to be successful
examples: The trick is in finding the (right) psychological moment to ask him for more money.

definitions.definition: to wait for a short time
examples: Can you wait a moment while I make a call?
Hey! Wait a minute! I'll come with you!

definitions.definition: used when you have just noticed or remembered something, or had a sudden idea
examples: Wait a moment—this isn't the right key.

origin: late Middle English: from Latinmomentum, frommovimentum, frommovere‘to move’.

definitions.definition: a method, style or manner of doing something
examples: I prefer to do things the easy way.
I love him and I think he feelsthe same way.
way to do somethingI'm sure we canfind a wayto make this work.
Using the subway isthe only way toavoid the traffic.
(informal, disapproving)That's no wayto speak to your mother!
way of doing somethingI'm not happy with this way of working.
way (that)…It's not what you say—it's the way that you say it.
Ihate the wayshe always criticizes me.
I told you we should have done itmy way!
in the… wayShe dressesin the same wayas her sister.
Infectious diseases can be acquired in several ways.
In this way, we can track the storms as they move across the ocean.
in the way (that)…It works in the way you'd expect.
New technology has radically changedthe way in whichthis work is done.
in a way that…She writes in a way that is easy to understand.
I generally get what I wantone way or another(= by some means).

definitions.definition: a particular manner or style of behaviour
examples: in a… wayThey grinned at her in a friendly way.
It was not his wayto admit that he had made a mistake.
Don't worry if she seems quiet—it's just her way.
He was showing off,as is the waywith adolescent boys.

definitions.definition: the typical way of behaving and living of a particular group of people
examples: After ten years I'm used to the strange British ways.
She always said that the old ways were the best.

definitions.definition: a route or road that you take in order to reach a place
examples: toask somebody the way
Do you know the way?
way to…Can youtell me the wayto Leicester Square?
way from… to…thebest/quickest/shortest wayfrom A to B
We went thelong way round.

definitions.definition: the route along which somebody/something is moving; the route that somebody/something would take if there was nothing stopping them/it
examples: Get out of my way!I'm in a hurry.
Riot police with shields were blocking the demonstrators' way.
along the wayUnfortunately they ran into a snowstorm along the way.
+ adv./prep.We fought our way through the dense vegetation.
(figurative)We will eventually find a way out of the crisis.

definitions.definition: a road, path or street for travelling along
examples: There's a way across the fields.

definitions.definition: used in the names of streets
examples: 106 Headley Way

definitions.definition: a particular direction; in a particular direction
examples: Which way did they go?
We just missed a car comingthe other way.
Lookboth ways(= look left and right)before crossing the road.
Make sure that sign'sthe right way up.
The pipe could be moved a few incheseither way.
Kids were runningthis way and that(= in all directions).
They decided to split the money four ways(= between four different people).
(figurative)Which way(= for which party)are you going to vote?

definitions.definition: a means of going into or leaving a place, such as a door or gate
examples: theway in/out
They escaped out the back way.

definitions.definition: a distance or period of time between two points
examples: A little way up on the left is the Museum of Modern Art.
We had to goa long waybefore we found a place to eat.
September wasa long way off.
You cameall this wayto see us?
(figurative)The economy still hasa way to goto a full recovery.
(North American English, informal)We still havea ways to go.

definitions.definition: an area, a part of a country, etc.
examples: over/down… wayI think he lives somewhere over Greenwich way.
down my/our/your wayI'll stop by and see you next time I'm down your way.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something
examples: I have changed in every way.
It's been quite a day,one way and another(= for several reasons).

definitions.definition: a particular condition or state
examples: in a… wayThe economy's in a bad way.
I don't know how we're going to manage,the way things are.

definitions.definition: on the other side of the street, etc.
examples: Music blared from the open window of the house across the way.

definitions.definition: during the whole journey/period of time
examples: She didn't speak a word to me all the way back home.
the whole way to Arizona
This bus doesn't go all the way so you'll have to change.
I watched the movie the whole way through.

definitions.definition: completely; as much as it takes to achieve what you want
examples: I'm fighting him all the way.
You can feel that the audience is with her all the way.

definitions.definition: as you make progress with something
examples: You'll learn something along the way.

definitions.definition: used to say that things often happen in a particular way, especially when it is not convenient
however you choose to look at a situation
to behave or do things in a particular manner because it is part of your character
examples: It's not his fault he's so pompous—he was born that way.

definitions.definition: to be easy compared with what came before
examples: It's downhill all the way from here. We'll soon be finished.

definitions.definition: to become worse or less successful
examples: I started work as a journalist and it was downhill all the way for my health.
It's been all downhill for his career since then, with four defeats in five games.

definitions.definition: to have habits or opinions that you have had for a long time and that you do not want to change
examples: Grandma is so set in her ways.

definitions.definition: to have nearly achieved something and be going to achieve it soon
examples: She is well on the way to recovery.
He is well on the way to establishing himself among the top ten players in the world.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a comment or question that is not directly related to what you have been talking about
examples: By the way, I found that book you were looking for.
What's the time, by the way?
Oh by the way, if you see Jackie, tell her I'll call her this evening.

definitions.definition: by a route that includes the place mentioned
examples: The artist recently arrived in Paris from Bulgaria by way of Vienna.
She came to TV by way of drama school.

definitions.definition: as a form of something; for something; as a means of something
examples: He received £600 by way of compensation from the company.
She rolled her eyes by way of an answer and left.

definitions.definition: to start to live or behave in a different way from before
examples: He was in trouble with the police as a teenager but now he’s completely changed his ways.
Your father is unlikely to change his ways now.

definitions.definition: to gradually achieve something or move somewhere by being determined and using a lot of effort
examples: She clawed her way to the top of her profession.
Slowly, he clawed his way out from under the collapsed building.

definitions.definition: to remove things that are stopping the progress or movement of something
examples: The ruling could clear the way for extradition proceedings.

definitions.definition: to happen to you by chance, or when you were not expecting it
examples: He took the first job that came his way.
Have any interesting articles come your way recently?

definitions.definition: to have two opposite effects or results
used to say that it does not matter which one of two possibilities happens, is chosen or is true
examples: Was it his fault or not? Either way, an explanation is due.
We could meet today or tomorrow—I don't mind one way or the other.

definitions.definition: in all directions
examples: Her hair tumbled every which way.

definitions.definition: to move along carefully, for example when it is dark, by touching walls, objects, etc.
to be careful about how you do things, usually because you are in a situation that you are not familiar with
examples: She was new in the job, still feeling her way.

definitions.definition: to discover the right route (to a place)
examples: I hope you can find your way home.

definitions.definition: to come to a place or a situation by chance or without intending to
examples: He eventually found his way into acting.

definitions.definition: to become used to doing something/to lose the habit of doing something
examples: The women had got into the way of going up on the deck every evening.

definitions.definition: to prevent somebody from doing something; to prevent something from happening
examples: He wouldn't allow emotions to get in the way of him doing his job.

definitions.definition: to get or do what you want, especially when somebody has tried to stop you
examples: She always gets her own way in the end.

definitions.definition: to break or fall down
examples: The pillars gave way and a section of the roof collapsed.
Her numb leg gave way beneath her and she stumbled clumsily.

definitions.definition: to stop resisting somebody/something; to agree to do something that you do not want to do
examples: He refused to give way on any of the points.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to be or go first
examples: Give way to traffic already on the roundabout.

definitions.definition: to allow yourself to be very strongly affected by something, especially an emotion
examples: Flinging herself on the bed, she gave way to helpless misery.

definitions.definition: to be replaced by something
examples: The storm gave way to bright sunshine.
As he drew nearer, his anxiety gave way to relief.

definitions.definition: to have fullsexual intercoursewith somebody
to have known each other for a long time
examples: We go back a long way, he and I.

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.
A small amount of this paint goes a long way(= covers a large area).
(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something
examples: The new law goes a long way towards solving the problem.

definitions.definition: to make a special effort to do something
examples: He would always go out of his way to be friendly towards her.
She went out of her way to help them.

definitions.definition: to do as you choose, especially when somebody has advised you against it
examples: It's best to let her go her own way if you don't want a fight.
He's always gone his own way when it comes to design.

definitions.definition: to end a relationship with somebody
examples: When the business was sold they went their separate ways.
They decided to go their separate ways after being together for five years.
When we finished school, we all went our separate ways.

definitions.definition: to go in a different direction from somebody you have been travelling with
to travel in the same direction as somebody
examples: I'm going your way—I'll walk with you.

definitions.definition: to go well for you; to be in your favour
examples: By the third round he knew the fight was going his way.

definitions.definition: to die
by having an unpleasant experience or by making mistakes
examples: She won't listen to my advice so she'll just have tolearn the hard way.
He learned about the dangers of drugs the hard way.

definitions.definition: to have made a lot of progress
examples: We've come a long way since the early days of the project.
The study of genes has come a long way in recent years.

definitions.definition: to be a sensible person
to have or want to have the advantages of two different situations or ways of behaving that are impossible to combine
examples: You can't have it both ways. If you can afford to go out all the time, you can afford to pay off some of your debts.

definitions.definition: used to say in an angry way that although you are not happy about something that somebody has said, you are not going to argue
examples: Oh OK, then. Have it your own way!

definitions.definition: to have something arranged in the way that you want, even though other people want it to be arranged differently
examples: The German team weren't going to let the English have everything their own way.

definitions.definition: to need to make a lot of progress before you can achieve something
examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: used to say that something often happens in a particular way, especially when it is out of your control
examples: First love affairs have a way of not working out.
Fate has a way of changing the best of plans.

definitions.definition: to be good at dealing with somebody/something
examples: He has a way with small children.
She has a way with words(= is very good at expressing herself).

definitions.definition: to persuade somebody to have sex with you
used to explain what you would do if you had total control over something
examples: If I had my way, social media would be banned completely.

definitions.definition: of any type
examples: I don't approve of violence in any shape or form.
I can't stand insects in any shape or form.
I do not condone bullying in any way, shape or form.

definitions.definition: on a large/small scale
examples: The new delivery service has taken off in a big way.
Many people are investing in a small way in the stock market.
He then started spending money in a big way.

definitions.definition: (to be/become) pregnant
used to show that a statement has more than one meaning
examples: With the first goal he used his head in more ways than one.

definitions.definition: used to say what normally happens in a particular situation
examples: In the ordinary way, she's not a nervous person.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: to some extent; not completely
examples: In a way it was one of our biggest mistakes.

definitions.definition: in a manner that is appropriate to or typical of a person or thing but that may seem unusual to other people
examples: I expect she does love you in her own way.
The building is very attractive in its own way.
He was attractive in his own way.

definitions.definition: stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: You'll have to move—you're in my way.
I left them alone, as I felt I was in the way.
There were several rocks in the way.

definitions.definition: used in questions and negative sentences to talk about the types of something that are available
examples: There isn't much in the way of entertainment in this place.

definitions.definition: to avoid somebody
examples: Let's keep out of her way while she's in such a bad mood.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to make a lot of money easily and feel very pleased about it
to get yourself into or out of a situation by lying
to deliberately avoid seeing somebody/something
examples: Prison officers know what's going on, but look the other way.

definitions.definition: to become lost
examples: We lost our way in the dark.
She lost her way in the fog.

definitions.definition: to forget or move away from the purpose or reason for something
examples: I feel that the project has lost its way.
This project seems to have lost its way.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to pass; to allow somebody/something to take the place of somebody/something
examples: Make way for the Lord Mayor!
Tropical forest is felled to make way for grassland.

definitions.definition: to move or get somewhere; to make progress
examples: Will you be able tomake your own wayto the airport(= get there without help, a lift, etc.)?
Is this your plan formaking your way in the world?

definitions.definition: to stop behaving badly
(to take/find) an acceptable course of action that avoids two extreme positions
used to show that you are certain about something
examples: It was the wrong decision—there are no two ways about it.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no possibility that you will do something or that something will happen
examples: ‘Do you want to help?’ ‘No way!’
No way am I going to drive them there.
There's no way we could afford that sort of money.

definitions.definition: during the journey
examples: He stopped for breakfast on the way.
She grabbed her phone and bag on her way out.

definitions.definition: going or coming
examples: I'd better be on my way(= I must leave)soon.
The letter should be on its way to you.
Then he went off on his merry way.

definitions.definition: not yet born
examples: They've got three kids and one on the way.

definitions.definition: to make it possible for somebody to do something or for something to happen
examples: The agreement could open the way for the country to pay off its debts.

definitions.definition: in the opposite position, direction or order
examples: I think it should go on the other way round.

definitions.definition: the opposite situation
examples: I didn't leave you. It was the other way round(= you left me).

definitions.definition: in a safe place where somebody/something cannot be hurt or injured or do any damage to somebody/something
examples: She put the knife in a drawer, out of harm’s way.
I prefer the children to play in the garden where they’re out of harm’s way.

definitions.definition: no longer stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: I moved my legs out of the way so that she could get past.
I didn't say anything until Dad was out of the way.

definitions.definition: finished; dealt with
examples: Our region is poised for growth once the election is out of the way.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences to mean ‘unusual’
examples: She had obviously noticed nothing out of the way.

definitions.definition: not on the route that you planned to take
examples: I'd love a lift home—if it's not out of your way.

definitions.definition: a point at which two people or groups of people decide to separate
examples: These events led to a final parting of the ways.

definitions.definition: to create a situation in which somebody will be able to do something or something can happen
examples: This decision paved the way for changes in employment rights for women.

definitions.definition: to make enough money to pay what it costs to keep it going
examples: The bridge is still not paying its way.

definitions.definition: to pay for everything yourself without having to rely on anyone else’s money
to walk carefully, choosing the safest, driest, etc. place to put your feet
examples: She picked her way delicately over the rough ground.
We picked our way carefully over the jagged rocks.

definitions.definition: to make somebody annoyed or angry, often without intending to, by doing or saying something that offends them
examples: She tends to rub people up the wrong way.

definitions.definition: to realize or admit that you have done something wrong and decide to change your behaviour
examples: Will they realize the error of their ways before it is too late?

definitions.definition: to find that it is possible or convenient to do something
examples: Small builders cannot see their way clear to take on many trainees.

definitions.definition: to get an idea of what is likely to happen before deciding what to do
to do something first so that other people can follow
to make it easier for somebody/something to develop or make progress
examples: These negotiations are intended to smooth the path to a peace treaty.
We’re trying to smooth the way for women who want to resume their careers.

definitions.definition: to (not) prevent somebody from doing something
examples: If you believe you can make her happy, I won't stand in your way.
He wanted to go to university and would let nothing stand in his way.

definitions.definition: to bebisexual(= sexually attracted to both men and women)
to end a difficult situation by choosing the simplest solution even if it is not the best one
examples: He took the easy way out and didn’t go to the meeting.
She took the easy way out and paid someone to write the article for her.

definitions.definition: to be offended by a remark that was not intended to be offensive
examples: Don't take this the wrong way, but maybe you're trying a bit too hard.

definitions.definition: to make excuses and give reasons for not doing something; to manage to get yourself out of a difficult situation
examples: I managed to talk my way out of having to give a speech.

definitions.definition: that is the situation and we cannot change it, so we must accept it
there are many different ways to achieve something
in my opinion
having started
examples: Preparations are well under way for a week of special events in May.

definitions.definition: a plan of action that is likely to be successful
examples: Personal electronic health records are the way forward for effective healthcare in the future.

definitions.definition: something that allows you to join a group of people, an industry, etc. that it is difficult to join, or to understand something that it is difficult to understand
the typical pattern of behaviour of a person or group
examples: the American way of life
She loved the Spanish way of life and immediately felt at home there.
the beliefs and practices of the Hindu way of life

definitions.definition: the way that most people behave; the way that things happen, which you cannot change
examples: The rich and powerful make the decisions—that's the way of the world.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody else has either to agree with your opinion or to leave
the methods and materials available for doing something
examples: ways and means of raising money
Newspapers have ways and means of getting hold of secret information.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that you are pleased about something they have done
examples: Good work, guys! Way to go!
Way to go! I wish I could do that!

definitions.definition: the way to make somebody like or love you
examples: The way to a man's heart is through his stomach(= by giving him good food).

definitions.definition: if you really want to do something then you will find a way of doing it
a series of victories or successes
examples: He'll look to continue his winning ways with a victory in Mexico on 16 February.

definitions.definition: to do something from beginning to end, especially when it takes a lot of time or effort
examples: She worked her way through the pile of documents.

definitions.definition: to have a job or series of jobs while studying, travelling, etc. in order to pay for your education, etc.
to move regularly to a more senior position in a company
examples: He worked his way up from messenger boy to account executive.

origin: Old Englishweg, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwegand GermanWeg, from a base meaning ‘move, carry’.

definitions.definition: the highest part or point of something
examples: at the top of somethingShe was standing at the top of the stairs.
The title isright at the topof the page.
at the topWrite your name at the top.
to the topHe filled my glass to the top.
to the top of somethingWe climbed to thevery topof the hill.
on the topSnow was falling on the mountain tops.
on the top of somethingHe was completely bald on the top of his head.
I was out of breath when Ireached the top.
(British English)the top of the milk(= the cream that rises to the top of a bottle of milk)
The wind was blowing in the tops of the trees.

definitions.definition: the upper flat surface of something
examples: Can you polish the top of the table?

definitions.definition: a piece of clothing worn on the upper part of the body
examples: I need a top to go with this skirt.
a tracksuit/bikini/pyjama top
He waswearing ablue hoodedtop.
She wore a pink skirt with a matching top.

definitions.definition: a thing that you put on the end of something to close it
examples: Where's the top of this pen?
a bottle with a screw top

definitions.definition: the highest or most important rank or position
examples: at the top of somethingThey finished the season at the top of the league.
He's at the top of his profession.
We have a lot of things to do, but packing is at thetop of the list.
at the topThe company needs to make a change at the top.
to the topShe is determined to make it to the top(= achieve fame or success).

definitions.definition: the end of a street, table, etc. that is furthest away from you or from where you usually come to it
examples: I'll meet you at the top of Thorpe Street.

definitions.definition: the leaves of a plant that is grown mainly for its root
examples: Remove the green tops from the carrots.

definitions.definition: used after an amount of money, time, etc. to show that it is the highest, longest, etc. possible
examples: It couldn't have cost more than £50, tops.

definitions.definition: a child’s toy that turns round on a point when it is moved very quickly by hand or by a string
examples: She was so confused—her mind wasspinning like a top.

definitions.definition: high up/low down in the structure of an organization or a society
examples: These workers are at the bottom of the economic heap.

definitions.definition: in the highest position or rank in a profession or career
as loudly as possible
examples: She was screaming at the top of her voice.
I was shouting at the top of my voice but she couldn't hear me.

definitions.definition: to get very angry
in the least/most important position in a group of people or things
examples: It’s been 20 years since a British player was top of the pile.

definitions.definition: to win a contest or an argument
examples: In most boardroom disputes he tends to come out on top.

definitions.definition: going to every part of a place in a very careful way
examples: We cleaned the house from top to bottom.

definitions.definition: completely; all over
examples: She was dressed in green from top to toe.

definitions.definition: to be too much for somebody to manage or deal with
examples: All this extra work is getting on top of him.

definitions.definition: to manage to control or deal with something
examples: How will I ever get on top of all this work?

definitions.definition: just guessing or using your memory, without taking time to think carefully or check the facts
examples: I can't remember the name off the top of my head, but I can look it up for you.

definitions.definition: on the highest point or surface
examples: a cake with cream on top

definitions.definition: in a leading position or in control
examples: She remained on top for the rest of the match.

definitions.definition: in addition
examples: Look, here's 30 dollars, and I'll buy you lunch on top.

definitions.definition: on, over or covering something/somebody
examples: Books were piled on top of one another.

definitions.definition: in addition to something
examples: He gets commission on top of his salary.

definitions.definition: very close to something/somebody
examples: We were all living on top of each other in that tiny apartment.

definitions.definition: in control of a situation
examples: Do you think he's really on top of his job?

definitions.definition: very happy or proud
done with too much acting, emotion or effort
examples: His performance is completely over the top.
an over-the-top reaction

definitions.definition: to go back to the beginning of a song, piece of music, etc. and repeat it
examples: OK, everybody, let's take it from the top.

definitions.definition: with little or no hair on the head
examples: He's starting to get a little thin on top(= he's losing his hair).

definitions.definition: used to talk about a person’s intelligence
examples: He hasn't got much up top(= he isn't very intelligent).
It’s what’s up top(= how intelligent you are)that matters.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 9 late Old Englishtopp(noun), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchtop‘summit, crest’.nounsense 10 late Old English, of unknown origin.

definitions.definition: what is measured in minutes, hours, days, etc.
examples: The world exists in time and space.

in timeA visit to the museum will take youback in timeto the 1930s.

definitions.definition: the time shown on a clock in minutes and hours
examples: What time is it/What’s the time?

(British English)What time do you make it?
(North American English)What time do you have?

(British English)Can shetell the timeyet(= say what time it is by looking at a clock)?
(North American English)Can shetell timeyet?

definitions.definition: the time measured in a particular part of the world
examples: 6 o’clock local time

definitions.definition: the time when something happens or when something should happen
examples: What time do you finish work?
The baby loves bath time.

time for somethingIt's time for lunch.
time (that)…It's time the kids were in bed.

by the time (that)…By the time you get there the meeting will be over.

at the timeI didn't realize it at the time, but that was the best day of my life.

You'll feel differently about itwhen the time comes(= when it happens).
around the time of somethingSeveral articles were published around the time of his death in 2018.

definitions.definition: an amount of time; the amount of time available to work, rest, etc.
examples: I can't go with you—I don'thave time.

time for somethingDo you have time for a chat?

I can probablymake the timeto see them.

What do you like to do in yourfree time?

Sorry, I can’t spare the time.
Don'twasteany moretime!
What awaste of time!

Jane's worked herefor some time(= for a fairly long period of time).

I don’t want to take up too much of your precious time.
What’s the hurry? We have all the time in the world.

definitions.definition: a period of time, either long or short, during which you do something or something happens
examples: His injuries willtake a long timeto heal.
It took them a long time to get home.
Did you have to wait a long time to see the doctor?
for a timeI lived in Egypt for a time.
I was only there fora short time.
The early morning is the besttime of day.
Her parents dieda long time ago.
At one time(= at a period of time in the past)Emily was my best friend.
Mr Curtis was the managerin my time(= when I was working there).

definitions.definition: a period of history connected with particular events or experiences in people’s lives
examples: at the time of somethingThe movie is set at the time of the Russian Revolution.
in… time(s)the politician who promised ‘peace in our time’
in ancient times

the violent times we live in(= the present period of history)
in times of somethingin times of trouble

Times are hard for the unemployed.
Times have changedsince Grandma was young.

definitions.definition: an occasion when you do something or when something happens
examples: Every timeI hear that song I feel happy.
It doesn't matter if you don't win every time(= every time you play).
Next timeyou're here let's have lunch together.
He failed his driving test three times.
He's determined to passthis time.

I see her two or threetimes a week.
How many times(= how often)do I have to tell you not to do that?
(especially North American English)I rememberone time(= once)we had to abandon our car in the snow.
(formal)At no time didI give my consent to the plan.

definitions.definition: an event or occasion that you experience in a particular way
examples: Did youhave a good timein Spain?
They'rehaving a hard timedealing with her illness.
I had an awful time in the hospital.
Mike and I shared some really good times.

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event
examples: The winner's time was 11.6 seconds.

one of the fastest times ever

definitions.definition: the number ofbeats(= units of rhythm)in abarof music
examples: This piece is in four-four time.
a slow waltz time

definitions.definition: the correct speed and rhythm of a piece of music
examples: in time to somethingTry and dance in time to the music(= with the same speed and rhythm).

in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)
He always plays in perfect time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.

examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.

examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study

examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: the possibility of something bad happening at some time in the future; a situation that could be dangerous or have a bad result
examples: Thehealth risksare very low.
risk of somethingahigh riskof failure
a serious/significant risk of something
The study found a slightlyincreased riskof cancer in this group.
Steps have been taken toreduce the riskof any trouble.
Use filters or boil water tominimize the riskof infection.
risk of doing somethingSmoking canincrease the riskof developing heart disease.
risk (that)…There is still a risk that the whole deal will fall through.
risk to somebody/somethingThe chemicalspose little risk(= are not dangerous)to human health.
a calculated risk(= one that you think is small compared with the possible benefits)
Trying new things can be a gamble, but it's worth the risk.
Patients should be made aware of the risks involved with this treatment.
Make sure you understand therisks and benefits.
Cigarette smoking is arisk factorfor this disease.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that is likely to cause problems or danger at some time in the future
examples: Those old boxes in the corridor are a fire risk.
a safety/health risk
risk to something/somebodyThe group was considered to be a risk to national security.
Pollutants in the riverpose arealriskto the fish.

definitions.definition: a person or business that a bank or an insurance company is willing/unwilling to lend money or sell insurance to because they are likely/unlikely to pay back the money, etc.
examples: With five previous claims, he's now a bad insurance risk.
He's a good insurance risk.

definitions.definition: in danger of something unpleasant or harmful happening
examples: As with all diseases, certain groups will be more at risk than others.
If we go to war, innocent lives will beput at risk.
at risk of (doing) somethingJournalists in the zone are at serious risk of being kidnapped.
at risk from (doing) somethingAdults were also at risk from epidemics.

definitions.definition: used to introduce something that may sound stupid or may offend somebody
examples: At the risk of showing my ignorance, how exactly does the internet work?

definitions.definition: with the possibility of harming yourself/somebody/something
examples: He dived in to save the dog at considerable risk to his own life.

definitions.definition: to do something even though you have been warned about the possible dangers and will have to take responsibility for anything bad that happens
examples: Persons swimming beyond this point do so at their own risk(= on a notice).

definitions.definition: to be or put yourself in a situation in which something bad could happen to you
examples: People who are overweight run a risk of a heart attack or stroke.

Investment is all about running risks.

definitions.definition: to do something even though you know that something bad could happen as a result
examples: That's a risk I'm not prepared to take.

origin: mid 17th cent.: from Frenchrisque(noun),risquer(verb), from Italianrisco‘danger’ andrischiare‘run into danger’.

definitions.definition: to or at a distance from somebody/something in space or time

away from somebody/somethingThe president was keptwell awayfrom the demonstrators.

definitions.definition: to a different place or in a different direction
examples: Go away!

definitions.definition: not present
examples: There were ten children away yesterday.

away from somebody/somethingShe was away from work for a week.

definitions.definition: used after verbs to say that something is done continuously or with a lot of energy
examples: She was still writing away furiously when the bell went.

definitions.definition: until disappearing completely
examples: The water boiled away.

definitions.definition: at the opponent’s ground or stadium

definitions.definition: used to say that you would like to be rid of somebody/something

definitions.definition: to help somebody have a fresh, lively state of mind again

definitions.definition: to dance for the whole evening or night
by a very great amount
examples: She's far and away the best player.

definitions.definition: immediately; without delay
examples: I want it sent right away.

definitions.definition: immediately; without delay
examples: I'll do it straight away.

origin: Old Englishonweg,aweg‘on one's way’ (froma-‘to, towards’ andway).

definitions.definition: to move backwards, especially in a vehicle

definitions.definition: to prepare a second copy of a file, program, etc. that can be used if the main one fails or needs extra support

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to or into the place, condition, situation or activity where somebody/something was before

definitions.definition: at a place previously left or mentioned

definitions.definition: in return or reply
examples: She's a tough kid, who, when attacked,fights backhard.

definitions.definition: at a distance away from something
examples: The barriers kept the crowd back.

definitions.definition: in or into the past; ago
examples: The cathedraldates back to1123.

definitions.definition: under control; prevented from being expressed or coming out

definitions.definition: fashionable again
examples: Beards are back.

definitions.definition: from one place to another and back again repeatedly
