definitions.definition: in the past
examples: My dad's always talking about how great everything was back in the day.

definitions.definition: at a particular time in the past
examples: I was a fan back in the days when the band wasn't yet famous.

definitions.definition: behind something
examples: the houses back of the church

definitions.definition: a return to the situation you were in at the beginning of a project, task, etc., because you have made no real progress
examples: If this suggestion isn't accepted, we'll be back to square one.

definitions.definition: to return, or to make somebody return, to a normal way of thinking or behaving after a time when you/they have been very excited, not very practical, etc.

origin: Old Englishbæc, of Germanic origin; related to Middle Dutch and Old Norsebak. The adverb use dates from late Middle English and is a shortening ofaback.

definitions.definition: the money that you spend on something
examples: The garden was transformedat great expense.
No expense was spared(= they spent as much money as was needed)to make the party a success.
He's arranged everything,no expense spared.
She always travels first-class regardless of expense.
The results are well worth the expense.

definitions.definition: something that makes you spend money
examples: Running a car is a big expense.
The company views its workers as an expense instead of an asset.

definitions.definition: money spent in doing a particular job, or for a particular purpose
examples: living/medical/travel expenses
legal/household expenses
The company has cutoperating expensesto their lowest levels in three years
Can I give you something towards expenses?
financial help to meet the expenses of an emergency
The payments he gets barelycoverhisexpenses.
Property investors are entitled to offset theexpenses incurredbuying the property.

definitions.definition: money that you spend while you are working and that your employer will pay back to you later
examples: You can claim back yourtravelling/travelexpenses.
Wepaid their expenses, but nothing more.
anall-expenses-paidtrip
on expenses(British English)to take a client out for a meal on expenses

definitions.definition: paid for by somebody
examples: We were taken out for a meal at the company's expense.

definitions.definition: if you make a jokeat somebody’s expense, you laugh at them and make them feel silly
with loss or damage to somebody/something
examples: He built up the business at the expense of his health.
an education system that benefits the brightest children at the expense of everyone else

definitions.definition: to spend money on something
examples: They went to all the expense of redecorating the house and then they moved.

definitions.definition: to make somebody spend money on something
examples: Their visit put us to a lot of expense.

origin: late Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French, alteration of Old Frenchespense, from late Latinexpensa (pecunia)‘(money) spent’, from Latinexpendere‘pay out’, fromex-‘out’ +pendere‘weigh, pay’.

definitions.definition: joined to something
examples: Please complete the attached application form.
Make sure all the wires remain firmly attached.
attached to somethingThe ball was attached to a length of thin chain.

definitions.definition: liking somebody/something very much
examples: I've never seen two people so attached to each other.
We've grown very attached to this house.
George is obviously very attached to you.

definitions.definition: married or having a romantic relationship with somebody; not single
examples: It would be nice to meet some more guys who aren't attached.

definitions.definition: working for or forming part of an organization
examples: The research unit is attached to the university.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: if one thing isbasedon another, it uses it or is developed from it
examples: The movie is based on a real-life incident.
The report is based on figures from six different European cities.
The novels are allloosely basedon the author's life.
Their research wasbased largelyon anecdotal evidence.

definitions.definition: if a person or business isbasedin a particular place, that is where they live or work, or where the work of the business is done
examples: based in…We're based in Chicago.
a Chicago-based company

definitions.definition: containing something as an important part or feature
examples: lead-based paints
a class-based society

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to be close to doing something; to be going to do something very soon
examples: I was just about to ask you the same thing.

definitions.definition: to not be willing to do something; to not intend to do something
examples: I've never done any cooking and I'm not about to start now.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: unpleasant; full of problems
examples: I have somebad newsfor you, I'm afraid.
bad weather/dreams/habits
I'm having a really bad day.
It was the worst experience of her life.
Bad breath may be helped by regularly using mouthwash.
War is always abad thing.
That was justbad luck.
Things arebad enoughwithout our own guns shelling us.

definitions.definition: of poor quality; below an acceptable standard
examples: I thought it was a very bad article.
Bad diet and lack of exercise can lead to serious health problems.
The movie wasn't as bad as I'd expected.
That's not a bad idea.

definitions.definition: serious; severe
examples: You're heading for a bad case of sunburn.
The engagement was a bad mistake.
My headache is getting worse.
It was a very bad winter that year.

definitions.definition: not appropriate in a particular situation
examples: I know that this is a bad time to ask for help.
He now realized that it had been a bad decision on his part.

definitions.definition: morally unacceptable
examples: The hero gets to shoot all the bad guys.
He said I must have done something bad to deserve it.
I will not tolerate this bad behaviour.
That girl's a bad influence on Tom.

definitions.definition: not behaving well
examples: Have you been a bad boy?

definitions.definition: not able to do something well or in an acceptable way
examples: a bad teacher
You're a bad liar!
He's a bad loser(= he complains when he loses a game).
bad at (doing) somethingShe is so bad at keeping secrets.

definitions.definition: harmful; causing or likely to cause damage
examples: Those shoes are bad for her feet.
Weather like this is bad for business.
Too much work is bad for your health.

definitions.definition: not healthy; painful
examples: I've got a bad back.

definitions.definition: not safe to eat because it hasdecayed(= started to be destroyed by natural processes)
examples: Put the meat in the fridge so it doesn'tgo bad.

definitions.definition: the state of feeling annoyed or angry
examples: It put me in a bad mood for the rest of the day.
John's in a pretty bad mood this morning.

definitions.definition: to feel guilty or sorry about something
examples: She felt pretty bad about leaving him.
Why should I want to make you feel bad?
I feel bad enough without you constantly telling me how it was all my fault!

definitions.definition: to feel or look ill
examples: I'm afraid I'm feeling pretty bad.

definitions.definition: good; excellent
used to try to persuade somebody to agree that something is good
examples: You'll save fifty dollars, which can't be bad, can it?

definitions.definition: to be very much in love
examples: You're not seeing him again tonight, are you? That's five times this week—you've got it bad!

definitions.definition: quite good; better than you expected
examples: ‘How are you?’ ‘Not too bad.’
That wasn't bad for a first attempt.

definitions.definition: used to say ‘bad luck’ or ‘it’s a shame’ when you do not really mean it
examples: If sometimes they're the wrong decisions, too bad.

definitions.definition: a shame; apity
examples: Too bad every day can't be as good as this.

definitions.definition: annoying
examples: Really, it was too bad of you to be so late.

origin: Middle English: perhaps from Old Englishbǣddel‘hermaphrodite, womanish man’.

definitions.definition: a period or state that something/somebody passes through while developing or making progress
examples: at… stageI can't make a decisionat this stage.
At one stageit looked as though they would win.
The product is at the design stage.
in… stageThis technology is still in itsearly stages.
A new version is in thefinal stagesof testing.
stage in somethingShe'sreached acrucialstagein her career.
stage of somethingThe children are at variousstages of development.
stage of doing somethingAll babies go through a stage of not wanting to leave their mother.

definitions.definition: a separate part that a process, etc. is divided into
examples: We did the first stage of the trip by train.
in stagesThe pay increase will be introduced in stages(= not all at once).
All the recipes are broken down into easy stages.
We can take the argument one stage further.

definitions.definition: a raised area, usually in a theatre, etc. where actors, dancers, etc. perform
examples: on stageThere were more than 50 people on stage in one scene.
off stageHalf the band walked off stage.
onto the stageThe audience threw flowers onto the stage.
The main character thentakes the stage(= comes onto it).
Rose exits stage left(= to the left hand side of the stage).

definitions.definition: the theatre and the world of acting as a form of entertainment
examples: The screenplay was originally written for the stage.
on the stageHis parents didn't want him to go on the stage(= to be an actor).
She was a popular star ofstage and screen(= theatre and cinema/movies).
a stage play/musical/show

definitions.definition: an area of activity where important things happen, especially in politics
examples: She was forced to the centre of the political stage.
The country is now a major player onthe world stage.
Germany is playing a leading role onthe international stage.

definitions.definition: astagecoach(= a largecarriagepulled by horses that was used in the past to carry passengers, and often mail, along a regular route)
to make it possible for something to happen; to make something likely to happen
examples: Family problems in childhood can set the stage for stress in adult life.
The thrilling semi-finals set the stage for what should be a great game.

origin: Middle English (denoting a floor of a building, platform, or stopping place): shortening of Old Frenchestage‘dwelling’, based on Latinstare‘to stand’. Current senses of the verb date from the early 17th cent.

definitions.definition: to say or believe that something is the result of a particular thing
examples: She attributes her success to hard work and a little luck.
The power failure was attributed to the recent storms and high winds.

definitions.definition: to say or believe that somebody is responsible for doing something, especially for saying, writing or painting something
examples: This play is usually attributed to Shakespeare.
a quote that has often been falsely attributed to George Patton

definitions.definition: to regard a quality or feature as belonging to somebody/something
examples: attribute somethingThe committee refused toattribute blamewithout further information.
attribute something to somebody/somethingAncient peoples attributed magical properties to the stones.
The goals commonly attributed to management are status, power, salary and security.

origin: late 15th cent.: the noun from Old Frenchattribut; the verb from Latinattribut-‘allotted’: both from the verbattribuere, fromad-‘to’ +tribuere‘assign’.

definitions.definition: the point at which something begins
examples: If we don't hurry, we'll miss the start.
They haven't won since the start of the season.
at the start (of something)Things didn't look too hopeful at the start of the year.
from the startWe've had problemsright from the start.
We've had problemsfrom the very start.
The trip was a disasterfrom start to finish.
a… start to somethinga perfect start to the day
After a slow start to his career, things improved.
The campaign isoff to a promising start.
ashaky/rocky start
(informal)This could be the start of something big.

definitions.definition: the act or process of beginning something
examples: We need togetan earlystartin the morning.
start on somethingI'll paint the ceiling if youmake a starton the walls.
She's moving abroad to make afresh start(= to begin a new life).
I think it's time we made a start.
It's not much, butit's a start(= something useful has been done, although there is a lot more still to do).

definitions.definition: the opportunity that you are given to begin something in a successful way
examples: Many criticsgot their startwriting for this magazine.
Moving to a different school gave Sally afresh start.
start in somethingThey worked hard to give their children a goodstart in life.
The job gave him his start in journalism.

definitions.definition: the place where a race begins
examples: The runners lined up at the start.
The runners walked up to the start line.

definitions.definition: an amount of time or distance that somebody has as an advantage over other people at the beginning of a race
examples: I gave the younger children a start.
start on somebodyShe went into the second round with a five-minute start on the rest of the cyclists.

definitions.definition: an occasion when somebody/something competes in a race or competition, or when somebody is a member of a team that starts a game
examples: She has been beaten only once in six starts.
He has scored three goals in his last three starts.

definitions.definition: an act of moving your body quickly and suddenly because you are surprised, afraid, etc.
examples: You gave me quite a start!
with a startShe woke from the dream with a start.

definitions.definition: frequently starting and stopping again; not continuously
examples: Because of other commitments I can only write my book in fits and starts.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the first of a list of reasons, opinions, etc.
examples: I'm not working there—for a start, it's too far to travel.

definitions.definition: from an initial position in which none of the necessary elements are in place
examples: The space project went from a standing start to the moon in ten years.

definitions.definition: to make a very good start; to begin something well
examples: She’s got off to a flying start in her new career.

origin: Old Englishstyrtan‘to caper, leap’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchstorten‘push’ and Germanstürzen‘fall headlong, fling’. From the sense ‘sudden movement’ arose the sense ‘initiation of movement, setting out on a journey’ and hence ‘beginning of a process, etc.’.

definitions.definition: knowing something; knowing that something exists and is important
examples: awareness of somethingan awareness of the importance of eating a healthy diet
There was an almost completelack of awarenessof the issues involved.
toraise/heighten/increasepublicawarenessof something
a greater/a growing/an increasing awarenessof something
a marketing campaign to increasebrand awareness
awareness that…There seems to be a general awareness that this is not the solution.

definitions.definition: interest in and concern about a particular situation or area of interest
examples: environmental awareness
Energy Awareness Week
the emergence of a new social awareness among young people
awareness about somethingThe group is trying to raise public awareness about homelessness.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: an institution at the highest level of education where you can study for a degree or do research
examples: Is there a university in this town?
Ohio State University
the University of York
York University
at a/the universityShe studied at the University of Chicago.
(British English)at universityBoth their children are at university.
(British English)He's hoping togo to universitynext year.
auniversity student/lecturer/professor
auniversity degree/course
The site lies on theuniversity campusnext to the lake.

definitions.definition: the experience of life thought of as giving somebody an education, instead of the person gaining formal qualifications
examples: a degree from the university of life

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchuniversite, from Latinuniversitas‘the whole’, in late Latin ‘society, guild’, fromuniversus‘combined into one, whole’, fromuni-‘one’ +versus‘turned’ (past participle ofvertere).

definitions.definition: the final part of a period of time, an event, an activity or a story
examples: It's the end of an era.
the end of the book
at the end of somethingat the end of the week
at the endThere’ll be a chance to ask questions at the end.
by the end of somethingHe wants the reports by the end of the month.
to the end of somethingShe remained active as an artist to the end of her life.
towards the end (of something)I was getting bored towards the end of the talk.
until the endWe didn't leave until thevery end.
until the end of somethingI'm staying until the end of this week.
We had to hear about the whole journeyfrom beginning to end.
He isnearing the endof his career.
Our end-of-season sale starts next week.

definitions.definition: a situation in which something does not exist any more
examples: It was the end of all his dreams.
That was by no means the end of the matter.
at an endThe war was finally at an end.
The meetingcame to an end(= finished).
The coupbroughthis corrupt regimeto an end.
This latest attack couldspell the end ofthe peace process.
an end to somethingThey havecalled for an end toviolence.
an end to the conflict/war
Let’sput an end to(= stop)these rumours once and for all.
There's noend in sightto the present crisis.
Cheap air travelmarked the endof the golden age of the British seaside holiday.
The release of ‘The Jazz Singer’signalled the endof the silent movie era.

definitions.definition: the part of an object or a place that is the furthest away from its centre
examples: I joined the end of the queue.
Go to the end of the line!
at the end of somethingTurn right at the end of the road.
His office is the room atthe other endof the corridor.
That's his wife sitting atthe far endof the table.
on the end of somethingYou've got something on the end of your nose.
Tie the ends of the string together.
These two products are fromopposite endsof the price range.
We've travelled from one end of Mexico to the other.
They live in the end house.

definitions.definition: an aim or a purpose
examples: They are prepared to use violence in pursuit of their ends.
She is exploiting the current situation for her own ends.
He joined the society for political ends.
With this end in view(= in order to achieve this)they employed 50 new staff.
We are willing to make any concessions necessaryto this end(= in order to achieve this).
They are working towards common ends.

definitions.definition: a part of an activity with which somebody is involved, especially in business
examples: We need somebody to handle the marketing end of the business.
Are there any problems at your end?
I have kept my end of the bargain.

definitions.definition: either of two places connected by a phone call, journey, etc.
examples: I answered the phone but there was no oneat the other end.
Jean is going to meet me atthe other end.

definitions.definition: one of the two halves of a sports field
examples: The teams changed ends at half-time.

definitions.definition: a small piece that is left after something has been used
examples: a cigarette end

definitions.definition: a person’s death. People say‘end’to avoid saying‘death’.
examples: She came toan untimely end(= died young).
I was with him at the end(= when he died).
(literary)Hemet his end(= died)at the Battle of Waterloo.

definitions.definition: alinemanwho is nearest to the side of the field
examples: a defensive end
He does as good a job as any end in football at helping his teammates.

definitions.definition: used to introduce the most important fact after everything has been considered
examples: At the end of the day, he'll still have to make his own decision.

definitions.definition: having nothing to do and not knowing what you want to do
examples: Come and see us, if you're at a loose end.

definitions.definition: something unpleasant that happens to somebody, for example punishment or a violent death, usually because of their own actions
examples: He'llcome to a sticky endone of these days if he carries on like that.

definitions.definition: to have almost nothing left of something
examples: I'm at the end of my patience.
They are at the end of their food supply.

definitions.definition: to feel that you cannot deal with a difficult situation any more because you are too tired, worried, etc.
examples: You’d better let her know you’re safe. She’s at the end of her tether.

definitions.definition: to be the person that an action, etc. is directed at, especially an unpleasant one
examples: She found herself on the receiving end of a great deal of criticism.

definitions.definition: to be so worried by a problem that you do not know what to do next
examples: She was at her wits’ end wondering how she’d manage it all in the time.
I'm at my wits' end trying to cope with his moods.

definitions.definition: when you say that people or situations arethe end, you mean that you are annoyed with them
the first sign of something ending
examples: The scandal was the beginning of the end of his career as a politician.

definitions.definition: to become very tired by trying to do too many things and going to bed late and getting up early
a thing that is itself important and not just a part of something more important
examples: For her, shopping had become an end in itself.
For him, travelling had become an end in itself rather than a means of seeing new places.

definitions.definition: bad or unfair methods of doing something are acceptable if the result of that action is good or positive
examples: He defended a morality in which the end justifies the means.
That's only OK if you believe that the end justifies the means.

definitions.definition: (to reach) the point at which something can no longer continue in the same way
examples: A defeat in the second round marked the end of the line for last year's champion.
The loss of this contract could signal the end of the line for the shipyard.
This latest disagreement could mean the end of the road for the band.

definitions.definition: used when you are stating that there is nothing more that can be said or done about something
examples: If she doesn’t agree, I won’t go ahead. End of story.

definitions.definition: in a line, with theendstouching
examples: They arranged the tables end to end.

definitions.definition: to have sex
to be the person in a group who is chosen or forced to perform an unpleasant duty or task
to understand something in the wrong way
to suddenly become very angry or emotional
to do everything possible, even if it is difficult, in order to get or achieve something
examples: I'd go to the ends of the earth to see her again.

definitions.definition: after a long period of time or series of events
examples: He tried various jobs and in the end became an accountant.
In the end, they decided to spend Christmas at home.

definitions.definition: after everything has been considered
examples: You can try your best to impress the interviewers but in the end it's often just a question of luck.

definitions.definition: to start or be made to start a new and difficult activity that you are not prepared for
examples: Junior hospital doctors are thrown in at the deep end in their first jobs.
The company believes in throwing new employees in at the deep end with no training.

definitions.definition: to continue to be cheerful in a difficult situation
something that shows you are nearly at the end of a long and difficult time or situation
examples: For the first time since the start of his treatment, we can now see light at the end of the tunnel.

definitions.definition: to earn just enough money to be able to buy the things you need
examples: Many families struggle to make ends meet.

definitions.definition: to shock or frighten somebody
examples: a chilling tale that will make your hair stand on end

definitions.definition: a thing or an action that is not interesting or important in itself but is a way of achieving something else
examples: He doesn't particularly like the work but he sees it as a means to an end.
I don't enjoy studying computing—it's just a means to an end.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: It upset me no end to hear they'd split up.

definitions.definition: a lot of something
examples: We had no end of trouble getting them to agree.

definitions.definition: not the worst thing that could happen to somebody
examples: Failing one exam is not the end of the world.
It won't be the end of the world if you don't get the job.

definitions.definition: to keep being reminded of something because somebody is always talking to you about it
examples: If we don't get her a dog we'll never hear the end of it.

definitions.definition: in a position standinguprightrather than lying flat
examples: It'll fit if you stand it on end.
Stand the box on end.
Stand it on end.

definitions.definition: for the stated length of time, without stopping
examples: He would disappear forweeks on end.

definitions.definition: to kill yourself
a situation in which somebody is treated badly or unfairly
the place or position of greatest difficulty or responsibility
examples: He started work at the sharp end of the business, as a salesman.

definitions.definition: an event or action that is the beginning of something more serious and/or unpleasant
examples: The introduction of a tax on workplace parking is seen by many as the thin end of the wedge.

definitions.definition: continuing until you have done everything you can, or until something is completely finished, despite difficulties and problems
examples: They were prepared to fight to the bitter end for their rights.
We will fight this case to the bitter end.

origin: Old Englishende(noun),endian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutcheinde(noun),einden(verb) and GermanEnde(noun),enden(verb).

definitions.definition: to let somebody/something do something; to let something happen or be done
examples: allow somebody/something to do somethingHis parents won't allow him to stay out late.
He allowed his mind to wander.
The law allowed companies to dismiss workers without any reason.
be allowed to do somethingHe is not allowed to stay out late.
Students areonly allowedto use the equipment under supervision.
This research must be allowed to continue.
allow yourself to do somethingHe refused to allow himself to be kept quiet.
She won't allow herself to be dictated to.
be allowedEating is not allowed in the classrooms.
He received the maximum sentence allowed by law.
allow somethingWe do not allow eating in the classrooms.

definitions.definition: to let somebody have something
examples: You're allowed an hour to complete the test.
I'm not allowed visitors.
I allow myself a treat now and then.
She allowed herself a thin smile of triumph.

definitions.definition: to let somebody/something go into, through, out of, etc. a place
examples: (be) allowedNo dogs allowed(= you cannot bring them in).
be allowed + adv./prep.The prisoners areallowed outof their cells for two hours a day.
They weren'tallowed intothe country.
He was knocking at the door waiting to beallowed in.
You won't beallowed up(= out of bed)for several days.
allow somebody/something + adv.prep.The crowd parted toallow her through.

definitions.definition: to make something possible
examples: allow somethingA ramp allows easy access for wheelchairs.
allow somebody somethingThe system allows people the opportunity to browse a wide selection of books.
allow somebody to do somethingThe new technology allows users to choose exactly what they watch and when.

definitions.definition: to make sure that you have enough of something for a particular purpose
examples: How much time would you allow for the trip?
You need to allow three metres of fabric for the dress.

definitions.definition: to accept or admit something; to agree that something is true or correct
examples: allow somethingThe judge allowed my claim.
(= in a court of law)‘Objection!’ ‘I'll allow it.’
allow that…He refuses to allow that such a situation could arise.
allow somebody somethingShe was very helpful when my mother was ill—I'll allow you that.

definitions.definition: used to offer help politely
examples: ‘Allow me,’ he said, taking the tray from her.

definitions.definition: to give somebody complete freedom of action; to allow a feeling to be expressed freely
examples: The designer was given free rein.
The script allows full rein to her larger-than-life acting style.
When I paint I just give my imagination free rein.

origin: Middle English (originally in the senses ‘commend, sanction’ and ‘assign as a right’): from Old Frenchalouer, from Latinallaudare‘to praise’, reinforced by medieval Latinallocare‘to place’, fromad-‘to’ +locare, fromlocus‘place’.

definitions.definition: unpleasant; full of problems
examples: I have somebad newsfor you, I'm afraid.
bad weather/dreams/habits
I'm having a really bad day.
It was the worst experience of her life.
Bad breath may be helped by regularly using mouthwash.
War is always abad thing.
That was justbad luck.
Things arebad enoughwithout our own guns shelling us.

definitions.definition: of poor quality; below an acceptable standard
examples: I thought it was a very bad article.
Bad diet and lack of exercise can lead to serious health problems.
The movie wasn't as bad as I'd expected.
That's not a bad idea.

definitions.definition: serious; severe
examples: You're heading for a bad case of sunburn.
The engagement was a bad mistake.
My headache is getting worse.
It was a very bad winter that year.

definitions.definition: not appropriate in a particular situation
examples: I know that this is a bad time to ask for help.
He now realized that it had been a bad decision on his part.

definitions.definition: morally unacceptable
examples: The hero gets to shoot all the bad guys.
He said I must have done something bad to deserve it.
I will not tolerate this bad behaviour.
That girl's a bad influence on Tom.

definitions.definition: not behaving well
examples: Have you been a bad boy?

definitions.definition: not able to do something well or in an acceptable way
examples: a bad teacher
You're a bad liar!
He's a bad loser(= he complains when he loses a game).
bad at (doing) somethingShe is so bad at keeping secrets.

definitions.definition: harmful; causing or likely to cause damage
examples: Those shoes are bad for her feet.
Weather like this is bad for business.
Too much work is bad for your health.

definitions.definition: not healthy; painful
examples: I've got a bad back.

definitions.definition: not safe to eat because it hasdecayed(= started to be destroyed by natural processes)
examples: Put the meat in the fridge so it doesn'tgo bad.

definitions.definition: the state of feeling annoyed or angry
examples: It put me in a bad mood for the rest of the day.
John's in a pretty bad mood this morning.

definitions.definition: to feel guilty or sorry about something
examples: She felt pretty bad about leaving him.
Why should I want to make you feel bad?
I feel bad enough without you constantly telling me how it was all my fault!

definitions.definition: to feel or look ill
examples: I'm afraid I'm feeling pretty bad.

definitions.definition: good; excellent
used to try to persuade somebody to agree that something is good
examples: You'll save fifty dollars, which can't be bad, can it?

definitions.definition: to be very much in love
examples: You're not seeing him again tonight, are you? That's five times this week—you've got it bad!

definitions.definition: quite good; better than you expected
examples: ‘How are you?’ ‘Not too bad.’
That wasn't bad for a first attempt.

definitions.definition: used to say ‘bad luck’ or ‘it’s a shame’ when you do not really mean it
examples: If sometimes they're the wrong decisions, too bad.

definitions.definition: a shame; apity
examples: Too bad every day can't be as good as this.

definitions.definition: annoying
examples: Really, it was too bad of you to be so late.

origin: Middle English: perhaps from Old Englishbǣddel‘hermaphrodite, womanish man’.

definitions.definition: to ask an amount of money for goods or a service
examples: Thefees chargedby some companies are excessive.
charge something for somethingWhat did they charge for the repairs?
The restaurant charges £40 for the set menu.
They are charging a reasonable price for the subscription.
charge somebody for somethingWe won't charge you for delivery.
charge somebody somethingHe only charged me half price.
charge somebody something for somethingSome councils will charge you a small fee for bulky waste collection.
charge something at somethingCalls are charged at 36p per minute.
charge for somethingDo you think museums should charge for admission?
charge to do somethingThe bank doesn't charge to stop a payment.
charge somebody to do somethingThey charge you to change money into euros.

origin: Middle English (in the general senses ‘to load’ and ‘a load’), from Old Frenchcharger(verb),charge(noun), from late Latincarricare,carcare‘to load’, from Latincarrus‘wheeled vehicle’.

definitions.definition: (used when talking about somebody’s age) at least

definitions.definition: after the time when an action could be successful

definitions.definition: the whole length of the day

examples: The phone call from Mike really made my day.

examples: We took a picnic lunch to the river and made a day of it.

definitions.definition: all the time; continuously

definitions.definition: a person or thing that makes people excited for a short time but does not last very long

examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

very obvious

definitions.definition: to think or believe that something will happen or that somebody will do something

it is expected that…It iswidely expectedthat interest rates will rise.

whenever there is a choice

examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.

examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time

definitions.definition: many times; frequently

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them

to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
Time has flown since the holiday began.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.
Time was when we never needed to lock our house at night.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: when a performergoes on, they begin their performance
examples: She doesn't go on until Act 2.

definitions.definition: to join a team as asubstituteduring a game
examples: Maguire went on in place of Cahill just before half-time.

definitions.definition: when a light, the electricity, etc.goes on, it starts to work
examples: Suddenly all the lights went on.

definitions.definition: to pass
examples: She became more and more talkative as the evening went on.

definitions.definition: to happen
examples: What's going onhere?

definitions.definition: if a situationgoes on, it continues without changing
examples: This cannot be allowed to go on.
How much longer will this hot weather go on for?
Wecan't go on like this—we seem to be always arguing.
The flight just seemed to go on and on.

definitions.definition: to continue speaking, often after stopping for a short time
examples: She hesitated for a moment and then went on.
+ speech‘You know,’ he went on, ‘I think my brother could help you.’

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody to do something
examples: Go on! Have another drink!
Go on—jump!

definitions.definition: to travel in front of somebody else
examples: You go on ahead—I'll catch you up in a few minutes.

definitions.definition: to base an opinion or a judgement on something
examples: The police don't have much to go on.

definitions.definition: to talk about somebody/something for a long time, especially in a boring or complaining way
examples: Hewent on and onabout how poor he was.
She does go on sometimes!
He goes on endlessly about his health problems.

definitions.definition: to complain to somebody about their behaviour, work, etc.
examples: She goes on at him continually.
Stop going on at me about that money.

definitions.definition: to continue an activity, especially after a break
examples: That's enough for now—let's go on with it tomorrow.
We'll go on with the presentations after lunch.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to think or believe that something is true or possible (based on the knowledge that you have)
examples: Getting a visa isn't as simple as you might suppose.
Prices will go up,I suppose.
suppose (that)…I don't suppose for a minute that he'll agree(= I'm sure that he won't).
It's reasonable to suppose that he left the note after he tried to call her.
Why do you supposehe resigned?
I suppose all the tickets have been sold now, have they?
There is no reason to supposeshe's lying.
I suppose you think it's funny, do you(= showing anger)?‘That’ is nearly always left out, especially in speech.

definitions.definition: used to make a statement, request or suggestion less direct or less strong
examples: I could take you in the car,I suppose(= but I don't really want to).
‘Can I borrow the car?’ ‘Isuppose so(= Yes, but I'm not happy about it).’
What I’m saying, I suppose, is that she’s not really suitable for the job.
suppose (that)…I don’t suppose (that)I could have a look at your newspaper, could I?
Suppose we take a later train?

definitions.definition: to pretend that something is true; to imagine what would happen if something were true
examples: suppose (that)…Suppose flights are fully booked on that day—which other day could we go?
Let us suppose, for example, that you are married with two children.
Let's suppose for a moment that these four points are correct.
suppose something(formal)The theory supposes the existence of life on other planets.
suppose somebody/something (to be/have) something(formal)Suppose him (to be) dead—what then?

definitions.definition: to be expected or required to do/be something according to a rule, a custom, an arrangement, etc.
examples: What am Isupposed to do?
You're supposed to buy a ticket, but not many people do.
I thought we were supposed to be paid today.
The engine doesn't sound like it's supposed to.
You were supposed to be here an hour ago!
Oops—that wasn't supposed to happen.
How was I supposed to know you were waiting for me?
‘Yes and no.’ ‘What is that supposed to mean?’(= showing that you are annoyed)

definitions.definition: to be generally believed or expected to be/do something
examples: I haven't seen it myself, but it's supposed to be a great movie.
This combination of qualities isgenerally supposedto be extremely rare.
She's supposed to have had hundreds of lovers.

definitions.definition: to not be allowed to do something
examples: You're not supposed to walk on the grass.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchsupposer, from Latinsupponere(fromsub-‘from below’ +ponere‘to place’), but influenced by Latinsuppositus‘set under’ and Old Frenchposer‘to place’.

definitions.definition: to put powder,lipstick, etc. on your/somebody’s face to make it more attractive or to prepare for an appearance in the theatre, on television, etc.
to form something
examples: Women make up 56 per cent of the student numbers.
be made up of somethingThe course is made up of 12 two-hour sessions.

definitions.definition: to put something together from several different things
to invent a story, etc., especially in order to trick or entertain somebody
examples: He made up some excuse about his daughter being sick.
I told the kids a story, making it up as I went along.
You made that up!

definitions.definition: to complete a number or an amount required
examples: We need one more person to make up a team.

definitions.definition: to replace something that has been lost; tocompensatefor something
examples: Can I leave early this afternoon and make up the time tomorrow?

definitions.definition: to prepare a medicine by mixing different things together
to prepare a bed for use; to create a temporary bed
examples: We made up the bed in the spare room.
They made up a bed for me on the sofa.

definitions.definition: to clean a hotel room and make the bed
examples: The maid asked if she could make up the room.
They had forgotten to make up the room.

definitions.definition: to end an argument with somebody and become friends again
examples: Why don't you twokiss and make up?
Has he made it up with her yet?
Have they made it up yet?

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a thing that somebody says or writes giving their opinion or stating a fact
examples: Shemadeseveral interestingpointsin the article.
Itake your point(= understand and accept what you are saying).
He's just saying that toprove a point(= to show his idea is right).
OK, you'vemade your point!

definitions.definition: the main or most important idea in something that is said or done
examples: The point isyou shouldn't have to wait so long to see a doctor.
I wish he wouldget to the point(= say it quickly).
I'llcome straight to the point: we need more money.
Do yousee my point(= understand)?
I think Imissed the point(= did not understand).
Youhave a point(= your idea is right)—it would be better to wait till this evening.
‘There won’t be anywhere to park.’ ‘Oh,that’s a (good) point.’(= I had not thought of that)
It just isn't true. That'sthe whole point(= the only important fact).
I thought the whole point was getting the same amount done with less effort.
‘He's been married before.’ ‘That's beside the point(= not important).’
I know it won't cost very much butthat's not the point(= not the important thing).
point behind somethingThe point behind the film was to help people understand the motivations of the criminals.

definitions.definition: the purpose or aim of something
examples: What's the point of all this violence?
‘Why don't you try again?’ ‘What's the point?’
The point of the lesson is to compare the two countries.
point of doing somethingIdon't see the point ofdoing it all again.
point in doing somethingThere'sno pointin getting angry.

definitions.definition: a particular detail or fact
examples: Can you explain that point again?
Summarize themain pointsof the argument.
I should like to highlight thekey points.
Animportant pointhas been missed out.
Youraisean interestingpoint.

definitions.definition: a particular quality or feature that somebody/something has
examples: Tact is not one of herstrong points.
Read the manual to learn the program'sfiner points(= small details).
Living in Scotlandhas its good pointsbut the weather is not one of them.
One of the hotel'splus points(= good features)is that it is very central.
There is not a single weak point in his essay.

definitions.definition: a particular time or stage of development
examples: We hadreached the pointwhen there was no money left.
at/on the point of somethingThe climber was at the point of death when they found him.
at one, some, this, etc. pointAt one pointhe looked like winning.
Many people experience anxietyat some pointin their lives.
At this point in timewe just have to wait.
At this pointI don't care what you decide to do.
up to/until that pointUp to that point we were living in London.

definitions.definition: a particular place or area
examples: I'll wait for you at themeeting pointin the arrivals hall.
the point at which the river divides
Draw a line from point A to point B.
No parking beyond this point.

definitions.definition: one of the marks of direction around acompass
examples: the points of the compass(= N, S, E, W, etc.)

definitions.definition: an individual unit that adds to a score in a game or sports competition
examples: towin/lose a point
Australia finished 20 points ahead.
point for (doing) somethingPlayersscore pointsfor finding words not found by any other player.
You getbonus pointsfor answering the mystery question.
on pointsThey won on points(= by scoring more points rather than by completely defeating their opponents).

definitions.definition: a mark or unit on a scale of measurement
examples: The party's share of the vote fell by tenpercentage points.

definitions.definition: a unit of credit towards an award or benefit
examples: Applicants are awarded points on criteria such as education, skills and knowledge of English.
Let children trade their earned points for privileges, like extra playtime.

definitions.definition: the sharp, thin end of something
examples: the point of a pencil/knife/pin

definitions.definition: a narrow piece of land that stretches into the sea
examples: The ship sailed around the point.
Pagoda Point

definitions.definition: a small round mark used in writing, especially the mark that separates a whole number from the part that comes after it
examples: two point six (2.6)
a decimal point
We broadcast on ninety-five point nine (95.9) FM.

definitions.definition: a very smalldotof light or colour
examples: The stars were points of light in the sky.

definitions.definition: a place in a wall, etc. where a piece of equipment can be connected to electricity
examples: a power/shaver/telephone point

definitions.definition: the hard tops of the toes of a type of shoe that aballetdancer balances on
a piece of track at a place where a railway line divides that can be moved to allow a train to change tracks
a unit of measurement for the size of letters in printing or on a computer screen, etc.
examples: Change the text to 10 point.

definitions.definition: to repeat an idea, argument, etc. many times to emphasize it, especially when it has already been mentioned or understood
examples: I don’t want to belabour the point, but it’s vital you understand how important this is.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: the more complicated or detailed aspects of something
examples: She seems at ease discussing the finer points of foreign policy.
He learnt the finer points of cricket during a trip to London.

definitions.definition: used when you have to decide something or say what you really think
examples: When it comes to the point, he always changes his mind.

definitions.definition: used to say what is true in a situation
examples: In point of fact, she is their adopted daughter.

definitions.definition: to continue to repeat or explain something that has already been said and understood
examples: I understand what you’re saying—there’s no need to labour the point.

definitions.definition: to be or make sure you do something because it is important or necessary
examples: I made a point of closing all the windows before leaving the house.

definitions.definition: a matter about which there may be difference of opinion or a lack of understanding
examples: Whether this should be enforced by law or not is a moot point.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is more important than something else
examples: I couldn't do the job—I've never been to Spain and, more to the point, I don't speak Spanish.
More to the point, did they get away?

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something that is expressed clearly and directly, especially a criticism
examples: Not to put too fine a point on it, I think you are lying.

definitions.definition: appropriate or relevant to the situation
examples: The quotation was directly on point.
Let's stay on point.
None of the replies was on point.

definitions.definition: perfect; exactly right for the occasion
examples: The music here is totally on point.

definitions.definition: to be close to doing something; to be going to do something very soon
examples: I was just on the point of calling you.
We were on the point of giving up.

definitions.definition: a place where you go or a person that you speak to when you are dealing with an organization
examples: The receptionist is the first point of contact most people have with the clinic.

definitions.definition: a place where a journey starts
an idea, a theory or an event that is used to start a discussion, an activity, etc.
examples: The author takes Freud's dream theories as the point of departure for his essay.

definitions.definition: a thing that somebody considers to be very important for their honour or reputation
the time when you must continue with what you have decided to do, because it is not possible to get back to an earlier situation
examples: We have passed the point of no return.

definitions.definition: used to say that you accept that somebody else is right when they have disagreed with you or criticized you
examples: Point taken. Let's drop the subject.

definitions.definition: to show that you are better than somebody, especially by making clever remarks, for example in an argument
examples: He was always trying to score points off his teachers.

definitions.definition: a subject that makes you feel angry or upset when it is mentioned
examples: It's a sore point with Sue's parents that the children have not been baptized yet.

definitions.definition: to allow or do something that is not usually acceptable, especially because of a particular situation
examples: I was hoping they would stretch a point and let me stay on for a while.

definitions.definition: expressed in a simple, clear way without any extra information or feelings
examples: The letter was short and to the point.

definitions.definition: to a degree that can be described as something
examples: He was rude to the point of being aggressive.

definitions.definition: to some extent; to some degree but not completely
examples: I agree with you up to a point.

origin: Middle English: the noun partly from Old Frenchpoint, from Latinpunctum‘something that is pricked’, giving rise to the senses ‘unit, mark, point in space or time’; partly from Old Frenchpointe, from Latinpuncta‘pricking’, giving rise to the senses ‘sharp tip, promontory’. The verb is from Old Frenchpointer, and in some senses from the English noun.

definitions.definition: to be owned by somebody/something
examples: Who does this watch belong to?
The islands belong to Spain.
a violin thatonce belonged toMozart
These resourcesrightfully belong toall Brazilians.

definitions.definition: an event, a competition, etc. thatbelongs tosomebody/something is one in which they are the most successful or popular
examples: British actors did well at the award ceremony, but the evening belonged to the Americans.

definitions.definition: to be a member of a club, an organization, etc.
examples: Have you ever belonged to a political party?

definitions.definition: to be part of a particular group, type or system
examples: Lions and tigers belong to the cat family.
people belonging to different ethnic groups
These gases belong to a class of molecules called alkanes.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to be owned by somebody/something
examples: Who does this watch belong to?
The islands belong to Spain.
a violin thatonce belonged toMozart
These resourcesrightfully belong toall Brazilians.

definitions.definition: an event, a competition, etc. thatbelongs tosomebody/something is one in which they are the most successful or popular
examples: British actors did well at the award ceremony, but the evening belonged to the Americans.

definitions.definition: to be a member of a club, an organization, etc.
examples: Have you ever belonged to a political party?

definitions.definition: to be part of a particular group, type or system
examples: Lions and tigers belong to the cat family.
people belonging to different ethnic groups
These gases belong to a class of molecules called alkanes.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to start doing something; to do the first part of something
examples: Shall I begin?
begin at somethingLet's begin at page 9.
begin with somethingI'd like to begin with a simple question.
begin by doing somethingShe began by thanking us all for coming.
begin somethingWebegan workon the project in May.
I began(= started reading)this novel last month and I still haven't finished it.
begin something with somethingHe always begins his lessons with a warm-up exercise.
begin something at somethingI began piano lessons at the age of six.
begin something by doing somethingShe began her speech by praising her opponent.
begin something as somethingHe began his political career as a student(= when he was a student).
begin to do somethingIbegan to feeldizzy.
Leavesare just beginning toappear.
We havealready begunto address the problem.
Iwas beginning to thinkyou'd never come.
begin doing somethingEveryone began talking at once.
Webegan workingon the project in May.

definitions.definition: to start to happen or exist, especially from a particular time
examples: When does the concert begin?
begin in…Work on the new bridge is due to begin in September.
begin at…The lecture begins at 8pm.
The evening began well.

definitions.definition: to have something as the first part or the point where something starts
examples: Where does Europe end and Asia begin?
begin with somethingUse ‘an’ before words beginning with a vowel.
‘I'm thinking of a country in Asia.’ ‘What does it begin with(= what is the first letter)?’
Each chapter begins with a quotation.
begin at…The path begins at Livingston village.
The story begins on the island of Corfu.

definitions.definition: to start or make something start for the first time
examples: The school began in 1920, with only ten pupils.
begin somethingHe began a new magazine on post-war architecture.

definitions.definition: to be something first, before becoming something else
examples: He began as an actor, before starting to direct films.
What began as a minor scuffle turned into a full-scale riot.

definitions.definition: to start speaking
examples: ‘Ladies and gentlemen,’ he began, ‘welcome to the Town Hall.’

definitions.definition: to make no attempt to do something or have no chance of doing something
examples: Ican't begin tothank you enough.
He didn't even begin to understand my problem.

definitions.definition: you should help and care for your own family, etc. before you start helping other people
at first
examples: I found it tiring to begin with but I soon got used to it.
We'll go slowly to begin with.

definitions.definition: used to introduce the first point you want to make
examples: ‘What was it you didn't like?’ ‘Well, to begin with, our room was far too small.’

origin: Old Englishbeginnan, of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanbeginnen.

definitions.definition: the job that a person does especially in order to earn money
examples: Hestarted workas a security guard.
I'm still looking for work.
It is difficult tofind workin the present economic climate.
She's planning toreturn to workonce the children start school.
full-time/part-time/unpaid/voluntary work
out of workShe had been out of work(= without a job)for a year.
in work(British English)They are in work(= have a job).
Whatline of workare you in(= what type of work do you do)?
off workShe's been off work(= not going to work)with a bad back since July.

definitions.definition: the place where you do your job; the time that you spend there
examples: Igo to workat 8 o'clock.
I was late for work again.
I have to leave work early today.
Her friends from work came to see her in the hospital.
I get on well with my work colleagues.
a pleasant work environment
at workThe new legislation concerns health and safety at work.
before/after workI like to go for a run after work.

definitions.definition: the duties that you have and the activities that you do as part of your job
examples: Police work is mainly routine.
The accountant described his work to the sales staff.

definitions.definition: tasks that need to be done
examples: Taking care of a baby ishard work.
Whenever am I going todoall thiswork?
There is plenty ofwork to be donein the garden.
Stop talking and get on with your work.
He completed the work shortly before his death.
important work being undertaken by volunteers
Big football matchesmakea lot ofworkfor the police.

definitions.definition: materials needed or used for doingwork, especially books, papers, etc.
examples: She often brings work(= for example, files and documents)home with her from the office.
His work was spread all over the floor.

definitions.definition: the use of physical strength or mental power in order to do or make something
examples: She earned her grades through sheerhard work.
work on somethingWebegan workon the project last year.
They performed pioneering work on the treatment of cancer.
Work continues on renovating the hotel.
work of doing somethingThe work of building the bridge took six months.
The art collection was hislife's work.
Sheset them to workpainting the fence.

definitions.definition: a thing or things that are produced as a result of work
examples: She's an artist whose work I really admire.
outstanding work in this area/field
Is thisall your own work(= did you do it without help from others)?
The book is a detailed and thoroughpiece of workcovering all aspects of the subject.
He left a substantialbody of work—drawings and watercolours as well as oils.

definitions.definition: a book, piece of music, painting, etc.
examples: the collected/complete works of Tolstoy
works of fiction/literature
Chopin’s piano works
Beethoven composed his greatest works in the latter part of his life.
work by somebodyHe recognized the sketch as an early work by Degas.

definitions.definition: the result of an action; what is done by somebody
examples: The damage is clearly the work of vandals.

definitions.definition: activities involving building or repairing something
examples: roadworks
They expanded the shipyards and started engineering works.
A contract can ensure that landlords carry out the works for which they are legally responsible.

definitions.definition: a place where things are made or industrial processes take place
examples: an engineering works
a brickworks
Raw materials were carried to the works by barge.

definitions.definition: the moving parts of a machine, etc.
everything
examples: We went to the chip shop and had the works: fish, chips, gherkins, mushy peas.

definitions.definition: the use of force to produce movement
part of your normal working life and not unusual
examples: Swimming with sharks or training a tiger cub—it's all in a day's work for animal wrangler Chris Bolton.

definitions.definition: it is not healthy to spend all your time working; you need to relax too
having an effect on something
examples: She suspected that secret influences were at work.

definitions.definition: busy doing something
examples: He is still at work on the painting.
Danger—men at work.

definitions.definition: people who do not have enough to do often start to do wrong
examples: She blamed the crimes on the local jobless teenagers. ‘The devil makes work for idle hands,’ she would say.

definitions.definition: (to do) the unpleasant or dishonest jobs that somebody else does not want to do
examples: Next time you can get somebody else to do your dirty work for you!
He always gets someone else to do the dirty work for him.
The drugs gang used children to do their dirty work for them.

definitions.definition: to begin; to make a start
examples: We set to work on the outside of the house(= for example, painting it).
Let's get to work.

definitions.definition: to give or tell somebody everything
to do/start to do your work
examples: She went cheerfully about her work.

definitions.definition: kind acts to help others
to be likely to have difficulty doing something
examples: You'll have your work cut out to get there by nine o'clock.

definitions.definition: something that isin the worksis being discussed, planned or prepared and will happen or exist soon
work that you are paid to do or that must be done
examples: There was a job of work waiting for him that he was not looking forward to.

definitions.definition: to use more time or energy on a task than is necessary
to do something quickly and with little effort
to defeat, deal with something/somebody quickly
examples: Liverpool made short work of the opposition(= in a football match).
He made short work of his lunch(= ate it quickly).
Mike made short work of fixing the engine.

definitions.definition: used to say that a job is made easier if a lot of people help
a person who is unpleasant, unkind or dishonest
examples: Don’t trust him; he’s a nasty piece of work.
He's a thoroughly nasty piece of work.

definitions.definition: used to show you are pleased when somebody has done something well
examples: You did a good job today. Nice work, James!

definitions.definition: used when you wish that you had somebody’s success or good luck and think they have achieved it with little effort
examples: He was paid £200 for a ten-minute speech? Nice work if you can get it.

definitions.definition: (to cause) a delay or problem with something that somebody is planning or doing
needing too much effort
examples: I can't be bothered making a hot meal—it's too much like hard work.

definitions.definition: a thing that takes a very short time to do
examples: It was the work of a few minutes to hide the damage.

origin: Old Englishweorc(noun),wyrcan(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwerkand GermanWerk, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekergon.

definitions.definition: to move or travel from one place to another
examples: + adv./prep.She went into her room and shut the door behind her.
I have to go to Rome on business.
She has gone to China(= is now in China or is on her way there).
She has been to China(= she went to China and has now returned).
Are you going home for Christmas?
go to do somethingShe has gone to see her sister this weekend.

definitions.definition: to move or travel, especially with somebody else, to a particular place or in order to be present at an event
examples: go (to something)Are you going to Dave's party?
Who else is going?
go with somebodyHis dog goes everywhere with him.
go (to something) (with somebody)He invited her to go with him to the concert.

definitions.definition: to move or travel in a particular way or over a particular distance
examples: + adv./prep.He's going too fast.
They went farther north into the mountains.
+ nounWe had gone about fifty miles when the car broke down.

definitions.definition: to move in a particular way or while doing something else
examples: She crashed into a waiter and his tray of drinks went flying.
The car went skidding off the road into a ditch.
She went sobbing up the stairs.
I tripped and went tumbling down the stairs.
Don't go charging in there!

definitions.definition: to leave one place in order to reach another
examples: I must be going now.
They came at six and went at nine.
Has she gone yet?
He's been gone an hour(= he left an hour ago).
When does the train go?

definitions.definition: to leave a place and do something different
examples: togo on a journey/tour/trip/cruise
Richard has gone on leave for two weeks.
(British English)togo on holiday
(North American English)togo on vacation

definitions.definition: to visit or attend a place for a particular purpose
examples: togo to school/college/university
(British English)I have togo to hospitalfor an operation.
(North American English)I have togo to the hospital.
(British English)togo to the cinema
(North American English)togo to the movies
togo to prison/jail(= to be sent there as punishment for a crime)
Hegoes to workby bus.
I think you shouldgo to the doctor's.
Do yougo to church(= regularly attend church services)?
I alwaysgo to the gymbefore work.

definitions.definition: to look at a particular page or website
examples: go on somethingShe went on Facebook and changed her relationship status.
go to somethingTo find out what the terms mean, go to the glossary.

definitions.definition: to leave a place or travel to a place in order to take part in an activity or a sport
examples: go for somethingtogo for a walk
to go for a ride/drive/run/swim
Shall we go for a drink(= at a pub or bar)after work?
go doingtogo swimming/fishing/jogging

if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.

He surfs the internet for hours at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.
The rain will become heavy at times in the afternoon.
He can be rather moody at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.
If you didn't win a prize, better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.
to deliberately make a situation difficult and unpleasant for somebody
examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
