definitions.definition: to develop or happen in a particular way
examples: The day followed the usual pattern.

definitions.definition: to do the same job, have the same style of life, etc. as somebody else, especially somebody in your family
examples: She works in television, following in her father's footsteps.
He wanted to follow in his mother's footsteps and be a ballroom dancer.

definitions.definition: to be guided by your sense of smell
to go straight forward
examples: The garage is a mile ahead up the hill—just follow your nose.

definitions.definition: to act according to what seems right or reasonable, rather than following any particular rules
to play a card of the samesuitthat has just been played
to act or behave in the way that somebody else has just done
a person or event that is so good or successful at something that it will be difficult for anyone/anything else coming after them to be as good or successful
examples: She has been an excellent principal and will be a hard act to follow.
Their contribution will prove a tough act to follow.

origin: Old Englishfolgian, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvolgenand Germanfolgen.

definitions.definition: a period of time
examples: for a whileI only stayed fora short while.
They chatted for a while.
I haven't seen him forquite a while(= a fairly long time).
He is fitter now than he has been fora long while.
after a whileAfter a while John started to relax a bit.
in a whileI'll be back ina little while(= a short time).
It took me a while(= a fairly long time)to realize what he meant.
This couldtake a while(= a long time), so be patient.
They walked back together, talkingall the while(= all the time).
for the whileThere's no need to do anything for the while(= for a short time).
He lefta while ago.
The problems starteda while back.

definitions.definition: occasionally
examples: Everybody makes a mistake once in a while.

definitions.definition: interesting or useful for somebody to do
examples: It will be worth your while to come to the meeting.
He'll do the job if youmake it worth his while(= pay him well).

origin: Old Englishhwīl‘period of time’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwijl, GermanWeile; the conjunction is an abbreviation of Old Englishthā hwīle the‘the while that’.

definitions.definition: to do somebody’s job for a short time while they are not there
to complete a form, etc. by writing information on it
examples: to fill in an application form
To order, fill in the coupon on p 54.

definitions.definition: to fill something completely
examples: The hole has been filled in.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something while waiting for something more important
examples: He filled in the rest of the day watching television.

definitions.definition: to complete a drawing, etc. by covering the space inside the outline with colour
to tell somebody about something that has happened

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: something such as an object, a fact or a situation that shows, explains or supports what you say
examples: Let megiveyouan example.
It is important tocite examplesto support your argument.
to provide/offer an example
I shall take four different examples from contemporary literature to illustrate my point.
example of somethingCan you show me an example of what you mean?
This dictionary has many examples of how words are used.
Just to give you an example of his generosity—he gave me his old car and wouldn't take any money for it.
The progress the students have madeserves as an exampleof the benefit of small class sizes.
By way of example, we'll look at our most recent advertising campaign.

definitions.definition: a thing that is typical of or represents a particular group or set
examples: This is agood exampleof the artist's early work.
It is aperfect exampleof this type of architecture.
Japan is often quoted as theprime exampleof a modern industrial nation.
It is aclassic exampleof how not to design a new city centre.

definitions.definition: a person or their behaviour that is thought to be a good model for others to copy
examples: example to somebodyHer courage is an example to us all.
example for somebodyHesets an examplefor the other students.
example of somethingThey are ashining exampleof the best of the human spirit.
by exampleHe is a captain wholeads by example.
Top management should be seen to lead by example.

definitions.definition: a person’s behaviour, either good or bad, that other people copy
examples: It would be a mistake tofollow his example.
They started to plan protests, inspired by the example of their neighbours.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something that explains or supports what you are saying; used to give an example of what you are saying
examples: There is a similar word in many languages, for example in French and Italian.
The report is incomplete; it does not include sales in France, for example.
It is possible to combine computer science with other subjects, for example physics.
An athlete, for example, might turn the pedals 80 times a minute.

definitions.definition: to punish somebody as a warning to others not to do the same thing
examples: The teacher made an example of him by suspending him from school.

origin: late Middle English: from Old French, from Latinexemplum, fromeximere‘take out’, fromex-‘out’ +emere‘take’. Compare withsample.

definitions.definition: a meal eaten in the middle of the day
examples: She's gone to lunch.
I'm ready for some lunch.
I alwayseat/have lunchat my desk.
for lunchWhat shall we have for lunch?
over lunchLopez suggested they meet over lunch(= at or during lunchtime).
We serve hot and cold lunches.
a one-hourlunch break
Let's do lunch(= have lunch together).
Christmas/Sunday lunch
a picnic/buffet lunch
We had a pub lunch(= lunch in a pub).

definitions.definition: behaving in a strange or confused way
used to say that it is not possible to get something for nothing

origin: early 19th cent.: abbreviation ofluncheon.

definitions.definition: to steal something and escape with it
examples: Thieves got away with computer equipment worth $30 000.

definitions.definition: to receive a relatively light punishment
examples: He was lucky to get away with only a fine.

definitions.definition: to do something wrong and not be punished for it
examples: Don't be tempted to cheat—you'll never get away with it.
get away with doing somethingNobody gets away with insulting me like that.

definitions.definition: to manage with less of something than you might expect to need
examples: After the first month, you should be able to get away with one lesson a week.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: an advantage that something gives you; a helpful and useful effect that something has
examples: Freedom of information brings great benefits.
health/economic/tax benefits
the benefits of modern medicine
I'vehad the benefit ofa good education.
It was good to see her finallyreaping the benefits(= enjoying the results)of all her hard work.
The industry will be one of the first toenjoy the benefitsof the recovery.
The company derived substantial benefit from the deal.
benefit of doing somethingHe couldn't see the benefit of arguing any longer.
be of benefit to somebody/somethingThe new regulations will be of benefit to everyone concerned.
to somebody's benefitIt will be to your benefit to arrive early.
benefit for somebody/somethingThe project will have major benefits for the local community.
With the benefit of hindsight(= knowing what we have learnt since), we would do things differently.
without the benefit of somethingNobody should be sent to prison without the benefit of legal advice.
For maximum benefit, take the tablets before meals.

definitions.definition: money provided by the government to people who need financial help because they are unemployed, ill, etc.
examples: You may be eligible toreceive benefits.
The number of peopleclaimingunemploymentbenefitfell last month.
on benefitsThe aim is to help people who are on benefits(= receiving benefits)to find jobs.

definitions.definition: an advantage that you get from a company in addition to the money that you earn
examples: Private health insurance is offered as part of the employees' benefits package.
These benefits will now be extended to agency workers.

definitions.definition: money from an insurance company
examples: The insurance plan will provide substantial cash benefits to your family in case of your death.

definitions.definition: an event such as a performance, a dinner, etc., organized in order to raise money for a particular person or charity
examples: abenefit match/concert
The proceeds from the benefit will go directly to the refugee camps.

definitions.definition: especially in order to help or be useful to somebody
examples: I have attached some lecture notes for the benefit of those people who were absent last week.
Don't go to any trouble for my benefit!

definitions.definition: to accept that somebody has told the truth or behaved well because you cannot prove that they have not
examples: She may have been lying, but I felt I had to give her the benefit of the doubt.

origin: late Middle English (originally denoting a kind deed or something well done): from Old Frenchbienfet, from Latinbenefactum‘good deed’, frombene facere‘do good (to)’.

definitions.definition: to return, especially to your home
examples: What time did you get back last night?
We only got back from our trip yesterday.

definitions.definition: to obtain something again after having lost it
examples: She's got her old job back.
I never lend books—you never get them back.

definitions.definition: to win an election after having lost the previous one

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: measuring or covering a great length or distance, or a greater length or distance than usual
examples: She had long dark hair.
He walked down the long corridor.
It was the world's longest bridge.
along journey/walk/drive/flight
We'rea long wayfrom anywhere here.
It's along way away.
He had long, thin fingers.
a lush meadow of long grass

definitions.definition: used for asking or talking about particular lengths or distances
examples: How longis the River Nile?
The table is six feet long.
The report is only three pages long.

definitions.definition: lasting or taking a great amount of time or more time than usual
examples: He’s been ill(for) a long time.
There was a long silence before she spoke.
I like it now the days are getting longer(= it stays light for more time each day).
along book/film/list(= taking a lot of time to read/watch/deal with)
Nurses have to worklong hours(= for more hours in the day than is usual).
(North American English)He stared at themfor the longest time(= for a very long time)before answering.
Chicago hasa long history ofcountry music.
to have a long life/career

definitions.definition: used for asking or talking about particular periods of time
examples: How longis the course?
I think it's only three weeks long.
How long a stay did you have in mind?

definitions.definition: seeming to last or take more time than it really does because, for example, you are very busy or not happy
examples: I'm tired. It's been a long day.
We were married for ten long years.

definitions.definition: covering all or most of your legs or arms
examples: She usually wears long skirts.
a long-sleeved shirt

definitions.definition: travelling a great distance
examples: The full-back intercepted an attempted long pass and ran through to score.
A hopeful long ball from out of defence led to a goal.
She's good with the driver and hits a long ball.

definitions.definition: travelling too far so that it goes out of play
examples: Her return of serve was long.

definitions.definition: having a lot of a particular quality
examples: The government is long on ideas but short on performance.

definitions.definition: alongvowel is pronounced for a longer time than other vowels
examples: Compare the long vowel in ‘beat’ and the short vowel in ‘bit’.

definitions.definition: very long
examples: There's a list of repairs as long as your arm.

definitions.definition: not longer than the particular time given
examples: It will take an hour at the longest.

definitions.definition: after a long time
examples: At long last his prayers had been answered.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: to have known each other for a long time
examples: We go back a long way, he and I.

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.
A small amount of this paint goes a long way(= covers a large area).
(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something
examples: The new law goes a long way towards solving the problem.

definitions.definition: to have made a lot of progress
examples: We've come a long way since the early days of the project.
The study of genes has come a long way in recent years.

definitions.definition: to need to make a lot of progress before you can achieve something
examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no definite answer to a question
examples: ‘How long will it take?’ ‘How long's a piece of string?’

definitions.definition: relating to a longer period in the future
examples: This measure inevitably means higher taxes in the long run.

definitions.definition: used to describe what will happen a long, short, etc. time in the future
examples: Such a development seems unlikely, at least in the short term(= it will not happen for quite a long time).
In the longer term, children of depressed mothers are more likely to suffer from childhood depression.
In the long term, our efforts will pay off.

definitions.definition: it makes no real difference which of two possible choices you make
used to say that the reasons for something are complicated and you would prefer not to give all the details
to reject something or treat it as not important; to stop dealing with a problem
examples: He tends to deal with disputes by kicking them into the long grass.

definitions.definition: used when you are telling somebody the essential facts about something or what effect it will have, without explaining all the details
the power and/or authority of something
examples: There is no escape from the long arm of the law.

definitions.definition: (to have) an unhappy or disappointed expression
examples: He took one look at her long face and said ‘What’s wrong?’
The news for the company isn't good, to judge from the long faces in the boardroom.

definitions.definition: old or too old
an attempt or a guess that is not likely to be successful but is worth trying
examples: It's a long shot, but it just might work.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that you will get to the point of what you are saying quickly, without including all the details
examples: To cut a long story short, we didn’t get home until 3 in the morning!
Anyway, long story short: we had this argument and I haven't seen him since.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
not nearly; not at all
examples: It's not over yet—not by a long chalk.

definitions.definition: to consider a problem or possibility very carefully and without hurrying
examples: We need to take a long, hard look at all the options.

definitions.definition: to consider what is likely to happen or be important over a long period of time rather than only considering the present situation
examples: As pension funds are investing for members’ retirements, they can take the long view.

origin: adjectiveOld Englishlang,long(adjective),lange,longe(adverb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanlang.

definitions.definition: a long distance away
examples: We didn't go far.
Have you come far?
It's not far to the beach.
There's not far to go now.
It'stoo farto walk.
+ adv./prep.The restaurant is not far from here.
countries as far apart as Japan and Brazil
He looked down at the traffic far below.
Far awayin the distance, a train whistled.
The farther north they went, the colder it became.
a concert of music fromnear and far
(figurative)Legends are usuallyfar removedfrom the reality.

definitions.definition: used when you are asking or talking about the distance between two places or the distance that has been travelled or is to be travelled
examples: How far is it to your house from here?
How far is Boston from New York?
How much further is it?
I'm not sure I can walkso far.
as far as somebody/somethingWe'll go by train as far as London, and then take a bus.
We didn't go as far as the others.

definitions.definition: a long time from the present; for a large part of a particular period of time
examples: far backThe band had their first hit as far back as 2012.
To answer that question we need to go further back in time.
far aheadLet's try to plan further ahead.
far into somethingWe worked far into the night.
The film takes place in a future not too far distant.

definitions.definition: very much; to a great degree
examples: That's afar betteridea.
The damage wasfar worsethan I'd expected.
a drug that gives heart attack victims afar greaterchance of survival
There arefar moreopportunities for young people than there used to be.
She always gives usfar too muchhomework.
The resultsfar exceededmy expectations.
The benefitsfar outweighthe cost.
The recent heat and continued drought further reduced yields.
far beyond (something)It had been a success far beyond their expectations.
far behind (something)He's fallen far behind in his work.

definitions.definition: used when you are asking or talking about the degree to which something is true or possible
examples: How far can we trust him?
as far as…His parents supported him as far as they could.
Plan your route in advance, using main roadsas far as possible.

definitions.definition: used to talk about how much progress has been made in doing or achieving something
examples: How far have you got with that report?
Half an hour later Duncan was no farther in his quest.
as/so far as somethingI read as far as the third chapter.
as/so far as doing somethingWe’ve got as far as collecting our data but we haven’t analysed it yet.

definitions.definition: to thehorizon(= where the sky meets the land or sea)
examples: The bleak moorland stretched on all sides as far as the eye could see.

definitions.definition: used to give your personal opinion on something
examples: As far as I am concerned, you can do what you like.

definitions.definition: used to say that you think you know, remember, understand, etc. something but you cannot be completely sure, especially because you do not know all the facts
examples: As far as we knew, there was no cause for concern.
As far as I can see, you've done nothing wrong.
She lived in Chicago, as far as I can remember.

definitions.definition: used to give facts or an opinion about a particular aspect of something
examples: She's doing fine as far as French is concerned, it's her German I'm worried about.

definitions.definition: to a limited degree, usually less than is enough
examples: It's a good plan as far as it goes, but there are a lot of things they haven't thought of.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: The last of these reasons is by far the most important.
Amy is the smartest by far.

definitions.definition: to continue doing something beyond reasonable limits
far away from home; to or in places that are not near
examples: You can hire a car if you want to explore further afield.
People commute to Londonfrom as far afield asFrance.

definitions.definition: by a very great amount
examples: She's far and away the best player.

definitions.definition: over a large area
examples: They searched far and wide for the missing child.
People came from far and wide for the show.

definitions.definition: used when you are just about to disagree with somebody or to criticize them and you would like them to think that you do not really want to do this
examples: Far be it from me to interfere in your affairs but I would like to give you just one piece of advice.

definitions.definition: almost the opposite of something or of what is expected
examples: It is far from clear(= it is not clear)what he intends to do.
New technology, far from destroying jobs, can create employment.

definitions.definition: used to say that the opposite of what somebody says is true
examples: ‘You're not angry then?’ ‘Far from it. I've never laughed so much in my life.’

definitions.definition: not happening often
to be very successful in the future
examples: She is very talented and should go far.

definitions.definition: to achieve all that is wanted
examples: The new legislation is welcome but does not go far enough.
Do these measures go far enough?
(disapproving)Stop it now. The joke has gone far enough(= it has continued too long).

definitions.definition: to be willing to go to extreme or surprising limits in dealing with something
examples: I wouldn't go as far as to say that he's a liar(= but I think he may be slightly dishonest).
In June last year he went so far as to offer his resignation.

definitions.definition: to behave in an extreme way that is not acceptable
examples: He's always been quite crude, but this time he's gone too far.
I never thought she'd go this far.

definitions.definition: to the degree that
examples: That's the truth, in so far as I know it.

definitions.definition: almost correct
examples: Your guess wasn't far out at all.
They weren't far out with their estimate of 100 000.
You're not far wrong when you say he's the richest guy in town.

definitions.definition: to not be enough to buy a lot of things
examples: Five pounds doesn't go very far these days.

definitions.definition: to not be enough for what is needed
examples: Four bottles of wine won't go far among twenty people.

definitions.definition: until now; up to this point
examples: What do you think of the show so far?
Detectives are so far at a loss to explain the reason for his death.

definitions.definition: only to a limited degree
examples: I trust him only so far.

definitions.definition: used to say that things have been successful until now and you hope that they will continue to be successful, but you know that the task, etc. is not finished yet
used to comment on something that was almost successful but in fact failed

origin: Old Englishfeorr, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchver, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritparaand Greekpera‘further’.

origin: Old Englishfeorr, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchver, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritparaand Greekpera‘further’.

definitions.definition: happening or coming before all other similar things or people; 1st
examples: her first husband
the first turning on the right
I didn't take the first bus.
It was thefirst timethey had ever met.
It's always exciting to visit a countryfor the first time.
students in theirfirst yearat college
First impressionscan be misleading.
She resolved to do it at the first(= earliest)opportunity.
King Edward I(= pronounced ‘King Edward the First’)
the first of May/May 1st
His second book is better than his first.

definitions.definition: the most important or best
examples: Your first duty is to your family.
She wonfirst prizein the competition.
an issue of the first importance

origin: Old Englishfyr(e)st; of Germanic origin, related to Old Norsefyrstrand GermanFürst‘prince’, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritprathama, Latinprimus, and Greekprōtos.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that it is important to do something or when you are annoyed about something
examples: Do be careful, for goodness' sake.
Oh, for heaven's sake!
For pity's sake, help me!

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
because of the interest or value something has, not because of the advantages it may bring
examples: I believe in education for its own sake.
art for art’s sake

definitions.definition: in order to help somebody/something or because you like somebody/something
examples: They stayed together for the sake of the children.
You can do it. Please, for my sake.
I hope you're right, for all our sakes(= because this is important for all of us).

definitions.definition: in order to get or keep something
examples: The translation sacrifices naturalness for the sake of accuracy.
She gave up smoking for the sake of her health.
Let's suppose,for the sake of argument(= in order to have a discussion), that interest rates went up by 2 per cent.

definitions.definition: for no particular reason
examples: Don't get married just for the sake of it.

origin: Old Englishsacu‘contention, crime’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchzaakand GermanSache, from a base meaning ‘affair, legal action, thing’. The phrasefor the sake ofmay be from Old Norse.

definitions.definition: a cause or an explanation for something that has happened or that somebody has done
examples: He said no but he didn'tgive a reason.
reason for somethingShe gave no reasons for her decision.
reason for doing somethingI have no particular reason for doubting him.
reason behind somethingThere were a number of reasons behind her departure.
reason (that)…We aren't going for thesimple reasonthat we can't afford it.
Job satisfaction is themain reasonpeople remain in jobs or careers.

definitions.definition: into the correct position

definitions.definition: to have done something successfully, especially when it was difficult
examples: I could see the finish line and thought I was home and dry.

definitions.definition: to be successful at something; to earn money for your family to live on
examples: His friends had all gone to college or were bringing home the bacon.

definitions.definition: to become completely clear to somebody, often in a way that is painful

definitions.definition: used to say that if somebody says or does something bad or wrong, it will affect them badly in the future
to make somebody understand or accept something by saying it often, loudly, angrily, etc.

definitions.definition: used to describe somebody who is stupid, not thinking clearly or not paying attention
not especially good; ordinary
examples: The team’s performance was nothing to write home about.

definitions.definition: to get as much advantage as possible from a situation by attacking or arguing in a determined way

definitions.definition: to emphasize an idea, argument, etc. very strongly to make sure people listen to it
examples: The ads are intended to ram home the dangers of driving too fast in fog.

definitions.definition: to easily win a race or competition
examples: Their horse romped home in the 2 o'clock race.

definitions.definition: for a very long time; forever
examples: You can talk till the cows come home—you’ll never make me change my mind.

origin: Old Englishhām, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchheemand GermanHeim.

definitions.definition: without particular plans or arrangements; not busy
examples: Are you free on Saturday? We're having a barbecue.

free for somethingIf Sarah is free for lunch I'll take her out.

definitions.definition: not being used
examples: He held out his free hand and I took it.

definitions.definition: costing nothing

free to somebodyUse of the pool is free to members.
free for somebodyUpdates are free for registered users.

definitions.definition: not a prisoner or slave

definitions.definition: not tied up or in acage

free to do somethingOur livestock is free to roam.

definitions.definition: clear; not blocked

thefree movementof traffic

definitions.definition: not containing or affected by something harmful or unpleasant

free of defects/problems/symptoms

definitions.definition: without the thing mentioned
examples: pure, additive-free ingredients
a trouble-free life

definitions.definition: not attached to something; not stuck somewhere and unable to move

free from somethingThey had to becut freefrom their car after the accident.
free of somethingThe boat hadbroken freeof its moorings.

definitions.definition: ready to give something, especially when it is not wanted

definitions.definition: afreetranslation is not exact but gives the general meaning
to have done something successfully, especially when it was difficult
examples: I could see the finish line and thought I was home free.

definitions.definition: informal; relaxed
examples: Life was never going to be so free and easy again.

examples: This time he gets a free pass but if he does it again, he’ll be sent to jail.
Just because someone has expensive designer gear, it doesn't mean they have a free pass to be mean to my staff.

definitions.definition: to get, have, etc. the opportunity to do what you want to do and to make your own decisions
examples: I was given a free hand in designing the syllabus.

definitions.definition: to get or take something without paying because somebody else is paying for it

examples: The designer was given free rein.

When I paint I just give my imagination free rein.

examples: It's a free country; I'll say what I like!

definitions.definition: used to say that it is not possible to get something for nothing
to be allowed to leave court, etc., without receiving any punishment
examples: She was acquitted and walked free from court.

origin: Old Englishfrēo(adjective),frēon(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvrijand Germanfrei, from an Indo-European root meaning ‘to love’, shared byfriend.

definitions.definition: to stand up after sitting, lying, etc.

examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
Time has flown since the holiday began.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.
Time was when we never needed to lock our house at night.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: to move or travel from one place to another
examples: + adv./prep.She went into her room and shut the door behind her.
I have to go to Rome on business.
She has gone to China(= is now in China or is on her way there).
She has been to China(= she went to China and has now returned).
Are you going home for Christmas?
go to do somethingShe has gone to see her sister this weekend.

definitions.definition: to move or travel, especially with somebody else, to a particular place or in order to be present at an event
examples: go (to something)Are you going to Dave's party?
Who else is going?
go with somebodyHis dog goes everywhere with him.
go (to something) (with somebody)He invited her to go with him to the concert.

definitions.definition: to move or travel in a particular way or over a particular distance
examples: + adv./prep.He's going too fast.
They went farther north into the mountains.
+ nounWe had gone about fifty miles when the car broke down.

definitions.definition: to move in a particular way or while doing something else
examples: She crashed into a waiter and his tray of drinks went flying.
The car went skidding off the road into a ditch.
She went sobbing up the stairs.
I tripped and went tumbling down the stairs.
Don't go charging in there!

definitions.definition: to leave one place in order to reach another
examples: I must be going now.
They came at six and went at nine.
Has she gone yet?
He's been gone an hour(= he left an hour ago).
When does the train go?

definitions.definition: to leave a place and do something different
examples: togo on a journey/tour/trip/cruise
Richard has gone on leave for two weeks.
(British English)togo on holiday
(North American English)togo on vacation

definitions.definition: to visit or attend a place for a particular purpose
examples: togo to school/college/university
(British English)I have togo to hospitalfor an operation.
(North American English)I have togo to the hospital.
(British English)togo to the cinema
(North American English)togo to the movies
togo to prison/jail(= to be sent there as punishment for a crime)
Hegoes to workby bus.
I think you shouldgo to the doctor's.
Do yougo to church(= regularly attend church services)?
I alwaysgo to the gymbefore work.

definitions.definition: to look at a particular page or website
examples: go on somethingShe went on Facebook and changed her relationship status.
go to somethingTo find out what the terms mean, go to the glossary.

definitions.definition: to leave a place or travel to a place in order to take part in an activity or a sport
examples: go for somethingtogo for a walk
to go for a ride/drive/run/swim
Shall we go for a drink(= at a pub or bar)after work?
go doingtogo swimming/fishing/jogging
I have togo shoppingthis afternoon.
We're going sailing on Saturday.
We went walking on the beach.

definitions.definition: to be sent or passed somewhere
examples: I want this memo to go to all managers.

definitions.definition: used to talk about how quickly or slowly time seems to pass
examples: Hasn't the time gone quickly?
Half an hour went past while we were sitting there.
The cruise went very quickly.

definitions.definition: to stop existing; to be lost or stolen
examples: Has your headache gone yet?
I left my bike outside the library and when I came out again it had gone.

definitions.definition: to lead or extend in a particular direction
examples: + adv./prep.Where does this road go?
The trail goes north at this point.
go from something to somethingI want a rope that will go from the top window to the ground.

definitions.definition: to have as a usual or correct position; to be placed
examples: This dictionary goes on the top shelf.
Where do you want the piano to go(= be put)?

definitions.definition: used to say that something does/did not fit into a particular place or space
examples: My clothes won't all go in that one suitcase.
He tried to push his hand through the gap but it wouldn't go.

definitions.definition: used to talk about how well or badly something makes progress or succeeds
examples: ‘How did the interview go?’ ‘Itwent well, thank you.’
Did everythinggo smoothly?
How's it going(= is your life enjoyable, successful, etc. at the moment)?
The way things are going, the company will be bankrupt by the end of the year.

definitions.definition: used in many expressions to show that somebody/something has reached a particular state/is no longer in a particular state
examples: go to somethingShewent to sleep.
The US and Mexicowent to warin 1846.
go into somethingThe lawwent into effecton 1 August.
go on somethingShareswent on salethis morning.
They're threatening togo on strikeagain.
go out of somethingThat colour has gone out of fashion.

definitions.definition: to become different in a particular way, especially a bad way
examples: togo crazy/mad/nuts/insane
She's beginning to go blind.
He suddenly went quiet.
The companywent bankruptlast year.
Our dogwent missingover a week ago.
Her hair is going grey.
The children went wild with excitement.

definitions.definition: to live or move around in a particular state
examples: to go naked/barefoot
She cannot bear the thought of childrengoing hungry.

definitions.definition: to spend a period of time in a particular way
examples: They went for two weeks without receiving any news.

definitions.definition: to not be noticed, reported, etc.
examples: Pippa's absence went unnoticed.
Police are worried that many crimes go unreported.
In these traditional stories, no crime goes unpunished.
Customer service calls went unanswered.

definitions.definition: to make a particular sound or movement
examples: + nounThe gun went ‘bang’.
+ adv./prep.She went like this with her hand.

definitions.definition: to be sounded as a signal or warning
examples: The whistle went for the end of the game.

definitions.definition: if a number willgo intoanother number, it is contained in that number an exact number of times
examples: (+ adj.)3 into 12 goes 4 times.
7 into 15 won’t go.
(North American English)7 into 15 doesn’t go.
go into something7 won’t go into 15.

definitions.definition: to combine well with something
examples: go with somethingDoes this jacket go with this skirt?
go (together)Those colours don’t really go (together).

definitions.definition: used to talk about what tune or words a song or poem has or what happens in a story
examples: + adv./prep.How does that song go?
I forget how the next line goes.
go that…The story goes that she's been married five times.

definitions.definition: to say
examples: I asked ‘How much?’ and he goes, ‘Fifty’ and I go, ‘Fifty? You must be joking!’

definitions.definition: to start an activity
examples: I'll say ‘One, two, three, go!’ as a signal for you to start.
As soon as he gets here we're ready to go.

definitions.definition: if a machinegoes, it works
examples: This clock doesn't go.
What makes it go?

definitions.definition: used to talk about wanting to get rid of somebody/something
examples: The old sofa will have to go.
He's useless—he'll have to go.

definitions.definition: to get worse; to become damaged or stop working correctly
examples: Her sight is beginning to go.
His mind is going(= he is losing his mental powers).
I was driving home when my brakes went.

definitions.definition: to die. People say‘go’to avoid saying‘die’.
examples: You can't take your money with you when you go.

definitions.definition: when moneygoes, it is spent or used for something
examples: I don't know where the money goes!
go on somethingMost of my salary goes on the rent.
go to do somethingThe money will go to finance a new community centre.

definitions.definition: to be sold
examples: We won't let the house go for less than $200 000.
There was usually some breadgoing cheap(= being sold cheaply)at the end of the day.

definitions.definition: to be willing to pay a particular amount of money for something
examples: He's offered £3 000 for the car and I don't think he'll go any higher.
I'll go to $1 000 but that's my limit.

definitions.definition: to help; to play a part in doing something
examples: This all goes to prove my theory.
It(= what has just happened)just goes to showyou can't always tell how people are going to react.

definitions.definition: to be available
examples: There just aren't any jobs going in this area.

definitions.definition: to use a toilet
examples: Do you need to go, Billy?

definitions.definition: anything that somebody says or does is accepted or allowed, even if it shocks or surprises people
examples: Almost anything goes these days.

definitions.definition: in comparison with the average person, thing, etc.
examples: As teachers go, he's not bad.

definitions.definition: to be nearly a particular age, time or number
examples: It was going on (for) midnight.

definitions.definition: used to show what somebody intends to do in the future
examples: We're going to buy a house when we've saved enough money.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is likely to happen very soon or in the future
examples: I think I'm going to faint.
If the drought continues there's going to be a famine.

definitions.definition: used to tell or warn somebody not to do something
examples: Don't go getting yourself into trouble.

definitions.definition: something that is enough for a short time
examples: £50 should be enough to be going on with.

definitions.definition: to make a very great effort to get something or do something
used to show that you are angry or annoyed that somebody has done something stupid
examples: Trust him to go and mess things up!
Why did you have to go and upset your mother like that?
You've really gone and done it(= done something very stupid)now!

definitions.definition: to suddenly become very angry
examples: Now and again she really goes off on one.

definitions.definition: used to express the fact that you do not believe something, or that you think something is bad
examples: Go on with you—you’re never forty. You don’t look a day over thirty.

definitions.definition: (to have) many/not many advantages
examples: You're young, intelligent, attractive—you have a lot going for you!

definitions.definition: not possible or allowed
examples: If the bank won't lend us the money it's no go, I'm afraid.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not want to talk about something in any more detail because you do not even want to think about it
examples: Don't ask me to choose. I don't want to go there.
‘There was a problem with his parents, wasn't there?’ ‘Don't even go there!’

definitions.definition: what you say to tell people to start a race
to start with; for now
examples: ‘One slice or two?’ ‘Just one to be going on with.’

definitions.definition: that remains; still left
examples: I only have one exam to go.

definitions.definition: if you buy cooked foodto goin a restaurant or shop, you buy it to take away and eat somewhere else
examples: Two pizzas to go.

definitions.definition: the way somebody behaves towards other people will affect the way those people behave towards them in the future
something that is not fashionable now will become fashionable again in the future
used to ask what action somebody should take, especially in order to improve the difficult situation that they are in
used by a soldier who is guarding a place to order somebody to say who they are
examples: Halt, who goes there?

origin: Old Englishgān, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchgaanand Germangehen; the formwentwas originally the past tense ofwend.

definitions.definition: of high quality or an acceptable standard
examples: a good book
good food
The piano was inreally goodcondition.
Your work is justnot good enough.
The results werepretty good.
Sorry, my English is not very good.
They've done a reasonablygood job.
Keep up thegood work!
It's agood wayto make friends.
The sound and picture are both ofgood quality.
Things arelooking goodat the moment.
You'll never marry her—she's muchtoo good foryou.

definitions.definition: pleasant; that you enjoy or want
examples: Did you have agood timein London?
This is verygood news.
Let's hope we have good weather tomorrow.
it is good to do somethingIt's good to see you again.
There are loads ofgood thingsabout working here.
We are still friends, though, which is good.

definitions.definition: sensible, logical or strongly supporting what is being discussed
examples: Thank you,good question.
Yes, that's agood point.
I havegood reasonto be suspicious.
What agood idea!
This is agood exampleof what I mean.

definitions.definition: showing or getting approval or respect
examples: The play had good reviews.
The hotel has a good reputation.
He comes from a good family.

definitions.definition: able to do something well
examples: agood player
a good actor/cook
good at somethingto be good at languages/your job
good at doing somethingNick has always been good at finding cheap flights.

definitions.definition: able to use something or deal with people well
examples: She's good with her hands(= able to make things, etc.).
He's very good with children.
She's good with figures.

definitions.definition: morally right; behaving in a way that is morally right
examples: She has tried to lead agood life.
I took Sarah's children to school so I've done mygood deedfor the day.
Giving her that money was a good thing to do.
He is a very good man.
She prayed that God would make her a better person.

definitions.definition: following strictly a set of rules or principles
examples: It isgood practiceto supply a written report to the buyer.
She was a good Catholic girl.

definitions.definition: willing to help; kind to other people
examples: You've always been such agood friend.
good to somebodyHe was very good to me when I was ill.
good of somebodyShe looked into it for me, which was very good of her.
good of somebody to do somethingIt was very good of you to come.
good about somethingI had to take a week off work but my colleagues were very good about it.

definitions.definition: behaving well or politely
examples: You can stay up late if you're good.
Get dressed now, there's a good girl.
‘That's a good dog,’ I said, patting its head.

definitions.definition: healthy or strong
examples: Can you speak into my good ear?
I don'tfeeltoogoodtoday.
‘How are you?’ ‘I'm good.’(= used as a general reply to a greeting)

definitions.definition: having a useful or helpful effect on somebody/something
examples: Living so close to a chemicals factory can't be good.
good for somebody/somethingToo much sun isn't good for you.
Vegetables are good for you.
policies that aregood for business
The deal will begood for the countryas a whole.
it is good for somebody/something to do somethingIt's probably good for you to get some criticism now and then.
(informal)Shut your mouth,if you know what's good for you(= used as a threat).

definitions.definition: suitable or appropriate
examples: Now is agood timeto buy a house.
Do you really think this is agood useof your time?
Students are not allowed to miss classes withoutgood reason.
good for something/to do somethingShe would be good for the job.
good for somebodyCan we change our meeting? Monday isn't good(= convenient)for me.
This isas good aplaceas anyto spend the night.

definitions.definition: used in speaking to show that you approve of or are pleased about something that has been said or done, or to show that you want to move on to a new topic of conversation
examples: ‘Dinner's ready.’ ‘Good—I'm starving.’
‘I got the job.’ ‘Oh, good.’
Good, I think we've come to a decision.

definitions.definition: used as a form of praise
examples: Good old Jack!
‘I've ordered some drinks.’ ‘Good man!’

definitions.definition: used in exclamations
examples: Good heavens!
Good God!

definitions.definition: great in number, amount or degree
examples: a good number of people
The kitchen is a good size.
We spent a good while(= quite a long time)looking for the house.
He devoteda good deal of(= a lot of)attention to the problem.
There'sa good chance(= it is likely)that I won't be here next year.

definitions.definition: not less than; rather more than
examples: We waited for a good hour.
It's a good three miles to the station.

definitions.definition: done to a high level or a great degree; complete
examples: We had a good laugh about it afterwards.
You'll feel better after a good sleep.

definitions.definition: funny or clever
examples: a good story/joke
(informal)That's a good one!

definitions.definition: having enough energy, health, strength, etc. to last for a particular length of time or distance
examples: You're good for(= you will live)a few years yet.

definitions.definition: acceptable for something
examples: The ticket is good for three months.

definitions.definition: likely to provide something
examples: He's always good for a laugh.
Bobby should be good for a few drinks.

definitions.definition: very nearly
examples: The matter is as good as settled.
He as good as called me a coward(= suggested that I was acowardwithout actually using the word ‘coward’).
She’s as good as won.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that a situation is not going to get any better
(of a thing) to be prepared and ready for use; (of a person) to be prepared and ready to do something
examples: By tomorrow afternoon the document will be good to go.
I’ve spent several months training for this race so now I’m good to go.

definitions.definition: completely
examples: I won't go until I'm good and ready.

definitions.definition: several
examples: There are still a good few empty seats.

definitions.definition: used to praise somebody for doing something well
examples: ‘I passed first time.’ ‘Good for you!’

definitions.definition: a lot of somebody/something
examples: There were a good many people there.

origin: Old Englishgōd, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchgoedand Germangut.

definitions.definition: in a more excellent or pleasant way; not as badly
examples: She singsmuch betterthan I do.

People are better educated now.

definitions.definition: more; to a greater degree
examples: You'll like her when you know her better.
A cup of tea? There's nothing I'd like better!

Researchers are hoping tobetter understandthe role of this protein in human disease.

definitions.definition: used to suggest that something would be a suitable or appropriate thing to do
examples: The money could be better spent on more urgent cases.

You'ddo better totell her everything before she finds out from someone else.

definitions.definition: to have more money
examples: Families will be better off under the new law.
Her promotion means she's $100 a week better off.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody is/would be happier or more satisfied because they are/if they were in a particular position or doing a particular thing
examples: She's better off without him.
The weather was so bad we'd have been better off staying at home.

definitions.definition: used to say that while one thing is good, another thing is even better
examples: You can see and, better yet, hear the effects of these changes on their website.
I wanted to make my parents proud by becoming a lawyer or, better yet, a doctor.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody what you think they should do
examples: You'd better go to the doctor about your cough.
We'd better leave now or we'll miss the bus.
You'd better not do that again.
‘I'll give you back the money tomorrow.’ ‘You'd better!’(= as a threat)
If you think it is going to be easy, you'd best think again.

origin: Old Englishbetera(adjective), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchbeterand Germanbesser, also tobest.

definitions.definition: if a law, contract, etc.goes through, it is officially accepted or completed
examples: The deal did not go through.

definitions.definition: to look at or examine something carefully, especially in order to find something
examples: She went through the company's accounts, looking for evidence of fraud.

definitions.definition: to study or consider something in detail, especially by repeating it
examples: Let's go through the arguments again.
