definitions.definition: to perform a series of actions; to follow a method or procedure
examples: Certain formalities have to be gone through before you can emigrate.

definitions.definition: to experience or suffer something
examples: She's been going through a bad patch recently.
He's amazingly cheerful considering all he's had to go through.

definitions.definition: to use up or finish something completely
examples: The boys went through two whole loaves of bread.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: the increase in temperature of the earth’s atmosphere that is caused by the increase of particular gases, especiallycarbon dioxide
examples: a conference on global warming

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to have information in your mind as a result of experience or because you have learned or been told it
examples: know somethingNo oneknows the answer.
I need toknow your name.
The truth about what happened is not yet known.
All I knowis that she used to work in a bank(= I have no other information about her).
know (that)…I know (that) people’s handwriting changes as they get older.
Weknow from experiencethat turning a hobby into a business is not easy.
Iknow for a fact(that) he didn't go to the party.
‘There's no one in.’ ‘How do you know?’
‘You've got a flat tyre.’ ‘I know.’
‘What's the answer?’ ‘I don't know.’
As you know, Emma and I are old friends.
‘Isn’t that his car?’ ‘I wouldn’t know./How should I know?’(= I don’t know and I am not the person you should ask.)
(informal)‘What are you two whispering about?’ ‘You don't want to know’(= because you would be shocked or wouldn't approve).
it is known that…It is widely known that CFCs can damage the ozone layer.
know where, who, etc…I knew where he was hiding.
No oneknows for surewho did it.
know about somethingYou know about Amanda's baby, don't you?
I don't know about you, butI'm ready for something to eat.
Iknow next to nothing(= know almost nothing)about opera.
know of somebody/somethingI know of someone who can help us.
‘Is anyone else coming?’ ‘Not that I know of.’
know to do somethingDoes he know to come here(= that he should come here)first?
know somebody/something to be/do somethingWe know her to be honest.
Strobe lights are known to cause seizures.

definitions.definition: to realize, understand or be aware of something
examples: know (that)…As soon as I walked in the room I knew (that) something was wrong.
She knew she was dying.
‘Martin was lying all the time.’ ‘I should have known.’
know what, how, etc…I knew perfectly well what she meant.
Iknow exactlyhow you feel.
know somethingThis case is hopelessand he knows it(= although he will not admit it).

definitions.definition: to feel certain about something
examples: know (that)…He knew (that) he could trust her.
I know it's here somewhere!
I know things will turn out all right.
I don't know that I can finish it by next week.
I just knew that it was something I wanted to do.
know (something)‘You were right—someone's been spreading rumours about you.’ ‘I knew it!’
‘She's the worst player in the team.’ ‘Oh,I don't know(= I am not sure that I agree)—she played well yesterday.’

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a person, place, thing, etc.
examples: I've known David for 20 years.
Do you two know each other(= have you met before)?
She was a secretary when I first knew her.
She's very nice when youget to knowher.
Knowing Ben, we could be waiting a long time(= it is typical of him to be late).
This manis known to the police(= as a criminal).
I don’t know anyone in Oxford.
IknowPariswell.
Do you know the play(= have you seen or read it before)?
How many of your neighbours do youknow by name?
The new rules could mean the end of footballas we know it(= in the form that we are familiar with).

definitions.definition: to have learned a skill or language and be able to use it
examples: know somethingDo you know any Japanese?
know how to do somethingDo you know how to use spreadsheets?

definitions.definition: to think that somebody/something is a particular type of person or thing or has particular characteristics
examples: be known as somethingHe has becomewidely knownas an expert in child psychology.
It's known as the most dangerous part of the city.
be known for somethingShe isbest knownfor her work on the human brain.
He is internationally known for his work with vaccines.
be known to be/do somethingHe's known to be an outstanding physicist.

definitions.definition: to give somebody/something a particular name or title
examples: be known as somebody/somethingIran wasformerly knownas Persia.
The H5N1 virus ismore commonly knownas bird flu.
Peter Wilson,also knownas ‘The Tiger’
be known as somebody/something to somebodyHe was known as Bonzo to his friends.
be known by somethingThe country was not yetknown by the name ofEngland.

definitions.definition: to be able to recognize somebody/something
examples: I couldn't see who was speaking, but I knew the voice.
She knows a bargain when she sees one.

definitions.definition: to understand the difference between one person or thing and another
examples: I hope we have taught our children to know right from wrong.

definitions.definition: to have seen, heard or experienced something
examples: know somebody/something (to) do somethingI’ve never known it (to) snow in July before.
be known to do somethingHe has been known to spend all morning in the bathroom.

definitions.definition: to have personal experience of something
examples: He has known both poverty and wealth.
She may be successful now, but she hasknown what it is like to bepoor.
She thought she would never know the joy of seeing a child grow up in her care.

definitions.definition: used to say that you think you know, remember, understand, etc. something but you cannot be completely sure, especially because you do not know all the facts
examples: As far as we knew, there was no cause for concern.
As far as I can see, you've done nothing wrong.
She lived in Chicago, as far as I can remember.

definitions.definition: very quickly or suddenly
examples: We were whisked off in a taxi before we knew where we were.

definitions.definition: to have no way of realizing or being aware that you have done something wrong
examples: ‘I'm sorry, I called when you were in bed.’ ‘Don't worry—you weren't to know.’

definitions.definition: used to say that it is easier and wiser to stay in a bad situation that you know and can deal with rather than change to a new situation that may be much worse
used to emphasize that you do not know something and that it is not important to you
examples: She could be dead for all I know.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you do not know something
examples: God knows what else they might find.
‘Where are they?’ ‘Goodness knows.’

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the truth of what you are saying
examples: She ought to pass the exam—goodness knows she's been working hard enough.

definitions.definition: to be confident that you know something, especially when you actually do not
examples: He thinks he knows all the answers.

definitions.definition: to know something perfectly so that you can repeat it at any time without having to think about it
examples: He had all the answers off pat.

definitions.definition: to be in poor condition
examples: Our car has seen better days!

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody’s behaviour
examples: I don't know how you can say things like that.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody the true facts about a situation, especially when these are not known by other people
examples: If the truth be known, I was afraid to tell anyone.

definitions.definition: used to agree with somebody or to show sympathy
examples: ‘What a ridiculous situation!’ ‘I know.’

definitions.definition: used to introduce a new idea or suggestion
examples: I know, let's see what's on at the theatre.

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody by saying that they should realize or understand something
examples: You know as well as I do that you're being unreasonable.

definitions.definition: to know somebody/something extremely well
examples: She must know the play backwards by now.

definitions.definition: to know what should be done, etc. better than other people
examples: The doctor told you to stay in bed, and she knows best.

definitions.definition: to be sensible enough not to do something
examples: He knows better than to judge by appearances.

definitions.definition: to recognize somebody without knowing them well
to have information or evidence that the opposite is true
examples: He says he doesn't care about what the critics write, but I know different.

definitions.definition: to be very aware of a fact and unable to deny or ignore it
examples: He knew full well what she thought of it.

definitions.definition: to be very familiar with somebody/something
examples: This is where I grew up. I know this area like the back of my hand.

definitions.definition: to have very clear and definite ideas about what you want to do
to know a lot about a particular subject or job
to know/tell somebody some useful, interesting or surprising information about somebody/something
examples: She's been married five times, so she knows a thing or two about men!

definitions.definition: to know something because of something unpleasant that has happened to you
examples: She is a tough competitor, as I know to my cost.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to have knowledge about something from your own experience
examples: I worked there for 20 years, so I know what I'm talking about.

definitions.definition: to know where you can get an advantage for yourself
to make sure that people are informed about something, especially by getting somebody else to tell them
examples: The President has let it be known that he does not intend to run for election again.

definitions.definition: to tell somebody about something
examples: I don't know if I can come, but I'll let you know tomorrow.
Let me know how I can help.
Please let me know if there's anything I can do to help.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying
examples: Lord knows, I tried to teach her.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not know the answer to something
examples: ‘Why did she say that?’ ‘Lord knows!’

definitions.definition: to introduce yourself to somebody
examples: I made myself known to the hotel manager.

definitions.definition: to behave badly, usually because you have not been taught the correct way to behave
examples: Don’t blame the children—they don’t know any better.

definitions.definition: to be very stupid; to have absolutely no skill
to know nothing about a subject
examples: I don’t know beans about making movies.

definitions.definition: to know nothing at all about somebody/something
examples: I’m afraid I don’t know the first thing about cars.

definitions.definition: to know nothing at all about something/somebody
examples: We’ve lived next to him for years, but we still don’t know the first thing about him.

definitions.definition: to not know at all who somebody is
to be so surprised by something that you do not know how to react
to feel very embarrassed and not know how to react
to be so excited or confused that you cannot behave or think in a sensible way
to have an easy life without realizing how easy it is
examples: You people without kids don't know you're born.

definitions.definition: to take no interest in something because you do not care about it or it is too much trouble
examples: I've tried to ask her advice, but she doesn't want to know(= about my problems).
‘How much was it?’ ‘You don't want to know’(= it is better if you don't know).

definitions.definition: old enough to behave in a more sensible way than you actually did
to show somebody/know/learn how a particular job should be done
used to say that it is impossible to say what might happen
examples: There's no knowing how he'll react.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody knows nothing about the subject you are talking about
examples: What does he know about football, anyway?

definitions.definition: used to express surprise
examples: Well, what do you know? Look who's here!

definitions.definition: (know) what things are useful, important, etc.
examples: She certainly knows what's what.

definitions.definition: used when you are thinking of what to say next
examples: Well, you know, it's difficult to explain.

definitions.definition: used to show that what you are referring to is known or understood by the person you are speaking to
examples: Guess who I've just seen? Maggie! You know—Jim's wife.
You know that restaurant round the corner? It's closed down.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something that you are saying
examples: I'm not stupid, you know.

definitions.definition: used to introduce an interesting or surprising opinion, piece of news, etc.
examples: You know something? I've never really enjoyed Christmas.

definitions.definition: used to refer to somebody/something without mentioning a name
used to say that you can never be certain about what will happen in the future, especially when you are suggesting that something good might happen

origin: Old Englishcnāwan(earliergecnāwan) ‘recognize, identify’, of Germanic origin; from an Indo-European root shared by Latin(g)noscere, Greekgignōskein, also bycanandken.

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to wait for a short time or to stop what they are doing
examples: Hang on—I'm not quite ready.
Now hang on a minute—you can't really believe what you just said!

definitions.definition: to hold something tightly
examples: Hang on tight—we're off!

definitions.definition: to wait for something to happen
examples: I haven't heard if I've got the job yet—they've kept me hanging on for days.

definitions.definition: used on the phone to ask somebody who is calling to wait until they can talk to the person they want
examples: Hang on—I'll just see if he's here.

definitions.definition: to continue doing something in difficult circumstances
examples: The team hung on for victory.

definitions.definition: to depend on something
examples: A lot hangs on this decision.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to move or travel from one place to another
examples: + adv./prep.She went into her room and shut the door behind her.
I have to go to Rome on business.
She has gone to China(= is now in China or is on her way there).
She has been to China(= she went to China and has now returned).
Are you going home for Christmas?
go to do somethingShe has gone to see her sister this weekend.

definitions.definition: to move or travel, especially with somebody else, to a particular place or in order to be present at an event
examples: go (to something)Are you going to Dave's party?
Who else is going?
go with somebodyHis dog goes everywhere with him.
go (to something) (with somebody)He invited her to go with him to the concert.

definitions.definition: to move or travel in a particular way or over a particular distance
examples: + adv./prep.He's going too fast.
They went farther north into the mountains.
+ nounWe had gone about fifty miles when the car broke down.

definitions.definition: to move in a particular way or while doing something else
examples: She crashed into a waiter and his tray of drinks went flying.
The car went skidding off the road into a ditch.
She went sobbing up the stairs.
I tripped and went tumbling down the stairs.
Don't go charging in there!

definitions.definition: to leave one place in order to reach another
examples: I must be going now.
They came at six and went at nine.
Has she gone yet?
He's been gone an hour(= he left an hour ago).
When does the train go?

definitions.definition: to leave a place and do something different
examples: togo on a journey/tour/trip/cruise
Richard has gone on leave for two weeks.
(British English)togo on holiday
(North American English)togo on vacation

definitions.definition: to visit or attend a place for a particular purpose
examples: togo to school/college/university
(British English)I have togo to hospitalfor an operation.
(North American English)I have togo to the hospital.
(British English)togo to the cinema
(North American English)togo to the movies
togo to prison/jail(= to be sent there as punishment for a crime)
Hegoes to workby bus.
I think you shouldgo to the doctor's.
Do yougo to church(= regularly attend church services)?
I alwaysgo to the gymbefore work.

definitions.definition: to look at a particular page or website
examples: go on somethingShe went on Facebook and changed her relationship status.
go to somethingTo find out what the terms mean, go to the glossary.

definitions.definition: to leave a place or travel to a place in order to take part in an activity or a sport
examples: go for somethingtogo for a walk
to go for a ride/drive/run/swim
Shall we go for a drink(= at a pub or bar)after work?
go doingtogo swimming/fishing/jogging
I have togo shoppingthis afternoon.
We're going sailing on Saturday.
We went walking on the beach.

definitions.definition: to be sent or passed somewhere
examples: I want this memo to go to all managers.

definitions.definition: used to talk about how quickly or slowly time seems to pass
examples: Hasn't the time gone quickly?
Half an hour went past while we were sitting there.
The cruise went very quickly.

definitions.definition: to stop existing; to be lost or stolen
examples: Has your headache gone yet?
I left my bike outside the library and when I came out again it had gone.

definitions.definition: to lead or extend in a particular direction
examples: + adv./prep.Where does this road go?
The trail goes north at this point.
go from something to somethingI want a rope that will go from the top window to the ground.

definitions.definition: to have as a usual or correct position; to be placed
examples: This dictionary goes on the top shelf.
Where do you want the piano to go(= be put)?

definitions.definition: used to say that something does/did not fit into a particular place or space
examples: My clothes won't all go in that one suitcase.
He tried to push his hand through the gap but it wouldn't go.

definitions.definition: used to talk about how well or badly something makes progress or succeeds
examples: ‘How did the interview go?’ ‘Itwent well, thank you.’
Did everythinggo smoothly?
How's it going(= is your life enjoyable, successful, etc. at the moment)?
The way things are going, the company will be bankrupt by the end of the year.

definitions.definition: used in many expressions to show that somebody/something has reached a particular state/is no longer in a particular state
examples: go to somethingShewent to sleep.
The US and Mexicowent to warin 1846.
go into somethingThe lawwent into effecton 1 August.
go on somethingShareswent on salethis morning.
They're threatening togo on strikeagain.
go out of somethingThat colour has gone out of fashion.

definitions.definition: to become different in a particular way, especially a bad way
examples: togo crazy/mad/nuts/insane
She's beginning to go blind.
He suddenly went quiet.
The companywent bankruptlast year.
Our dogwent missingover a week ago.
Her hair is going grey.
The children went wild with excitement.

definitions.definition: to live or move around in a particular state
examples: to go naked/barefoot
She cannot bear the thought of childrengoing hungry.

definitions.definition: to spend a period of time in a particular way
examples: They went for two weeks without receiving any news.

definitions.definition: to not be noticed, reported, etc.
examples: Pippa's absence went unnoticed.
Police are worried that many crimes go unreported.
In these traditional stories, no crime goes unpunished.
Customer service calls went unanswered.

definitions.definition: to make a particular sound or movement
examples: + nounThe gun went ‘bang’.
+ adv./prep.She went like this with her hand.

definitions.definition: to be sounded as a signal or warning
examples: The whistle went for the end of the game.

definitions.definition: if a number willgo intoanother number, it is contained in that number an exact number of times
examples: (+ adj.)3 into 12 goes 4 times.
7 into 15 won’t go.
(North American English)7 into 15 doesn’t go.
go into something7 won’t go into 15.

definitions.definition: to combine well with something
examples: go with somethingDoes this jacket go with this skirt?
go (together)Those colours don’t really go (together).

definitions.definition: used to talk about what tune or words a song or poem has or what happens in a story
examples: + adv./prep.How does that song go?
I forget how the next line goes.
go that…The story goes that she's been married five times.

definitions.definition: to say
examples: I asked ‘How much?’ and he goes, ‘Fifty’ and I go, ‘Fifty? You must be joking!’

definitions.definition: to start an activity
examples: I'll say ‘One, two, three, go!’ as a signal for you to start.
As soon as he gets here we're ready to go.

definitions.definition: if a machinegoes, it works
examples: This clock doesn't go.
What makes it go?

definitions.definition: used to talk about wanting to get rid of somebody/something
examples: The old sofa will have to go.
He's useless—he'll have to go.

definitions.definition: to get worse; to become damaged or stop working correctly
examples: Her sight is beginning to go.
His mind is going(= he is losing his mental powers).
I was driving home when my brakes went.

definitions.definition: to die. People say‘go’to avoid saying‘die’.
examples: You can't take your money with you when you go.

definitions.definition: when moneygoes, it is spent or used for something
examples: I don't know where the money goes!
go on somethingMost of my salary goes on the rent.
go to do somethingThe money will go to finance a new community centre.

definitions.definition: to be sold
examples: We won't let the house go for less than $200 000.
There was usually some breadgoing cheap(= being sold cheaply)at the end of the day.

definitions.definition: to be willing to pay a particular amount of money for something
examples: He's offered £3 000 for the car and I don't think he'll go any higher.
I'll go to $1 000 but that's my limit.

definitions.definition: to help; to play a part in doing something
examples: This all goes to prove my theory.
It(= what has just happened)just goes to showyou can't always tell how people are going to react.

definitions.definition: to be available
examples: There just aren't any jobs going in this area.

definitions.definition: to use a toilet
examples: Do you need to go, Billy?

definitions.definition: anything that somebody says or does is accepted or allowed, even if it shocks or surprises people
examples: Almost anything goes these days.

definitions.definition: in comparison with the average person, thing, etc.
examples: As teachers go, he's not bad.

definitions.definition: to be nearly a particular age, time or number
examples: It was going on (for) midnight.

definitions.definition: used to show what somebody intends to do in the future
examples: We're going to buy a house when we've saved enough money.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is likely to happen very soon or in the future
examples: I think I'm going to faint.
If the drought continues there's going to be a famine.

definitions.definition: used to tell or warn somebody not to do something
examples: Don't go getting yourself into trouble.

definitions.definition: something that is enough for a short time
examples: £50 should be enough to be going on with.

definitions.definition: to make a very great effort to get something or do something
used to show that you are angry or annoyed that somebody has done something stupid
examples: Trust him to go and mess things up!
Why did you have to go and upset your mother like that?
You've really gone and done it(= done something very stupid)now!

definitions.definition: to suddenly become very angry
examples: Now and again she really goes off on one.

definitions.definition: used to express the fact that you do not believe something, or that you think something is bad
examples: Go on with you—you’re never forty. You don’t look a day over thirty.

definitions.definition: (to have) many/not many advantages
examples: You're young, intelligent, attractive—you have a lot going for you!

definitions.definition: not possible or allowed
examples: If the bank won't lend us the money it's no go, I'm afraid.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not want to talk about something in any more detail because you do not even want to think about it
examples: Don't ask me to choose. I don't want to go there.
‘There was a problem with his parents, wasn't there?’ ‘Don't even go there!’

definitions.definition: what you say to tell people to start a race
to start with; for now
examples: ‘One slice or two?’ ‘Just one to be going on with.’

definitions.definition: that remains; still left
examples: I only have one exam to go.

definitions.definition: if you buy cooked foodto goin a restaurant or shop, you buy it to take away and eat somewhere else
examples: Two pizzas to go.

definitions.definition: the way somebody behaves towards other people will affect the way those people behave towards them in the future
something that is not fashionable now will become fashionable again in the future
used to ask what action somebody should take, especially in order to improve the difficult situation that they are in
used by a soldier who is guarding a place to order somebody to say who they are
examples: Halt, who goes there?

origin: Old Englishgān, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchgaanand Germangehen; the formwentwas originally the past tense ofwend.

definitions.definition: if somethinghappens tosomebody/something, they experience it
examples: It's the best thing that has ever happened to me.
What's happened to your car?
I hope nothing(= nothing unpleasant)has happened to them.
Do you know what happened to Gill Lovecy(= have you any news about her)?

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: 60 minutes; one of the 24 parts that a day is divided into
examples: Ispent an houron the phone.
It willtakeaboutan hourto get there.
It usually takes me two hours to walk there.
The interviewlasted half an hour.
It was a three-hour exam.
Most cats sleep 13–16hours a day.
Architects can expect to spend 40 hours a week in meetings.
for an hourI waited for a couple of hours and then I left.
in an hourHe'll be back in an hour.
In less thantwenty-four hoursit would be Christmas.
by the hourWe're paid by the hour.
per/an hourTop speed is 120 miles per hour.
The rate of pay is £10.50 an hour.
within the hourWe hope to be there within the hour(= in less than an hour).
York was within an hour's drive.
He crossed the line in a time of 2 hours and 40 minutes.
Chicago is two hours away(= it takes two hours to get there).
We're four hours ahead of New York(= referring to the time difference).
I sat there,hour after hour, staring at a blank computer screen.

definitions.definition: a period of about an hour, used for a particular purpose
examples: I spent mylunch hourshopping.

definitions.definition: a fixed period of time during which people work, an office is open, etc.
examples: Opening hoursare from 10 to 6 each day.
Most people in this kind of job tend to worklong hours.
during office, business, etc. hoursThe library is open during normalworking hours.
What are youroffice hours?
a hospital’svisiting hours
Britain'slicensing hours(= when pubs are allowed to open)used to be very restricted.
after hoursThis is the only place to get a drink after hours(= after the normal closing time for pubs).
out of hoursYou can contact me on this number out of hours(= when the office is closed).

definitions.definition: a long time
examples: Ittook hoursgetting there.
It took us hours to find out what was wrong.
‘How long did it last?’ ‘Oh,hours and hours.’
Where have you been? I've beenwaiting hours.
for hoursI've beenwaiting for hours.

definitions.definition: a particular point in time
examples: You can't turn him away at this hour of the night.
The hour had come for us to leave.

definitions.definition: the time when something important happens
examples: This was often thought of as the country'sfinest hour.
She thought herlast hourhad come.
Don't desert me in myhour of need.
She helped me in my hour of need.

definitions.definition: the time when it is exactly 1 o’clock, 2 o’clock, etc.
examples: The clockstruck the hour.
ten minutes past the hour
on the hourThere's a bus every hour on the hour

definitions.definition: used when giving the time according to the 24-hour clock, usually in military or other official language
examples: The first missile was launched at 2300 hours(= at 11 p.m.).This is pronounced ‘23 hundred hours’.

definitions.definition: any time, especially a time that is not usual or suitable
examples: He's started staying outtill all hours(= until very late at night).
She thinks she can call meat all hours of the day and night.

definitions.definition: at the last possible moment; just in time
very early, especially when this is annoying
examples: The job involved getting up at some unearthly hour to catch the first train.

definitions.definition: very early or very late and therefore annoying
examples: I apologize for calling you at this ungodly hour.

definitions.definition: the time when you have to do something difficult or unpleasant
examples: I’d better go and see the dentist—I can’t put off the evil hour any longer.
If you keep on borrowing, you are only postponing the evil day when you have to pay it all back.

definitions.definition: if you keep regular, strange, etc.hours, the times at which you do things (especially getting up or going to bed) are regular, strange, etc.
to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: the period of time very early in the morning, soon after midnight
examples: We worked well into the small hours.
The fighting began in the early hours of Saturday morning.
The party continued well into the early hours.

origin: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman Frenchure, via Latin from Greekhōra‘season, hour’.

definitions.definition: of high quality or an acceptable standard
examples: a good book
good food
The piano was inreally goodcondition.
Your work is justnot good enough.
The results werepretty good.
Sorry, my English is not very good.
They've done a reasonablygood job.
Keep up thegood work!
It's agood wayto make friends.
The sound and picture are both ofgood quality.
Things arelooking goodat the moment.
You'll never marry her—she's muchtoo good foryou.

definitions.definition: pleasant; that you enjoy or want
examples: Did you have agood timein London?
This is verygood news.
Let's hope we have good weather tomorrow.
it is good to do somethingIt's good to see you again.
There are loads ofgood thingsabout working here.
We are still friends, though, which is good.

definitions.definition: sensible, logical or strongly supporting what is being discussed
examples: Thank you,good question.
Yes, that's agood point.
I havegood reasonto be suspicious.
What agood idea!
This is agood exampleof what I mean.

definitions.definition: showing or getting approval or respect
examples: The play had good reviews.
The hotel has a good reputation.
He comes from a good family.

definitions.definition: able to do something well
examples: agood player
a good actor/cook
good at somethingto be good at languages/your job
good at doing somethingNick has always been good at finding cheap flights.

definitions.definition: able to use something or deal with people well
examples: She's good with her hands(= able to make things, etc.).
He's very good with children.
She's good with figures.

definitions.definition: morally right; behaving in a way that is morally right
examples: She has tried to lead agood life.
I took Sarah's children to school so I've done mygood deedfor the day.
Giving her that money was a good thing to do.
He is a very good man.
She prayed that God would make her a better person.

definitions.definition: following strictly a set of rules or principles
examples: It isgood practiceto supply a written report to the buyer.
She was a good Catholic girl.

definitions.definition: willing to help; kind to other people
examples: You've always been such agood friend.
good to somebodyHe was very good to me when I was ill.
good of somebodyShe looked into it for me, which was very good of her.
good of somebody to do somethingIt was very good of you to come.
good about somethingI had to take a week off work but my colleagues were very good about it.

definitions.definition: behaving well or politely
examples: You can stay up late if you're good.

‘That's a good dog,’ I said, patting its head.

definitions.definition: healthy or strong
examples: Can you speak into my good ear?
I don'tfeeltoogoodtoday.
‘How are you?’ ‘I'm good.’(= used as a general reply to a greeting)

definitions.definition: having a useful or helpful effect on somebody/something
examples: Living so close to a chemicals factory can't be good.
good for somebody/somethingToo much sun isn't good for you.
Vegetables are good for you.
policies that aregood for business
The deal will begood for the countryas a whole.
it is good for somebody/something to do somethingIt's probably good for you to get some criticism now and then.
(informal)Shut your mouth,if you know what's good for you(= used as a threat).

definitions.definition: suitable or appropriate
examples: Now is agood timeto buy a house.
Do you really think this is agood useof your time?
Students are not allowed to miss classes withoutgood reason.
good for something/to do somethingShe would be good for the job.
good for somebodyCan we change our meeting? Monday isn't good(= convenient)for me.
This isas good aplaceas anyto spend the night.

definitions.definition: used in speaking to show that you approve of or are pleased about something that has been said or done, or to show that you want to move on to a new topic of conversation
examples: ‘Dinner's ready.’ ‘Good—I'm starving.’
‘I got the job.’ ‘Oh, good.’
Good, I think we've come to a decision.

definitions.definition: used as a form of praise
examples: Good old Jack!
‘I've ordered some drinks.’ ‘Good man!’

definitions.definition: used in exclamations
examples: Good heavens!
Good God!

definitions.definition: great in number, amount or degree
examples: a good number of people
The kitchen is a good size.
We spent a good while(= quite a long time)looking for the house.
He devoteda good deal of(= a lot of)attention to the problem.
There'sa good chance(= it is likely)that I won't be here next year.

definitions.definition: not less than; rather more than
examples: We waited for a good hour.
It's a good three miles to the station.

definitions.definition: done to a high level or a great degree; complete
examples: We had a good laugh about it afterwards.
You'll feel better after a good sleep.

definitions.definition: funny or clever
examples: a good story/joke
(informal)That's a good one!

definitions.definition: having enough energy, health, strength, etc. to last for a particular length of time or distance
examples: You're good for(= you will live)a few years yet.

definitions.definition: acceptable for something
examples: The ticket is good for three months.

definitions.definition: likely to provide something
examples: He's always good for a laugh.
Bobby should be good for a few drinks.

definitions.definition: very nearly
examples: The matter is as good as settled.
He as good as called me a coward(= suggested that I was acowardwithout actually using the word ‘coward’).
She’s as good as won.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that a situation is not going to get any better
(of a thing) to be prepared and ready for use; (of a person) to be prepared and ready to do something
examples: By tomorrow afternoon the document will be good to go.
I’ve spent several months training for this race so now I’m good to go.

definitions.definition: completely
examples: I won't go until I'm good and ready.

definitions.definition: several
examples: There are still a good few empty seats.

definitions.definition: used to praise somebody for doing something well

origin: late Middle English (as a verb): perhaps from Middle Low German or Middle Dutchlucken. The noun use (late 15th cent.) is from Middle Low Germanlucke, related to Dutchgeluk, GermanGlück, of West Germanic origin and possibly related tolock(verb).

She doesn’t have muchspare time.
Sorry, I can’t spare the time.
Don'twasteany moretime!
What awaste of time!
Time's running outfor them to complete the project.
I didn't finish the test—I ran out of time.
Time's up—have you worked out the answer yet?
He never takes anytime off(= time spent not working).
Jane's worked herefor some time(= for a fairly long period of time).
Do it now please—not in three hours' time(= three hours from now).
The journey time is two hours.
I don’t want to take up too much of your precious time.
What’s the hurry? We have all the time in the world.

definitions.definition: a period of time, either long or short, during which you do something or something happens
examples: His injuries willtake a long timeto heal.
It took them a long time to get home.
Did you have to wait a long time to see the doctor?
for a timeI lived in Egypt for a time.
I was only there fora short time.
The early morning is the besttime of day.
Her parents dieda long time ago.
At one time(= at a period of time in the past)Emily was my best friend.
Mr Curtis was the managerin my time(= when I was working there).

definitions.definition: a period of history connected with particular events or experiences in people’s lives
examples: at the time of somethingThe movie is set at the time of the Russian Revolution.
in… time(s)the politician who promised ‘peace in our time’
in ancient times
The Industrial Revolution took place in Victorian times.
the violent times we live in(= the present period of history)
in times of somethingin times of trouble
The business was successful, even during difficult times.
Times are hard for the unemployed.
Times have changedsince Grandma was young.

definitions.definition: an occasion when you do something or when something happens
examples: Every timeI hear that song I feel happy.
It doesn't matter if you don't win every time(= every time you play).
Next timeyou're here let's have lunch together.
He failed his driving test three times.
He's determined to passthis time.
When was thelast timeyou saw her?
I see her two or threetimes a week.
How many times(= how often)do I have to tell you not to do that?
(especially North American English)I rememberone time(= once)we had to abandon our car in the snow.
(formal)At no time didI give my consent to the plan.

definitions.definition: an event or occasion that you experience in a particular way
examples: Did youhave a good timein Spain?
They'rehaving a hard timedealing with her illness.
I had an awful time in the hospital.
Mike and I shared some really good times.

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event
examples: The winner's time was 11.6 seconds.
She completed the 500 metresin record time(= faster than any previous runner).
one of the fastest times ever

definitions.definition: the number ofbeats(= units of rhythm)in abarof music
examples: This piece is in four-four time.
a slow waltz time
The conductorbeat timewith a baton.

definitions.definition: the correct speed and rhythm of a piece of music
examples: in time to somethingTry and dance in time to the music(= with the same speed and rhythm).
Clap your hands tokeep time(= sing or move with the correct speed and rhythm).
in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)
He always plays in perfect time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.
The plane took off an hour behind time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.
Hot water is available at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.
She ran up the stairs two at a time.
He surfs the internet for hours at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.
The rain will become heavy at times in the afternoon.
He can be rather moody at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.
If you didn't win a prize, better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.
to deliberately make a situation difficult and unpleasant for somebody
examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.
You'll get used to the work in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?
The ambulance got there just in time(= to save somebody's life).

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
Time has flown since the holiday began.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.
Time was when we never needed to lock our house at night.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: (in the US and some other countries) a school for young people between the ages of 14 and 18
examples: I went to high school in Ohio.
I was a senior in high school at the time.
He dropped out of high school when he was seventeen.

definitions.definition: often used in England, Wales and Northern Ireland in the names of schools for young people between the ages of 11 and 18
examples: Worthing High School

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a person rather than an animal or a machine
examples: Dogs can hear much better than humans.
That is no way to treat another human being.
the colonization of Europe bymodern humans

origin: late Middle Englishhumaine, from Old Frenchhumain(e), from Latinhumanus, fromhomo‘man, human being’. The present spelling became usual in the 18th cent.; compare withhumane.

definitions.definition: in what way or manner
examples: How does it work?
He did not know how he ought to behave.
I'll show you how to load the software.
‘Her behaviour was very odd.’ ‘How so?’
It's funny how(= that)people always remember him.
Do youremember how(= that)the kids always loved going there?
How everdid you get here so quickly?

definitions.definition: used to ask about somebody’s health or how well things are going for them
examples: How are you?
How are you feeling now?
(informal)Hey,how are you doing?

definitions.definition: used to ask whether something is successful or fun
examples: How was your trip?
How did they play?

definitions.definition: used before an adjective or adverb to ask about the amount, degree, etc. of something, or about somebody’s age
examples: How oftendo you go swimming?
I didn't know how much to bring.
How muchare those earrings(= What do they cost)?
How manypeople were there?
How old is she?

definitions.definition: used to express surprise, pleasure, etc.
examples: How kind of you to help!
How he wished he had been there!

definitions.definition: in any way in which
examples: I'll dress how I like in my own house!

definitions.definition: used when asking for information about somebody/something
examples: I'm not going. How about you?

definitions.definition: used to make a suggestion
examples: How about a break?
How about going for a meal?
(especially North American English)How about we go for a meal?

definitions.definition: used to show that you think somebody’s behaviour is very bad or are very surprised by it
examples: Ben! How could you? After all they've done for us!
Ugh! How can you eat that stuff?

definitions.definition: used to say you do not understand how something can happen and would like an explanation
examples: ‘I think you owe me some money.’ ‘How come?’
If she spent five years in Paris, how come her French is so bad?

definitions.definition: used as a formalgreetingwhen you meet somebody for the first time. The usual reply is alsoHow do you do?
used to ask the reason for something
examples: ‘I left work early today.’ ‘How's that(= Why)?’

definitions.definition: used when asking somebody’s opinion of something
examples: I'll tuck your sheets in for you. How's that? Comfortable?
Two o'clock on the dot!How's that forpunctuality!

origin: Old Englishhū, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchhoe, also towhoandwhat.

definitions.definition: to own, hold or possess something
examples: He had a new car and a boat.
Have you got a job yet?
I don't have that much money on me.
She's got a BA in English.

definitions.definition: be made up of
examples: In 2023 the party had 10 000 members.

definitions.definition: to show a quality or feature
examples: have somethingThe ham had a smoky flavour.
The house has gas-fired central heating.
The car has four-wheel drive.
They have a lot of courage.
have something + adj.He's got a front tooth missing.

definitions.definition: to show a particular quality by your actions
examples: Surely she didn't have the nerve to say that to him?

definitions.definition: used to show a particular relationship
examples: He's got three children.
Do you have a client named Peters?

definitions.definition: to be able to make use of something because it is available
examples: Have you got time to call him?
We have no choice in the matter.

definitions.definition: to be in a position where you ought to do something
examples: We have a duty to care for the refugees.

definitions.definition: to be in a position of needing to do something
examples: have somethingI've got a lot of homework tonight.
have something to doI must go—I have a bus to catch.

definitions.definition: to let a feeling or thought come into your mind
examples: He had the strong impression that someone was watching him.
We've got a few ideas for the title.
(informal)I've got it!We'll call it ‘Word Magic’.

definitions.definition: to suffer from an illness or a disease
examples: I've got a headache.

definitions.definition: to hold somebody/something in the way mentioned
examples: She'd got him by the collar.
He had his head in his hands.

definitions.definition: to place or keep something in a particular position
examples: Mary had her back to me.
I soon had the fish in a net.

definitions.definition: to experience something
examples: I went to a few parties and had a good time.
I was having difficulty in staying awake.
She'll have an accident one day.

definitions.definition: to organize or hold an event
examples: Let's have a party.

definitions.definition: to eat, drink or smoke something
examples: tohave breakfast/lunch/dinner
I'll have the salmon(= for example, in a restaurant).
I had a coffee while I was waiting.

definitions.definition: to perform a particular action
examples: I had a swim to cool down.
(British English)tohave a wash/shower/bath
We had a very interesting discussion about climate change.

definitions.definition: to give birth to somebody/something
examples: She's going to have a baby.
Our cat has just had five kittens.

definitions.definition: to produce a particular effect
examples: His paintings had a strong influence on me as a student.
The colour green has a restful effect.

definitions.definition: to receive something from somebody
examples: I had a letter from my brother this morning.
Can I havea Coke, please?

definitions.definition: to be given something; to have something done to you
examples: I'm having treatment for my back problem.
How many driving lessons have you had so far?

definitions.definition: to experience the effects of somebody’s actions
examples: We have orders coming in from all over the world.

definitions.definition: to suffer the effects of what somebody else does to you
examples: She had her bag stolen.

definitions.definition: to cause something to be done for you by somebody else
examples: You've had your hair cut!
We're having our car repaired.

definitions.definition: to tell or arrange for somebody to do something for you
examples: have somebody do somethingHe had the bouncers throw them out of the club.
(informal)I'll have you know(= I'm telling you)I'm a black belt in judo.
have somebody + adv./prep.She's always having the builders in to do something or other.

definitions.definition: to cause somebody/something to be in a particular state; to make somebody react in a particular way
examples: have somebody/something + adj.I want to have everything ready in good time.
have somebody/something doing somethingHe had his audience listening attentively.

definitions.definition: to be with somebody
examples: She had some friends with her.

definitions.definition: to take care of somebody/something in your home, especially for a limited period
examples: We're having the kids for the weekend.

definitions.definition: to entertain somebody in your home
examples: We had some friends to dinner last night.

definitions.definition: to allow something; to accept something without complaining
examples: have somethingI'm sick of your rudeness—I won't have it any longer!
have somebody/something doing somethingWe can't have people arriving late all the time.

definitions.definition: to put somebody at a disadvantage in an argument
examples: You've got me there. I hadn't thought of that.

definitions.definition: to have sex with somebody
examples: He had her in his office.

definitions.definition: to trick or cheat somebody
examples: be hadI'm afraid you've been had.

definitions.definition: to take or accept somebody for a particular role
examples: Who can we have as treasurer?

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody because of something bad they have done to you in the past
examples: I don’t have anything against her, we just don’t get along.

definitions.definition: to finish something unpleasant so that it does not continue
examples: Let's have done with this silly argument.

definitions.definition: to be in a very bad condition; to be unable to be repaired
examples: The car had had it.

definitions.definition: to be extremely tired
examples: I've had it! I'm going to bed.

definitions.definition: to have lost all chance of surviving something
examples: When the truck smashed into me, I thought I'd had it.

definitions.definition: to be going to experience something unpleasant
examples: Dad saw you scratch the car—you've had it now!

definitions.definition: to be unable to accept a situation any longer
examples: I’ve had it (up to here) with him—he’s done it once too often.

definitions.definition: to claim that it is a fact that…
examples: Rumour has itthat we'll have a new manager soon.

definitions.definition: to be likely to suffer the unpleasant effects of your actions and to deserve to do so
examples: It was no surprise when she left him—everyone knew he had it coming to him.

definitions.definition: to not like somebody and be unpleasant to them
to be capable of doing something
examples: Everyone thinks he has it in him to produce a literary classic.
You were great. I didn't know you had it in you.
You spoke really well at that meeting, standing up for us all. I never knew you had it in you.

definitions.definition: to have sex with somebody
to be not nearly as good as somebody/something
not willing to listen to or believe something
examples: I tried to persuade her to wait but she wasn't having any.

definitions.definition: other things, people, etc. of the same kind
examples: There's room in the cellar to store old furniture and what have you.

origin: Old Englishhabban, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhebbenand Germanhaben, also probably toheave.

examples: How does it work?

definitions.definition: used to ask whether something is successful or fun
examples: How was your trip?

examples: How oftendo you go swimming?
I didn't know how much to bring.
How muchare those earrings(= What do they cost)?
How manypeople were there?
How old is she?

definitions.definition: used to express surprise, pleasure, etc.
examples: How kind of you to help!

definitions.definition: in any way in which
examples: I'll dress how I like in my own house!

definitions.definition: used when asking for information about somebody/something
examples: I'm not going. How about you?

definitions.definition: used to make a suggestion
examples: How about a break?
How about going for a meal?
(especially North American English)How about we go for a meal?

definitions.definition: used to show that you think somebody’s behaviour is very bad or are very surprised by it
examples: Ben! How could you? After all they've done for us!

definitions.definition: used to say you do not understand how something can happen and would like an explanation
examples: ‘I think you owe me some money.’ ‘How come?’

definitions.definition: used as a formalgreetingwhen you meet somebody for the first time. The usual reply is alsoHow do you do?
used to ask the reason for something
examples: ‘I left work early today.’ ‘How's that(= Why)?’

definitions.definition: used when asking somebody’s opinion of something
examples: I'll tuck your sheets in for you. How's that? Comfortable?
