definitions.definition: that is needed for a purpose or a reason
examples: necessary to do somethingIt may be necessary to buy a new one.
necessary for somebody to do somethingIt doesn't seem necessary for us to meet.
necessary for somethingFood is necessary for survival.
I'll make the necessary arrangements.
Radical change was anecessary stepfor reform.
if necessaryIf necessary, you can contact me at home.
when necessaryOnly use your car whenabsolutely necessary.
where necessaryChanges can easily be made where necessary.
He has the expertise necessary to make it work.
I was determined not to stay in hospital for any longer than wasstrictly necessary.

definitions.definition: that must exist or happen and cannot be avoided
examples: This is a necessary consequence of progress.
A necessary condition for a steep fall in demand is a widespread collapse in confidence.

definitions.definition: a thing that is bad or that you do not like but that you must accept for a particular reason
examples: The loss of jobs is regarded as a necessary evil in the fight against inflation.

origin: late Middle English: from Latinnecessarius, fromnecesse‘be needful’.

definitions.definition: used to say that one thing can, will or might happen or be true, depending on another thing happening or being true
examples: If you see him, give him this note.
I'll only stay if you offer me more money.
If necessary I can come at once.
You can stay for the weekend if you like.
If anyone calls, tell them I'm not at home.
If he improved his IT skills, he'd(= he would)easily get a job.
You would know what was going on if you'd(= you had)listened.
They would have been here by now if they'd caught the early train.
If I was in charge, I'd do things differently.
(rather formal)If I were in charge…

definitions.definition: when; whenever; every time
examples: If metal gets hot it expands.
She glares at me if I go near her desk.

definitions.definition: used afterask,know,find out,wonder, etc. to introduce one of two or more possibilities
examples: Do you know if he's married?
I wonder if I should wear a coat or not.
He couldn't tell if she was laughing or crying.
Listen to the tune and see if you can remember the words.

definitions.definition: used after verbs or adjectives expressing feelings
examples: I am sorry if I disturbed you.
I'd be grateful if you would keep it a secret.
Do you mind if I turn the TV off?

definitions.definition: used to admit that something is possible, but to say that it is not very important
examples: If she has any weakness, it is her Italian.
So what ifhe was late. Who cares?

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to listen to your opinion
examples: If you ask me, she's too scared to do it.
If you think about it, those children must be at school by now.
If you remember, Mary was always fond of animals.

definitions.definition: used before an adjective or adverb to introduce a contrast
examples: He's a good driver, if a little over-confident.
We'll only do it once—if at all.

definitions.definition: used withwillorwouldto ask somebody politely to do something
examples: If you will sit down for a few moments, I'll tell the manager you're here.
If you would care to leave your name, we'll contact you as soon as possible.

definitions.definition: used beforecould,mayormightto suggest something or to interrupt somebody politely
examples: If I may make a suggestion, perhaps we could begin a little earlier next week.

definitions.definition: despite the possibility that; no matter whether
examples: I'll get there, even if I have to walk.

definitions.definition: despite the fact that; even though
examples: He is a great leader, even if he has many enemies.

definitions.definition: used to say something about an event that may or may not happen
examples: If and when we ever meet again I hope he remembers what I did for him.

definitions.definition: used to express an opinion about something, or after a negative statement to suggest that the opposite is true
examples: I'd say he was more like his father, if anything.
She's not thin—if anything she's on the plump side.

definitions.definition: used to give somebody advice
examples: If I were you I'd start looking for another job.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a different suggestion, after a sentence withif
examples: I'll go if you're going. If not(= if you are not), I'd rather stay at home.

definitions.definition: used after ayes/noquestion to say what will or should happen if the answer is ‘no’
examples: Are you ready? If not, I'm going without you.
Do you want that cake? If not, I'll have it.

definitions.definition: used to suggest that something may be even larger, more important, etc. than was first stated
examples: They cost thousands if not millions of pounds to build.

definitions.definition: used to say that you wish something was true or that something had happened
examples: If only I were rich.
If only I knew her name.
If only he'd remembered to send that letter.
If only I had gone by taxi.

definitions.definition: if that is the case
examples: Do you do research in your studies? If so, what research methods do you use?

definitions.definition: used to say that something that is happening is surprising
examples: I'm surprised they've invited me to their wedding—it's not as if I know them well.

definitions.definition: used to state the only situation in which something can happen
examples: Only if a teacher has given permission is a student allowed to leave the room.
Only if the red light comes on is there any danger to employees.

origin: Old Englishgif, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchofand Germanob.

definitions.definition: having a great effect on people or things; of great value
examples: animportant issue/question/point/factor
We have an important decision to make.
Money played animportant rolein his life.
Listening is animportant partof the job.
one of the most important collections of American art
The important thingis to keep trying.
important to somebody/somethingThe centre isextremely importantto many local people.
Banks arevitally importantto the economy.
important for somebody/somethingMany chemicals are important for our health.
it is important to do somethingIt is important to follow the manufacturer's instructions.
It is important to note thattotal government funding has increased.
It's important to remember thatour figures are averages only.
it is important that…It is important that he attend every day.
(British English)It is important that he should attend every day.
it is important to somebody that…It's very important to me that you be there.
(British English)It's very important to me that you should be there.
it is important for somebody/something to do somethingIt is important for him to attend every day.

definitions.definition: having great influence or authority
examples: an important member of the team
He likes to feel important.

origin: late Middle English: from medieval Latinimportant-‘being of consequence’, from the verbimportare‘bring in’ (in medieval Latin ‘imply, mean, be of consequence’), fromin-‘in’ +portare‘carry’.

definitions.definition: the main street of a town, where most shops, banks, etc. are
examples: Peckham High Street
106 High Street, Peckham
high-street banks/shops
High street giants are struggling against out-of-town retail outlets.
on the high streetThe best shops are on the high street.

definitions.definition: used to refer to shops and businesses that serve the general public
examples: high-street fashion
on the high streetSales on the UK high street are in decline.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: the part of a person that makes them able to be aware of things, to think and to feel
examples: theconscious/subconscious mind
There were all kinds of thoughts running through my mind.
There was no doubt in his mind that he'd get the job.
‘Drugs’ are associated in most people's minds with drug abuse.
She was in a disturbedstate of mind.
I could not have completepeace of mindbefore they returned.
The campaign to win thehearts and mindsof the public continues.
I felt refreshed inmind and body.

definitions.definition: your ability to think and reason; your intelligence; the particular way that somebody thinks
examples: to have a brilliant/good/keen mind
a creative/evil/suspicious mind
She had a lively and enquiring mind.
His mind is as sharp as ever.
I've no idea how her mind works!
He had the body of a man and the mind of a child.
Their evidence might give us some insights into the criminal mind.

definitions.definition: a person who is very intelligent
examples: She was one of the greatest minds of her generation.
a problem that has defeated the world's finest minds
Larry is one of the best trained minds in the industry.

definitions.definition: your thoughts, interest, etc.
examples: Keep your mind onyour work!
Your mind’s not on the job.
Her mind was completely occupied by the previous day's events.
The lecture dragged on and my mind wandered.
Hegave his mind tothe arrangements for the next day.
As for avoiding you,nothing could be further from my mind(= I was not thinking of it at all).

definitions.definition: your ability to remember things
examples: When I saw the exam questions mymindjustwent blank(= I couldn't remember anything).
Sorry—your name has gone right out of my mind.
terrible images that will be imprinted on our minds forever

definitions.definition: if a thought, etc. isat the back of your mind, you are aware of it but it is not what you are mainly thinking about
examples: At the back of my mind was a small, warning voice, telling me it was wrong.
The problem was always at the back of my mind.

definitions.definition: to be something that only exists in somebody’s imagination
examples: These problems are all in your mind, you know.

definitions.definition: to remember somebody/something; to remember or consider that…
examples: You don't have to do anything about it now… just bear it in mind.

definitions.definition: to be extremely bored, etc.
if something isetchedon your heart, memory, etc. you remember it because it has made a strong impression on you
if something isetchedon your mind, heart, etc. you remember it because it has made a strong impression on you
to be unable to decide what you think about somebody/something, or whether to do something or not
examples: I was in two minds about the book(= I didn't know if I liked it or not).
She's in two minds about accepting his invitation.

definitions.definition: to think very hard about one particular thing
examples: She bent her mind to the problem of escape.

definitions.definition: to think very hard about or put a lot of effort into one particular thing
examples: She bent her mind to the problem of escape.

definitions.definition: to have the same opinion about somebody/something
to be unable to think or behave in a reasonable way, especially because of extreme shock or worry
examples: (informal)You're lending them money? You must be out of your tiny mind!

definitions.definition: to be extremely worried, etc.
to produce a very strong feeling of pleasure or shock
examples: Wait till you hear this. It'll blow your mind.
a collection of photographs that will blow your mind

definitions.definition: if somethingboggles the mindorthe mind bogglesat it, it is so unusual that people find it hard to imagine or accept
examples: The vastness of space really boggles the mind.
‘He says he's married to his cats!’ ‘The mind boggles!’

definitions.definition: to remember somebody/something
examples: She couldn't call to mind where she had seen him before.

definitions.definition: to remind you of somebody/something
examples: The painting brings to mind some of Picasso's early works.

definitions.definition: to make yourself think about something that happened in the past
examples: I want you to cast your minds back to the first time you met.

definitions.definition: to change a decision or an opinion
examples: Nothing will make me change my mind.
What made you change your mind?

definitions.definition: an act of changing what you think about a situation, etc.
to refuse to think about something as a possibility
if somethingcomes/springs to mind, you suddenly remember or think of it
examples: When discussing influential modern artists, three names immediately come to mind.
I'm sure someone can help you, but no one immediately springs to mind.

definitions.definition: to come into your mind
examples: It never crossed my mind that she might lose(= I was sure that she would win).
The thought never crossed my mind!

definitions.definition: to tell somebody that you think their behaviour is bad or are angry with them
used to say that you and another person must both be very clever because you have had the same idea or agree about something
used to say that you think you will do something, although you are not sure
examples: I've half a mind to come with you tomorrow.

definitions.definition: used to say that you think that what somebody has done is bad and should do something about it, although you probably will not
examples: I've a good mind to write and tell your parents about it.

definitions.definition: to be thinking of somebody/something, especially for a particular job, etc.
examples: Do you have anyone in mind for this job?
Watching TV all evening wasn't exactly what I had in mind!
What kind of party do you have in mind?

definitions.definition: to intend to do something
examples: For a long time he had it in mind to write a novel about his experiences.

definitions.definition: to have a very bad memory; to forget things easily
to have your own opinion and make your own decisions without being influenced by other people
examples: She has a mind of her own and isn't afraid to say what she thinks.
(humorous)My computer seems to have a mind of its own!

definitions.definition: to be willing to listen to or accept new ideas or suggestions
examples: Try to keep an open mind until you've heard all the facts.

definitions.definition: (not) thinking clearly; (not) able to make sensible decisions
examples: Who in their right mind would spend $50 000 on a pair of shoes?

definitions.definition: to have very clear and definite ideas about what you want to do
to become mentally ill
examples: He feared he was losing his mind.

definitions.definition: to decide something
examples: They're both beautiful—I can't make up my mind.
Have you made up your minds where to go for your honeymoon?
You'll never persuade him to stay—hismind's made up(= he has definitely decided to go).
Come on—it'smake your mind up time!

definitions.definition: a close understanding between people with similar ideas, especially when they meet to do something or meet for the first time
examples: a meeting of minds between the two artists

definitions.definition: the use of the power of your mind to deal with physical problems
examples: He said that keeping running in spite of the pain was just a question of mind over matter.

definitions.definition: your imagination
examples: He pictured the scene in his mind's eye.

definitions.definition: to give no attention to somebody/something
examples: People call him names sometimes, but he doesn't pay them any mind.

definitions.definition: not responsible for your actions because of a mental illness
if somebody/something ison your mind, you are thinking and worrying about them/it a lot
examples: You've been on my mind all day.
Don't bother your father tonight—he'sgot a lot on his mind.

definitions.definition: to become or make somebody aware of new ideas or experiences
examples: He’s eager to open the minds of his audience to different kinds of music.

definitions.definition: used to say somebody will quickly be forgotten when they are no longer with you
to make somebody think and worry about it all the time
examples: The thought that he could have helped more preyed on his mind.
It's been preying on my mind ever since it happened.

definitions.definition: to try to forget about something unpleasant
examples: I tried to push the thought to the back of my mind.

definitions.definition: to make somebody think of somebody/something; to remind somebody of somebody/something
to do or say something to make somebody stop worrying about something
to decide you want to achieve something and give this all your attention
examples: She could have been a brilliant pianist if she'd put her mind to it.
You can do whatever you set your mind to.

definitions.definition: to stop thinking about somebody/something; to deliberately forget somebody/something
examples: I just can't get her out of my mind.

definitions.definition: if somethingslips your mind, you forget it or forget to do it
examples: I’m sorry I didn’t tell you. It completely slipped my mind.

definitions.definition: to say exactly what you think, in a very direct way
examples: She’s never hesitated about speaking her mind.
Everyone should have the right to speak their mind.

definitions.definition: to be remembered for a long time
examples: One of his paintings in particular sticks in my mind.

definitions.definition: to make you forget about something unpleasant for a short time
examples: Painting helped take her mind off her troubles.

definitions.definition: in my opinion
examples: It was a ridiculous thing to do, to my mind.

definitions.definition: a particular way of thinking about things

origin: Old Englishgemynd‘memory, thought’, of Germanic origin, from an Indo-European root meaning ‘revolve in the mind, think’, shared by Sanskritmanasand Latinmens‘mind’.

definitions.definition: to feel that something is important and worth worrying about
examples: I don't care(= I will not be upset)if I never see him again!
He threatened to fire me,as if I cared!
care about somethingShecares deeplyabout environmental issues.
Idon't really careabout how much it costs.
care what, whether, etc…I don't care what he thinks.
care that…No one seems to care that we need help urgently.

definitions.definition: to like or love somebody and worry about what happens to them
examples: He genuinely cares about his employees.
They care an awful lot about each other.
It is good to know that there is someone who cares.

definitions.definition: to make the effort to do something
examples: I've done this job more times than I care to remember.

definitions.definition: used to say that a person is no longer worried about or interested in somebody/something
examples: I'm past caring what he does.

definitions.definition: used to say, often rudely, that you do not think that somebody/something is important or worth worrying about
examples: Quite honestly, I couldn't care less what they do.

definitions.definition: used to say that a person is not worried about or interested in what happens to somebody/something
examples: I could be dead for all he cares!

definitions.definition: to not care at all about somebody/something
not to care at all about something; to think that something is not important
not to care at all
examples: I don’t care two hoots about having money, as long as I’m happy.

definitions.definition: to think that somebody/something is not important or that they have no value
used to say, often rudely, that you do not think that something is important or interesting
examples: Who cares what she thinks?

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody politely if they would like something or would like to do something, or if they would be willing to do something
examples: Would you care for another drink?
If you'd care to follow me, I'll show you where his office is.

origin: Old Englishcaru(noun),carian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Old High Germanchara‘grief, lament’,charon‘grieve’, and Old Norsekǫr‘sickbed’.

definitions.definition: to think about something and try to decide what is true, what will happen, what you should do, etc.
examples: ‘Why do you want to know?’ ‘No particular reason. I wasjust wondering.’
wonder who, where, etc…I wonder who she is.
I was justbeginning to wonderwhere you were.
Youhave to wonderjust what he sees in her.
Ican't help wonderingwhether I'm missing something here.
Have youever wonderedwhat your cat really thinks of you?
Ialways wonderedwhy you never got married.
I'm still wondering how that could have happened.
I sometimes wonder where all this is going to end.
I often wonder what became of him.
wonder about somebody/somethingWe were wondering about next April for the wedding.
+ speech‘Where's Natasha?’ hewondered aloud.

definitions.definition: used as a polite way of asking a question or asking somebody to do something
examples: I wonder if you can help me.
I was wondering whether you'd like to come to a party.

definitions.definition: to be very surprised by something
examples: wonder (at something)She wondered at her own stupidity.
(British English, informal)He's gone and left us to do all the work,I shouldn't wonder(= I wouldn't be surprised if he had).
wonder (that)…I wonder (that) he didn’t hurt himself jumping over that wall.
I don't wonderyou're tired. You've had a busy day.

origin: Old Englishwundor(noun),wundrian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwonderand GermanWunder, of unknown ultimate origin.

definitions.definition: to have something as a meaning in the same or another language
examples: mean somethingWhat does this sentence mean?
What does ‘lark’ mean?
The word ‘Kuching’ means ‘cat’ in Malay.
Many languages use a single word to mean both music and dance.
What is meant by‘batch processing’?

definitions.definition: to have something as a meaning; to represent something
examples: mean something by somethingWhat do we mean by democracy?
mean something to somebodyDoes the name ‘Jos Vos’mean anything to you(= do you know who he is)?
mean (that)…The flashing light means (that) you must stop.
mean somethingThe Olympics havecome to meana wide variety of things.

definitions.definition: to intend to say something on a particular occasion
examples: mean something by somethingWhat did he mean by that remark?
mean something‘Perhaps we should try another approach.’ ‘What do you mean?(= I don't understand what you are suggesting.)’
‘What's that supposed to mean?’ demanded John.
What do you mean, you thought I wouldn't mind?(= of course I mind and I am very angry)
What she means isthat there's no point in waiting here.
I always found him a little strange,if you know what I mean(= if you understand what I mean by ‘strange’).
I know what you mean(= I understand and feel sympathy). I hated learning to drive too.
(informal)It was like—weird.Know what I mean?
I see what you mean(= I understand although I may not agree), but I still think it's worth trying.
See what I mean(= I was right and this proves it, doesn't it)? She never agrees to anything I suggest.
‘But Pete doesn't know we're here!’ ‘That's what I mean!(= that's what I have been trying to tell you.)’
Do you mean Ann Smith or Mary Smith?
mean (that)…Did he mean (that) he was dissatisfied with our service?
You mean(= are you telling me)we have to start all over again?

definitions.definition: to have something as a purpose or intention
examples: mean somethingWhat did she mean byleaving so early(= why did she do it)?
Don't laugh!I mean it(= I am serious).
Hemeans trouble(= to cause trouble).
mean something as somethingDon't be upset—I'm sure she meant it as a compliment.
mean what…Hemeans what he says(= is not joking, exaggerating, etc.).
mean something for somebody/somethingThe chair was clearly meant for a child.
Don't be angry. I'm sure shemeant it for the best(= intended to be helpful).
mean to do somethingShe means to succeed.
I'm sorry I hurt you.I didn't mean to.
I'm feeling very guilty—I've been meaningto call my parents for days, but still haven't got around to it.
mean somebody/something to do somethingI didn't mean you to read the letter.
You're meant to(= you are supposed to)pay before you go in.
mean (that)…(formal)I never meant (that) you should come alone.

definitions.definition: to have something as a result or a likely result
examples: mean somethingSpending too much now will mean a shortage of cash next year.
Being frugaldoesn't necessarily meandoing without.
Lower energy consumption means less pollution.
Touching the wires means instant death.
mean to be/do somethingDo you have any ideawhat it means tobe poor?
mean (that)…We’ll have to be careful with money butthat doesn’t mean(that) we can’t enjoy ourselves.
mean doing somethingThis new order will mean working overtime.
mean somebody/something doing somethingThe injury could mean him missing next week's game.
High unemployment means people are spending less.

definitions.definition: to be of value or importance to somebody
examples: Your friendship means a great deal to me.
$20means a lot(= represents a lot of money)when you live on $100 a week.
Moneymeans nothing to him.
Her childrenmean the world to her.

definitions.definition: to intend somebody to be or do something
examples: be meant for something/somebodyIwas never meant forthe army(= did not have the qualities needed to become a soldier).
Duncan and Makikowere meant for each other(= are very suitable as partners).
mean somebody/something to be somethingHis father meant him to be an engineer.
She did everything to get the two of them together, but I guess it just wasn'tmeant to be.

definitions.definition: to be generally considered to be something
examples: This restaurant is meant to be excellent.

definitions.definition: used to explain or correct what you have just said
examples: It was so boring—I mean, nothing happened for the first hour!
She's English—Scottish, I mean.

definitions.definition: to be serious in your intentions
examples: He has the look of a man who means business.
He says he's going to make changes, and I think he means business.

definitions.definition: to not have any intention of hurting somebody
used to emphasize what you are saying or to ask somebody if they really mean what they say
examples: I mean to say, you should have known how he would react!
Do you mean to say you've lost it?

definitions.definition: to have good intentions, although their effect may not be good

origin: verbOld Englishmænan, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchmeenenand Germanmeinen, from an Indo-European root shared bymind.

definitions.definition: feeling fear; frightened because you think that you might be hurt or suffer
examples: Don't be afraid.
afraid of somebody/somethingIt's all over. There's nothing to be afraid of now.
He had always been afraid of dogs.
They know I'm afraid of heights.
Are you afraid of spiders?
afraid of doing somethingI started to feel afraid of going out alone at night.
afraid to do somethingThe children were afraid to open the door.

definitions.definition: worried about what might happen
examples: afraid of doing somethingShe was afraid of upsetting her parents.
He was afraid of making a fool of himself.
afraid to do somethingDon't be afraid to ask if you don't understand.
She certainly isn't afraid to speak her mind.
afraid (that…)We were afraid (that) we were going to capsize the boat.

definitions.definition: worried or frightened that something unpleasant, dangerous, etc. will happen to a particular person or thing
examples: I'm not afraid for me, but for the baby.
They had already fired three people and he was afraid for his job.
I am very afraid for their safety.
I was afraid for my life.

definitions.definition: used as a polite way of telling somebody something that is unpleasant or disappointing, or that you are sorry about
examples: I can't help you, I'm afraid.
I'm afraid we can't come.
I'm afraid that it's not finished yet.
He's no better,I'm afraid to say.
‘Is there any left?’ ‘I'm afraid not.’
‘Will it hurt?’ ‘I'm afraid so.’

origin: Middle English: past participle of the obsolete verbaffray, from Anglo-Norman Frenchafrayer‘disturb, startle’, based on an element of Germanic origin related to Old Englishfrithu‘peace, safety’.

definitions.definition: measuring or covering a great length or distance, or a greater length or distance than usual
examples: She had long dark hair.
He walked down the long corridor.
It was the world's longest bridge.
along journey/walk/drive/flight
We'rea long wayfrom anywhere here.
It's along way away.
He had long, thin fingers.
a lush meadow of long grass

definitions.definition: used for asking or talking about particular lengths or distances
examples: How longis the River Nile?
The table is six feet long.
The report is only three pages long.

definitions.definition: lasting or taking a great amount of time or more time than usual
examples: He’s been ill(for) a long time.
There was a long silence before she spoke.
I like it now the days are getting longer(= it stays light for more time each day).
along book/film/list(= taking a lot of time to read/watch/deal with)
Nurses have to worklong hours(= for more hours in the day than is usual).
(North American English)He stared at themfor the longest time(= for a very long time)before answering.
Chicago hasa long history ofcountry music.
to have a long life/career

definitions.definition: used for asking or talking about particular periods of time
examples: How longis the course?
I think it's only three weeks long.
How long a stay did you have in mind?

definitions.definition: seeming to last or take more time than it really does because, for example, you are very busy or not happy
examples: I'm tired. It's been a long day.
We were married for ten long years.

definitions.definition: covering all or most of your legs or arms
examples: She usually wears long skirts.
a long-sleeved shirt

definitions.definition: travelling a great distance
examples: The full-back intercepted an attempted long pass and ran through to score.
A hopeful long ball from out of defence led to a goal.
She's good with the driver and hits a long ball.

definitions.definition: travelling too far so that it goes out of play
examples: Her return of serve was long.

definitions.definition: having a lot of a particular quality
examples: The government is long on ideas but short on performance.

definitions.definition: alongvowel is pronounced for a longer time than other vowels
examples: Compare the long vowel in ‘beat’ and the short vowel in ‘bit’.

definitions.definition: very long
examples: There's a list of repairs as long as your arm.

definitions.definition: not longer than the particular time given
examples: It will take an hour at the longest.

definitions.definition: after a long time
examples: At long last his prayers had been answered.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: to have known each other for a long time
examples: We go back a long way, he and I.

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.
A small amount of this paint goes a long way(= covers a large area).
(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something
examples: The new law goes a long way towards solving the problem.

definitions.definition: to have made a lot of progress
examples: We've come a long way since the early days of the project.
The study of genes has come a long way in recent years.

definitions.definition: to need to make a lot of progress before you can achieve something
examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no definite answer to a question
examples: ‘How long will it take?’ ‘How long's a piece of string?’

definitions.definition: relating to a longer period in the future
examples: This measure inevitably means higher taxes in the long run.

definitions.definition: used to describe what will happen a long, short, etc. time in the future
examples: Such a development seems unlikely, at least in the short term(= it will not happen for quite a long time).

In the long term, our efforts will pay off.

definitions.definition: it makes no real difference which of two possible choices you make
used to say that the reasons for something are complicated and you would prefer not to give all the details
to reject something or treat it as not important; to stop dealing with a problem
examples: He tends to deal with disputes by kicking them into the long grass.

definitions.definition: used when you are telling somebody the essential facts about something or what effect it will have, without explaining all the details
the power and/or authority of something
examples: There is no escape from the long arm of the law.

definitions.definition: (to have) an unhappy or disappointed expression
examples: He took one look at her long face and said ‘What’s wrong?’
The news for the company isn't good, to judge from the long faces in the boardroom.

definitions.definition: old or too old
an attempt or a guess that is not likely to be successful but is worth trying
examples: It's a long shot, but it just might work.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that you will get to the point of what you are saying quickly, without including all the details
examples: To cut a long story short, we didn’t get home until 3 in the morning!
Anyway, long story short: we had this argument and I haven't seen him since.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
not nearly; not at all
examples: It's not over yet—not by a long chalk.

definitions.definition: to consider a problem or possibility very carefully and without hurrying
examples: We need to take a long, hard look at all the options.

definitions.definition: to consider what is likely to happen or be important over a long period of time rather than only considering the present situation
examples: As pension funds are investing for members’ retirements, they can take the long view.

origin: adjectiveOld Englishlang,long(adjective),lange,longe(adverb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanlang.

definitions.definition: to try and give an answer or make a judgement about something without being sure of all the facts
examples: I don't really know. I'm just guessing.
guess at somethingWe can only guess at her reasons for leaving.
guess + adv.Heguessed right/wrong.
guess (that)…I'd guess that she's about 30.
guess where, who, etc…Can you guess where I've been?
guess somethingCan you guess his age?

definitions.definition: to find the right answer to a question or the truth without knowing all the facts
examples: guess somethingShe guessed the answer straight away.
guess what, where, etc…You'll never guesswhat she told me.
guess (that)…You would never guess (that) she had problems. She’s always so cheerful.

definitions.definition: the most important part of something
examples: heart of somethingtheheart of the matter/problem
The committee's reportwent to the heart ofthe government's dilemma.
at the heart of somethingThe distinction between right and wronglies at the heart ofall questions of morality.
the issue at the heart of modern government
at the/its heartThe story, at its heart, is a simple tale of loss and rediscovery.
Buddhism, at the heart, is about harmony.

definitions.definition: the part that is in the centre of something
examples: heart of somethinga quiet hotelin the very heartof the city
at the/its heartan open space with a small arena at the heart

definitions.definition: the smaller leaves in the middle of acabbage,lettuce, etc.
one of the foursuits(= sets)in apackof cards, with red heart symbols on them
examples: the queen of hearts
Hearts is/are trumps.

definitions.definition: one card from thesuitcalledhearts
examples: Who played that heart?

definitions.definition: used to say that when you are away from somebody that you love, you love them even more
used to say what somebody is really like even though they may seem to be something different
examples: He's still a socialist at heart.

definitions.definition: if something isetchedon your heart, memory, etc. you remember it because it has made a strong impression on you
to make somebody feel very unhappy
examples: She broke his heart when she called off the engagement.
It breaks my heart to see you like this.

definitions.definition: using only your memory
examples: I've seen the show so often I know all the songs by heart.
She's learnt the whole speech off by heart.

definitions.definition: if you havea change of heart, your attitude towards something changes, usually making you feel more friendly, helpful, etc.
examples: Dan did not want to get married but recently he’s had a change of heart.
She said she's not coming, but she might have a change of heart.

definitions.definition: having a lot of importance and interest for somebody
used to emphasize that you are telling the truth or will do what you promise
examples: I saw him do it—cross my heart.

definitions.definition: used to compare two things and say that one of them is better
examples: Look at him dance! Eat your heart out, Fred Astaire(= he dances even better than Fred Astaire).

definitions.definition: to be able or willing to do something
examples: Can you find it in your heart to forgive her?
He couldn't find it in himself to trust anyone again.

definitions.definition: in a way that is sincere
examples: I beg you, from the bottom of my heart, to spare his life.
It was clearly an offer that came from the heart.
I could tell he spoke from the heart.

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel positive, especially when they thought that they had no chance of achieving something
to give your love to one person
used to ask somebody to be kind and/or reasonable
examples: Have a heart! I’ve nowhere else to stay!

definitions.definition: to be a very kind person
to be a person who does not show other people sympathy
to want somebody to be happy and successful even though your actions may not show this
examples: Although he was sometimes too strict with his children, he had their best interests at heart.

definitions.definition: to be very dear to somebody
examples: He'll always have a place in my heart.

definitions.definition: with a lot of energy and enthusiasm
examples: They threw themselves heart and soul into the project.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not feel sympathy for somebody
examples: ‘I have to go to Brazil on business.’ ‘My heart bleeds for you!’

definitions.definition: used to say that you feel a lot of sympathy for somebody
examples: Our hearts go out to the families of the victims.

definitions.definition: somebody feels nervous or frightened about something
examples: My heart was in my mouth as I opened the envelope.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody’s intentions are kind and sincere even though they sometimes do the wrong thing
used to say that you are not very interested in or enthusiastic about something
examples: Her heart isn't in her job.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody has a sudden feeling of happiness or excitement
examples: My heart leapt at the news.
Her heart leaped with joy.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody has a sudden feeling of fear, excitement, etc.
examples: My heart missed a beat when I saw who it was.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody suddenly feels sad or depressed about something
examples: My heart sank when I saw how much work there was left.
She watched him gowith a sinking heart.

definitions.definition: a home is where the people you love are
happy and cheerful
if you know somethingin your heart, you have a strong feeling that it is true
examples: She knew in her heart of hearts that she was making the wrong decision.
In my heart, I knew it wasn't true.

definitions.definition: it makes somebody feel happy when they see or hear something
examples: It does my heart good to see the old place being taken care of so well.

definitions.definition: to act according to what you feel rather than to what you think is sensible
to stop hoping for something or trying to do something because you no longer feel confident
to fall in love with somebody/something
a man/woman who likes the same things or has the same opinions as you
to be unable to do something because you know that it will make somebody sad or upset
using only your memory
examples: She's learnt the whole speech off by heart.
I've seen the show so often I know all the songs by heart.

definitions.definition: because of kind feelings, without thinking about what advantage there will be for you
examples: You're not telling me he offered to lend you the money out of the goodness of his heart?

definitions.definition: to tell somebody all your problems, feelings, etc.
examples: Finally, he broke down in tears and poured out his heart to her.

definitions.definition: to want something very much
examples: They’ve set their heart on a house in the country.

definitions.definition: very unhappy or disappointed
to cry noisily for a long time because you are very sad
examples: She flung herself at his chest and sobbed her heart out.

definitions.definition: to make somebody fall in love with you
to make somebody be afraid, etc.
examples: The sound of gunfire struck fear into the hearts of the villagers.

definitions.definition: to feel more positive about something, especially when you thought that you had no chance of achieving something
examples: The government can take heart from the latest opinion polls.

definitions.definition: to be very upset by something that somebody says or does
examples: You shouldn’t take everything he says to heart.

definitions.definition: to strongly affect you in an emotional way
to destroy the most important part or aspect of something
examples: Closing the factory tore the heart out of the community.

definitions.definition: as much as you want
examples: a supervised play area where children can run around to their heart’s content

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel happy orsympathetic
examples: Doesn’t that story just warm the cockles of your heart?

definitions.definition: the way to make somebody like or love you
examples: The way to a man's heart is through his stomach(= by giving him good food).

definitions.definition: to allow your feelings to be seen by other people
examples: He’s not one to wear his heart on his sleeve.

definitions.definition: if a person does not know about something that they would normally think was bad, then it cannot hurt them
examples: What does it matter if I use his flat while he’s away? What the eye doesn’t see…!

definitions.definition: to make somebody love you
completely
examples: I hope with all my heart that things work out for you.
I wish you well with all my heart.

definitions.definition: thinking and behaving like a young person even when you are old
examples: My father is 76 but he’s still young at heart.
He's over 70, but he's young at heart.

origin: Old Englishheorte, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhartand GermanHerz, from an Indo-European root shared by Latincor,cord-and Greekkēr,kardia.

definitions.definition: according to a rule or the way that somebody says that something should be done
examples: in accordance with legal requirements
We acted in accordance with my parents’ wishes.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchacordance, fromacorder‘bring to an agreement’, from Latinad-‘to’ +cor,cord-‘heart’; influenced byconcord.

definitions.definition: a particular situation or a situation of a particular type
examples: in… casesIn some casespeople have had to wait several weeks for an appointment.
In most casesthe increases have been marginal.
In many casesit is the pressure of the job that causes managers to behave like this.
What should be donein such cases?
The substances causes nausea, sickness and,in extreme cases, death.
in cases of somethingThe company only dismisses its employees in cases of gross misconduct.
in the case of somethingIn the case of banks, the law can limit activities.
Sometimes compost isn't enough,in which caseartificial products may have to be used.
a case of somethingIt's aclassic case(= a very typical case)of bad planning.
This appears to be a case of mistaken identity.

definitions.definition: the true situation
examples: be the caseIf that is the case(= if the situation described is true), we need more staff.
it's the case that…It is simply not the case that prison conditions are improving.
It remains the case that not enough graduates are going into teaching.

definitions.definition: a situation that relates to a particular person or thing
examples: in somebody's caseI cannot make an exception in your case(= for you and not for others).
In your case, we are prepared to be lenient.
in this caseDon't underestimate the power of the pen, or in this case, the power of the keyboard.
In the present case, the facts are not disputed.
Every application will be decidedon a case-by-case basis(= each one will be considered separately).

definitions.definition: a matter that is being officially investigated, especially by the police
examples: They never solved the Jones murder case.
Police believe it is a simple case of theft.
Four officers areinvestigating the case.

definitions.definition: a question to be decided in court
examples: acourt case
acriminal/civil case
Thecase will be heardnext week.
The Supreme Court heard the case yesterday.
towin/lose a case
When does her case come before the court?

definitions.definition: a set of facts or arguments that support one side in a trial, a discussion, etc.
examples: Our lawyer didn't think wehad a case(= had enough good arguments to win in a court of law).
case for somethingthe case for the defence/prosecution
case against somebody/somethingThe case against her was very weak.
Let us considerthe case for and againstprivate education.
case for doing somethingThe reportmakes out astrongcase(= gives good arguments)for spending more money on hospitals.
He successfullyargued the casefor accepting the agreement.
case that…They try tomake the casethat this war is necessary.
You will each be given the change to state your case.

definitions.definition: a container or cover used to protect or store things; a container with its contents or the amount that it contains
examples: a jewellery case

case of somethinga case(= 12 bottles)of champagne

definitions.definition: a container with flat sides and a handle, used for carrying clothes, etc. when you are travelling
examples: Let me carry your case for you.

definitions.definition: the fact of somebody having a disease or an injury; a person who has a disease or an injury
examples: a severe case of food poisoning
Over 130 000casesof cholerawere reportedin 2016.
The most serious cases were treated at the scene of the accident.

definitions.definition: a person who needs, or is thought to need, special treatment or attention
examples: He's a hopeless case.
After the exams, the teacher gave all borderline cases a spoken test.

definitions.definition: the form of a noun, an adjective or a pronoun in some languages, that shows its relationship to another word
examples: the nominative/accusative/genitive case
Latin nouns have case, number and gender.
In Polish the verb ‘to be’ takes the instrumental case.

definitions.definition: used to say that one of two or more possibilities is true, but which one is true depends on the circumstances
examples: There may be an announcement about this tomorrow—or not, as the case may be.

definitions.definition: to criticize somebody all the time
examples: She's always on my case about cleaning my room.

definitions.definition: to be dealing with a particular matter, especially a criminal investigation
examples: We have two agents on the case.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: a situation in business, politics, etc. where there is a lot of competition and people are willing to harm each other in order to succeed
examples: I'm afraid in this line of work it's a case of dog eat dog.
We're operating in a dog-eat-dog world.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to stop criticizing you
whatever happens or may have happened
examples: There's no point complaining now—we're leaving tomorrow in any case.

definitions.definition: because of the possibility of something happening
examples: You'd better take the keys in case I'm out.
You probably won't need to call—but take my number, just in case.

definitions.definition: if something happens
examples: In case of fire, ring the alarm bell.

definitions.definition: if that happens or has happened; if that is the situation
examples: ‘I’ve made up my mind.’ ‘In that case, there’s no point discussing it.’

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not need to say any more about something because you think that you have proved your point
used by lawyers in court to say that they have finished presenting their case
examples: The prosecution rests its case.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 6 andnounsenses 9 to 11 Middle English: from Old Frenchcas, from Latincasus‘fall’, related tocadere‘to fall’; in sense (11) directly from Latin, translating Greekptōsis, literally ‘fall’.nounsenses 7 to 8 late Middle English: from Old Frenchcasse,chasse(moderncaisse‘trunk, chest’,châsse‘reliquary, frame’), from Latincapsa, related tocapere‘to hold’.

definitions.definition: something that connects two facts, ideas, etc.
examples: connection between A and BScientists haveestablished a connectionbetween cholesterol levels and heart disease.
connection with somethingadirect/close/strong connectionwith something
His resignation musthavesomeconnectionwith the recent scandal.
connection to somethingConsumers are more likely to buy something from a person or shop they feel apersonal connectionto.
How did youmake the connection(= realize that there was a connection between two facts that did not seem to be related)?

definitions.definition: the act of connecting or the state of being connected
examples: abroadband/wireless/Wi-Fi/network connection
I'm having problems with myinternet connection.
connection to somethingConnection to the gas supply was delayed for three days.

definitions.definition: a point, especially in an electrical system, where two parts connect
examples: A faulty connection caused the machine to stop.

definitions.definition: a train, bus or plane at a station or an airport that a passenger can take soon after getting off another in order to continue their journey
examples: I missed the connection by five minutes.

connection to…We arrived in good time for the connection to Paris.

definitions.definition: a means of travelling to another place
examples: There are good bus and train connections between the resort and major cities.
good connections with New York

definitions.definition: a person or an organization that you know and that can help or advise you in your social or professional life
examples: One of my business connections gave them my name.

definitions.definition: people who are your relatives, but not members of your close family
examples: She is British but also has German connections.
a network of family connections in Italy

definitions.definition: for reasons connected with somebody/something
examples: A man has been arrested in connection with the murder of the teenager.

definitions.definition: for reasons connected with something recently mentioned

origin: late Middle English: from Latinconnexio(n-), fromconnectere, fromcon-‘together’ +nectere‘bind’. The spelling-ct(18th cent.) is fromconnect, on the pattern of pairs such ascollect,collection.

definitions.definition: a thing that is added to something else

addition to somethingthe latest addition to our range of cars

definitions.definition: the act of adding something to something else
examples: Add salt, tasting with every addition.
addition of somethingPasta's basic ingredients are flour and water, sometimeswith the addition ofeggs or oil.

examples: architects who specialize in home additions

examples: children learning addition and subtraction

examples: In addition to these arrangements, extra ambulances will be on duty until midnight.

origin: late Middle English: from Latinadditio(n-), from the verbaddere, fromad-‘to’ + the base ofdare‘put’.

definitions.definition: a difference between two or more people or things that you can see clearly when they are compared or put close together; the fact of comparing two or more things in order to show the differences between them
examples: The two cities make an interesting contrast.
contrast between A and BThere is an obvious contrast between the cultures of East and West.
in contrastIn contrast, the south suffered very little hurricane damage.
in contrast to somebody/somethingThe situation when we arrived was inmarked contrastto the news reports.
The poverty of her childhoodstands intotalcontrast toher life in Hollywood.

contrast with somebody/somethingto show asharp/stark/striking contrastwith something
contrast in somethingA wool jacket complements the silk trousers andprovidesan interestingcontrastin texture.
by contrastWhen you look at their new system, ours seems very old-fashioned by contrast.
contrast of A and BCareful contrast of the two plans shows some important differences.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that is clearly different from somebody/something else
examples: The work you did today is quite a contrast to(= very much better/worse than)what you did last week.

definitions.definition: differences in colour or in light and dark, used in photographs and paintings to create a special effect
examples: The artist's use of contrast is masterly.

definitions.definition: the amount of difference between light and dark in a photograph or the picture on a television screen
examples: Use this button to adjust the contrast.

origin: late 17th cent. (as a term in fine art, in the sense ‘juxtapose so as to bring out differences in form and colour’): from Frenchcontraste(noun),contraster(verb), via Italian from medieval Latincontrastare, from Latincontra-‘against’ +stare‘stand’.

definitions.definition: a piece of furniture for sleeping on
examples: asingle/double bed
on the bedShe lay on the bed(= on top of the covers).
in bedHe lay in bed(= under the covers).
I'm tired—I'mgoing to bed.
I'll justputthe kidsto bed.
It'stime for bed(= time to go to sleep).
He likes to have a mug of cocoa before bed(= before going to bed).
toget into/out of bed
tomake the bed(= arrange the covers in a tidy way)

He has beenconfined to bedwith flu for the past couple of days.

I ran and hid under a bed.
She came over and sat at the foot(= bottom)of the bed.

definitions.definition: the bottom of a river, the sea, etc.
examples: the ocean bed
oyster beds(= an area in the sea where there are manyoysters)

definitions.definition: an area of ground in a garden or park for growing flowers, vegetables, etc.
examples: flower beds
Raised flower beds are easier to keep tidy.
bed of somethingornamental beds of roses

definitions.definition: a layer of something that other things lie or rest on
examples: grilled chicken, served on a bed of rice
The blocks should be laid on a bed of concrete.

definitions.definition: a layer ofclay, rock, etc. in the ground
(not) an easy or a pleasant situation
examples: Their life together hasn't exactly been a bed of roses.

definitions.definition: to die because you are old or ill
to be in a bad mood for the whole day for no particular reason
to have sex with somebody
used to refer to sexual activity
examples: What's he like in bed?

definitions.definition: to go to bed and stay there because you are ill
tourinatein your bed by accident
examples: It is quite common for small children to wet their beds.

definitions.definition: you must accept the results of your actions

origin: Old Englishbed,bedd(noun),beddian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchbedand GermanBett.

definitions.definition: to speak or tell somebody something, using words
examples: + speech‘Hello!’ she said.
‘That was marvellous,’ said Daniel.
A legendary football coachonce said, ‘Winning is everything.’

definitions.definition: to repeat words, phrases, etc.
examples: to say a prayer
Try to say that line with more conviction.

definitions.definition: to give particular information or instructions
examples: + speechThe notice said ‘Keep Out’.
say somethingThe clock said three o'clock.
say (that)…The instructions say (that) we should leave it to set for four hours.
say where, why, etc…The book doesn't say where he was born.
say to do somethingThe guidebook says to turn left.

definitions.definition: to express an opinion on something
examples: say somethingSay what you like(= although you disagree)about her, she's a fine singer.
I'll say this for them, they're a very efficient company.
Anna thinks I'm lazy—what do you say(= what is your opinion)?
say (that)…I can't sayI blame her for resigning(= I think she was right).
I have to sayI enjoyed every minute.
I say(= suggest)we go without them.
I wouldn't saythey were rich(= in my opinion they are not rich).
That's not to sayit's a bad movie(= it is good but it is not without faults).
say what, how, etc…It's hard to saywhat caused the accident.
It's difficult to saywhether the story is true.
‘When will it be finished?’ ‘I couldn't say(= I don't know).’
Is it worth it? I would say not.

definitions.definition: to make thoughts, feelings, etc. clear to somebody by using words, looks, movements, etc.
examples: say somethingHis angry glancesaid it all.
Just what is the artist trying to say in her work?
say something about somebody/somethingThe museum's architects have managed to say something about our national story.

definitions.definition: to show, sometimes indirectly, what somebody/something is like
examples: say somethingThatsays it allreally, doesn't it?(= it shows clearly what is true)
say something about somebody/somethingThe incident says an awful lot about his character.
say something to somebodyThis says to me that there is more here than you are letting on.

definitions.definition: to suggest or give something as an example or a possibility
examples: say something/somebodyYou could learn the basics in,let's say, three months.
Let’s take any writer, say(= for example)Dickens…
say (that)…Say you lose your job: what would you do then?
Just say he refuses to talk to you.

definitions.definition: to be much more difficult to do than to talk about
examples: ‘Why don't you get yourself a job?’ ‘That's easier said than done.’

definitions.definition: very quickly; in a very short time
used to say that you understand a situation and there is no need to say any more
examples: ‘He's a politician, remember.’ ‘Enough said.’

definitions.definition: to be very obvious or easy to predict
examples: Of course I'll help you.That goes without saying.

definitions.definition: to be ready, unwilling, etc. to talk or give your views on something
examples: She doesn't have much to say for herself(= doesn't take part in conversation).
He had plenty to say for himself(= he had a lot of opinions and was willing to talk).
Late again—what have you got to say for yourself(= what is your excuse)?

definitions.definition: to be angry
examples: He knew that if he wasn't back by midnight, his parents would have something to say about it.

definitions.definition: used to introduce an opinion that makes what you have just said seem less strong
examples: I sometimes get worried in this job. Having said that, I enjoy doing it, it's a challenge.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that something is likely
examples: I dare say you know about it already.

definitions.definition: used when you are going to criticize somebody or say something that might upset them
examples: That colour doesn't really suit you, if you don't mind my saying so.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that you have listened to their opinion, especially when you are going to express a different opinion
examples: I hear what you're saying, but you're wrong.

definitions.definition: used for emphasis to say ‘yes’
examples: ‘Does she see him often?’ ‘I'll say! Nearly every day.’

definitions.definition: used when you are commenting on a situation and saying that you are happy about it
examples: Most teachers, I'm glad to say, take their jobs very seriously.

definitions.definition: used for saying that something is disappointing
examples: He didn't accept the job, I'm sorry to say.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an opinion
examples: Well, I must say, that's the funniest thing I've heard all week.

definitions.definition: used to express surprise, shock, etc.
examples: I say! What a huge cake!

definitions.definition: used to attract somebody’s attention or introduce a new subject of conversation
examples: I say, can you lend me five pounds?

definitions.definition: it shows a good/bad quality that somebody/something has
examples: It says a lot for her that she never lost her temper.
It didn't say much for their efficiency that the order arrived a week late.

definitions.definition: used to say that you would like something or to accept something that is offered
examples: I wouldn't say no to a pizza.
‘Tea, Brian?’ ‘I wouldn't say no.’

definitions.definition: a bad situation will pass or be forgotten most quickly if nothing more is said about it
the best thing to do is say as little as possible about something
used when commenting on a situation to suggest that you could say something more or worse but prefer not to
examples: Let's just say he wasn't very happy about the decision(= he was very unhappy or angry about it).

definitions.definition: used when making a suggestion or giving an example
examples: I can let you have it for, well let's say £100.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying or to ask somebody if they really mean what they say
examples: I mean to say, you should have known how he would react!
Do you mean to say you've lost it?

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that the information you are giving is obvious
examples: The problem, needless to say, is the cost involved.

definitions.definition: do not stop hoping
used to say that something was, or will be, done immediately
to never say anything good about somebody/something
examples: Nobody had a good word to say about him.

definitions.definition: to be very shy or gentle
examples: He's so nervous he wouldn't say boo to a goose.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a stronger way of describing something
examples: a difficult, not to say impossible, task

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to smile before you take their photograph
to say something important
examples: You said a mouthful there, buddy.

definitions.definition: to refuse an offer, a suggestion, etc.
examples: If you don't invest in this, you're saying no to a potential fortune.

definitions.definition: used to say that you understand exactly what somebody means or is trying to say, so it is unnecessary to say anything more
examples: ‘They went to Paris together.’ ‘Say no more!’

definitions.definition: to say exactly what you feel or think
used to express surprise at what somebody has just said
examples: ‘He's getting married.’ ‘Say what?’

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to tell you when you should stop pouring a drink or serving food for them because they have enough
to give an order; to make a request
examples: Just say the word, and I'll go.

definitions.definition: used to suggest that although you could say more, what you do say will be enough to explain what you mean
examples: I won’t go into all the details. Suffice it to say that the whole event was a complete disaster.

definitions.definition: in other words
examples: three days from now, that is to say on Friday

definitions.definition: used to introduce an opinion that makes what you have just said seem less strong
used to say that something is not very unusual or special
examples: She's a better player than me, but that's not saying much(= because I am a very bad player).

definitions.definition: used to say that it is impossible to predict what might happen
examples: There's no saying how he'll react.

definitions.definition: there are/are not good reasons for doing something, believing something or agreeing with something
without making something seem better, worse, more serious, etc. than it really is
examples: I was surprised, to say the least.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a further fact or thing in addition to those already mentioned
examples: It was too expensive, to say nothing of the time it wasted.

definitions.definition: I agree completely
examples: ‘We must stand up for ourselves.’ ‘Well said, John.’

definitions.definition: would you like something/to do something?
examples: What would you say to eating out tonight?
Let's go away for a weekend. What do you say?

definitions.definition: used to agree to somebody’s suggestion because you do not want to argue
a particular person must be obeyed
examples: Sarah wanted the kitchen painted green, and what she says, goes.

definitions.definition: when everything is considered
examples: I know you're upset, but when all's said and done it isn't exactly a disaster.

definitions.definition: used to say that nobody knows the answer to a question
examples: Who can say what will happen next year?

definitions.definition: used to disagree with a statement or an opinion
examples: Who says I can't do it?

definitions.definition: used to say that something might happen or might have happened in a particular way, because nobody really knows
examples: Who's to say we would not have succeeded if we'd had more time?

definitions.definition: I agree with you completely
examples: ‘He's in a bad mood today.’ ‘You can say that again!’

definitions.definition: used to say that you think the offer you are making is reasonable or generous
examples: Look, I'll give you £100 for it. You can't say fairer than that.

definitions.definition: used to express surprise
examples: ‘They left without us.’ ‘You don't say!’(= I'm not surprised)

definitions.definition: used to agree with somebody when they say something about themselves that you would not have been rude enough to say yourself
examples: ‘I know I'm not the world's greatest cook.’ ‘You said it!’

definitions.definition: used to agree with somebody’s suggestion

origin: Old Englishsecgan, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchzeggenand Germansagen.

definitions.definition: used in polite offers or invitations
examples: Would you likea sandwich?
Would you have dinner with me on Friday?

definitions.definition: used to say what you like, love, hate, etc.
examples: would like, love, hate, prefer, etc. somethingI'd love a coffee.
would like, etc. to do somethingI'd be only too glad to help.
would like, etc. somebody to do somethingI'd hate you to think I was criticizing you.
would rather do somethingI'd rather come with you.
would rather somebody did somethingI'd rather you came with us.

definitions.definition: used as the past form ofwillwhen reporting what somebody has said or thought
examples: He said he would be here at eight o'clock(= His words were: ‘I will be there at eight o'clock.’).
She asked if I would help.
They told me that they probably wouldn't come.

definitions.definition: used for talking about the result of an event that you imagine
examples: She'd look better with shorter hair.
If you went to see him, he would be delighted.
Hurry up! It would be a shame to miss the beginning of the play.
She'd be a fool to accept it(= if she accepted).

definitions.definition: used for describing a possible action or event that did not in fact happen, because something else did not happen first
examples: If I had seen the advertisement in time, I would have applied for the job.
They would never have met if she hadn't gone to Emma's party.

definitions.definition: used for saying why somebody does something
examples: so that somebody/something would do somethingShe burned the letters so that her husband would never read them.
in order that somebody/something would do somethingHe worked hard in order that he would pass all his exams.

definitions.definition: used for saying what you want to happen
examples: I wish that you'd be quiet for a minute.

definitions.definition: used to show that somebody/something was not willing or refused to do something
examples: She wouldn't change it, even though she knew it was wrong.
My car wouldn't start this morning.

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody politely to do something
examples: Would you mindleaving us alone for a few minutes?
Would you open the door for me, please?

definitions.definition: used to give opinions that you are not certain about
examples: I would imagine the job will take about two days.
I'd say he was about fifty.

definitions.definition: used to give advice
examples: I wouldn'thave any more to drink,if I were you.

definitions.definition: used for talking about things that often happened in the past
examples: When my parents were away, my grandmother would take care of me.
He'd always be the first to offer to help.

definitions.definition: used for talking about behaviour that you think is typical
examples: ‘She said it was your fault.’ ‘Well,she would say that,wouldn't she?She's never liked me.’

definitions.definition: used to express a strong wish
examples: Would that he had lived to see it.

origin: Old Englishwolde, past ofwyllan, of Germanic origin.

definitions.definition: the process of doing something in order to make something happen or to deal with a situation
examples: The time has come for action if these beautiful animals are to survive.
to threaten/demand/call for action
Firefighterstook actionimmediately to stop the blaze spreading.
Divers were close at hand, ready togo into actionif the stunt went wrong.
What is the bestcourse of actionin the circumstances?
She began to explain herplan of actionto the group.
The men facedisciplinary action.

definitions.definition: a thing that somebody does
examples: Each of us must take responsibility for our own actions.

definitions.definition: things that workers do, especially stopping work, to protest to their employers about something
examples: Workers voted in favour ofstrike action.
They called off their action at 7.40 a.m. after the meeting was arranged.
Unions said the action could be suspended early on Friday if talks between the two sides resumed.
The government began taking a hard line against labour actions.

definitions.definition: a legal process to stop a person or company from doing something, or to make them pay for a mistake, etc.
examples: A libel action is being brought against the magazine that published the article.
action against somebody/somethingHe is consideringtaking legal actionagainst the hospital.

definitions.definition: fighting in a battle or war
examples: The possibility oftaking military actionhas not been ruled out.
I never saw action during the war.
in actionsoldiers killed in action

definitions.definition: the events in a story, play, etc.
examples: The action takes place in France.

definitions.definition: exciting events
examples: I like films with plenty of action.
New York is where the action is.
If you want to know where all the action is around here, just ask Jo.

definitions.definition: the effect that something such as a chemical has on something
examples: Vitamin D can be made in the body by the action of sunlight on the skin.
The action of the rushing water cleans the gully.

definitions.definition: the way a part of the body moves or functions
examples: a study of the action of the liver

definitions.definition: themechanicalparts of a piano, gun, clock, etc. or the way the parts move
what a person actually does means more than what they say they will do
if somebody/something isin action, they are doing the activity or work that is typical for them
examples: Just press the button to see your favourite character in action.
I've yet to see all the players in action.
I have not yet seen the machines in action.

definitions.definition: if you put an idea or a planinto action, you start making it happen or work
examples: The new plan for traffic control is being put into action on an experimental basis.
We need to put these ideas into action.

definitions.definition: not able to work or be used because of injury or damage
examples: Jon will be out of action for weeks with a broken leg.
The photocopier is out of action today.
She is out of action following an ankle injury.

definitions.definition: a share or role in an interesting or exciting activity, especially one that makes money
examples: Foreign firms will all want a piece of the action if the new airport goes ahead.

definitions.definition: to suddenly start working or doing something
examples: ‘Let's go!’ he said, springing into action.
The town springs to life(= becomes busy)during the carnival.

definitions.definition: to start doing something quickly and with a lot of energy
examples: The ambulance crew swung into action to resuscitate the patient.
The rescue operation swung into action immediately.
The emergency services swung into action as soon as the disaster was reported.

definitions.definition: to act in order to avoid danger or an unpleasant situation
examples: The pilot had to take evasive action to avoid a mid-air collision.

origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinactio(n-), fromagere‘do, act’.

definitions.definition: the amount of money that somebody asks for goods and services
examples: admission charges
charge for somethingWe have to make a small charge for refreshments.
Delivery isfree of charge.

definitions.definition: acharge account
examples: Would you like to put that on your charge?
‘Are you paying cash?’ ‘No, it'll be a charge.’

definitions.definition: a position of having control over somebody/something; responsibility for somebody/something
examples: She has charge of the day-to-day running of the business.
Hetook charge ofthe farm after his father's death.
in charge of somebody/somethingThey left the au pair in charge of the children for a week.
in somebody's chargeI'm leaving the school in your charge.

definitions.definition: a person that you have responsibility for and care for
an official claim made by the police that somebody has committed a crime
examples: criminal charges
a murder/an assault charge
charge of somethinga charge of theft/rape/attempted murder
He will be sent back to Englandto face a charge of(= to be on trial for)armed robbery.
Both mendeny the charges.
charge against somebody/somethingThey decided todrop the chargesagainst the newspaper and settle out of court.
without chargeAfter being questioned by the police, she was released without charge.

definitions.definition: a statement accusing somebody of doing something wrong or bad
examples: She rejected the charge that the story was untrue.
Be careful you don'tleave yourself open to chargesof political bias.

definitions.definition: the amount of electricity that is put into a battery or carried by a substance
examples: apositive/negative charge

definitions.definition: the act of putting electricity into a battery; the electricity in a battery
examples: on chargeHe put his phone on charge.
My laptop had run out of charge.

definitions.definition: a sudden rush or violent attack, for example by soldiers, wild animals or players in some sports
examples: He led the charge down the field.

definitions.definition: the amount ofexplosiveneeded to fire a gun or make an explosion
the power to cause strong feelings
examples: the emotional charge of the piano piece
a film in which every scene carries an emotional charge

definitions.definition: a task or duty
examples: His charge was to obtain specific information.

definitions.definition: to accuse somebody formally of a crime so that there can be a trial in court
examples: Police have brought a charge of dangerous driving against the man.

She laid charges against the firm for not complying with the regulations.

definitions.definition: to get a strong feeling of excitement or pleasure from something
examples: I get a real charge out of working hard and seeing good results.

definitions.definition: to be the first to make the effort to do something new
examples: Denmark, Norway and Sweden are united in leading the charge to a cash-free economy.

origin: Middle English (in the general senses ‘to load’ and ‘a load’), from Old Frenchcharger(verb),charge(noun), from late Latincarricare,carcare‘to load’, from Latincarrus‘wheeled vehicle’.

word: in an attempt to do sth

definitions.definition: an act of trying to do something, especially something difficult, often with no success
examples: I passed my driving testat the first attempt.
attempt to do somethingTheymade no attemptto escape.
The launch will go ahead despite the failure of previous attempts to create an online business.
Why haverepeated attemptsto find a permanent solution to the problem failed?
They were frustrated in their attempts to get an explanation.
He has failed in three previous attempts to win the world title.
in an attempt to do somethingTwo factories were closed in an attempt to cut costs.
attempt at somethingThe couple made an unsuccessful attempt at a compromise.
attempt at doing somethingThey were praised for making an attempt at solving the problem.

definitions.definition: an act of trying to kill somebody
examples: attempt on somebody/sb’s lifeSomeone hasmade an attempt onthe president'slife.
anassassination attempt
a murder/suicide attempt

definitions.definition: an effort to do better than something, such as a very good performance in sport
examples: his attempt on the world land speed record
a world-record attempt

definitions.definition: a thing that you produce as a result of trying to make or achieve something
examples: He was clearly pleased with his first attempt at a cake.

origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchattempter, from Latinattemptare, fromad-‘to’ +temptare‘to tempt’.

definitions.definition: the activity of making, buying, selling or supplying goods or services for money
examples: It's been a pleasure todo business withyou.
There will be some changes to the way weconduct business.
in businessShe has set up in business as a hairdresser.

the music/entertainment/movie business
the insurance/banking business
the food/restaurant/hotel business

in the… businessShe works in the software business.

business owners/leaders
abusiness partner
thebusiness community
business opportunities/interests/ventures
the business cycle/sector/world

definitions.definition: work that is part of your job
examples: Is the trip to Rome for business or pleasure?
a business trip/meeting/lunch
business travel/travellers
on businessHe's away on business.

definitions.definition: the amount of work done by a company, etc.; the rate or quality of this work
examples: Business is booming.

How's business?

to begood for business

definitions.definition: a commercial organization such as a company, shop or factory
examples: an organization that representssmall businesses

asuccessful/profitable business
a retail/an online business
torun/start a business
togrow/expand/build a business
