definitions.definition: something that a particular person or organization is responsible for
examples: It is the business of the police to protect the community.
I shallmake it my businessto find out who is responsible.
My private life isnone of your business(= not something that you need to know about).

Keep your nose out of my business!

definitions.definition: important matters that need to be dealt with or discussed
examples: He has someunfinished businessto deal with.
the main business of the meeting
business of doing somethingThey should leave the business of making policy to the experts.

definitions.definition: a matter, an event or a situation
examples: That plane crash was a terrible business.

definitions.definition: the fact of a person or people buying goods or services at a shop or business
examples: We're grateful for your business.

definitions.definition: the things that are discussed at the end of an official meeting that do not appear on the agenda
examples: I think we've finished item four. Now is there any other business?

definitions.definition: to be very good
to have everything that you need in order to be able to start something immediately
examples: All we need is a car and we'll be in business.

definitions.definition: to regard a particular activity as an essential part of what you do
examples: Restaurant owners everywhere are in the business of building relationships.

definitions.definition: a way of saying that things will continue as normal despite a difficult situation
examples: It's business as usual at the factory, even while investigators sift through the bomb wreckage.

definitions.definition: a way of saying that financial and commercial matters are the important things to consider and you should not be influenced by friendship, etc.
to start dealing with the matter that needs to be dealt with, or doing the work that needs to be done
examples: Let’s get down to business right away—we’ll stop for coffee later.
OK, let's get down to business.

definitions.definition: to do the things that you normally do
examples: streets filled with people going about their daily business
market traders going about their daily business

definitions.definition: to have no right to do something
examples: You have no business being here.

definitions.definition: very much, very fast, very well, etc.
examples: I've been working like nobody's business to get it finished in time.

definitions.definition: to be serious in your intentions
examples: He has the look of a man who means business.

examples: ‘What are you reading?’ ‘Mind your own business!’

examples: I'm not in the business of getting other people to do my work for me.

definitions.definition: having stopped operating as a business because there is no more money or work available
examples: The new regulations willputmany small businessesout of business.

definitions.definition: to look for customers, passengers, etc. in order to do business
examples: taxis plying for hire outside the theatre

origin: Old Englishbisignis‘anxiety’ (seebusy,-ness); the sense ‘state of being busy’ was used from Middle English down to the 18th cent., but is now differentiated asbusyness. The use ‘appointed task’ dates from late Middle English, and from it all the other current senses have developed.

definitions.definition: progress or a development in a particular activity or area of understanding
examples: We live in an age of rapidtechnological advance.
advance in somethingRecentadvances in technologyhave made the procedure safe.
recent advances in medical science
major advances in the field of physics
advance on somethingan advance on the existing techniques
advance towards somethingan advance towards a better understanding of God

definitions.definition: the forward movement of a group of people, especially armed forces
examples: We feared that an advance on the capital would soon follow.
enemy advances

definitions.definition: money paid for work before it has been done or money paid earlier than expected
examples: They offered an advance of £5 000 after the signing of the contract.

advance on somethingShe asked for an advance on her salary.

definitions.definition: attempts to start a sexual relationship with somebody
examples: He hadmade advancesto one of his students.

definitions.definition: an increase in the price or value of something
examples: Share prices showed significant advances.

definitions.definition: before the time that is expected; before something happens
examples: aweek/month/year in advance
The rent is due one month in advance.
Thanks in advancefor your help.

It would have helped to know in advance that you were coming.
(formal)The fee ispayable in advance.
(formal)People were evacuated from the coastal regions in advance of the hurricane.

definitions.definition: more developed than somebody/something else
examples: Galileo's ideas were well in advance of the age in which he lived.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchavance(noun),avancer(verb), from late Latinabante‘in front’, fromab‘from’ +ante‘before’. The initiala-was mistakenly assimilated toad-in the 16th cent.

definitions.definition: a word that joins words, phrases or sentences, for example ‘and’, ‘but’, ‘or’ or ‘because’
a combination of events, etc., that causes a particular result
examples: The conjunction of low inflation and low unemployment came as a very pleasant surprise.

definitions.definition: the fact of stars, planets, etc. passing close together, as seen from the earth

definitions.definition: together with somebody/something
examples: The police are working in conjunction with tax officers on the investigation.

origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinconjunctio(n-), from the verbconjungere, fromcon-‘together’ +jungere‘to join’.

definitions.definition: the fourth month of the year, between March and May
examples: in AprilShe was born in April.

(North American English)The meeting is on April fifth.

last April’s election
April showers(= light rain that falls in the spring)
an April wedding

definitions.definition: a first name for girls

origin: Old English, from LatinAprilis.

definitions.definition: the possibility of something happening that will injure, harm or kill somebody, or damage or destroy something
examples: Danger! Keep Out!

in dangerChildren's lives are in danger every time they cross this road.
in grave/mortal danger

in danger of somethingspecies in imminent danger of extinction
out of dangerDoctors said she is now out of danger(= not likely to die).

definitions.definition: the possibility of something bad or unpleasant happening
examples: danger of somethingThere is no danger of a bush fire now.

danger that…There is a danger that the political disorder of the past will return.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that may cause damage, or harm somebody
examples: There are many hidden dangers for small children in the home.

definitions.definition: to be so ill that you may die; to no longer be very ill
examples: He was admitted to hospital in critical condition, but is now off the danger list.

origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘jurisdiction or power’, specifically ‘power to harm’): from Old Frenchdangier, based on Latindominus‘lord’.

definitions.definition: to have a desire or a wish for something/somebody
examples: want somebody/somethingDo you want some more tea?
towant children/kids

If you want more information, visit the website.

want something for somethingWhat do you want for Christmas?

want somebody/something as somethingThe party wants her as leader.
want to do somethingWhat do you want to do tomorrow?

want somebody/something doing somethingI don't want you coming home so late.
want somebody/something + adj.Do you want your coffee black or white?

want somebody/something doneThey want him arrested.

definitions.definition: to need something
examples: want somethingWe'll want more furniture for the new office.

want doing somethingThe plants want watering daily.
want to be somethingThe cake mixture wants to be smooth and light.
want to be/have something doneThe plants want to be watered daily.

definitions.definition: to need somebody to be present in the place or for the purpose mentioned
examples: She's wanted immediately in the director's office.

definitions.definition: used to give advice to somebody, meaning ‘should’ or ‘ought to’
examples: If possible, you want to avoid alcohol.

definitions.definition: to feel sexual desire for somebody
examples: ‘I want you so much,’ she whispered in his ear.

examples: He doesn't want courage.

definitions.definition: to have or want to have the advantages of two different situations or ways of behaving that are impossible to combine
examples: You can't have it both ways. If you can afford to go out all the time, you can afford to pay off some of your debts.

examples: I offered to pay but he was having none of it.

definitions.definition: to refuse to deal with somebody; to refuse to accept or consider something
examples: We in this party will have no truck with illegal organizations.

definitions.definition: to take no interest in something because you do not care about it or it is too much trouble
examples: I've tried to ask her advice, but she doesn't want to know(= about my problems).

definitions.definition: to want to be free of somebody/something that has been annoying you or that you do not want
examples: Are you trying to say you want rid of me?

definitions.definition: if you never waste anything, especially food or money, you will always have it when you need it
used to ask somebody in a rude or angry way why they are there or what they want you to do

origin: Middle English: the noun from Old Norsevant, neuter ofvanr‘lacking’; the verb from Old Norsevanta‘be lacking’. The original notion of “lack” was early extended to “need” and from this developed the sense ‘desire’.

definitions.definition: a group of students who are taught together

examples: I have a history class at 9 o'clock.

in classShe works hard in class(= during the class).

examples: Are you still doing your French evening class?

the freshman/sophomore/junior/senior class

examples: theworking/middle/upper class

examples: He came first in his class at every event.

definitions.definition: an attractive quality or a high level of skill that is impressive

topics being discussed at the breakfast tables of the chattering classes

definitions.definition: feeling sad and ashamed about something that has been done
examples: sorry about somethingWe're very sorry about the damage to your car.

sorry for somethingShe is obviously deeply sorry for what she has done.

sorry for doing somethingHe says he's really sorry for taking the car without asking.
sorry (that)…She was sorry that she'd lost her temper.
I am so sorry that this happened.
If yousay you're sorrywe'll forgive you.

definitions.definition: feeling sad and showing sympathy
examples: sorry (that)…I'm sorry that your husband lost his job.
sorry to do somethingWe'resorry to hearthat your father's in hospital again.
I wassorry to seehim go.
sorry about somebody/somethingNo one is sorrier than I am about what happened.

I'm sorry about your mother. I do hope she'll soon be feeling better.
sorry for somethingI'm very sorry for your loss(= expressing sympathy when somebody has died).

definitions.definition: feeling disappointed about something and wishing you had done something different or had not done something
examples: sorry (that)…She was sorry that she'd lost contact with Mary.
I'll make you sorry you were ever born!
You'll be sorry if I catch you!
sorry to do somethingI was genuinely sorry to be leaving college.
She was almost sorry to stop work.

definitions.definition: very sad or bad, especially making you feelpityordisapproval
examples: The business is in asorry state.
They were asorry sightwhen they eventually got off the boat.

definitions.definition: to be sad or feel sympathy for somebody
examples: He decided to help Jan as he felt sorry for her.

definitions.definition: used to say that it is wiser to be too careful than to act too quickly and do something you may later wish you had not
examples: She decided to avoid travelling at night. Better safe than sorry.

definitions.definition: to feel unhappy; to be sad about yourself because you are suffering
examples: Stop feeling sorry for yourself and think about other people for a change.

examples: I'm sorry, I forgot.

definitions.definition: used for disagreeing with somebody or politely saying ‘no’
examples: I'm sorry, I don't agree.

definitions.definition: used for introducing bad news
examples: I'm sorry to have to tell you you've failed.

definitions.definition: used for saying that something is disappointing
examples: He didn't accept the job, I'm sorry to say.

origin: Old Englishsārig‘pained, distressed’, of West Germanic origin, from the base of the nounsore. The shortening of the root vowel has given the word an apparent connection with the unrelatedsorrow.

definitions.definition: a particular situation or a situation of a particular type
examples: in… casesIn some casespeople have had to wait several weeks for an appointment.
In most casesthe increases have been marginal.
In many casesit is the pressure of the job that causes managers to behave like this.
What should be donein such cases?
The substances causes nausea, sickness and,in extreme cases, death.
in cases of somethingThe company only dismisses its employees in cases of gross misconduct.
in the case of somethingIn the case of banks, the law can limit activities.
Sometimes compost isn't enough,in which caseartificial products may have to be used.
a case of somethingIt's aclassic case(= a very typical case)of bad planning.
This appears to be a case of mistaken identity.

definitions.definition: the true situation
examples: be the caseIf that is the case(= if the situation described is true), we need more staff.
it's the case that…It is simply not the case that prison conditions are improving.
It remains the case that not enough graduates are going into teaching.

definitions.definition: a situation that relates to a particular person or thing
examples: in somebody's caseI cannot make an exception in your case(= for you and not for others).
In your case, we are prepared to be lenient.
in this caseDon't underestimate the power of the pen, or in this case, the power of the keyboard.
In the present case, the facts are not disputed.
Every application will be decidedon a case-by-case basis(= each one will be considered separately).

definitions.definition: a matter that is being officially investigated, especially by the police
examples: They never solved the Jones murder case.
Police believe it is a simple case of theft.
Four officers areinvestigating the case.

definitions.definition: a question to be decided in court
examples: acourt case
acriminal/civil case
Thecase will be heardnext week.
The Supreme Court heard the case yesterday.
towin/lose a case
When does her case come before the court?

definitions.definition: a set of facts or arguments that support one side in a trial, a discussion, etc.
examples: Our lawyer didn't think wehad a case(= had enough good arguments to win in a court of law).
case for somethingthe case for the defence/prosecution
case against somebody/somethingThe case against her was very weak.
Let us considerthe case for and againstprivate education.
case for doing somethingThe reportmakes out astrongcase(= gives good arguments)for spending more money on hospitals.
He successfullyargued the casefor accepting the agreement.
case that…They try tomake the casethat this war is necessary.
You will each be given the change to state your case.

definitions.definition: a container or cover used to protect or store things; a container with its contents or the amount that it contains
examples: a jewellery case
The museum was full of stuffed animals in glass cases.
case of somethinga case(= 12 bottles)of champagne

definitions.definition: a container with flat sides and a handle, used for carrying clothes, etc. when you are travelling
examples: Let me carry your case for you.

definitions.definition: the fact of somebody having a disease or an injury; a person who has a disease or an injury
examples: a severe case of food poisoning
Over 130 000casesof cholerawere reportedin 2016.
The most serious cases were treated at the scene of the accident.

definitions.definition: a person who needs, or is thought to need, special treatment or attention
examples: He's a hopeless case.
After the exams, the teacher gave all borderline cases a spoken test.

definitions.definition: the form of a noun, an adjective or a pronoun in some languages, that shows its relationship to another word
examples: the nominative/accusative/genitive case
Latin nouns have case, number and gender.
In Polish the verb ‘to be’ takes the instrumental case.

definitions.definition: used to say that one of two or more possibilities is true, but which one is true depends on the circumstances
examples: There may be an announcement about this tomorrow—or not, as the case may be.

definitions.definition: to criticize somebody all the time
examples: She's always on my case about cleaning my room.

definitions.definition: to be dealing with a particular matter, especially a criminal investigation
examples: We have two agents on the case.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: a situation in business, politics, etc. where there is a lot of competition and people are willing to harm each other in order to succeed
examples: I'm afraid in this line of work it's a case of dog eat dog.
We're operating in a dog-eat-dog world.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to stop criticizing you
whatever happens or may have happened
examples: There's no point complaining now—we're leaving tomorrow in any case.

definitions.definition: because of the possibility of something happening
examples: You'd better take the keys in case I'm out.
You probably won't need to call—but take my number, just in case.
In case(= if it is true that)you’re wondering why Jo’s here—let me explain…

definitions.definition: if something happens
examples: In case of fire, ring the alarm bell.

definitions.definition: if that happens or has happened; if that is the situation
examples: ‘I’ve made up my mind.’ ‘In that case, there’s no point discussing it.’

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not need to say any more about something because you think that you have proved your point
used by lawyers in court to say that they have finished presenting their case
examples: The prosecution rests its case.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 6 andnounsenses 9 to 11 Middle English: from Old Frenchcas, from Latincasus‘fall’, related tocadere‘to fall’; in sense (11) directly from Latin, translating Greekptōsis, literally ‘fall’.nounsenses 7 to 8 late Middle English: from Old Frenchcasse,chasse(moderncaisse‘trunk, chest’,châsse‘reliquary, frame’), from Latincapsa, related tocapere‘to hold’.

definitions.definition: to have a particular idea or opinion about something/somebody; to believe something
examples: think (that)…Do youreally think(that) he'll win?
I thought I heard a scream.
I didn't think people were allowed to park here.
Am I right in thinkingthat you used to live here?
I think this is their house, but I'm not sure.
He ought to resign, I think.
That's my opinion, but you mightthink otherwise(= have a different opinion).
We'll need about 20 chairs,I should think.
Ican't help thinking(that) things could have been different.
it is thought that…It was once thought that the sun travelled around the earth.
think somethingWell, I like it. What do you think?
think something about somethingWhat did you think about the idea?
think something of somethingWhat did you think of the film?
think so‘Is he any good?’ ‘I don't think so.’
‘Will we make it in time?’ ‘I think so.’
think somebody/something + adj.Ithink ithighly unlikely that I'll get the job.
She thought him kind and generous.
It was better than I thought possible.
think somebody/something + nounI thought it a good idea to go with him.
be thought to be somebody/somethingHe's thought to be one of the richest men in Europe.
think of somebody/something as somebody/somethingI think of this place as my home.
She is thought of as a possible director.

definitions.definition: to use your mind to consider something, to form connected ideas, to try to solve problems, etc.
examples: Are animals able to think?
Let me think(= give me time before I answer).
Stop and think for a moment before making any decisions.
I'm sorry,I wasn't thinking(= said when you have upset or offended somebody accidentally).
think about somethingI can't tell you now—I'll have tothink about it.
The government needs tothink carefullyabout the issues raised.
All he ever thinks about is money.
think what, how, etc…He was trying to think what to do.

definitions.definition: to have ideas, words or images in your mind
examples: think somethingYou're very quiet. What are you thinking?
think what, how, etc…I was just thinking what a long way it is.
+ speech‘I must be crazy,’ she thought.
‘What can I do now?’ he thought frantically.

definitions.definition: to form an idea of something; to imagine something
examples: think where, how, etc…We couldn't think where you'd gone.
Just think how nice it would be to see them again.
I hate to think what would have happened if we hadn't arrived.
think (that)…Ilike to think(that) he would help if I needed it.
I can’t think (that) he would be so stupid.
think (something)If I'm home late, my mother always thinks the worst.
Just think—we'll be lying on the beach this time tomorrow.
Try tothink yourself intothe role.

definitions.definition: to expect something
examples: think (that)…I never thought (that) I’d see her again.
The job took longer than we thought.
You'd think she'd have been grateful for my help(= but she wasn't).
I never thought you would carry out your threat.
think to do something(formal)Who would have thought to find you here?

definitions.definition: to think in a particular way or on a particular subject
examples: + adj.Let's think positive.
You need tothink big(= aim to achieve a lot).
think somethingIf you want to make money, you've got to think money.

definitions.definition: used in questions to show that you are angry or surprised
examples: What do you think you're doing?

definitions.definition: used to make something you say sound less definite or more polite
examples: think (that)…I thought we could go out tonight.
Twenty guests are enough, I would have thought.
Do you thinkyou could open the window?
think so‘You've made a mistake.’ ‘I don't think so.’

definitions.definition: to intend something; to have a plan about something
examples: I think I'll go for a swim.
I'm thinking in terms of about 70 guests at the wedding.

definitions.definition: to remember something; to have something come into your mind
examples: think to do somethingI didn't think(= it did not occur to me)to tell her.
think where, what, etc…I can't think where I put the keys.

definitions.definition: to behave in a very confident way that annoys other people, for example by telling them what to do
examples: She was acting as if she owned the place.

definitions.definition: used when you suddenly remember something or realize that it might be important
examples: Come to think of it, he did mention seeing you.

definitions.definition: used to say that you and another person must both be very clever because you have had the same idea or agree about something
used to say very strongly that you do not agree with something, or that something is not possible
examples: Me? Fail? I don't think so.

definitions.definition: used to draw attention to a fact that is not obvious or has not previously been mentioned
examples: It was a difficult situation, when you think about it.

definitions.definition: used to say that you hope or believe that something is true
examples: I like to think I'm broad-minded.
I’d like to think that you were helping me because you wanted to, not because you felt you had to.

definitions.definition: that is what I expected or suspected
examples: ‘He said he'd forgotten.’ ‘I thought as much.’

definitions.definition: used when you are thinking or trying to remember something
examples: Now let me see—where did he say he lived?

definitions.definition: to consider it right or acceptable to do something; to decide or choose to do something
examples: You must do as you think fit(= but I don't agree with your decision).
The newspaper did not see fit to publish my letter(= and I criticize it for that).

definitions.definition: to say or think bad things about somebody
examples: Don't speak ill of the dead.

definitions.definition: to consider a situation again and perhaps change your idea or intention
examples: You really should think again about that.

definitions.definition: to say what your thoughts are as you have them
to have a higher opinion of somebody
examples: She has behaved appallingly—I must say I thought better of her.

definitions.definition: to decide not to do something after thinking further about it
examples: Rosie was about to protest but thought better of it.

definitions.definition: to form your own opinions and make decisions without depending on others
to say or think bad things about somebody
examples: I wouldn't want them to think ill of me.

definitions.definition: to consider an activity to be normal and not particularly unusual or difficult
examples: She thinks nothing of walking thirty miles a day.

definitions.definition: used as a polite response when somebody has said sorry to you or thanked you
to be able to think and react to things very quickly and effectively without any preparation
to think about something, or how to do something, in a way that is new, different or shows imagination
to think in a clear or logical way
examples: The drugs were affecting her and she couldn't think straight.

definitions.definition: to think carefully before deciding to do something
examples: You should think twice about employing someone you've never met.

definitions.definition: to have a very good, poor, etc. opinion of somebody/something
examples: He thinks the world of his daughter.
I don't think much of her idea.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are surprised or shocked by something
examples: To think that my mother wrote all those books and I never knew!

origin: Old Englishthencan, of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germandenken.

definitions.definition: affecting all or most people, places or things
examples: books ofgeneral interest(= of interest to most people)
The general feeling was one of relief.
The general opinion is that the conference was a success.
There is general agreement on that point.
The general trend has been a slow decline in output.
The bad weather has been fairly general(= has affected most areas).

definitions.definition: normal; usual
examples: There is one exception to thisgeneral principle.
As a general rule(= usually)he did what he could to be helpful.
This opinion is common among thegeneral population(= ordinary people).

definitions.definition: including the most important aspects of something; not exact or detailed
examples: I check the bookings to get ageneral ideaof what activities to plan.
I know how it worksin general terms.
They gave a general description of the man.
Carton here uses the word ‘business’ in a very general sense.

definitions.definition: approximately, but not exactly, the direction/area mentioned
examples: They fired in the general direction of the enemy.

definitions.definition: not limited to a particular subject, use or activity
examples: a general hospital
general education
We shall keep the discussion fairly general.

definitions.definition: not limited to one part or aspect of a person or thing
examples: a general anaesthetic
The building was in a general state of disrepair.

definitions.definition: highest in rank; chief
examples: the general manager
the Inspector General of Police

definitions.definition: usually; mainly
examples: In general, Japanese cars are very reliable and breakdowns are rare.

definitions.definition: as a whole
examples: This is a crucial year for your relationships in general and your love life in particular.

origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latingeneralis, fromgenus,gener-‘class, race, kind’. The noun primarily denotes a person having overall authority: the sense ‘army commander’ is an abbreviation ofcaptain general, from Frenchcapitaine général‘commander-in-chief’.

definitions.definition: a thing that you do to help somebody
examples: Could youdo me a favourand pick up Sam from school today?
Can I ask a favour?
I would never ask for any favours from her.
I'll ask Steve to take it. Heowes me a favour.
Thanks for helping me out. I'llreturn the favour(= help you because you have helped me)some time.
as a favour (to somebody)I'm going as a favour to Ann, not because I want to.
Do yourself a favour(= help yourself)and wear a helmet on the bike.

definitions.definition: approval or support for somebody/something
examples: Artists sought the favour of wealthy patrons.
favour with somebodyThe suggestion to close the road hasfound favourwith(= been supported by)local people.
to gain/win/lose favour with somebody
The programme has lost favour with viewers recently.
favour among somebodyThe policy is gaining favour among voters.
in favour (with somebody)She's not in favour with(= supported or liked by)the media just now.
It seems Tim is back in favour with the boss(= the boss likes him again).
out of favour (with somebody)Reality TV has begun tofall out of favourwith viewers.
an athlete whofell from favourafter a drugs scandal
(formal)The governmentlooks with favour upon(= approves of)the report's recommendations.

definitions.definition: treatment that is generous to one person or group in a way that seems unfair to others
examples: As an examiner, she showed no favour to any candidate.

definitions.definition: a small gift given to children at a party
agreement to have sex with somebody
examples: demands for sexual favours

definitions.definition: you are likely to succeed because the conditions are good and you have an advantage
to try to get somebody to like or support you by praising or helping them a lot
examples: He’s always trying to curry favour with the boss.
She tried to curry favour with the teachers.

definitions.definition: used in reply to a question that you think is silly
examples: ‘Do you think they'll win?’ ‘Do me a favour! They haven't got a single decent player.’

definitions.definition: to do something that is not helpful to somebody or that gives a bad impression of them
examples: You're not doing yourself any favours, working for nothing.
The orchestra did Beethoven no favours.

definitions.definition: if you arein favourof somebody/something, you support and agree with them/it
examples: He argued in favour of a strike.
There were 247 votes in favour (of the motion) and 152 against.
I'mall in favour of(= completely support)equal pay for equal work.
Most of the ‘don't knows’ in the opinion pollscame down in favour of(= eventually chose to support)the Democrats.

definitions.definition: in exchange for another thing (because the other thing is better or you want it more)
examples: He abandoned teaching in favour of a career as a musician.
Early in his musical career he abandoned blues in favour of jazz.

definitions.definition: if something isin somebody’s favour, it gives them an advantage or helps them
examples: The exchange rate is in our favour at the moment.
She was willing to bend the rules in Mary's favour.
The golf tournament went in the Americans' favour.

definitions.definition: a decision or judgement that isin somebody’s favourbenefits that person or says that they were right
examples: The court decided in Ms Smith’s favour and she received compensation for unfair dismissal.
The High Court found in favour of the plaintiffs.

definitions.definition: in a fair way
examples: They undertook to make their judgement without fear or favour.

origin: Middle English (in the noun sense ‘liking, preference’): via Old French from Latinfavor, fromfavere‘show kindness to’ (related tofovere‘cherish’).

definitions.definition: all the events that happened in the past
examples: in historyThe battle was a turning point inhuman history.
one of the worst disasters inrecent history
throughout historyMany people throughout history have dreamt of a world without war.
a people with no sense of history
The area was inhabited long before the dawn ofrecorded history(= before people wrote about events).
These events changed thecourse of history.
This was her attempt torewrite historywith herself in the role of heroine.

definitions.definition: the past events connected with the development of a particular place, subject, etc.
examples: the history of science/music/philosophy
She is an expert in the history of the United States.
Thelocal historyof the area is fascinating.
The museumtraces the historyof chocolate.

definitions.definition: the study of past events, especially as a subject at school or university
examples: a history professor/teacher
a history lesson/textbook
a degree in History
cultural/social/economic/political history
modern/ancient history
She's studyingart history.
She studieshistory of art.

definitions.definition: a written or spoken account of real past events
examples: She's writing a new history of Europe.
She went on to catalogue a long history of disasters.
theoral historiesof ordinary people

definitions.definition: the set of facts that are known about somebody/something's past
examples: I know nothing about his personal history.
The group has quite a history.
The doctor will need some details of yourmedical history.

definitions.definition: a record of something happening frequently in the past life of a person, family or place
examples: Hehas a historyof violent crime.
She has along historyof drug addiction.
There is a history of heart disease in my family.

definitions.definition: a record kept by aweb browserof the web pages and other files it has been used to access
examples: Clearing your browser history prevents websites from following and tracking your actions.

definitions.definition: a relationship between two people, groups, companies, etc. that started in the past and continued for a period of time, especially one involving bad or complicated feelings
examples: Sure there's history between Anna Mae and me, like you said.

definitions.definition: a historical play
examples: Shakespeare's comedies, histories and tragedies

definitions.definition: to be dead or no longer important
examples: Another mistake like that and you're history.
We won't talk about that—that's history.
That'spast historynow.
They had an affair once, but that's past history now.

definitions.definition: the record of great achievements in history
examples: She has earned her place in the history books.

definitions.definition: used to say that things often happen later in the same way as before
examples: Years later family history repeated itself with Eve’s daughters.

definitions.definition: to be or do something so important that it will be recorded in history
examples: a discovery that made medical history

definitions.definition: used when you are telling a story to say that you do not need to tell the end of it, because everyone knows it already

origin: late Middle English (also as a verb): via Latin from Greekhistoria‘finding out, narrative, history’, fromhistōr‘learned, wise man’, from an Indo-European root shared bywit‘have knowledge’.

definitions.definition: a method, style or manner of doing something
examples: I prefer to do things the easy way.
I love him and I think he feelsthe same way.
way to do somethingI'm sure we canfind a wayto make this work.
Using the subway isthe only way toavoid the traffic.
(informal, disapproving)That's no wayto speak to your mother!
way of doing somethingI'm not happy with this way of working.
way (that)…It's not what you say—it's the way that you say it.
Ihate the wayshe always criticizes me.
I told you we should have done itmy way!
in the… wayShe dressesin the same wayas her sister.
Infectious diseases can be acquired in several ways.
In this way, we can track the storms as they move across the ocean.
in the way (that)…It works in the way you'd expect.
New technology has radically changedthe way in whichthis work is done.
in a way that…She writes in a way that is easy to understand.
I generally get what I wantone way or another(= by some means).

definitions.definition: a particular manner or style of behaviour
examples: in a… wayThey grinned at her in a friendly way.
It was not his wayto admit that he had made a mistake.
Don't worry if she seems quiet—it's just her way.
He was showing off,as is the waywith adolescent boys.

definitions.definition: the typical way of behaving and living of a particular group of people
examples: After ten years I'm used to the strange British ways.
She always said that the old ways were the best.

definitions.definition: a route or road that you take in order to reach a place
examples: toask somebody the way
Do you know the way?
way to…Can youtell me the wayto Leicester Square?
way from… to…thebest/quickest/shortest wayfrom A to B
We went thelong way round.

definitions.definition: the route along which somebody/something is moving; the route that somebody/something would take if there was nothing stopping them/it
examples: Get out of my way!I'm in a hurry.
Riot police with shields were blocking the demonstrators' way.
along the wayUnfortunately they ran into a snowstorm along the way.
+ adv./prep.We fought our way through the dense vegetation.
(figurative)We will eventually find a way out of the crisis.

definitions.definition: a road, path or street for travelling along
examples: There's a way across the fields.

definitions.definition: used in the names of streets
examples: 106 Headley Way

definitions.definition: a particular direction; in a particular direction
examples: Which way did they go?
We just missed a car comingthe other way.
Lookboth ways(= look left and right)before crossing the road.
Make sure that sign'sthe right way up.
The pipe could be moved a few incheseither way.
Kids were runningthis way and that(= in all directions).
They decided to split the money four ways(= between four different people).
(figurative)Which way(= for which party)are you going to vote?

definitions.definition: a means of going into or leaving a place, such as a door or gate
examples: theway in/out
They escaped out the back way.

definitions.definition: a distance or period of time between two points
examples: A little way up on the left is the Museum of Modern Art.
We had to goa long waybefore we found a place to eat.
September wasa long way off.
You cameall this wayto see us?
(figurative)The economy still hasa way to goto a full recovery.
(North American English, informal)We still havea ways to go.

definitions.definition: an area, a part of a country, etc.
examples: over/down… wayI think he lives somewhere over Greenwich way.
down my/our/your wayI'll stop by and see you next time I'm down your way.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something
examples: I have changed in every way.
It's been quite a day,one way and another(= for several reasons).

definitions.definition: a particular condition or state
examples: in a… wayThe economy's in a bad way.
I don't know how we're going to manage,the way things are.

definitions.definition: on the other side of the street, etc.
examples: Music blared from the open window of the house across the way.

definitions.definition: during the whole journey/period of time
examples: She didn't speak a word to me all the way back home.
the whole way to Arizona
This bus doesn't go all the way so you'll have to change.
I watched the movie the whole way through.

definitions.definition: completely; as much as it takes to achieve what you want
examples: I'm fighting him all the way.
You can feel that the audience is with her all the way.

definitions.definition: as you make progress with something
examples: You'll learn something along the way.

definitions.definition: used to say that things often happen in a particular way, especially when it is not convenient
however you choose to look at a situation
to behave or do things in a particular manner because it is part of your character
examples: It's not his fault he's so pompous—he was born that way.

definitions.definition: to be easy compared with what came before
examples: It's downhill all the way from here. We'll soon be finished.

definitions.definition: to become worse or less successful
examples: I started work as a journalist and it was downhill all the way for my health.
It's been all downhill for his career since then, with four defeats in five games.

definitions.definition: to have habits or opinions that you have had for a long time and that you do not want to change
examples: Grandma is so set in her ways.

definitions.definition: to have nearly achieved something and be going to achieve it soon
examples: She is well on the way to recovery.
He is well on the way to establishing himself among the top ten players in the world.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a comment or question that is not directly related to what you have been talking about
examples: By the way, I found that book you were looking for.
What's the time, by the way?
Oh by the way, if you see Jackie, tell her I'll call her this evening.

definitions.definition: by a route that includes the place mentioned
examples: The artist recently arrived in Paris from Bulgaria by way of Vienna.
She came to TV by way of drama school.

definitions.definition: as a form of something; for something; as a means of something
examples: He received £600 by way of compensation from the company.
She rolled her eyes by way of an answer and left.

definitions.definition: to start to live or behave in a different way from before
examples: He was in trouble with the police as a teenager but now he’s completely changed his ways.
Your father is unlikely to change his ways now.

definitions.definition: to gradually achieve something or move somewhere by being determined and using a lot of effort
examples: She clawed her way to the top of her profession.
Slowly, he clawed his way out from under the collapsed building.

definitions.definition: to remove things that are stopping the progress or movement of something
examples: The ruling could clear the way for extradition proceedings.

definitions.definition: to happen to you by chance, or when you were not expecting it
examples: He took the first job that came his way.
Have any interesting articles come your way recently?

definitions.definition: to have two opposite effects or results
used to say that it does not matter which one of two possibilities happens, is chosen or is true
examples: Was it his fault or not? Either way, an explanation is due.
We could meet today or tomorrow—I don't mind one way or the other.

definitions.definition: in all directions
examples: Her hair tumbled every which way.

definitions.definition: to move along carefully, for example when it is dark, by touching walls, objects, etc.
to be careful about how you do things, usually because you are in a situation that you are not familiar with
examples: She was new in the job, still feeling her way.

definitions.definition: to discover the right route (to a place)
examples: I hope you can find your way home.

definitions.definition: to come to a place or a situation by chance or without intending to
examples: He eventually found his way into acting.

definitions.definition: to become used to doing something/to lose the habit of doing something
examples: The women had got into the way of going up on the deck every evening.

definitions.definition: to prevent somebody from doing something; to prevent something from happening
examples: He wouldn't allow emotions to get in the way of him doing his job.

definitions.definition: to get or do what you want, especially when somebody has tried to stop you
examples: She always gets her own way in the end.

definitions.definition: to break or fall down
examples: The pillars gave way and a section of the roof collapsed.
Her numb leg gave way beneath her and she stumbled clumsily.

definitions.definition: to stop resisting somebody/something; to agree to do something that you do not want to do
examples: He refused to give way on any of the points.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to be or go first
examples: Give way to traffic already on the roundabout.

definitions.definition: to allow yourself to be very strongly affected by something, especially an emotion
examples: Flinging herself on the bed, she gave way to helpless misery.

definitions.definition: to be replaced by something

examples: He kissed her full sensual lips.

definitions.definition: made with plenty of cloth; fitting loosely
examples: a full skirt

definitions.definition: deep, strong and rich
examples: He draws a unique full sound from the instrument.
the full fruity flavour of the coffee

definitions.definition: not telling the truth; tending to make things seem better, worse or more important than they actually are
examples: ‘You are so full of it!’ she retorted furiously.

definitions.definition: very proud; thinking only of yourself
including the whole of something
examples: The address must be printed in full.

definitions.definition: to the greatest possible degree
examples: I've always believed in living life to the full.

origin: Old English, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvoland Germanvoll.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you are referring to one individual person, thing or type of thing and not others

definitions.definition: greater than usual; special
examples: We must payparticular attentionto this point.

definitions.definition: very definite about what you like and careful about what you choose

definitions.definition: especially or particularly
examples: He loves science fiction in particular.

definitions.definition: special or specific

origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchparticuler, from Latinparticularis‘concerning a small part’, fromparticula‘small part’.

definitions.definition: to believe or think that…

definitions.definition: your opinion that is the result of careful thought
examples: It is my considered opinion that you are wrong.

definitions.definition: to think that somebody/something is good, bad, etc.
examples: The boss has a very high opinion of her.

definitions.definition: a region or an area

fluoride levels of 0.2 parts per million

definitions.definition: a line on a person’s head where the hair is divided with acomb
to be better or more effective as a group than you would think just by looking at the individual members of the group
examples: The team is greater than the sum of its parts.

definitions.definition: most of something, especially a period of time; more than half of something

definitions.definition: you should avoid danger and not take unnecessary risks

definitions.definition: to accept something slightly unpleasant without complaining or being offended

case that…They try tomake the casethat this war is necessary.

definitions.definition: a container or cover used to protect or store things; a container with its contents or the amount that it contains
examples: a jewellery case

case of somethinga case(= 12 bottles)of champagne

definitions.definition: a container with flat sides and a handle, used for carrying clothes, etc. when you are travelling
examples: Let me carry your case for you.

definitions.definition: the fact of somebody having a disease or an injury; a person who has a disease or an injury
examples: a severe case of food poisoning

definitions.definition: a person who needs, or is thought to need, special treatment or attention
examples: He's a hopeless case.

definitions.definition: the form of a noun, an adjective or a pronoun in some languages, that shows its relationship to another word
examples: the nominative/accusative/genitive case

In Polish the verb ‘to be’ takes the instrumental case.

definitions.definition: used to say that one of two or more possibilities is true, but which one is true depends on the circumstances
examples: There may be an announcement about this tomorrow—or not, as the case may be.

definitions.definition: to criticize somebody all the time
examples: She's always on my case about cleaning my room.

definitions.definition: to be dealing with a particular matter, especially a criminal investigation
examples: We have two agents on the case.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: a situation in business, politics, etc. where there is a lot of competition and people are willing to harm each other in order to succeed
examples: I'm afraid in this line of work it's a case of dog eat dog.
We're operating in a dog-eat-dog world.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to stop criticizing you
whatever happens or may have happened
examples: There's no point complaining now—we're leaving tomorrow in any case.

definitions.definition: because of the possibility of something happening
examples: You'd better take the keys in case I'm out.
You probably won't need to call—but take my number, just in case.
In case(= if it is true that)you’re wondering why Jo’s here—let me explain…

definitions.definition: if something happens
examples: In case of fire, ring the alarm bell.

definitions.definition: if that happens or has happened; if that is the situation
examples: ‘I’ve made up my mind.’ ‘In that case, there’s no point discussing it.’

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not need to say any more about something because you think that you have proved your point
used by lawyers in court to say that they have finished presenting their case
examples: The prosecution rests its case.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 6 andnounsenses 9 to 11 Middle English: from Old Frenchcas, from Latincasus‘fall’, related tocadere‘to fall’; in sense (11) directly from Latin, translating Greekptōsis, literally ‘fall’.nounsenses 7 to 8 late Middle English: from Old Frenchcasse,chasse(moderncaisse‘trunk, chest’,châsse‘reliquary, frame’), from Latincapsa, related tocapere‘to hold’.

definitions.definition: a series of lessons or lectures on a particular subject
examples: aFrench/chemistry course

course on somethingSheteaches a courseon internet crime.
on a courseMy boss hasbeen on a coursethis week.

definitions.definition: a period of study at a college or university that leads to an exam or a qualification
examples: What degree course did you choose?
a two-year postgraduate course leading to a master’s degree

definitions.definition: a large area of land that is designed for playing golf on
examples: He set a new course record.

definitions.definition: an area of land or water where races are held
examples: She was overtaken on the last stretch of the course.

definitions.definition: any of the separate parts of a meal
examples: a four-course dinner

for… courseWe had chicken for our main course.

definitions.definition: a direction or route followed by a ship or an aircraft
examples: He radioed the pilot tochange course.
on/off courseThe plane was off course(= not going in the right direction).

definitions.definition: the general direction in which somebody’s ideas or actions are moving or in which things are developing
examples: The president appears likely tochange courseon some key issues.
The dollar fell sharply for two days, and thenreversed course.
Her careerfollowed asimilarcourseto her sister's.
Politicians are often obliged to steer a course between incompatible interests.

definitions.definition: a way of acting in or dealing with a particular situation
examples: There are various courses open to us.
What course of action would you recommend?

The wisest course would be to say nothing.

definitions.definition: the way something develops or should develop
examples: course of somethingThis was an event that changedthe course of history.

during the course of somethingShe repeatedly raised the issue during the course of the campaign.

definitions.definition: the direction a river moves in
examples: The path follows the course of the river.

definitions.definition: a series of medical treatments, tablets, etc.
examples: to prescribe a course of antibiotics

When taking antibiotics it is important to finish the course.

definitions.definition: a continuous layer ofbrick, stone or other building material in a wall
examples: A new damp-proof course could cost £1 000 or more.

definitions.definition: to be in a situation that is almost certain to cause an argument
examples: I was on a collision course with my boss over the sales figures.

definitions.definition: to be moving in a direction in which it is likely that you will crash into somebody/something
examples: A giant iceberg was on a collision course with the ship.

definitions.definition: to be just what you would expect to happen or expect somebody to do in a particular situation
examples: Starting early and working long hours is par for the course in this job.

definitions.definition: the act of matching people with suitable jobs or tasks
going through a particular process
examples: The new textbook is in course of preparation.

definitions.definition: during
examples: He's seen many changes in the course of his long life.
The company faces major challenges over the course of the next few years.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: at the right time and not before
examples: Your request will be dealt with in due course.

definitions.definition: as things usually happen
examples: In the normal course of things we would not treat her disappearance as suspicious.

definitions.definition: to allow natural processes to happen, without trying to stop or change them (used especially in the context of illness)
examples: The midwife must judge when to let nature take its course and when to intervene.
When the dog responded so badly to the treatment, we decided to let nature take its course.

definitions.definition: (as) the usual and correct thing to do
examples: We always check people's addresses as a matter of course.

definitions.definition: (to take/find) an acceptable course of action that avoids two extreme positions
used to emphasize that what you are saying is true or correct
examples: ‘Don't you like my mother?’ ‘Of course I do!’
‘Will you be there?’ ‘Course I will.’

definitions.definition: used as a polite way of giving somebody permission to do something
examples: ‘Can I come, too?’ ‘Course you can.’
‘Can I have one of those pens?’ ‘Of course—help yourself.’

definitions.definition: used as a polite way of agreeing with what somebody has just said
examples: ‘I did all I could to help.’ ‘Of course,’ he murmured gently.

definitions.definition: used to show that what you are saying is not surprising or is generally known or accepted
examples: Ben, of course, was the last to arrive.
Of course, there are other ways of doing this.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the fact that you are saying ‘no’
examples: ‘Are you going?’ ‘Of course not.’
‘Do you mind?’ ‘No, of course not.’

definitions.definition: likely to achieve or do something because you have already started to do it
examples: The American economy is on course for higher inflation than Britain by the end of the year.
Victory in Saturday’s match will put them on course to qualify for the European championships.

definitions.definition: to tell a lie or to do something in order to prevent the police, etc. from finding out the truth about a crime
to develop in the usual way and come to the usual end
examples: Sometimes it is better to simply let stress and grief run their course.
With minor ailments the best thing is often to let nature take its course.

definitions.definition: to continue doing something until it has finished or been completed, even though it is difficult
examples: Very few of the trainees have stayed the course.
He insists that he will not quit but will stay the course.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchcours, from Latincursus, fromcurs-‘run’, from the verbcurrere.

definitions.definition: the time that will come after the present or the events that will happen then

the futures market

examples: I hope we’ll see each other again in the not too distant future.

examples: I'm changing my email address but for the meantime you can use the old one.

definitions.definition: the fact of being in a particular place

examples: The government is maintaining a heavy police presence in the area.

definitions.definition: a person or spirit that you cannot see but that you feel is near
examples: She felt a presence behind her.

definitions.definition: the quality of making a strong impression on other people by the way you talk or behave; a person who has this quality

definitions.definition: when something exists in a particular place

examples: She’s only been here a couple of weeks but she is already making her presence felt.

origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latinpraesentia‘being at hand’, from the verbpraeesse, fromprae‘before’ +esse‘be’.

definitions.definition: the final part of a period of time, an event, an activity or a story
examples: It's the end of an era.
the end of the book
at the end of somethingat the end of the week
at the endThere’ll be a chance to ask questions at the end.
by the end of somethingHe wants the reports by the end of the month.
to the end of somethingShe remained active as an artist to the end of her life.
towards the end (of something)I was getting bored towards the end of the talk.
until the endWe didn't leave until thevery end.
until the end of somethingI'm staying until the end of this week.

definitions.definition: a situation in which something does not exist any more
examples: It was the end of all his dreams.

at an endThe war was finally at an end.

an end to somethingThey havecalled for an end toviolence.

definitions.definition: the part of an object or a place that is the furthest away from its centre
examples: I joined the end of the queue.

at the end of somethingTurn right at the end of the road.

on the end of somethingYou've got something on the end of your nose.

definitions.definition: an aim or a purpose
examples: They are prepared to use violence in pursuit of their ends.

definitions.definition: a part of an activity with which somebody is involved, especially in business
examples: We need somebody to handle the marketing end of the business.

definitions.definition: either of two places connected by a phone call, journey, etc.
examples: I answered the phone but there was no oneat the other end.

definitions.definition: one of the two halves of a sports field
examples: The teams changed ends at half-time.

definitions.definition: a small piece that is left after something has been used
examples: a cigarette end

definitions.definition: a person’s death. People say‘end’to avoid saying‘death’.
examples: She came toan untimely end(= died young).

definitions.definition: alinemanwho is nearest to the side of the field
examples: a defensive end

definitions.definition: used to introduce the most important fact after everything has been considered
examples: At the end of the day, he'll still have to make his own decision.

definitions.definition: having nothing to do and not knowing what you want to do
examples: Come and see us, if you're at a loose end.

definitions.definition: something unpleasant that happens to somebody, for example punishment or a violent death, usually because of their own actions
examples: He'llcome to a sticky endone of these days if he carries on like that.

definitions.definition: to have almost nothing left of something
examples: I'm at the end of my patience.

examples: She found herself on the receiving end of a great deal of criticism.

definitions.definition: to be so worried by a problem that you do not know what to do next
examples: She was at her wits’ end wondering how she’d manage it all in the time.

definitions.definition: when you say that people or situations arethe end, you mean that you are annoyed with them
the first sign of something ending
examples: The scandal was the beginning of the end of his career as a politician.

definitions.definition: to become very tired by trying to do too many things and going to bed late and getting up early
a thing that is itself important and not just a part of something more important
examples: For her, shopping had become an end in itself.
For him, travelling had become an end in itself rather than a means of seeing new places.

definitions.definition: bad or unfair methods of doing something are acceptable if the result of that action is good or positive
examples: He defended a morality in which the end justifies the means.

definitions.definition: (to reach) the point at which something can no longer continue in the same way
examples: A defeat in the second round marked the end of the line for last year's champion.

definitions.definition: used when you are stating that there is nothing more that can be said or done about something
examples: If she doesn’t agree, I won’t go ahead. End of story.

definitions.definition: in a line, with theendstouching
examples: They arranged the tables end to end.

definitions.definition: to have sex
to be the person in a group who is chosen or forced to perform an unpleasant duty or task
to understand something in the wrong way
to suddenly become very angry or emotional
to do everything possible, even if it is difficult, in order to get or achieve something
examples: I'd go to the ends of the earth to see her again.

definitions.definition: after a long period of time or series of events
examples: He tried various jobs and in the end became an accountant.

definitions.definition: after everything has been considered
examples: You can try your best to impress the interviewers but in the end it's often just a question of luck.

definitions.definition: to start or be made to start a new and difficult activity that you are not prepared for

definitions.definition: to continue to be cheerful in a difficult situation
something that shows you are nearly at the end of a long and difficult time or situation
examples: For the first time since the start of his treatment, we can now see light at the end of the tunnel.

definitions.definition: to earn just enough money to be able to buy the things you need
examples: Many families struggle to make ends meet.

definitions.definition: to shock or frighten somebody
examples: a chilling tale that will make your hair stand on end

definitions.definition: a thing or an action that is not interesting or important in itself but is a way of achieving something else
examples: He doesn't particularly like the work but he sees it as a means to an end.

definitions.definition: very much
examples: It upset me no end to hear they'd split up.

definitions.definition: a lot of something
examples: We had no end of trouble getting them to agree.

definitions.definition: not the worst thing that could happen to somebody
examples: Failing one exam is not the end of the world.
