be alone with somebody/somethingShe waited until she was alone with him to speak.
He was afraid of being alone with his thoughts.
Helives alone.
She was sittingall alonein the hall.
Finally the two of us were alone together.
I don't like going out alone at night.

definitions.definition: without the help of other people or things
examples: It's hard bringing up children alone.
The assassin said he hadacted alone.

definitions.definition: lonely and unhappy or without any friends
examples: Carolfelt all alonein the world.
I've been so alone since you went away.

definitions.definition: used after a noun or pronoun to show that the person or thing mentioned is the only one
examples: You can't blame anyone else; you alone made the decision.

definitions.definition: used after a noun or pronoun to emphasize one particular thing
examples: The shoes alone cost £200.

definitions.definition: to do something without help from anyone
examples: Andrew decided to go it alone and start his own business.

definitions.definition: to stop annoying somebody or trying to get their attention
examples: She's asked to be left alone but the press photographers follow her everywhere.
Don't touch me! Leave me alone!

definitions.definition: to stop touching, changing, or moving something
examples: I've told you before—leave my things alone!

definitions.definition: used after a statement to emphasize that because the first thing is not true or possible, the next thing cannot be true or possible either
examples: There isn't enough room for us, let alone any guests.
I didn’t have any clothes, let alone a passport.

definitions.definition: to be independent or not connected with other people, organizations or ideas
examples: These islands are too small to stand alone as independent states.
The problems that research is designed to solve do not stand alone, but are part of a wider context.

definitions.definition: to be not near other objects or buildings
examples: The arch once stood alone at the entrance to the castle.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

origin: Middle English: fromall+one.

definitions.definition: to gain knowledge or skill by studying, from experience, from being taught, etc.
examples: learn somethingtolearn a language/skill/trade
He had the opportunity to learn English in Australia.
learn (something) from somebody/somethingIlearned a lotfrom my father.
Everyone in the class had theopportunity to learnfrom each other.
learn (something) from doing somethingYou can learn a great deal just from watching other players.
learn (about something)She's very keen to learn about Japanese culture.
The book is about how children learn.
She received no training but quicklylearnt on the job(= while doing the job).
learn to do somethingHe's learning to play the trumpet.
Most people learn to read as children.
learn how to do somethingToday we learnt how to use the new software.
learn what, where, etc…Students need to learn what to do in an emergency.

definitions.definition: to study and repeat something in order to be able to remember it
examples: Ilearntthe poemby heart.
We have to learn one of Hamlet's speeches for school tomorrow.
You'll have to learn your lines by next week.

definitions.definition: to gradually change your attitudes about something so that you behave in a different way
examples: Some people never learn, do they?
learn from somethingI'm sure she'lllearn from her mistakes.
Why do people so often fail tolearn from experience?
learn (that)…He’ll just have to learn (that) he can’t always have his own way.
They quickly learn that bad behaviour is a sure-fire way of getting attention.
learn to do somethingThey eventuallylearned to loveliving in the countryside.
I soon learned not to ask too many questions.

definitions.definition: to become aware of something by hearing about it from somebody else
examples: learn of somethingI first learnt of his death many years later.
learn about somethingWe only learned about the problems in May of this year.
learn (that)…We were verysurprised to learn (that)she had got married again.
learn who, what, etc…We only learned who the new teacher was a few days ago.
learn somethingHow did they react when they learned the news?
it is learned that…It has been learned that 500 jobs are to be lost at the factory.

definitions.definition: to know something because of something unpleasant that has happened to you
examples: She is a tough competitor, as I know to my cost.

definitions.definition: to find out how to behave by learning from your mistakes or from unpleasant experiences, rather than from being told
to learn what to do or not to do in the future because you have had a bad experience in the past
to show somebody/know/learn how a particular job should be done
used to express surprise at something new or unexpected you have been told

origin: Old Englishleornian‘learn’ (in Middle English also ‘teach’), of West Germanic origin; related to Germanlernen, also tolore.

definitions.definition: a place with many houses, shops, etc. where people live and work. It is larger than a village but smaller than a city.
examples: The nearest town is ten miles away.
a university/seaside/market town
in a townI live in a small town.
How many people live in the town?
We spent a month in the French town of Le Puy.
They live in a rough part of town.

definitions.definition: the people who live in a particulartown
examples: The whole town is talking about it.

definitions.definition: the area of atownwhere most of the shops and businesses are
examples: We are only five minutes from the centre of town.
in townMum's in town doing some shopping.
Can you give me a lift into town?
Millie hit the town, looking for excitement.

definitions.definition: a particulartownwhere somebody lives and works or one that has just been referred to
examples: in townI'll be in town next week if you want to meet.
This restaurant serves the best steaks in town.
out of townHe married a girl from out of town.
The gossips finally drove her out of town.

definitions.definition: life intownsor cities as opposed to life in the country
examples: Pollution is just one of the disadvantages of living in the town.
Do you prefer the town to the country?

definitions.definition: to do something with a lot of energy, enthusiasm, etc., especially by spending a lot of money
examples: They really went to town on the decorations for the party.

definitions.definition: a man who frequently goes to fashionable parties, clubs, theatres, etc.
examples: In his new suit, he looked quite the man about town.

definitions.definition: the most important thing of a particular type, or the only thing that is available
visiting restaurants, clubs, theatres, etc. for entertainment, especially at night
examples: a night on the town
How about going out on the town tonight?

definitions.definition: to go to a lot of different bars, clubs, etc. and enjoy yourself

origin: Old Englishtūn‘enclosed piece of land, homestead, village’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchtuin‘garden’ and GermanZaun‘fence’.

definitions.definition: to pay attention to somebody/something that you can hear
examples: Listen! What's that noise? Can you hear it?
Sorry, I wasn't really listening.
He had been listening at the door.
listen to somebody/somethingtolisten to music/the radio
to listen to a song/an album
Ilistened carefully toher story.
He waslistening intently towhat she was saying.
You haven’t been listening to a word I’ve said!

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to take notice of what you are going to say
examples: Listen, there's something I have to tell you.

definitions.definition: to take notice of what somebody says to you so that you follow their advice or believe them
examples: I tried to warn her, but she wouldn't listen.
listen to somebody/somethingNone of this would have happened if you'd listened to me.
The government iswilling to listen toany concerns that people have.
Why won't you listen to reason?

origin: Old Englishhlysnan‘pay attention to’, of Germanic origin.

definitions.definition: the front part of the head, where the eyes, nose and mouth are
examples: a pretty/pale/round face
Heburied his facein his hands.
to hide/cover your face
on somebody's faceYou should have seen the look on her face when I told her!
The expression on his face never changed.
in the faceShe was red in the face with embarrassment.
At that time, her face was on the covers of all the magazines.

definitions.definition: an expression that is shown on somebody’sface
examples: a smiling/happy/sad face
Herface lit up(= showed happiness)when she spoke of the past.
Hisface fell(= showed disappointment, sadness, etc.)when he read the headlines.
I could tell by his face it hadn't gone well.
Sue'sface was a picture(= she looked very surprised, angry, etc.)as she listened to her husband's speech.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a person of the type mentioned
examples: She looked around for afamiliar face.
He's afresh face(= somebody new)at the company.
It's nice to see somenew faceshere this evening.
The movie is full offamous faces.
a well-known face on our television screens
I'm tired of seeing the same old faces every time we go out!

definitions.definition: having the type offaceor expression mentioned
examples: pale-faced
a freckle-faced boy
grim-faced
a sad-faced woman

definitions.definition: a side or surface of something
examples: the north face of the mountain
the dark face of the moon
A steep path zigzags down thecliff face.
We could see tiny figures climbing therock face.
Ivy covered the front face of the house.
A banner hung across the face of the building.
How many faces does a cube have?

definitions.definition: the front part of a clock or watch
examples: a dial like the face of a clock

definitions.definition: the particular character of something
examples: face of somethingthe changing face of Britain
This discoverychanged thewholefaceof science.
with a… facebureaucracy with ahuman face

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something
examples: Social deprivation is the unacceptable face of capitalism.
He has become the public face of the company.

definitions.definition: used to describe something that you have failed to see, even though it was obvious and should have been easy to see
examples: The answer was staring us in the face but we never saw it until it was too late.

definitions.definition: to be certain to happen
examples: Defeat was staring them in the face.

definitions.definition: to be unable to avoid something
examples: They were staring defeat in the face.

definitions.definition: to be very obvious to other people from the expression on somebody’s face
examples: Guilt was written all over his face.

definitions.definition: if a plan, etc.blows up in your face, it goes badly wrong in a way that causes you damage or makes you feel embarrassed
to do something when you are angry that is meant to harm somebody else but that also harms you
to disappear completely
examples: Keep looking—they can't just have vanished off the face of the earth.

definitions.definition: to try to do something as hard and as long as you possibly can but without success
examples: You can argue till you're blue in the face, but you won't change my mind.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody will/will not get a particular job or position because they have/do not have the appearance, personality, etc. that the employer wants, even when this should not be important
examples: If your face fits, you'll get the job.
It doesn't matter how well qualified you are; if your face doesn't fit, you don't stand a chance.

definitions.definition: somebody looks very angry
examples: Mr Hibbs came in with a face like thunder.

definitions.definition: close to and looking at somebody
examples: The two have never met face to face before.
The room fell silent as she came face to face with the man who had tried to kill her.

definitions.definition: in a situation where you have to accept that something is true and deal with it
examples: She was at an early age brought face to face with the horrors of war.

definitions.definition: with your face and stomach facing upwards/downwards
examples: She lay face down on the bed.

definitions.definition: with the front part or surface facing upwards/downwards
examples: Place the card face up on the pile.
She placed the cards face down on the table.

definitions.definition: to fall so that you are lying on your front
to fail completely, usually in an embarrassing way
examples: His next television venture fell flat on its face.

definitions.definition: to eat a lot of food or too much food
to oppose or be the opposite of something that is usual or expected
examples: Such a proposal is flying in the face of common sense.

definitions.definition: to do something that other people think is rude or shows a lack of respect, without feeling embarrassed or ashamed
examples: I don't know how you have the face to complain after everything they've done for you!

definitions.definition: to be made to look stupid
examples: They were left with egg on their faces when only ten people showed up.

definitions.definition: annoying somebody by criticizing them or telling them what to do all the time
examples: Why are you always in my face?

definitions.definition: despite problems, difficulties, etc.
examples: She showed great courage in the face of danger.
The campaign continued in the face of great opposition.

definitions.definition: as a result of something
examples: He was unable to deny the charges in the face of new evidence.

definitions.definition: to show in a very obvious way that you have no respect for somebody
to be forced to change from feeling pleased or satisfied to feeling disappointed or annoyed
examples: He’ll be laughing on the other side of his face when he reads my letter.

definitions.definition: (to have) an unhappy or disappointed expression
examples: He took one look at her long face and said ‘What’s wrong?’
The news for the company isn't good, to judge from the long faces in the boardroom.

definitions.definition: to look straight at somebody without feeling embarrassed or ashamed
examples: Can you look me in the eye and tell me you're not lying?
She looked her father straight in the eye and answered his question truthfully.
I'll never be able to look her in the face again!

definitions.definition: to be less respected or look stupid because of something you have done
examples: Many leaders don't want to lose face by admitting failures.

definitions.definition: the state of being less respected by other people or looking stupid because of something you have done
examples: Failure to pass the exams means a massive loss of face for the students and their parents.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you have particular skills or qualities
examples: ‘I didn't know you could play the piano.’ ‘I'm not just a pretty face, you know!’

definitions.definition: used to say that something seems to be good, true, etc. but that this opinion may need to be changed when you know more about it
examples: On the face of it, it seems like a great deal.
What may, on the face of it, seem obvious often turns out to be far more complicated.

definitions.definition: very obvious
to produce an expression on your face to show that you do not like somebody/something or in order to make somebody laugh
examples: What are you pulling a face at now?
Do you think it’s funny to make faces behind my back?

definitions.definition: to put onmake-up
to pretend that you feel confident and happy when you do not
examples: I had to put on a brave face and try to show him that I wasn’t worried.
He put a brave face on his illness.

definitions.definition: to avoid or help somebody avoid being embarrassed
examples: She was fired, but she saved face by telling everyone she'd resigned.

definitions.definition: to be determined to oppose somebody/something
examples: It is a shame that the council has set its face against the scheme.

definitions.definition: to appear among your friends or in public
examples: She stayed at home, afraid to show her face.

definitions.definition: to shut a door hard when somebody is trying to come in
to refuse to talk to somebody or meet them, in a rude way
a rude way of telling somebody to be quiet or stop talking
an action that seems to be intended as a deliberate way of offending and showing lack of respect for somebody
examples: The closure of the school is a slap in the face to the local community.

definitions.definition: if you keep astraight face, you do not laugh or smile, although you find something funny
if you say somethingto somebody’s face, you say it to them directly rather than to other people
examples: He’s a liar, and I’ve told him so to his face many times.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a person whose name you cannot remember
examples: Are you still working for what's her face?

definitions.definition: to destroy or remove somebody/something completely

origin: Middle English: from Old French, based on Latinfacies‘form, appearance, face’.

definitions.definition: to live in the same house
examples: I still live with my mum.

definitions.definition: to share a home and have a sexual relationship without being married
examples: She disapproves of unmarried couples living together.

definitions.definition: to accept something unpleasant
examples: I just had to learn to live with the pain.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: having a good chance of happening or being something;probableor expected
examples: themost likely explanation/outcome/scenario
the likely cause/consequence
a likely nominee/contender/winner
A threat level of ‘Severe’ means a terrorist attack ishighly likely.
They might ask for help or,more likely, just give up.
likely to do somethingTickets are likely to be expensive.
The beetles are likely to cause damage.
There is likely to be heavy snowfall.
likely (that)…It'shardly likely(that) they'll refuse.
It isquite likelythe discussion may never take place.

definitions.definition: seeming suitable for a purpose
examples: She seems the mostlikely candidatefor the job.

definitions.definition: used to show that you do not believe what somebody has said
examples: You just found it lying in the street? A likely story!
He said he'd met Rihanna. A likely story.

origin: Middle English: from Old Norselíkligr, fromlíkr; related toalike.

definitions.definition: a room in a house where people sit together, watch television, etc.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: the time when somebody in a group of people should or is allowed to do something
examples: When it's your turn, take another card.
Pleasewait your turn.
somebody's turn to do somethingWhose turn is itto cook?

If you can't put any cards down you have to miss a turn.

definitions.definition: a change in direction in a vehicle
examples: Make a left/right turninto West Street.
turn to the left/rightHe made a sudden turn to the right.

definitions.definition: a place where a road leads away from the one you are travelling on
examples: Take the next turnon the right.
He took awrong turnand ended up on the coast road.

definitions.definition: a bend or corner in a road
examples: a lane full oftwists and turns

definitions.definition: an act of turning somebody/something around
examples: Give the handle a few turns.

definitions.definition: an unusual or unexpected change in what is happening
examples: a surprisingturn of events
His health hastaken a turn for theworse(= suddenly got worse).

by turnsThe book is, by turns, funny and very sad.

definitions.definition: a short performance or piece of entertainment such as a song, etc.
examples: Everyone got up on stage to do a turn.

definitions.definition: a short walk
examples: We took a turn around the park.

definitions.definition: a feeling of illness
examples: a funny turn(= a feeling that you may faint)
Grandma had one of her turns.

definitions.definition: everywhere or every time you try and do something
examples: At every turn I met with disappointment.

definitions.definition: cooked for exactly the right amount of time
to frighten or shock somebody
examples: You gave me quite a turn, creeping up on me like that!

definitions.definition: (to do) something that helps somebody
examples: Well, that's my good turn for the day.

definitions.definition: one after the other in a particular order
examples: The children called out their names in turn.

definitions.definition: as a result of something in a series of events
examples: Increased production will, in turn, lead to increased profits.

definitions.definition: to do no work
examples: She hasn't done a hand's turn all week.

definitions.definition: you should help somebody who has helped you
going to change soon
examples: His luck is on the turn.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingout of turn, you do it when you are not supposed to be the next person to do something
examples: Batista had batted out of turn.
I'm writing to you out of turn because I have some very important news.

definitions.definition: to be useful for a particular purpose or period of time
to say something that you should not because it is the wrong situation or because it offends somebody
if peopletake turnsortake it in turnsto do something, they do it one after the other to make sure it is done fairly
examples: The male and female birds take turns in sitting on the eggs.
We take it in turns to do the housework.
The kids took turns on the swing.

definitions.definition: the time when a new century/year starts
examples: It was built at the turn of the century.
The team are unbeaten since the turn of the year.

definitions.definition: a particular way of thinking about things
a particular way of describing something
examples: He is meticulous in his choice of words and turns of phrase.

definitions.definition: an extra amount of pressure,cruelty, etc. added to a situation that is already difficult to bear or understand
a sudden increase in your speed or rate of progress; the ability to suddenly increase your speed
examples: He put on an impressive turn of speed in the last lap.
The kangaroo has a fast turn of speed.
For a small car it has a good turn of speed.

origin: Old Englishtyrnan,turnian(verb), from Latintornare, fromtornus‘lathe’, from Greektornos‘lathe, circular movement’; probably reinforced in Middle English by Old Frenchturner. The noun (Middle English) is partly from Anglo-Norman Frenchtourn, partly from the verb.

definitions.definition: to a smaller degree; not so much
examples: less expensive/likely/intelligent
less often/enthusiastically
less than…I read much less now than I used to.

She wasn'tany the lesshappy for(= she was perfectly happy)being on her own.

definitions.definition: continuing to become smaller in amount
examples: She found the job less and less attractive.

definitions.definition: almost
examples: I've more or less finished the book.

definitions.definition: approximately
examples: She could earn $200 a night, more or less.

definitions.definition: and certainly not
examples: No explanation was offered, still less an apology.
He’s too shy to ask a stranger the time, much less speak to a room full of people.

origin: Old Englishlǣssa, of Germanic origin; related to Old Frisianlēssa, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekloisthos‘last’.

definitions.definition: the period from 1 January to 31 December, that is 365 or 366 days, divided into 12 months
examples: Elections take place every year.

next/last year

in the year…She was born in the year 1865.
in… yearsThe crowd was much smaller than inprevious years.

definitions.definition: a period of twelve months, measured from any particular time

in the… year (of something)in the first year of their marriage
in… yearsProduction has declined inrecent years.

the pre-war/war/post-war years(= the period before/during/after the war)

definitions.definition: age; time of life
examples: He was 14years oldwhen it happened.

a twenty-year-old man

definitions.definition: a period of twelve months connected with a particular activity
examples: thetax/fiscal year
theschool year

definitions.definition: a level that you stay in for one year; a student at a particular level
examples: in year…We started German in year seven.
in somebody's yearShe was in my year at school.

definitions.definition: a long time
examples: It's years since we last met.
for yearsThey haven't seen each other foryears and years.
in yearsThat's the best movie I've seen in years.

over the yearsWe've had a lot of fun over the years.

definitions.definition: at some time in the future
examples: There are certain to be more job losses further down the road.

definitions.definition: the last years of somebody’s life
a very long time
examples: We've known each other for donkey's years.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that people decide is the best in a particular field in a particular year
examples: He was chosen as TV personality of the year.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you will/would never do something
examples: I'd never have thought of that in a million years.

definitions.definition: used in polite expressions to describe somebody as ‘very old’
examples: He was a man of advanced years.
(humorous)Even at my advanced age I still know how to enjoy myself!

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel or look older
examples: The illness put years on him.

definitions.definition: the desire for new sexual experience that is thought to be felt after seven years of marriage
since a very long time ago
examples: I've been going there every summer since the year dot.

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel or look younger
examples: That haircut takes years off her.
Careful make-up and styling can take years off you.

definitions.definition: the time when a new century/year starts
examples: It was built at the turn of the century.
The team are unbeaten since the turn of the year.

definitions.definition: every year for many years
examples: People return year after year.

definitions.definition: as the years pass; each year
examples: Year by year their affection for each other grew stronger.

definitions.definition: every year
any particular year after the birth of Christ
each year, compared with the last year
examples: Spending has increased year on year.
a year-on-year increase in spending

origin: Old Englishgē(a)r, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchjaarand GermanJahr, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekhōra‘season’.

definitions.definition: a particular situation or a situation of a particular type
examples: in… casesIn some casespeople have had to wait several weeks for an appointment.
In most casesthe increases have been marginal.
In many casesit is the pressure of the job that causes managers to behave like this.

The substances causes nausea, sickness and,in extreme cases, death.
in cases of somethingThe company only dismisses its employees in cases of gross misconduct.
in the case of somethingIn the case of banks, the law can limit activities.

a case of somethingIt's aclassic case(= a very typical case)of bad planning.

definitions.definition: the true situation
examples: be the caseIf that is the case(= if the situation described is true), we need more staff.
it's the case that…It is simply not the case that prison conditions are improving.

definitions.definition: a situation that relates to a particular person or thing
examples: in somebody's caseI cannot make an exception in your case(= for you and not for others).

in this caseDon't underestimate the power of the pen, or in this case, the power of the keyboard.

Every application will be decidedon a case-by-case basis(= each one will be considered separately).

definitions.definition: a matter that is being officially investigated, especially by the police
examples: They never solved the Jones murder case.

definitions.definition: a question to be decided in court
examples: acourt case
acriminal/civil case

towin/lose a case

definitions.definition: a set of facts or arguments that support one side in a trial, a discussion, etc.
examples: Our lawyer didn't think wehad a case(= had enough good arguments to win in a court of law).
case for somethingthe case for the defence/prosecution
case against somebody/somethingThe case against her was very weak.

case for doing somethingThe reportmakes out astrongcase(= gives good arguments)for spending more money on hospitals.

case that…They try tomake the casethat this war is necessary.

definitions.definition: a container or cover used to protect or store things; a container with its contents or the amount that it contains
examples: a jewellery case

case of somethinga case(= 12 bottles)of champagne

definitions.definition: a container with flat sides and a handle, used for carrying clothes, etc. when you are travelling
examples: Let me carry your case for you.

definitions.definition: the fact of somebody having a disease or an injury; a person who has a disease or an injury
examples: a severe case of food poisoning

definitions.definition: a person who needs, or is thought to need, special treatment or attention
examples: He's a hopeless case.

definitions.definition: the form of a noun, an adjective or a pronoun in some languages, that shows its relationship to another word
examples: the nominative/accusative/genitive case

In Polish the verb ‘to be’ takes the instrumental case.

definitions.definition: used to say that one of two or more possibilities is true, but which one is true depends on the circumstances
examples: There may be an announcement about this tomorrow—or not, as the case may be.

definitions.definition: to criticize somebody all the time
examples: She's always on my case about cleaning my room.

definitions.definition: to be dealing with a particular matter, especially a criminal investigation
examples: We have two agents on the case.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: a situation in business, politics, etc. where there is a lot of competition and people are willing to harm each other in order to succeed
examples: I'm afraid in this line of work it's a case of dog eat dog.
We're operating in a dog-eat-dog world.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to stop criticizing you
whatever happens or may have happened
examples: There's no point complaining now—we're leaving tomorrow in any case.

definitions.definition: because of the possibility of something happening
examples: You'd better take the keys in case I'm out.
You probably won't need to call—but take my number, just in case.
In case(= if it is true that)you’re wondering why Jo’s here—let me explain…

definitions.definition: if something happens
examples: In case of fire, ring the alarm bell.

definitions.definition: if that happens or has happened; if that is the situation
examples: ‘I’ve made up my mind.’ ‘In that case, there’s no point discussing it.’

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not need to say any more about something because you think that you have proved your point
used by lawyers in court to say that they have finished presenting their case
examples: The prosecution rests its case.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 6 andnounsenses 9 to 11 Middle English: from Old Frenchcas, from Latincasus‘fall’, related tocadere‘to fall’; in sense (11) directly from Latin, translating Greekptōsis, literally ‘fall’.nounsenses 7 to 8 late Middle English: from Old Frenchcasse,chasse(moderncaisse‘trunk, chest’,châsse‘reliquary, frame’), from Latincapsa, related tocapere‘to hold’.

definitions.definition: used to form the comparative of most adjectives and adverbs with two or more syllables
examples: She was far more intelligent than her sister.
He read the letter more carefully the second time.

definitions.definition: to a greater degree than something else; to a greater degree than usual
examples: I like the film more than the book.
a course for more advanced students
It had more the appearance of a deliberate crime than of an accident.
I had no complaints andno more(= neither)did Tom.
Signing the forms islittle more than(= only)a formality.
I'mmore thanhappy(= extremely happy)to take you there in my car.
She wasmore than a littleshaken(= extremely shaken)by the experience.
(formal)I will torment youno more(= no longer).

definitions.definition: again
examples: Could you repeat thatonce more(= one more time)?

definitions.definition: at a rate that keeps increasing
examples: I was becoming more and more irritated by his behaviour.

definitions.definition: used to show that two things change to the same degree
examples: The more she thought about it, the more depressed she became.
The less said about the whole thing, the happier I'll be.

definitions.definition: almost
examples: I've more or less finished the book.

definitions.definition: approximately
examples: She could earn $200 a night, more or less.

definitions.definition: used to add a point that is even more important
examples: You're wrong, and what's more you know it!

origin: Old Englishmāra, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchmeerand Germanmehr.

definitions.definition: to be thinking with pleasure about something that is going to happen (because you expect to enjoy it)
examples: I'm looking forward to the weekend.
look forward to doing somethingWe're really looking forward to seeing you again.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to have information in your mind as a result of experience or because you have learned or been told it
examples: know somethingNo oneknows the answer.
I need toknow your name.
The truth about what happened is not yet known.
All I knowis that she used to work in a bank(= I have no other information about her).
know (that)…I know (that) people’s handwriting changes as they get older.
Weknow from experiencethat turning a hobby into a business is not easy.
Iknow for a fact(that) he didn't go to the party.
‘There's no one in.’ ‘How do you know?’
‘You've got a flat tyre.’ ‘I know.’
‘What's the answer?’ ‘I don't know.’
As you know, Emma and I are old friends.
‘Isn’t that his car?’ ‘I wouldn’t know./How should I know?’(= I don’t know and I am not the person you should ask.)
(informal)‘What are you two whispering about?’ ‘You don't want to know’(= because you would be shocked or wouldn't approve).
it is known that…It is widely known that CFCs can damage the ozone layer.
know where, who, etc…I knew where he was hiding.
No oneknows for surewho did it.
know about somethingYou know about Amanda's baby, don't you?
I don't know about you, butI'm ready for something to eat.
Iknow next to nothing(= know almost nothing)about opera.
know of somebody/somethingI know of someone who can help us.
‘Is anyone else coming?’ ‘Not that I know of.’
know to do somethingDoes he know to come here(= that he should come here)first?
know somebody/something to be/do somethingWe know her to be honest.
Strobe lights are known to cause seizures.

definitions.definition: to realize, understand or be aware of something
examples: know (that)…As soon as I walked in the room I knew (that) something was wrong.
She knew she was dying.
‘Martin was lying all the time.’ ‘I should have known.’
know what, how, etc…I knew perfectly well what she meant.
Iknow exactlyhow you feel.
know somethingThis case is hopelessand he knows it(= although he will not admit it).

definitions.definition: to feel certain about something
examples: know (that)…He knew (that) he could trust her.
I know it's here somewhere!
I know things will turn out all right.
I don't know that I can finish it by next week.
I just knew that it was something I wanted to do.
know (something)‘You were right—someone's been spreading rumours about you.’ ‘I knew it!’
‘She's the worst player in the team.’ ‘Oh,I don't know(= I am not sure that I agree)—she played well yesterday.’

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a person, place, thing, etc.
examples: I've known David for 20 years.
Do you two know each other(= have you met before)?
She was a secretary when I first knew her.
She's very nice when youget to knowher.
Knowing Ben, we could be waiting a long time(= it is typical of him to be late).
This manis known to the police(= as a criminal).
I don’t know anyone in Oxford.
IknowPariswell.
Do you know the play(= have you seen or read it before)?
How many of your neighbours do youknow by name?
The new rules could mean the end of footballas we know it(= in the form that we are familiar with).

definitions.definition: to have learned a skill or language and be able to use it
examples: know somethingDo you know any Japanese?
know how to do somethingDo you know how to use spreadsheets?

definitions.definition: to think that somebody/something is a particular type of person or thing or has particular characteristics
examples: be known as somethingHe has becomewidely knownas an expert in child psychology.
It's known as the most dangerous part of the city.
be known for somethingShe isbest knownfor her work on the human brain.
He is internationally known for his work with vaccines.
be known to be/do somethingHe's known to be an outstanding physicist.

definitions.definition: to give somebody/something a particular name or title
examples: be known as somebody/somethingIran wasformerly knownas Persia.
The H5N1 virus ismore commonly knownas bird flu.
Peter Wilson,also knownas ‘The Tiger’
be known as somebody/something to somebodyHe was known as Bonzo to his friends.
be known by somethingThe country was not yetknown by the name ofEngland.

definitions.definition: to be able to recognize somebody/something
examples: I couldn't see who was speaking, but I knew the voice.
She knows a bargain when she sees one.

definitions.definition: to understand the difference between one person or thing and another
examples: I hope we have taught our children to know right from wrong.

definitions.definition: to have seen, heard or experienced something
examples: know somebody/something (to) do somethingI’ve never known it (to) snow in July before.
be known to do somethingHe has been known to spend all morning in the bathroom.

definitions.definition: to have personal experience of something
examples: He has known both poverty and wealth.
She may be successful now, but she hasknown what it is like to bepoor.
She thought she would never know the joy of seeing a child grow up in her care.

definitions.definition: used to say that you think you know, remember, understand, etc. something but you cannot be completely sure, especially because you do not know all the facts
examples: As far as we knew, there was no cause for concern.
As far as I can see, you've done nothing wrong.
She lived in Chicago, as far as I can remember.

definitions.definition: very quickly or suddenly
examples: We were whisked off in a taxi before we knew where we were.

definitions.definition: to have no way of realizing or being aware that you have done something wrong
examples: ‘I'm sorry, I called when you were in bed.’ ‘Don't worry—you weren't to know.’

definitions.definition: used to say that it is easier and wiser to stay in a bad situation that you know and can deal with rather than change to a new situation that may be much worse
used to emphasize that you do not know something and that it is not important to you
examples: She could be dead for all I know.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you do not know something
examples: God knows what else they might find.
‘Where are they?’ ‘Goodness knows.’

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the truth of what you are saying
examples: She ought to pass the exam—goodness knows she's been working hard enough.

definitions.definition: to be confident that you know something, especially when you actually do not
examples: He thinks he knows all the answers.

definitions.definition: to know something perfectly so that you can repeat it at any time without having to think about it
examples: He had all the answers off pat.

definitions.definition: to be in poor condition
examples: Our car has seen better days!

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody’s behaviour
examples: I don't know how you can say things like that.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody the true facts about a situation, especially when these are not known by other people
examples: If the truth be known, I was afraid to tell anyone.

definitions.definition: used to agree with somebody or to show sympathy
examples: ‘What a ridiculous situation!’ ‘I know.’

definitions.definition: used to introduce a new idea or suggestion
examples: I know, let's see what's on at the theatre.

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody by saying that they should realize or understand something
examples: You know as well as I do that you're being unreasonable.

definitions.definition: to know somebody/something extremely well
examples: She must know the play backwards by now.

definitions.definition: to know what should be done, etc. better than other people
examples: The doctor told you to stay in bed, and she knows best.

definitions.definition: to be sensible enough not to do something
examples: He knows better than to judge by appearances.

definitions.definition: to recognize somebody without knowing them well
to have information or evidence that the opposite is true
examples: He says he doesn't care about what the critics write, but I know different.

definitions.definition: to be very aware of a fact and unable to deny or ignore it
examples: He knew full well what she thought of it.

definitions.definition: to be very familiar with somebody/something
examples: This is where I grew up. I know this area like the back of my hand.

definitions.definition: to have very clear and definite ideas about what you want to do
to know a lot about a particular subject or job
to know/tell somebody some useful, interesting or surprising information about somebody/something
examples: She's been married five times, so she knows a thing or two about men!

definitions.definition: to know something because of something unpleasant that has happened to you
examples: She is a tough competitor, as I know to my cost.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to have knowledge about something from your own experience
examples: I worked there for 20 years, so I know what I'm talking about.

definitions.definition: to know where you can get an advantage for yourself
to make sure that people are informed about something, especially by getting somebody else to tell them
examples: The President has let it be known that he does not intend to run for election again.

definitions.definition: to tell somebody about something
examples: I don't know if I can come, but I'll let you know tomorrow.
Let me know how I can help.
Please let me know if there's anything I can do to help.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying
examples: Lord knows, I tried to teach her.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not know the answer to something
examples: ‘Why did she say that?’ ‘Lord knows!’

definitions.definition: to introduce yourself to somebody
examples: I made myself known to the hotel manager.

definitions.definition: to behave badly, usually because you have not been taught the correct way to behave
examples: Don’t blame the children—they don’t know any better.

definitions.definition: to be very stupid; to have absolutely no skill
to know nothing about a subject
examples: I don’t know beans about making movies.

definitions.definition: to know nothing at all about somebody/something
examples: I’m afraid I don’t know the first thing about cars.

definitions.definition: to know nothing at all about something/somebody
examples: We’ve lived next to him for years, but we still don’t know the first thing about him.

definitions.definition: to not know at all who somebody is
to be so surprised by something that you do not know how to react
to feel very embarrassed and not know how to react
to be so excited or confused that you cannot behave or think in a sensible way
to have an easy life without realizing how easy it is
examples: You people without kids don't know you're born.

definitions.definition: to take no interest in something because you do not care about it or it is too much trouble
examples: I've tried to ask her advice, but she doesn't want to know(= about my problems).
‘How much was it?’ ‘You don't want to know’(= it is better if you don't know).

definitions.definition: old enough to behave in a more sensible way than you actually did
to show somebody/know/learn how a particular job should be done
used to say that it is impossible to say what might happen
examples: There's no knowing how he'll react.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody knows nothing about the subject you are talking about
examples: What does he know about football, anyway?

definitions.definition: used to express surprise
examples: Well, what do you know? Look who's here!

definitions.definition: (know) what things are useful, important, etc.
examples: She certainly knows what's what.

definitions.definition: used when you are thinking of what to say next
examples: Well, you know, it's difficult to explain.

definitions.definition: used to show that what you are referring to is known or understood by the person you are speaking to
examples: Guess who I've just seen? Maggie! You know—Jim's wife.
You know that restaurant round the corner? It's closed down.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something that you are saying
examples: I'm not stupid, you know.

definitions.definition: used to introduce an interesting or surprising opinion, piece of news, etc.
examples: You know something? I've never really enjoyed Christmas.

definitions.definition: used to refer to somebody/something without mentioning a name
used to say that you can never be certain about what will happen in the future, especially when you are suggesting that something good might happen

origin: Old Englishcnāwan(earliergecnāwan) ‘recognize, identify’, of Germanic origin; from an Indo-European root shared by Latin(g)noscere, Greekgignōskein, also bycanandken.

definitions.definition: what is measured in minutes, hours, days, etc.
examples: The world exists in time and space.
The changing seasons mark the passage of time.
in timeA visit to the museum will take youback in timeto the 1930s.
At this point in time, it seems unlikely that he will write any more books.
over timePerceptions change over time(= as time passes).
As time goes onI love this city more and more.
As time went bywe saw less and less of each other.
The main character is able to travel through time.

definitions.definition: the time shown on a clock in minutes and hours
examples: What time is it/What’s the time?
Do you have the time?
(British English)What time do you make it?
(North American English)What time do you have?
Let me just check the time.
The time is now half past ten.
(British English)Can shetell the timeyet(= say what time it is by looking at a clock)?
(North American English)Can shetell timeyet?
My watchkeeps perfect time(= always shows the correct time).
Look at the time! We'll be late.
This time tomorrowI'll be in Canada.

definitions.definition: the time measured in a particular part of the world
examples: 6 o’clock local time
We're two hours behind Central European Time.

definitions.definition: the time when something happens or when something should happen
examples: What time do you finish work?
The baby loves bath time.
A computer screen shows arrival and departure times.
time to do somethingI think it's time to go to bed.
This is hardly the time to discuss politics.
time for somethingIt's time for lunch.
time (that)…It's time the kids were in bed.
It's time that farmers listened to consumers.
by the time (that)…By the time you get there the meeting will be over.
at a… timeHave I called at a bad time? Shall I call back later?
We stock six different beers at any one time.
at the timeI didn't realize it at the time, but that was the best day of my life.
The promotion came at just theright timefor me.
The new product will launchwhen the time is right.
You'll feel differently about itwhen the time comes(= when it happens).
around the time of somethingSeveral articles were published around the time of his death in 2018.

definitions.definition: an amount of time; the amount of time available to work, rest, etc.
examples: I can't go with you—I don'thave time.
Hespendsa lot of histimeworking.
time for somethingDo you have time for a chat?
time to do somethingAllowplenty of timeto get to the airport.
I can probablymake the timeto see them.
Ittakes timeto make changes in the law.
We haveno time to lose(= we must hurry).
We got to the airport with time to spare.
She plans to study overseas for an extendedperiod of time.
What do you like to do in yourfree time?
She doesn’t have muchspare time.
Sorry, I can’t spare the time.
Don'twasteany moretime!
What awaste of time!
Time's running outfor them to complete the project.
I didn't finish the test—I ran out of time.
Time's up—have you worked out the answer yet?
He never takes anytime off(= time spent not working).
Jane's worked herefor some time(= for a fairly long period of time).
Do it now please—not in three hours' time(= three hours from now).
The journey time is two hours.
I don’t want to take up too much of your precious time.
What’s the hurry? We have all the time in the world.

definitions.definition: a period of time, either long or short, during which you do something or something happens
examples: His injuries willtake a long timeto heal.
It took them a long time to get home.
Did you have to wait a long time to see the doctor?
for a timeI lived in Egypt for a time.
I was only there fora short time.
The early morning is the besttime of day.
Her parents dieda long time ago.
At one time(= at a period of time in the past)Emily was my best friend.
Mr Curtis was the managerin my time(= when I was working there).

definitions.definition: a period of history connected with particular events or experiences in people’s lives
examples: at the time of somethingThe movie is set at the time of the Russian Revolution.
in… time(s)the politician who promised ‘peace in our time’
in ancient times
The Industrial Revolution took place in Victorian times.
the violent times we live in(= the present period of history)
in times of somethingin times of trouble
The business was successful, even during difficult times.
Times are hard for the unemployed.
Times have changedsince Grandma was young.

definitions.definition: an occasion when you do something or when something happens
examples: Every timeI hear that song I feel happy.
It doesn't matter if you don't win every time(= every time you play).
Next timeyou're here let's have lunch together.
He failed his driving test three times.
He's determined to passthis time.
When was thelast timeyou saw her?
I see her two or threetimes a week.
How many times(= how often)do I have to tell you not to do that?
(especially North American English)I rememberone time(= once)we had to abandon our car in the snow.
(formal)At no time didI give my consent to the plan.

definitions.definition: an event or occasion that you experience in a particular way
examples: Did youhave a good timein Spain?
They'rehaving a hard timedealing with her illness.
I had an awful time in the hospital.
Mike and I shared some really good times.

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event
examples: The winner's time was 11.6 seconds.
She completed the 500 metresin record time(= faster than any previous runner).
one of the fastest times ever

definitions.definition: the number ofbeats(= units of rhythm)in abarof music
examples: This piece is in four-four time.
a slow waltz time
The conductorbeat timewith a baton.

definitions.definition: the correct speed and rhythm of a piece of music
examples: in time to somethingTry and dance in time to the music(= with the same speed and rhythm).
Clap your hands tokeep time(= sing or move with the correct speed and rhythm).
in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)
He always plays in perfect time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.
The plane took off an hour behind time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.
Hot water is available at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.
She ran up the stairs two at a time.
He surfs the internet for hours at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.
The rain will become heavy at times in the afternoon.
He can be rather moody at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.
If you didn't win a prize, better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.
to deliberately make a situation difficult and unpleasant for somebody
examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.
You'll get used to the work in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?
The ambulance got there just in time(= to save somebody's life).

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted

used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

way to do somethingI'm sure we canfind a wayto make this work.

way of doing somethingI'm not happy with this way of working.
way (that)…It's not what you say—it's the way that you say it.

in the way (that)…It works in the way you'd expect.

in a way that…She writes in a way that is easy to understand.

examples: After ten years I'm used to the strange British ways.

way to…Can youtell me the wayto Leicester Square?
way from… to…thebest/quickest/shortest wayfrom A to B

+ adv./prep.We fought our way through the dense vegetation.

definitions.definition: a road, path or street for travelling along
examples: There's a way across the fields.

definitions.definition: used in the names of streets
examples: 106 Headley Way

definitions.definition: a particular direction; in a particular direction
examples: Which way did they go?

Make sure that sign'sthe right way up.

examples: theway in/out

examples: A little way up on the left is the Museum of Modern Art.

definitions.definition: an area, a part of a country, etc.
examples: over/down… wayI think he lives somewhere over Greenwich way.
down my/our/your wayI'll stop by and see you next time I'm down your way.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something
examples: I have changed in every way.

definitions.definition: a particular condition or state
examples: in a… wayThe economy's in a bad way.

definitions.definition: on the other side of the street, etc.
examples: Music blared from the open window of the house across the way.

examples: She didn't speak a word to me all the way back home.
the whole way to Arizona

definitions.definition: completely; as much as it takes to achieve what you want
examples: I'm fighting him all the way.

definitions.definition: as you make progress with something
examples: You'll learn something along the way.

definitions.definition: used to say that things often happen in a particular way, especially when it is not convenient
however you choose to look at a situation
to behave or do things in a particular manner because it is part of your character
examples: It's not his fault he's so pompous—he was born that way.

definitions.definition: to be easy compared with what came before
examples: It's downhill all the way from here. We'll soon be finished.

definitions.definition: to become worse or less successful
examples: I started work as a journalist and it was downhill all the way for my health.

definitions.definition: to have habits or opinions that you have had for a long time and that you do not want to change
examples: Grandma is so set in her ways.

definitions.definition: to have nearly achieved something and be going to achieve it soon
examples: She is well on the way to recovery.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

examples: By the way, I found that book you were looking for.

examples: The artist recently arrived in Paris from Bulgaria by way of Vienna.

definitions.definition: as a form of something; for something; as a means of something
examples: He received £600 by way of compensation from the company.

examples: He was in trouble with the police as a teenager but now he’s completely changed his ways.

examples: She clawed her way to the top of her profession.

examples: He took the first job that came his way.

examples: Was it his fault or not? Either way, an explanation is due.

definitions.definition: in all directions
examples: Her hair tumbled every which way.

definitions.definition: to move along carefully, for example when it is dark, by touching walls, objects, etc.

examples: She was new in the job, still feeling her way.

examples: I hope you can find your way home.

definitions.definition: to have fullsexual intercoursewith somebody

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.

(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something

definitions.definition: to make a special effort to do something
examples: He would always go out of his way to be friendly towards her.

definitions.definition: to go in a different direction from somebody you have been travelling with
to travel in the same direction as somebody
examples: I'm going your way—I'll walk with you.

definitions.definition: to go well for you; to be in your favour
examples: By the third round he knew the fight was going his way.

definitions.definition: to die
by having an unpleasant experience or by making mistakes

definitions.definition: to be a sensible person

examples: You can't have it both ways. If you can afford to go out all the time, you can afford to pay off some of your debts.

definitions.definition: used to say in an angry way that although you are not happy about something that somebody has said, you are not going to argue

examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: to be good at dealing with somebody/something

definitions.definition: to persuade somebody to have sex with you

definitions.definition: of any type
examples: I don't approve of violence in any shape or form.

definitions.definition: (to be/become) pregnant

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people

examples: I expect she does love you in her own way.

definitions.definition: stopping somebody from moving or doing something

examples: Let's keep out of her way while she's in such a bad mood.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to make a lot of money easily and feel very pleased about it
to get yourself into or out of a situation by lying

examples: Prison officers know what's going on, but look the other way.

definitions.definition: to become lost
examples: We lost our way in the dark.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to pass; to allow somebody/something to take the place of somebody/something

(to take/find) an acceptable course of action that avoids two extreme positions

definitions.definition: to (not) prevent somebody from doing something

definitions.definition: to be offended by a remark that was not intended to be offensive

there are many different ways to achieve something
in my opinion
having started

definitions.definition: a plan of action that is likely to be successful
examples: Personal electronic health records are the way forward for effective healthcare in the future.

the typical pattern of behaviour of a person or group

the beliefs and practices of the Hindu way of life

definitions.definition: to be responsible for or to take care of somebody/something/yourself
examples: Who's going to look after the children while you're away?

origin: N/A

examples: At last things were beginning to look up.

examples: Can you look up the opening times on the website?

examples: to look back on your childhood

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: something that is being carried (usually in large amounts) by a person, vehicle, etc.

load of somethingThe women came down the hill with their loads of firewood.

examples: Get a load of that dress!

origin: Old Englishlād‘way, journey, conveyance’, of Germanic origin: related to GermanLeite, also tolead; compare withlode. The verb dates from the late 15th cent.

Strobe lights are known to cause seizures.

definitions.definition: to realize, understand or be aware of something
examples: know (that)…As soon as I walked in the room I knew (that) something was wrong.
She knew she was dying.
‘Martin was lying all the time.’ ‘I should have known.’
know what, how, etc…I knew perfectly well what she meant.
Iknow exactlyhow you feel.
know somethingThis case is hopelessand he knows it(= although he will not admit it).

definitions.definition: to feel certain about something
examples: know (that)…He knew (that) he could trust her.
I know it's here somewhere!
I know things will turn out all right.
I don't know that I can finish it by next week.
I just knew that it was something I wanted to do.
know (something)‘You were right—someone's been spreading rumours about you.’ ‘I knew it!’
‘She's the worst player in the team.’ ‘Oh,I don't know(= I am not sure that I agree)—she played well yesterday.’

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a person, place, thing, etc.
examples: I've known David for 20 years.
Do you two know each other(= have you met before)?
She was a secretary when I first knew her.
She's very nice when youget to knowher.
Knowing Ben, we could be waiting a long time(= it is typical of him to be late).
This manis known to the police(= as a criminal).
I don’t know anyone in Oxford.
IknowPariswell.
Do you know the play(= have you seen or read it before)?
How many of your neighbours do youknow by name?
The new rules could mean the end of footballas we know it(= in the form that we are familiar with).

definitions.definition: to have learned a skill or language and be able to use it
examples: know somethingDo you know any Japanese?
know how to do somethingDo you know how to use spreadsheets?

definitions.definition: to think that somebody/something is a particular type of person or thing or has particular characteristics
examples: be known as somethingHe has becomewidely knownas an expert in child psychology.
It's known as the most dangerous part of the city.
be known for somethingShe isbest knownfor her work on the human brain.
He is internationally known for his work with vaccines.
be known to be/do somethingHe's known to be an outstanding physicist.

definitions.definition: to give somebody/something a particular name or title
examples: be known as somebody/somethingIran wasformerly knownas Persia.
The H5N1 virus ismore commonly knownas bird flu.
Peter Wilson,also knownas ‘The Tiger’
be known as somebody/something to somebodyHe was known as Bonzo to his friends.
be known by somethingThe country was not yetknown by the name ofEngland.

definitions.definition: to be able to recognize somebody/something
examples: I couldn't see who was speaking, but I knew the voice.
She knows a bargain when she sees one.

definitions.definition: to understand the difference between one person or thing and another
examples: I hope we have taught our children to know right from wrong.

definitions.definition: to have seen, heard or experienced something
examples: know somebody/something (to) do somethingI’ve never known it (to) snow in July before.
be known to do somethingHe has been known to spend all morning in the bathroom.

definitions.definition: to have personal experience of something
examples: He has known both poverty and wealth.
She may be successful now, but she hasknown what it is like to bepoor.
She thought she would never know the joy of seeing a child grow up in her care.

definitions.definition: used to say that you think you know, remember, understand, etc. something but you cannot be completely sure, especially because you do not know all the facts
examples: As far as we knew, there was no cause for concern.
As far as I can see, you've done nothing wrong.
She lived in Chicago, as far as I can remember.

definitions.definition: very quickly or suddenly
examples: We were whisked off in a taxi before we knew where we were.

definitions.definition: to have no way of realizing or being aware that you have done something wrong
examples: ‘I'm sorry, I called when you were in bed.’ ‘Don't worry—you weren't to know.’

definitions.definition: used to say that it is easier and wiser to stay in a bad situation that you know and can deal with rather than change to a new situation that may be much worse
used to emphasize that you do not know something and that it is not important to you
examples: She could be dead for all I know.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you do not know something
examples: God knows what else they might find.
‘Where are they?’ ‘Goodness knows.’

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the truth of what you are saying
examples: She ought to pass the exam—goodness knows she's been working hard enough.

definitions.definition: to be confident that you know something, especially when you actually do not
examples: He thinks he knows all the answers.

definitions.definition: to know something perfectly so that you can repeat it at any time without having to think about it
examples: He had all the answers off pat.

definitions.definition: to be in poor condition
examples: Our car has seen better days!

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody’s behaviour
examples: I don't know how you can say things like that.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody the true facts about a situation, especially when these are not known by other people
examples: If the truth be known, I was afraid to tell anyone.

definitions.definition: used to agree with somebody or to show sympathy
examples: ‘What a ridiculous situation!’ ‘I know.’

definitions.definition: used to introduce a new idea or suggestion
examples: I know, let's see what's on at the theatre.

definitions.definition: used to criticize somebody by saying that they should realize or understand something
examples: You know as well as I do that you're being unreasonable.

definitions.definition: to know somebody/something extremely well
examples: She must know the play backwards by now.

definitions.definition: to know what should be done, etc. better than other people
examples: The doctor told you to stay in bed, and she knows best.

definitions.definition: to be sensible enough not to do something
examples: He knows better than to judge by appearances.

definitions.definition: to recognize somebody without knowing them well
to have information or evidence that the opposite is true
examples: He says he doesn't care about what the critics write, but I know different.

definitions.definition: to be very aware of a fact and unable to deny or ignore it
examples: He knew full well what she thought of it.

definitions.definition: to be very familiar with somebody/something
examples: This is where I grew up. I know this area like the back of my hand.

definitions.definition: to have very clear and definite ideas about what you want to do
to know a lot about a particular subject or job
to know/tell somebody some useful, interesting or surprising information about somebody/something
examples: She's been married five times, so she knows a thing or two about men!

definitions.definition: to know something because of something unpleasant that has happened to you
examples: She is a tough competitor, as I know to my cost.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to have knowledge about something from your own experience
examples: I worked there for 20 years, so I know what I'm talking about.

definitions.definition: to know where you can get an advantage for yourself
