to make sure that people are informed about something, especially by getting somebody else to tell them
examples: The President has let it be known that he does not intend to run for election again.

definitions.definition: to tell somebody about something
examples: I don't know if I can come, but I'll let you know tomorrow.
Let me know how I can help.
Please let me know if there's anything I can do to help.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying
examples: Lord knows, I tried to teach her.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not know the answer to something
examples: ‘Why did she say that?’ ‘Lord knows!’

definitions.definition: to introduce yourself to somebody
examples: I made myself known to the hotel manager.

definitions.definition: to behave badly, usually because you have not been taught the correct way to behave
examples: Don’t blame the children—they don’t know any better.

definitions.definition: to be very stupid; to have absolutely no skill
to know nothing about a subject
examples: I don’t know beans about making movies.

definitions.definition: to know nothing at all about somebody/something
examples: I’m afraid I don’t know the first thing about cars.

definitions.definition: to know nothing at all about something/somebody
examples: We’ve lived next to him for years, but we still don’t know the first thing about him.

definitions.definition: to not know at all who somebody is
to be so surprised by something that you do not know how to react
to feel very embarrassed and not know how to react
to be so excited or confused that you cannot behave or think in a sensible way
to have an easy life without realizing how easy it is
examples: You people without kids don't know you're born.

definitions.definition: to take no interest in something because you do not care about it or it is too much trouble
examples: I've tried to ask her advice, but she doesn't want to know(= about my problems).
‘How much was it?’ ‘You don't want to know’(= it is better if you don't know).

definitions.definition: old enough to behave in a more sensible way than you actually did
to show somebody/know/learn how a particular job should be done
used to say that it is impossible to say what might happen
examples: There's no knowing how he'll react.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody knows nothing about the subject you are talking about
examples: What does he know about football, anyway?

definitions.definition: used to express surprise
examples: Well, what do you know? Look who's here!

definitions.definition: (know) what things are useful, important, etc.
examples: She certainly knows what's what.

definitions.definition: used when you are thinking of what to say next
examples: Well, you know, it's difficult to explain.

definitions.definition: used to show that what you are referring to is known or understood by the person you are speaking to
examples: Guess who I've just seen? Maggie! You know—Jim's wife.
You know that restaurant round the corner? It's closed down.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something that you are saying
examples: I'm not stupid, you know.

definitions.definition: used to introduce an interesting or surprising opinion, piece of news, etc.
examples: You know something? I've never really enjoyed Christmas.

definitions.definition: used to refer to somebody/something without mentioning a name
used to say that you can never be certain about what will happen in the future, especially when you are suggesting that something good might happen

origin: Old Englishcnāwan(earliergecnāwan) ‘recognize, identify’, of Germanic origin; from an Indo-European root shared by Latin(g)noscere, Greekgignōskein, also bycanandken.

definitions.definition: an action or an opinion that is not correct, or that produces a result that you did not want
examples: It's easy tomake a mistake.
Don't worry, we allmake mistakes.
You are making abig mistake.
Leaving school so young was the biggest mistake of my life.
aterrible/serious/huge mistake
It’s acommon mistake(= one that a lot of people make).
You must try tolearn from your mistakes.
He is determined not to repeat the mistakes of his predecessors.
This letter is addressed to someone else—there must be some mistake.
the mistake of doing somethingImade the mistake ofgiving him my address.
it is a mistake to do somethingIt would be a mistake to ignore his opinion.
It was a big mistake on my part to have trusted her.

definitions.definition: a word, figure, etc. that is not said or written down correctly
examples: It's acommon mistakeamong learners of English.
Her essay is full ofspelling mistakes.
If students correct their own mistakes, learning improves.
mistake (in) doing somethingThe waitermade a mistake (in)adding up the bill.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are sure about the truth of what you have just said
examples: This is a strange business and no mistake.

definitions.definition: by accident; without intending to
examples: I took your bag instead of mine by mistake.

definitions.definition: thinking that something is something else
examples: Children may eat pills in mistake for sweets.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are saying, especially when you want to warn somebody about something
examples: Make no mistake (about it), this is one crisis that won’t just go away.

origin: late Middle English (as a verb): from Old Norsemistaka‘take in error’, probably influenced in sense by Old Frenchmesprendre.

definitions.definition: a method, style or manner of doing something
examples: I prefer to do things the easy way.
I love him and I think he feelsthe same way.
way to do somethingI'm sure we canfind a wayto make this work.
Using the subway isthe only way toavoid the traffic.
(informal, disapproving)That's no wayto speak to your mother!
way of doing somethingI'm not happy with this way of working.
way (that)…It's not what you say—it's the way that you say it.
Ihate the wayshe always criticizes me.
I told you we should have done itmy way!
in the… wayShe dressesin the same wayas her sister.
Infectious diseases can be acquired in several ways.
In this way, we can track the storms as they move across the ocean.
in the way (that)…It works in the way you'd expect.
New technology has radically changedthe way in whichthis work is done.
in a way that…She writes in a way that is easy to understand.
I generally get what I wantone way or another(= by some means).

definitions.definition: a particular manner or style of behaviour
examples: in a… wayThey grinned at her in a friendly way.
It was not his wayto admit that he had made a mistake.
Don't worry if she seems quiet—it's just her way.
He was showing off,as is the waywith adolescent boys.

definitions.definition: the typical way of behaving and living of a particular group of people
examples: After ten years I'm used to the strange British ways.
She always said that the old ways were the best.

definitions.definition: a route or road that you take in order to reach a place
examples: toask somebody the way
Do you know the way?
way to…Can youtell me the wayto Leicester Square?
way from… to…thebest/quickest/shortest wayfrom A to B
We went thelong way round.

definitions.definition: the route along which somebody/something is moving; the route that somebody/something would take if there was nothing stopping them/it
examples: Get out of my way!I'm in a hurry.
Riot police with shields were blocking the demonstrators' way.
along the wayUnfortunately they ran into a snowstorm along the way.
+ adv./prep.We fought our way through the dense vegetation.
(figurative)We will eventually find a way out of the crisis.

definitions.definition: a road, path or street for travelling along
examples: There's a way across the fields.

definitions.definition: used in the names of streets
examples: 106 Headley Way

definitions.definition: a particular direction; in a particular direction
examples: Which way did they go?
We just missed a car comingthe other way.
Lookboth ways(= look left and right)before crossing the road.
Make sure that sign'sthe right way up.
The pipe could be moved a few incheseither way.
Kids were runningthis way and that(= in all directions).
They decided to split the money four ways(= between four different people).
(figurative)Which way(= for which party)are you going to vote?

definitions.definition: a means of going into or leaving a place, such as a door or gate
examples: theway in/out
They escaped out the back way.

definitions.definition: a distance or period of time between two points
examples: A little way up on the left is the Museum of Modern Art.
We had to goa long waybefore we found a place to eat.
September wasa long way off.
You cameall this wayto see us?
(figurative)The economy still hasa way to goto a full recovery.
(North American English, informal)We still havea ways to go.

definitions.definition: an area, a part of a country, etc.
examples: over/down… wayI think he lives somewhere over Greenwich way.
down my/our/your wayI'll stop by and see you next time I'm down your way.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something
examples: I have changed in every way.
It's been quite a day,one way and another(= for several reasons).

definitions.definition: a particular condition or state
examples: in a… wayThe economy's in a bad way.
I don't know how we're going to manage,the way things are.

definitions.definition: on the other side of the street, etc.
examples: Music blared from the open window of the house across the way.

definitions.definition: during the whole journey/period of time
examples: She didn't speak a word to me all the way back home.
the whole way to Arizona
This bus doesn't go all the way so you'll have to change.
I watched the movie the whole way through.

definitions.definition: completely; as much as it takes to achieve what you want
examples: I'm fighting him all the way.
You can feel that the audience is with her all the way.

definitions.definition: as you make progress with something
examples: You'll learn something along the way.

definitions.definition: used to say that things often happen in a particular way, especially when it is not convenient
however you choose to look at a situation
to behave or do things in a particular manner because it is part of your character
examples: It's not his fault he's so pompous—he was born that way.

definitions.definition: to be easy compared with what came before
examples: It's downhill all the way from here. We'll soon be finished.

definitions.definition: to become worse or less successful
examples: I started work as a journalist and it was downhill all the way for my health.
It's been all downhill for his career since then, with four defeats in five games.

definitions.definition: to have habits or opinions that you have had for a long time and that you do not want to change
examples: Grandma is so set in her ways.

definitions.definition: to have nearly achieved something and be going to achieve it soon
examples: She is well on the way to recovery.
He is well on the way to establishing himself among the top ten players in the world.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a comment or question that is not directly related to what you have been talking about
examples: By the way, I found that book you were looking for.
What's the time, by the way?
Oh by the way, if you see Jackie, tell her I'll call her this evening.

definitions.definition: by a route that includes the place mentioned
examples: The artist recently arrived in Paris from Bulgaria by way of Vienna.
She came to TV by way of drama school.

definitions.definition: as a form of something; for something; as a means of something
examples: He received £600 by way of compensation from the company.
She rolled her eyes by way of an answer and left.

definitions.definition: to start to live or behave in a different way from before
examples: He was in trouble with the police as a teenager but now he’s completely changed his ways.
Your father is unlikely to change his ways now.

definitions.definition: to gradually achieve something or move somewhere by being determined and using a lot of effort
examples: She clawed her way to the top of her profession.
Slowly, he clawed his way out from under the collapsed building.

definitions.definition: to remove things that are stopping the progress or movement of something
examples: The ruling could clear the way for extradition proceedings.

definitions.definition: to happen to you by chance, or when you were not expecting it
examples: He took the first job that came his way.
Have any interesting articles come your way recently?

definitions.definition: to have two opposite effects or results
used to say that it does not matter which one of two possibilities happens, is chosen or is true
examples: Was it his fault or not? Either way, an explanation is due.
We could meet today or tomorrow—I don't mind one way or the other.

definitions.definition: in all directions
examples: Her hair tumbled every which way.

definitions.definition: to move along carefully, for example when it is dark, by touching walls, objects, etc.
to be careful about how you do things, usually because you are in a situation that you are not familiar with
examples: She was new in the job, still feeling her way.

definitions.definition: to discover the right route (to a place)
examples: I hope you can find your way home.

definitions.definition: to come to a place or a situation by chance or without intending to
examples: He eventually found his way into acting.

definitions.definition: to become used to doing something/to lose the habit of doing something
examples: The women had got into the way of going up on the deck every evening.

definitions.definition: to prevent somebody from doing something; to prevent something from happening
examples: He wouldn't allow emotions to get in the way of him doing his job.

definitions.definition: to get or do what you want, especially when somebody has tried to stop you
examples: She always gets her own way in the end.

definitions.definition: to break or fall down
examples: The pillars gave way and a section of the roof collapsed.
Her numb leg gave way beneath her and she stumbled clumsily.

definitions.definition: to stop resisting somebody/something; to agree to do something that you do not want to do
examples: He refused to give way on any of the points.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to be or go first
examples: Give way to traffic already on the roundabout.

definitions.definition: to allow yourself to be very strongly affected by something, especially an emotion
examples: Flinging herself on the bed, she gave way to helpless misery.

definitions.definition: to be replaced by something
examples: The storm gave way to bright sunshine.
As he drew nearer, his anxiety gave way to relief.

definitions.definition: to have fullsexual intercoursewith somebody
to have known each other for a long time
examples: We go back a long way, he and I.

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.
A small amount of this paint goes a long way(= covers a large area).
(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something
examples: The new law goes a long way towards solving the problem.

definitions.definition: to make a special effort to do something
examples: He would always go out of his way to be friendly towards her.
She went out of her way to help them.

definitions.definition: to do as you choose, especially when somebody has advised you against it
examples: It's best to let her go her own way if you don't want a fight.
He's always gone his own way when it comes to design.

definitions.definition: to end a relationship with somebody
examples: When the business was sold they went their separate ways.
They decided to go their separate ways after being together for five years.
When we finished school, we all went our separate ways.

definitions.definition: to go in a different direction from somebody you have been travelling with
to travel in the same direction as somebody
examples: I'm going your way—I'll walk with you.

definitions.definition: to go well for you; to be in your favour
examples: By the third round he knew the fight was going his way.

definitions.definition: to die
by having an unpleasant experience or by making mistakes
examples: She won't listen to my advice so she'll just have tolearn the hard way.
He learned about the dangers of drugs the hard way.

definitions.definition: to have made a lot of progress
examples: We've come a long way since the early days of the project.
The study of genes has come a long way in recent years.

definitions.definition: to be a sensible person
to have or want to have the advantages of two different situations or ways of behaving that are impossible to combine
examples: You can't have it both ways. If you can afford to go out all the time, you can afford to pay off some of your debts.

definitions.definition: used to say in an angry way that although you are not happy about something that somebody has said, you are not going to argue
examples: Oh OK, then. Have it your own way!

definitions.definition: to have something arranged in the way that you want, even though other people want it to be arranged differently
examples: The German team weren't going to let the English have everything their own way.

definitions.definition: to need to make a lot of progress before you can achieve something
examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: used to say that something often happens in a particular way, especially when it is out of your control
examples: First love affairs have a way of not working out.
Fate has a way of changing the best of plans.

definitions.definition: to be good at dealing with somebody/something
examples: He has a way with small children.
She has a way with words(= is very good at expressing herself).

definitions.definition: to persuade somebody to have sex with you
used to explain what you would do if you had total control over something
examples: If I had my way, social media would be banned completely.

definitions.definition: of any type
examples: I don't approve of violence in any shape or form.
I can't stand insects in any shape or form.
I do not condone bullying in any way, shape or form.

definitions.definition: on a large/small scale
examples: The new delivery service has taken off in a big way.
Many people are investing in a small way in the stock market.
He then started spending money in a big way.

definitions.definition: (to be/become) pregnant
used to show that a statement has more than one meaning
examples: With the first goal he used his head in more ways than one.

definitions.definition: used to say what normally happens in a particular situation
examples: In the ordinary way, she's not a nervous person.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: to some extent; not completely
examples: In a way it was one of our biggest mistakes.

definitions.definition: in a manner that is appropriate to or typical of a person or thing but that may seem unusual to other people
examples: I expect she does love you in her own way.
The building is very attractive in its own way.
He was attractive in his own way.

definitions.definition: stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: You'll have to move—you're in my way.
I left them alone, as I felt I was in the way.
There were several rocks in the way.

definitions.definition: used in questions and negative sentences to talk about the types of something that are available
examples: There isn't much in the way of entertainment in this place.

definitions.definition: to avoid somebody
examples: Let's keep out of her way while she's in such a bad mood.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to make a lot of money easily and feel very pleased about it
to get yourself into or out of a situation by lying
to deliberately avoid seeing somebody/something
examples: Prison officers know what's going on, but look the other way.

definitions.definition: to become lost
examples: We lost our way in the dark.
She lost her way in the fog.

definitions.definition: to forget or move away from the purpose or reason for something
examples: I feel that the project has lost its way.
This project seems to have lost its way.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to pass; to allow somebody/something to take the place of somebody/something
examples: Make way for the Lord Mayor!
Tropical forest is felled to make way for grassland.

definitions.definition: to move or get somewhere; to make progress
examples: Will you be able tomake your own wayto the airport(= get there without help, a lift, etc.)?
Is this your plan formaking your way in the world?

definitions.definition: to stop behaving badly
(to take/find) an acceptable course of action that avoids two extreme positions
used to show that you are certain about something
examples: It was the wrong decision—there are no two ways about it.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no possibility that you will do something or that something will happen
examples: ‘Do you want to help?’ ‘No way!’
No way am I going to drive them there.
There's no way we could afford that sort of money.

definitions.definition: during the journey
examples: He stopped for breakfast on the way.
She grabbed her phone and bag on her way out.

definitions.definition: going or coming
examples: I'd better be on my way(= I must leave)soon.
The letter should be on its way to you.
Then he went off on his merry way.

definitions.definition: not yet born
examples: They've got three kids and one on the way.

definitions.definition: to make it possible for somebody to do something or for something to happen
examples: The agreement could open the way for the country to pay off its debts.

definitions.definition: in the opposite position, direction or order
examples: I think it should go on the other way round.

definitions.definition: the opposite situation
examples: I didn't leave you. It was the other way round(= you left me).

definitions.definition: in a safe place where somebody/something cannot be hurt or injured or do any damage to somebody/something
examples: She put the knife in a drawer, out of harm’s way.
I prefer the children to play in the garden where they’re out of harm’s way.

definitions.definition: no longer stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: I moved my legs out of the way so that she could get past.
I didn't say anything until Dad was out of the way.

definitions.definition: finished; dealt with
examples: Our region is poised for growth once the election is out of the way.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences to mean ‘unusual’
examples: She had obviously noticed nothing out of the way.

definitions.definition: not on the route that you planned to take
examples: I'd love a lift home—if it's not out of your way.

definitions.definition: a point at which two people or groups of people decide to separate
examples: These events led to a final parting of the ways.

definitions.definition: to create a situation in which somebody will be able to do something or something can happen
examples: This decision paved the way for changes in employment rights for women.

definitions.definition: to make enough money to pay what it costs to keep it going
examples: The bridge is still not paying its way.

definitions.definition: to pay for everything yourself without having to rely on anyone else’s money
to walk carefully, choosing the safest, driest, etc. place to put your feet
examples: She picked her way delicately over the rough ground.
We picked our way carefully over the jagged rocks.

definitions.definition: to make somebody annoyed or angry, often without intending to, by doing or saying something that offends them
examples: She tends to rub people up the wrong way.

definitions.definition: to realize or admit that you have done something wrong and decide to change your behaviour
examples: Will they realize the error of their ways before it is too late?

definitions.definition: to find that it is possible or convenient to do something
examples: Small builders cannot see their way clear to take on many trainees.

definitions.definition: to get an idea of what is likely to happen before deciding what to do
to do something first so that other people can follow
to make it easier for somebody/something to develop or make progress
examples: These negotiations are intended to smooth the path to a peace treaty.
We’re trying to smooth the way for women who want to resume their careers.

definitions.definition: to (not) prevent somebody from doing something
examples: If you believe you can make her happy, I won't stand in your way.
He wanted to go to university and would let nothing stand in his way.

definitions.definition: to bebisexual(= sexually attracted to both men and women)
to end a difficult situation by choosing the simplest solution even if it is not the best one
examples: He took the easy way out and didn’t go to the meeting.
She took the easy way out and paid someone to write the article for her.

definitions.definition: to be offended by a remark that was not intended to be offensive
examples: Don't take this the wrong way, but maybe you're trying a bit too hard.

definitions.definition: to make excuses and give reasons for not doing something; to manage to get yourself out of a difficult situation
examples: I managed to talk my way out of having to give a speech.

definitions.definition: that is the situation and we cannot change it, so we must accept it
there are many different ways to achieve something
in my opinion
having started
examples: Preparations are well under way for a week of special events in May.

definitions.definition: a plan of action that is likely to be successful
examples: Personal electronic health records are the way forward for effective healthcare in the future.

definitions.definition: something that allows you to join a group of people, an industry, etc. that it is difficult to join, or to understand something that it is difficult to understand
the typical pattern of behaviour of a person or group
examples: the American way of life
She loved the Spanish way of life and immediately felt at home there.
the beliefs and practices of the Hindu way of life

definitions.definition: the way that most people behave; the way that things happen, which you cannot change
examples: The rich and powerful make the decisions—that's the way of the world.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody else has either to agree with your opinion or to leave
the methods and materials available for doing something
examples: ways and means of raising money
Newspapers have ways and means of getting hold of secret information.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that you are pleased about something they have done
examples: Good work, guys! Way to go!
Way to go! I wish I could do that!

definitions.definition: the way to make somebody like or love you
examples: The way to a man's heart is through his stomach(= by giving him good food).

definitions.definition: if you really want to do something then you will find a way of doing it
a series of victories or successes
examples: He'll look to continue his winning ways with a victory in Mexico on 16 February.

definitions.definition: to do something from beginning to end, especially when it takes a lot of time or effort
examples: She worked her way through the pile of documents.

definitions.definition: to have a job or series of jobs while studying, travelling, etc. in order to pay for your education, etc.
to move regularly to a more senior position in a company
examples: He worked his way up from messenger boy to account executive.

origin: Old Englishweg, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwegand GermanWeg, from a base meaning ‘move, carry’.

definitions.definition: the way in which two people or things are not like each other; the way in which somebody/something has changed
examples: Americans and Brits speak the same language, but there arebigculturaldifferences.
difference between A and BThere are nosignificant differencesbetween the education systems of the two countries.
He was studying thesimilarities and differencesbetween humans and animals.
I can nevertell the difference(= distinguish)between the twins.
difference in somethingThere's no difference in the results.
There is afundamental differencein approach to conservation in Japan.
She noticed amarked differencein the children on her second visit.
Her work questions notions of gender difference.
What a difference!You look great with your hair like that.
difference among somethingDifferences among beetle species may prove to be a key factor.
despite differencesDespite their differences, the two are great friends.

definitions.definition: the amount that something is greater or smaller than something else
examples: difference in somethingWe measured the difference in temperature.
difference between A and BThe difference between the two numbers gives you the profit.
There's an age difference of six years between the boys(= one is six years older than the other).
difference in something between A and BThere is not much difference in price between the two brands.
I'll lend you £500 and you'll have to find the difference(= the rest of the money that you need).

definitions.definition: adisagreementbetween people
examples: Wehave our differences, but she's still my sister.
Why don't yousettle your differencesand be friends again?
There wasa difference of opinionover who had won.

definitions.definition: to stop not being friendly and become friends again
examples: After not speaking to each other for years, the two brothers decided to bury the hatchet.

definitions.definition: a point that somebody wants to suggest is a difference when in fact it is not different at all
examples: His government did not brand them terrorists, but rather ‘an entity that supports terrorism’. That may prove to be a distinction without a difference.

definitions.definition: to have an important effect on somebody/something; to make somebody feel better
examples: A few kind words at the right time make all the difference.
Just five minutes' exercise a day could make all the difference.

definitions.definition: to have an effect/no effect on somebody/something
examples: The rain didn't make much difference to the game.
Your age shouldn't make any difference to whether you get the job or not.
Changing schoolsmade a big differenceto my life.
What difference will it make if he knows or not?
I don't think itmakes a lot of differencewhat colour it is(= it is not important).
‘Shall we go on Friday or Saturday?’ ‘It makes no difference (to me).’

definitions.definition: used to say that you think the differences between two things are not important
examples: ‘That's not a xylophone, it's a glockenspiel.’ ‘Same difference.’

definitions.definition: to agree to forget about yourdisagreements
examples: We need to sink our differences and present a united opposition to the plan.

definitions.definition: (when discussing a price, etc.) to agree on an amount that is at an equal distance between the two amounts that have been suggested
examples: I offered €200 but he wanted €300. In the end, we split the difference and I paid him €250.
(figurative)I wanted to leave early and Ian wanted to leave late, so we split the difference and left at noon.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is interesting or unusual
examples: The traditional backpack with a difference—it's waterproof.
If you want a holiday with a difference, come to Iceland.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize how much difference there is between two things
examples: There's a world of difference between liking someone and loving them.

origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latindifferentia‘difference’, fromdifferent-‘carrying away’, from the verbdifferre, fromdis-‘from, away’ +ferre‘bring, carry’.

definitions.definition: to carry or move something from one place to another
examples: take somethingRemember to take your coat when you leave.
take something with youI forgot to take my bag with me when I got off the bus.
take somebody somethingShall I take my host family a gift?
take something to somebody/somethingCan you take my suit to the dry-cleaner's?
You need to take your laptop to the technician.
take something for somebody/somethingDon't forget to take a present for Catherine's new baby.

definitions.definition: to go with somebody from one place to another, especially to guide or lead them
examples: take somebodyIt's too far to walk—I'll take you by car.
take somebody to somethingA boy took us to our room.
take somebody doing somethingI'm taking the kids swimming later.
take somebody to do somethingThe boys were taken to see their grandparents most weekends.

definitions.definition: to make somebody/something go from one level, situation, etc. to another
examples: Her energy and talent took her to the top of her profession.
The new loan takes the total debt to $100 000.
I'd like to take my argument a stage further.
He believes he has the skills to take the club forward.
We'll take the matter forward at our next meeting(= discuss it further).

definitions.definition: to need or require a particular amount of time
examples: take somethingThe process took about a year.
The journey to the airport takes about half an hour.
take something to do somethingIt takesabout half an hour to get to the airport.
That cut is taking a long time to heal.
The official seemed to take hours to examine my passport.
take somebody somethingThat should only take you ten minutes.
take somebody something to do somethingIt took her three hours to repair her bike.
It'll take her time to recover from the illness.
take something for somebody to do somethingIt'lltake time(= take a long time)for her to recover from the illness.
+ adv.I need a shower—Iwon't take long.

definitions.definition: to photograph somebody/something
examples: take somethingto take apicture/photo/shotof somebody/something
tohave your picture/photo taken

definitions.definition: to use a form of transport, a road, a path, etc. to go to a place
examples: totake the bus/train
to take a cab/plane
Take the second road on the right.
It's more interesting to take the coast road.

definitions.definition: to put your hands or arms around somebody/something and hold them/it; to reach for somebody/something and hold them/it
examples: I passed him the rope and he took it.
Free newspapers: please take one.
Can you take(= hold)the baby for a moment?
He took her hand/took her by the hand(= held her hand, for example to lead her somewhere).
She took the child in her arms and kissed him.

definitions.definition: to remove something/somebody from a place or a person
examples: Will you take your books off the table?
The sign must be taken down.
He took some keys out of his pocket.
My name had been taken off the list.
She was playing with a knife, so I took it away from her.
(informal)She was playing with a knife, so I took it off her.
(figurative)The new sports centre willtake the pressure offthe old one.

definitions.definition: to remove something without permission or by mistake
examples: Someone has taken my scarf.
Did the burglars take anything valuable?
All she had taken was her passport and driving licence.
(figurative)The stormstook the livesof 50 people.

definitions.definition: to get something from a particular source
examples: take something from somethingThe scientists are taking water samples from the river.
The machine takes its name from its inventor.
take something out of somethingPart of her article is taken straight(= copied)out of my book.

definitions.definition: to sit down in or use a chair, etc.
examples: Are these seats taken?
Come in;take a seat.

definitions.definition: to eat, drink, etc. something
examples: Do you take sugar in your coffee?
The doctor has given me some medicine to take for my cough.
He started taking drugs(= illegal drugs)at college.

definitions.definition: to find out and record something; to write something down
examples: The police officer took my name and address.
Did youtake notesin the class?

definitions.definition: to do an exam or a test
examples: When did you take your driving test?

definitions.definition: to study a subject at school, college, etc.
examples: She is planning totake a coursein web design.
Last year I started taking yoga classes.
How many subjects are you taking this year?

definitions.definition: to use a particular course of action in order to deal with or achieve something
examples: take somethingWe need totakea differentapproachto the problem.
The government istaking actionto combat drug abuse.

definitions.definition: used with nouns to say that somebody is doing something, performing an action, etc.
examples: totake a look
totake a break
totake a shotat somebody/something
to take a step/walk/stroll
totake a bath/shower/wash
to take a bite/drink/sip
totake adeepbreath
(British English)We willtake a decisionon the matter next week.
She's taken a nasty fall and has her leg in plaster.
Experts have urged ministers totakethe necessarystepsto resolve the issue.

definitions.definition: to test or measure something
examples: to take somebody’s temperature
I need to have my blood pressure taken.

definitions.definition: to capture a place or person; to get control of something
examples: take somethingThe rebels succeeded in taking the town.
The state hastaken control ofthe company.
take something from somebodyThe militants took the city from government forces.
take somebody + nounThe rebels took him prisoner.
He was taken prisoner by the rebels.
The bank robbers took several employees hostage.

definitions.definition: to choose, buy or rent something
examples: I'll take the grey jacket.
We took a room at the hotel for two nights.

definitions.definition: to buy a newspaper or magazine regularly
examples: We take the ‘Express’.

definitions.definition: to accept or receive something
examples: If they offer me the job, I'll take it.
She was accused of taking bribes.
I'll take the call in my office.
Itakefullresponsibilityfor my actions.
Why should Itake the blamefor somebody else's mistakes?
If you choose deceit, then you must take the consequences of your actions.
If youtake my adviceyou'll have nothing more to do with him.
Will you take $10 for the book(= will you sell it for $10)?
The store took(= sold goods worth)$100 000 last week.

definitions.definition: to act in response to an opportunity
examples: When the bus stopped for fuel, wetook the opportunityto get something to eat.
England failed totake their chancesand had to settle for a draw.
He isn't afraid totake risks.
Take the initiative and fill your life with exciting experiences.

definitions.definition: to accept somebody as a customer, patient, etc.
examples: The school doesn't take boys(= only has girls).
The dentist can't take any new patients.

definitions.definition: to experience or be affected by something
examples: The school took the full force of the explosion.
Can the ropestake the strain(= not break)?
The team took a terrible beating.
I'm afraid your husband has taken a turn for the worse.

definitions.definition: to be able to bear something
examples: She can't take criticism.
I don't think I can take much more of this heat.
I find his attitude a little hard to take.
It was clear that she wasn't going to take any nonsense.

definitions.definition: to react to something/somebody in a particular way
examples: He took the criticism surprisingly well.
These threats are not to be taken lightly.
I wish you'dtake me seriously.
I was being sarcastic but you took my comment literally.
She took it in the spirit in which it was intended.

definitions.definition: to understand or consider something in a particular way
examples: take somethingHow am I supposed to take that remark?
Taken overall, the project was a success.
take something as somethingShe took what he said as a compliment.
take something to do somethingWhat did you take his comments to mean?

definitions.definition: to consider somebody/something to be somebody/something, especially when you are wrong
examples: take somebody/something for somebody/somethingEven the experts took the painting for a genuine Van Gogh.
Of course I didn't do it!What do you take me for(= what sort of person do you think I am)?
take somebody/something to be somebody/somethingI took the man with him to be his father.

definitions.definition: to have a particular feeling, opinion or attitude
examples: My parents alwaystook an interestin my hobbies.
Don'ttake offence(= be offended)at what I said.
I took a dislike to him.
Hetakes the viewthat children are responsible for their own actions.

definitions.definition: to reduce one number by the value of another
examples: take A (away) (from B)Take 5 from 12 and you're left with 7.
B take away A(informal)80 take away 5 is 75.

definitions.definition: used to introduce somebody/something as an example
examples: Lots of couples have problems in the first year of marriage. Take Ann and Paul.

definitions.definition: to have or start to have a particular form, position or state
examples: Our next class willtake the formof a debate.
The new presidenttakes officein January.
The home side had a chance totake the lead.

definitions.definition: to need or require something in order to happen or be done
examples: take somebody/something to do somethingIt only takes one careless driver to cause an accident.
It doesn't take much to make her angry.
take something(informal)He didn't take much persuading(= he was easily persuaded).

definitions.definition: to use something in order to work
examples: The buses have been adapted to take biofuel.

definitions.definition: to wear a particular size in shoes or clothes
examples: What size shoes do you take?

definitions.definition: to have enough space for something/somebody; to be able to hold or contain a particular quantity
examples: The bus can take 60 passengers.
The tank takes 50 litres.

definitions.definition: to be the teacher or leader in a class or a religious service
examples: take somebody (for something)The head teacher usually takes us for French.
take somethingMr Perkins took the morning service.

definitions.definition: to go over or around something
examples: The horse took the first fence well.
He takes bends much too fast.

definitions.definition: to kick or throw the ball from a fixed or agreed position
examples: to take a penalty/free kick/corner

definitions.definition: to use a particular method to find out people’s opinions
examples: to take a vote/poll/survey

definitions.definition: to be successful; to work
examples: The skin graft failed to take.

definitions.definition: to have or require something when used in a sentence or other structure
examples: The verb ‘rely’ takes the preposition ‘on’.

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not care if somebody accepts or rejects your offer
used to say that somebody does not have a strong opinion about something
examples: Dancing? I can take it or leave it.

definitions.definition: to have the qualities, ability, etc. needed to be successful
used to say that you cannot trust somebody to behave well in public
examples: You haven’t spilled your coffee again! I can’t take you anywhere!

definitions.definition: to accept something/somebody without wishing it/them to be different or without thinking about it/them very much in advance
examples: She takes life as it comes.

definitions.definition: to suppose; to assume
examples: I take it you won't be coming to the party?

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that what you are going to say is the truth
examples: Take it from me—he'll be a millionaire before he's 30.

definitions.definition: to make somebody physically or mentally tired
examples: Looking after small children really takes it out of you.

definitions.definition: to decide to do something without asking permission or advice
to need a lot of effort or time; to be very difficult to do
used as an exclamation when you are hitting somebody or attacking them in some other way

origin: late Old Englishtacan‘get (especially by force), capture’, from Old Norsetaka‘grasp, lay hold of’, of unknown ultimate origin.

definitions.definition: a large number or amount of somebody/something
examples: What a lot of presents!
A lot of people are coming to the meeting.
black coffee with lots of sugar
I saw a lot of her(= I saw her often)last summer.

origin: Old Englishhlot(noun), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlot, GermanLos. The original meaning was ‘by lot’ and (by extension) the sense ‘a portion assigned to someone’; this gave rise to the other noun senses. The pronoun and adverb uses date from the early 19th cent.

definitions.definition: to turn your eyes in a particular direction
examples: Look closelyand tell me what you see.
If youlook carefullyyou can just see our house from here.
‘Has the mail come yet?’ ‘I'll look and see.’
Look! I'm sure that's Jennifer Lawrence!
Don't look now, but there's someone staring at you!
look at somebody/somethingShe looked at me and smiled.
look + adv./prep.I got up and looked out of the window.
She looked across to the other side of the room.

definitions.definition: to try to find somebody/something
examples: look for somebody/somethingWhere have you been? We've been looking for you.
Are you still looking for a job?
We’re looking for someone with experience for this post.
They are always looking for ways to save money.
look + adv./prep.I can't find my book—I've looked everywhere.

definitions.definition: to pay attention to something
examples: look at somethingLook at the time! We're going to be late.
look where, what, etc…Can't you look where you're going?

definitions.definition: to seem; to appear
examples: How do I look?
+ adj.Youlook great!
The gardenlooks nice.
Don't worry—it looks worse than it is.
The filmlooks likelyto offend many people.
That book looks interesting.
look like somebody/somethingThat looks like an interesting book.
look like somebody/something to somebodyIt doesn't look like fun to me.
+ nounYou made me look a complete fool!
That looks an interesting book.

definitions.definition: to have a particular appearance
examples: look like somebody/somethingThat photograph doesn't look like her at all.
‘What does your cousin look like?’ ‘He's tall and thin with brown hair.’
look as if…/as though…You look as though you slept badly.

definitions.definition: to seem likely to happen or be true
examples: Itlooks like rain(= it looks as if it's going to rain).
look as if…/as though…It doesn't look as if we'll be moving after all.
look to somebody as if…/as though…It looks to me as though the company is in real trouble.
look like…(informal)They don’t look like they’re trying to win.
look to somebody like…(informal)It looks to me like they have a plan.

definitions.definition: to face a particular direction
examples: The house looks east.
The hotel looks out over the harbour.
The kitchen looks onto the garden.

definitions.definition: used in a shop to say that you are not ready to buy something
examples: ‘Can I help you?’ ‘I'm just looking, thank you.’

definitions.definition: to try to find ways of doing something
examples: The government is looking to reduce inflation.

definitions.definition: to be considered bad behaviour or bad manners
examples: It looks bad not going to your own brother's wedding.

definitions.definition: to show that something bad might happen
examples: He's had another heart attack; things are looking bad for him, I'm afraid.

definitions.definition: to show success or that something good might happen
examples: This year's sales figures are looking good.

definitions.definition: used to protest about something
examples: Now look here, it wasn't my fault.

definitions.definition: used to give an example that proves what you are saying or makes it clearer
examples: Look how lazy we've become.
Be careful climbing that ladder. Look what happened last time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is exactly what somebody needs and so they do not need to consider any other options
examples: Those looking for an enjoyable evening need look no further than the hotel's nightclub.

definitions.definition: to look at somebody in a careful or critical way
to (not) have your normal healthy appearance
examples: You're not looking yourself today(= you look tired or ill/sick).

definitions.definition: to become more and more successful
examples: Her first novel was published in 2007 and since then she hasn't looked back.

definitions.definition: not attractive
judging by the appearance of somebody/something
examples: To look at him you'd never think he was nearly fifty.

origin: Old Englishlōcian(verb), of West Germanic origin; related to German dialectlugen.

definitions.definition: to continue to live or exist
examples: She died ten years ago but her memory lives on.
He lived on for fifteen years after his wife died.

definitions.definition: to eat a particular type of food to live
examples: Small birds live mainly on insects.

definitions.definition: to eat only or a lot of a particular type of food
examples: She lives on burgers.

definitions.definition: to have enough money for the basic things you need to live
examples: You can't live on forty pounds a week.
My salary isn't enough for us to live on.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a larger number or amount of
examples: I want some more!
more somethingmore bread/cars
Only two more days to go!
people with more money than sense
more of something/somebodyI can't stand much more of this.
I hope we'll see more of you(= see you again or more often).
more than…She earns a lot more than I do.
There is room forno more thanthree cars.

definitions.definition: continuing to become larger in number or amount
examples: More and more people are banking online.
She spends more and more time alone in her room.

origin: Old Englishmāra, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchmeerand Germanmehr.

examples: a population of half a million
tens of millions of dollars
It must be worth a million(= pounds, dollars, etc.).
millions of years old

definitions.definition: a very large amount
examples: I still have a million things to do.
There were millions of people there.
Hemadehismillions(= all his money)on currency deals.

definitions.definition: to look/feel extremely good
examples: Wow, you look like a million dollars.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that is very unusual or special
examples: He's a man in a million.

origin: late Middle English: from Old French, probably from Italianmilione, frommille‘thousand’ + the augmentative suffix-one.

definitions.definition: a phone that does not have wires and works by radio, that you can carry with you and use anywhere
examples: Please make sure all mobile phones are switched off during the performance.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to control or be in charge of a business, a team, an organization, land, etc.
examples: to manage a business/factory/bank/hotel/soccer team
to manage a department/project
I think that Forestry Tasmania manages the forest.
The school has been badly managed for several years.
We need people who are good at managing.

definitions.definition: to succeed in doing something, especially something difficult
examples: I don't know exactly how we'llmanage it, but we will, somehow.
Can you manage another piece of cake?(= eat one)
manage to do somethingWe managed to get to the airport in time.
How did you manage to persuade him?
Shesomehow managedto escape through the window.
(humorous)He always manages to say the wrong thing.
They managed to find a place to stay.
We couldn't have managed without you.
‘Need any help?’ ‘No, thanks. I can manage.’

definitions.definition: to succeed in achieving or producing something
examples: In spite of his disappointment, he managed a weak smile.
Davies manages light humour without seeming silly.

definitions.definition: to be able to solve your problems, deal with a difficult situation, etc.
examples: She's 82 and can't manage on her own any more.
manage with/without somebody/somethingHow do you manage without a car?
I can manage without a dishwasher.
I don’t know how she manages on her own with four kids.

definitions.definition: to be able to live or survive without having much money, support, sleep, etc.
examples: young families who are just about managing
She was finding it difficult to manage financially.
manage on somethingHe has to manage on less than £100 a week.
Many find it difficult to manage on their weekly income.
He generally managed on five hours' sleep a night.
manage with somethingEveryone will just have to manage with less.

definitions.definition: to use money, time, information, etc. in a sensible way
examples: Don't tell me how to manage my affairs.
a computer program that helps you manage data efficiently
This enables pension funds to manage risk.

definitions.definition: to be able to do something at a particular time
examples: Let's meet up again—can you manage next week sometime?

definitions.definition: to keep somebody/something under control; to be able to deal with somebody/something
examples: It's like trying to manage an unruly child.
Can you manage that suitcase?
She manages horses better than anyone I know.
I really liked the chapter about how to manage stress.

definitions.definition: to control how land is used, so that it is used in a sensible way
examples: The landowners manage their land to provide habitat for the desired species.
All the timber comes from sustainably managed forests.

origin: mid 16th cent. (in the sense ‘put (a horse) through the paces of the manège’): from Italianmaneggiare, based on Latinmanus‘hand’.

definitions.definition: to have something as a meaning in the same or another language
examples: mean somethingWhat does this sentence mean?
What does ‘lark’ mean?
The word ‘Kuching’ means ‘cat’ in Malay.
Many languages use a single word to mean both music and dance.
What is meant by‘batch processing’?

definitions.definition: to have something as a meaning; to represent something
examples: mean something by somethingWhat do we mean by democracy?
mean something to somebodyDoes the name ‘Jos Vos’mean anything to you(= do you know who he is)?
mean (that)…The flashing light means (that) you must stop.
mean somethingThe Olympics havecome to meana wide variety of things.

definitions.definition: to intend to say something on a particular occasion
examples: mean something by somethingWhat did he mean by that remark?
mean something‘Perhaps we should try another approach.’ ‘What do you mean?(= I don't understand what you are suggesting.)’
‘What's that supposed to mean?’ demanded John.
What do you mean, you thought I wouldn't mind?(= of course I mind and I am very angry)
What she means isthat there's no point in waiting here.
I always found him a little strange,if you know what I mean(= if you understand what I mean by ‘strange’).
I know what you mean(= I understand and feel sympathy). I hated learning to drive too.
(informal)It was like—weird.Know what I mean?
I see what you mean(= I understand although I may not agree), but I still think it's worth trying.
See what I mean(= I was right and this proves it, doesn't it)? She never agrees to anything I suggest.
‘But Pete doesn't know we're here!’ ‘That's what I mean!(= that's what I have been trying to tell you.)’
Do you mean Ann Smith or Mary Smith?
mean (that)…Did he mean (that) he was dissatisfied with our service?
You mean(= are you telling me)we have to start all over again?

definitions.definition: to have something as a purpose or intention
examples: mean somethingWhat did she mean byleaving so early(= why did she do it)?
Don't laugh!I mean it(= I am serious).
Hemeans trouble(= to cause trouble).
mean something as somethingDon't be upset—I'm sure she meant it as a compliment.
mean what…Hemeans what he says(= is not joking, exaggerating, etc.).
mean something for somebody/somethingThe chair was clearly meant for a child.
Don't be angry. I'm sure shemeant it for the best(= intended to be helpful).
mean to do somethingShe means to succeed.
I'm sorry I hurt you.I didn't mean to.
I'm feeling very guilty—I've been meaningto call my parents for days, but still haven't got around to it.
mean somebody/something to do somethingI didn't mean you to read the letter.
You're meant to(= you are supposed to)pay before you go in.
mean (that)…(formal)I never meant (that) you should come alone.

definitions.definition: to have something as a result or a likely result
examples: mean somethingSpending too much now will mean a shortage of cash next year.
Being frugaldoesn't necessarily meandoing without.
Lower energy consumption means less pollution.
Touching the wires means instant death.
mean to be/do somethingDo you have any ideawhat it means tobe poor?
mean (that)…We’ll have to be careful with money butthat doesn’t mean(that) we can’t enjoy ourselves.
mean doing somethingThis new order will mean working overtime.
mean somebody/something doing somethingThe injury could mean him missing next week's game.
High unemployment means people are spending less.

definitions.definition: to be of value or importance to somebody
examples: Your friendship means a great deal to me.
$20means a lot(= represents a lot of money)when you live on $100 a week.
Moneymeans nothing to him.
Her childrenmean the world to her.

definitions.definition: to intend somebody to be or do something
examples: be meant for something/somebodyIwas never meant forthe army(= did not have the qualities needed to become a soldier).
Duncan and Makikowere meant for each other(= are very suitable as partners).
mean somebody/something to be somethingHis father meant him to be an engineer.
She did everything to get the two of them together, but I guess it just wasn'tmeant to be.

definitions.definition: to be generally considered to be something
examples: This restaurant is meant to be excellent.

definitions.definition: used to explain or correct what you have just said
examples: It was so boring—I mean, nothing happened for the first hour!
She's English—Scottish, I mean.

definitions.definition: to be serious in your intentions
examples: He has the look of a man who means business.
He says he's going to make changes, and I think he means business.

definitions.definition: to not have any intention of hurting somebody
used to emphasize what you are saying or to ask somebody if they really mean what they say
examples: I mean to say, you should have known how he would react!
Do you mean to say you've lost it?

definitions.definition: to have good intentions, although their effect may not be good

origin: verbOld Englishmænan, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchmeenenand Germanmeinen, from an Indo-European root shared bymind.

definitions.definition: a method, style or manner of doing something
examples: I prefer to do things the easy way.
I love him and I think he feelsthe same way.
way to do somethingI'm sure we canfind a wayto make this work.
Using the subway isthe only way toavoid the traffic.
(informal, disapproving)That's no wayto speak to your mother!
way of doing somethingI'm not happy with this way of working.
way (that)…It's not what you say—it's the way that you say it.
Ihate the wayshe always criticizes me.
I told you we should have done itmy way!
in the… wayShe dressesin the same wayas her sister.
Infectious diseases can be acquired in several ways.
In this way, we can track the storms as they move across the ocean.
in the way (that)…It works in the way you'd expect.
New technology has radically changedthe way in whichthis work is done.
in a way that…She writes in a way that is easy to understand.
I generally get what I wantone way or another(= by some means).

definitions.definition: a particular manner or style of behaviour
examples: in a… wayThey grinned at her in a friendly way.
It was not his wayto admit that he had made a mistake.
Don't worry if she seems quiet—it's just her way.
He was showing off,as is the waywith adolescent boys.

definitions.definition: the typical way of behaving and living of a particular group of people
examples: After ten years I'm used to the strange British ways.
She always said that the old ways were the best.

definitions.definition: a route or road that you take in order to reach a place
examples: toask somebody the way
Do you know the way?
way to…Can youtell me the wayto Leicester Square?
way from… to…thebest/quickest/shortest wayfrom A to B
We went thelong way round.

definitions.definition: the route along which somebody/something is moving; the route that somebody/something would take if there was nothing stopping them/it
examples: Get out of my way!I'm in a hurry.
Riot police with shields were blocking the demonstrators' way.
along the wayUnfortunately they ran into a snowstorm along the way.
+ adv./prep.We fought our way through the dense vegetation.
(figurative)We will eventually find a way out of the crisis.

definitions.definition: a road, path or street for travelling along
examples: There's a way across the fields.

definitions.definition: used in the names of streets
examples: 106 Headley Way

definitions.definition: a particular direction; in a particular direction
examples: Which way did they go?
We just missed a car comingthe other way.
Lookboth ways(= look left and right)before crossing the road.
Make sure that sign'sthe right way up.
The pipe could be moved a few incheseither way.
Kids were runningthis way and that(= in all directions).
They decided to split the money four ways(= between four different people).
(figurative)Which way(= for which party)are you going to vote?

definitions.definition: a means of going into or leaving a place, such as a door or gate
examples: theway in/out
They escaped out the back way.

definitions.definition: a distance or period of time between two points
examples: A little way up on the left is the Museum of Modern Art.
We had to goa long waybefore we found a place to eat.
September wasa long way off.
You cameall this wayto see us?
(figurative)The economy still hasa way to goto a full recovery.
(North American English, informal)We still havea ways to go.

definitions.definition: an area, a part of a country, etc.
examples: over/down… wayI think he lives somewhere over Greenwich way.
down my/our/your wayI'll stop by and see you next time I'm down your way.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something
examples: I have changed in every way.
It's been quite a day,one way and another(= for several reasons).

definitions.definition: a particular condition or state
examples: in a… wayThe economy's in a bad way.
I don't know how we're going to manage,the way things are.

definitions.definition: on the other side of the street, etc.
examples: Music blared from the open window of the house across the way.

definitions.definition: during the whole journey/period of time
examples: She didn't speak a word to me all the way back home.
the whole way to Arizona
This bus doesn't go all the way so you'll have to change.
I watched the movie the whole way through.

definitions.definition: completely; as much as it takes to achieve what you want
examples: I'm fighting him all the way.
You can feel that the audience is with her all the way.

definitions.definition: as you make progress with something
examples: You'll learn something along the way.

definitions.definition: used to say that things often happen in a particular way, especially when it is not convenient
however you choose to look at a situation
to behave or do things in a particular manner because it is part of your character
examples: It's not his fault he's so pompous—he was born that way.

definitions.definition: to be easy compared with what came before
examples: It's downhill all the way from here. We'll soon be finished.

definitions.definition: to become worse or less successful
examples: I started work as a journalist and it was downhill all the way for my health.
It's been all downhill for his career since then, with four defeats in five games.

definitions.definition: to have habits or opinions that you have had for a long time and that you do not want to change
examples: Grandma is so set in her ways.

definitions.definition: to have nearly achieved something and be going to achieve it soon
examples: She is well on the way to recovery.
He is well on the way to establishing himself among the top ten players in the world.

definitions.definition: by a great amount
examples: He was the best by a long way.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a comment or question that is not directly related to what you have been talking about
examples: By the way, I found that book you were looking for.
What's the time, by the way?
Oh by the way, if you see Jackie, tell her I'll call her this evening.

definitions.definition: by a route that includes the place mentioned
examples: The artist recently arrived in Paris from Bulgaria by way of Vienna.
She came to TV by way of drama school.

definitions.definition: as a form of something; for something; as a means of something
examples: He received £600 by way of compensation from the company.
She rolled her eyes by way of an answer and left.

definitions.definition: to start to live or behave in a different way from before
examples: He was in trouble with the police as a teenager but now he’s completely changed his ways.
Your father is unlikely to change his ways now.

definitions.definition: to gradually achieve something or move somewhere by being determined and using a lot of effort
examples: She clawed her way to the top of her profession.
Slowly, he clawed his way out from under the collapsed building.

definitions.definition: to remove things that are stopping the progress or movement of something
examples: The ruling could clear the way for extradition proceedings.

definitions.definition: to happen to you by chance, or when you were not expecting it
examples: He took the first job that came his way.
Have any interesting articles come your way recently?

definitions.definition: to have two opposite effects or results
used to say that it does not matter which one of two possibilities happens, is chosen or is true
examples: Was it his fault or not? Either way, an explanation is due.
We could meet today or tomorrow—I don't mind one way or the other.

definitions.definition: in all directions
examples: Her hair tumbled every which way.

definitions.definition: to move along carefully, for example when it is dark, by touching walls, objects, etc.
to be careful about how you do things, usually because you are in a situation that you are not familiar with
examples: She was new in the job, still feeling her way.

definitions.definition: to discover the right route (to a place)
examples: I hope you can find your way home.

definitions.definition: to come to a place or a situation by chance or without intending to
examples: He eventually found his way into acting.

definitions.definition: to become used to doing something/to lose the habit of doing something
examples: The women had got into the way of going up on the deck every evening.

definitions.definition: to prevent somebody from doing something; to prevent something from happening
examples: He wouldn't allow emotions to get in the way of him doing his job.

definitions.definition: to get or do what you want, especially when somebody has tried to stop you
examples: She always gets her own way in the end.

definitions.definition: to break or fall down
examples: The pillars gave way and a section of the roof collapsed.
Her numb leg gave way beneath her and she stumbled clumsily.

definitions.definition: to stop resisting somebody/something; to agree to do something that you do not want to do
examples: He refused to give way on any of the points.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to be or go first
examples: Give way to traffic already on the roundabout.

definitions.definition: to allow yourself to be very strongly affected by something, especially an emotion
examples: Flinging herself on the bed, she gave way to helpless misery.

definitions.definition: to be replaced by something
examples: The storm gave way to bright sunshine.
As he drew nearer, his anxiety gave way to relief.

definitions.definition: to have fullsexual intercoursewith somebody
to have known each other for a long time
examples: We go back a long way, he and I.

definitions.definition: to last a long time
examples: She seems to make her money go a long way.
A small amount of this paint goes a long way(= covers a large area).
(ironic)I find thata little ofJerry's company cango a long way(= I quickly get tired of being with him).

definitions.definition: to help very much/a little in achieving something
examples: The new law goes a long way towards solving the problem.

definitions.definition: to make a special effort to do something
examples: He would always go out of his way to be friendly towards her.
She went out of her way to help them.

definitions.definition: to do as you choose, especially when somebody has advised you against it
examples: It's best to let her go her own way if you don't want a fight.
He's always gone his own way when it comes to design.

definitions.definition: to end a relationship with somebody
examples: When the business was sold they went their separate ways.
They decided to go their separate ways after being together for five years.
When we finished school, we all went our separate ways.

definitions.definition: to go in a different direction from somebody you have been travelling with
to travel in the same direction as somebody
examples: I'm going your way—I'll walk with you.

definitions.definition: to go well for you; to be in your favour
examples: By the third round he knew the fight was going his way.

definitions.definition: to die
by having an unpleasant experience or by making mistakes
examples: She won't listen to my advice so she'll just have tolearn the hard way.
He learned about the dangers of drugs the hard way.

definitions.definition: to have made a lot of progress
examples: We've come a long way since the early days of the project.
The study of genes has come a long way in recent years.

definitions.definition: to be a sensible person
to have or want to have the advantages of two different situations or ways of behaving that are impossible to combine
examples: You can't have it both ways. If you can afford to go out all the time, you can afford to pay off some of your debts.

definitions.definition: used to say in an angry way that although you are not happy about something that somebody has said, you are not going to argue
examples: Oh OK, then. Have it your own way!

definitions.definition: to have something arranged in the way that you want, even though other people want it to be arranged differently
examples: The German team weren't going to let the English have everything their own way.

definitions.definition: to need to make a lot of progress before you can achieve something
examples: She still has a long way to go before she's fully fit.

definitions.definition: used to say that something often happens in a particular way, especially when it is out of your control
examples: First love affairs have a way of not working out.
Fate has a way of changing the best of plans.

definitions.definition: to be good at dealing with somebody/something
examples: He has a way with small children.
She has a way with words(= is very good at expressing herself).

definitions.definition: to persuade somebody to have sex with you
used to explain what you would do if you had total control over something
examples: If I had my way, social media would be banned completely.

definitions.definition: of any type
examples: I don't approve of violence in any shape or form.
I can't stand insects in any shape or form.
I do not condone bullying in any way, shape or form.

definitions.definition: on a large/small scale
examples: The new delivery service has taken off in a big way.
Many people are investing in a small way in the stock market.
He then started spending money in a big way.

definitions.definition: (to be/become) pregnant
used to show that a statement has more than one meaning
examples: With the first goal he used his head in more ways than one.

definitions.definition: used to say what normally happens in a particular situation
examples: In the ordinary way, she's not a nervous person.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: to some extent; not completely
examples: In a way it was one of our biggest mistakes.

definitions.definition: in a manner that is appropriate to or typical of a person or thing but that may seem unusual to other people
examples: I expect she does love you in her own way.
The building is very attractive in its own way.
He was attractive in his own way.

definitions.definition: stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: You'll have to move—you're in my way.
I left them alone, as I felt I was in the way.
There were several rocks in the way.

definitions.definition: used in questions and negative sentences to talk about the types of something that are available
examples: There isn't much in the way of entertainment in this place.

definitions.definition: to avoid somebody
examples: Let's keep out of her way while she's in such a bad mood.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to make a lot of money easily and feel very pleased about it
to get yourself into or out of a situation by lying
to deliberately avoid seeing somebody/something
examples: Prison officers know what's going on, but look the other way.

definitions.definition: to become lost
examples: We lost our way in the dark.
She lost her way in the fog.

definitions.definition: to forget or move away from the purpose or reason for something
examples: I feel that the project has lost its way.
This project seems to have lost its way.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to pass; to allow somebody/something to take the place of somebody/something
examples: Make way for the Lord Mayor!
Tropical forest is felled to make way for grassland.

definitions.definition: to move or get somewhere; to make progress
examples: Will you be able tomake your own wayto the airport(= get there without help, a lift, etc.)?
Is this your plan formaking your way in the world?

definitions.definition: to stop behaving badly
(to take/find) an acceptable course of action that avoids two extreme positions
used to show that you are certain about something
examples: It was the wrong decision—there are no two ways about it.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no possibility that you will do something or that something will happen
examples: ‘Do you want to help?’ ‘No way!’
No way am I going to drive them there.
There's no way we could afford that sort of money.

definitions.definition: during the journey
examples: He stopped for breakfast on the way.
She grabbed her phone and bag on her way out.

definitions.definition: going or coming
examples: I'd better be on my way(= I must leave)soon.
The letter should be on its way to you.
Then he went off on his merry way.

definitions.definition: not yet born
examples: They've got three kids and one on the way.

definitions.definition: to make it possible for somebody to do something or for something to happen
examples: The agreement could open the way for the country to pay off its debts.

definitions.definition: in the opposite position, direction or order
examples: I think it should go on the other way round.

definitions.definition: the opposite situation
examples: I didn't leave you. It was the other way round(= you left me).

definitions.definition: in a safe place where somebody/something cannot be hurt or injured or do any damage to somebody/something
examples: She put the knife in a drawer, out of harm’s way.
I prefer the children to play in the garden where they’re out of harm’s way.

definitions.definition: no longer stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: I moved my legs out of the way so that she could get past.
I didn't say anything until Dad was out of the way.

definitions.definition: finished; dealt with
examples: Our region is poised for growth once the election is out of the way.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences to mean ‘unusual’
examples: She had obviously noticed nothing out of the way.

definitions.definition: not on the route that you planned to take
examples: I'd love a lift home—if it's not out of your way.

definitions.definition: a point at which two people or groups of people decide to separate
examples: These events led to a final parting of the ways.

definitions.definition: to create a situation in which somebody will be able to do something or something can happen
examples: This decision paved the way for changes in employment rights for women.

definitions.definition: to make enough money to pay what it costs to keep it going
examples: The bridge is still not paying its way.

definitions.definition: to pay for everything yourself without having to rely on anyone else’s money
to walk carefully, choosing the safest, driest, etc. place to put your feet
examples: She picked her way delicately over the rough ground.
We picked our way carefully over the jagged rocks.

definitions.definition: to make somebody annoyed or angry, often without intending to, by doing or saying something that offends them
examples: She tends to rub people up the wrong way.

definitions.definition: to realize or admit that you have done something wrong and decide to change your behaviour
examples: Will they realize the error of their ways before it is too late?

definitions.definition: to find that it is possible or convenient to do something
examples: Small builders cannot see their way clear to take on many trainees.

definitions.definition: to get an idea of what is likely to happen before deciding what to do
to do something first so that other people can follow
to make it easier for somebody/something to develop or make progress
examples: These negotiations are intended to smooth the path to a peace treaty.
We’re trying to smooth the way for women who want to resume their careers.

definitions.definition: to (not) prevent somebody from doing something
examples: If you believe you can make her happy, I won't stand in your way.
He wanted to go to university and would let nothing stand in his way.

definitions.definition: to bebisexual(= sexually attracted to both men and women)
to end a difficult situation by choosing the simplest solution even if it is not the best one
examples: He took the easy way out and didn’t go to the meeting.
She took the easy way out and paid someone to write the article for her.
