definitions.definition: to be offended by a remark that was not intended to be offensive
examples: Don't take this the wrong way, but maybe you're trying a bit too hard.

definitions.definition: to make excuses and give reasons for not doing something; to manage to get yourself out of a difficult situation
examples: I managed to talk my way out of having to give a speech.

definitions.definition: that is the situation and we cannot change it, so we must accept it
there are many different ways to achieve something
in my opinion
having started
examples: Preparations are well under way for a week of special events in May.

definitions.definition: a plan of action that is likely to be successful
examples: Personal electronic health records are the way forward for effective healthcare in the future.

definitions.definition: something that allows you to join a group of people, an industry, etc. that it is difficult to join, or to understand something that it is difficult to understand
the typical pattern of behaviour of a person or group
examples: the American way of life
She loved the Spanish way of life and immediately felt at home there.
the beliefs and practices of the Hindu way of life

definitions.definition: the way that most people behave; the way that things happen, which you cannot change
examples: The rich and powerful make the decisions—that's the way of the world.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody else has either to agree with your opinion or to leave
the methods and materials available for doing something
examples: ways and means of raising money
Newspapers have ways and means of getting hold of secret information.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that you are pleased about something they have done
examples: Good work, guys! Way to go!
Way to go! I wish I could do that!

definitions.definition: the way to make somebody like or love you
examples: The way to a man's heart is through his stomach(= by giving him good food).

definitions.definition: if you really want to do something then you will find a way of doing it
a series of victories or successes
examples: He'll look to continue his winning ways with a victory in Mexico on 16 February.

definitions.definition: to do something from beginning to end, especially when it takes a lot of time or effort
examples: She worked her way through the pile of documents.

definitions.definition: to have a job or series of jobs while studying, travelling, etc. in order to pay for your education, etc.
to move regularly to a more senior position in a company
examples: He worked his way up from messenger boy to account executive.

origin: Old Englishweg, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwegand GermanWeg, from a base meaning ‘move, carry’.

definitions.definition: to turn your eyes in a particular direction
examples: Look closelyand tell me what you see.
If youlook carefullyyou can just see our house from here.
‘Has the mail come yet?’ ‘I'll look and see.’
Look! I'm sure that's Jennifer Lawrence!
Don't look now, but there's someone staring at you!
look at somebody/somethingShe looked at me and smiled.
look + adv./prep.I got up and looked out of the window.
She looked across to the other side of the room.

definitions.definition: to try to find somebody/something
examples: look for somebody/somethingWhere have you been? We've been looking for you.
Are you still looking for a job?
We’re looking for someone with experience for this post.
They are always looking for ways to save money.
look + adv./prep.I can't find my book—I've looked everywhere.

definitions.definition: to pay attention to something
examples: look at somethingLook at the time! We're going to be late.
look where, what, etc…Can't you look where you're going?

definitions.definition: to seem; to appear
examples: How do I look?
+ adj.Youlook great!
The gardenlooks nice.
Don't worry—it looks worse than it is.
The filmlooks likelyto offend many people.
That book looks interesting.
look like somebody/somethingThat looks like an interesting book.
look like somebody/something to somebodyIt doesn't look like fun to me.
+ nounYou made me look a complete fool!
That looks an interesting book.

definitions.definition: to have a particular appearance
examples: look like somebody/somethingThat photograph doesn't look like her at all.
‘What does your cousin look like?’ ‘He's tall and thin with brown hair.’
look as if…/as though…You look as though you slept badly.

definitions.definition: to seem likely to happen or be true
examples: Itlooks like rain(= it looks as if it's going to rain).
look as if…/as though…It doesn't look as if we'll be moving after all.
look to somebody as if…/as though…It looks to me as though the company is in real trouble.
look like…(informal)They don’t look like they’re trying to win.
look to somebody like…(informal)It looks to me like they have a plan.

definitions.definition: to face a particular direction
examples: The house looks east.
The hotel looks out over the harbour.
The kitchen looks onto the garden.

definitions.definition: used in a shop to say that you are not ready to buy something
examples: ‘Can I help you?’ ‘I'm just looking, thank you.’

definitions.definition: to try to find ways of doing something
examples: The government is looking to reduce inflation.

definitions.definition: to be considered bad behaviour or bad manners
examples: It looks bad not going to your own brother's wedding.

definitions.definition: to show that something bad might happen
examples: He's had another heart attack; things are looking bad for him, I'm afraid.

definitions.definition: to show success or that something good might happen
examples: This year's sales figures are looking good.

definitions.definition: used to protest about something
examples: Now look here, it wasn't my fault.

definitions.definition: used to give an example that proves what you are saying or makes it clearer
examples: Look how lazy we've become.
Be careful climbing that ladder. Look what happened last time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is exactly what somebody needs and so they do not need to consider any other options
examples: Those looking for an enjoyable evening need look no further than the hotel's nightclub.

definitions.definition: to look at somebody in a careful or critical way
to (not) have your normal healthy appearance
examples: You're not looking yourself today(= you look tired or ill/sick).

definitions.definition: to become more and more successful
examples: Her first novel was published in 2007 and since then she hasn't looked back.

definitions.definition: not attractive
judging by the appearance of somebody/something
examples: To look at him you'd never think he was nearly fifty.

definitions.definition: used to protest about something
examples: Now look here, it wasn't my fault.

definitions.definition: used to give an example that proves what you are saying or makes it clearer
examples: Look how lazy we've become.
Be careful climbing that ladder. Look what happened last time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is exactly what somebody needs and so they do not need to consider any other options
examples: Those looking for an enjoyable evening need look no further than the hotel's nightclub.

definitions.definition: to look at somebody in a careful or critical way
to (not) have your normal healthy appearance
examples: You're not looking yourself today(= you look tired or ill/sick).

definitions.definition: to become more and more successful
examples: Her first novel was published in 2007 and since then she hasn't looked back.

definitions.definition: not attractive
judging by the appearance of somebody/something
examples: To look at him you'd never think he was nearly fifty.

origin: Old Englishlōcian(verb), of West Germanic origin; related to German dialectlugen.

definitions.definition: an understanding about something; an ability to judge something
examples: One of the most important things in a partner is asense of humour(= the ability to find things funny or make people laugh).
He has a very goodsense of direction(= finds the way to a place easily).
(figurative)She has lost all sense of direction in her life(= the idea of what she should do in her life).
Always try to keep asense of proportion(= of the relative importance of different things).
a sense of rhythm/timing
a sense of fun/adventure
Readers gain a real sense of what life was like in the camp.
Alex doesn't have anydress sense(= does not know which clothes look attractive).

definitions.definition: good understanding and judgement; knowledge of what is sensible or practical behaviour
examples: You shouldhave the sense totake advice when it is offered.
There's no sense in(= it is not sensible)worrying about it now.
Can't youtalk sense(= say something sensible)?
There'sa lot of sensein what Mary says.

definitions.definition: the meaning that a word or phrase has; a way of understanding something
examples: That word has three senses.
in … senseThe word ‘love’ is used in different senses by different people.
Globalizationin the broadest senseis nothing new.
The word ‘perspective’ is being used here in a technical sense.
He was a true friend,in every sense of the word(= in every possible way).
In a sense(= in one way)it doesn't matter any more.
In some senses(= in one or more ways)the criticisms were justified.
(formal)In no sensecan the issue be said to be resolved.
in the sense of somethingI am using ‘cold’ in the sense of ‘unfriendly’.
in the sense that…I don't mean that the press ought to be free in the sense that no one ought to pay for it.
There is a sense in which we are all to blame for the tragedy.

definitions.definition: one of the five powers (sight, hearing, smell, taste and touch) that your body uses to get information about the world around you
examples: the five senses
sense of somethingDogs have akeen sense(= strong sense)of smell.
the sense organs(= eyes, ears, nose, etc.)
I could hardly believe the evidence of my own senses(= what I could see, hear, etc.).
The mixture of sights, smells and sounds around her made her senses reel.

definitions.definition: a feeling about something important
examples: sense of somethingHis career was guided by astrong senseof duty.
There is now asense of urgencyto fix the problem.
I have found asense of purposein the work I do here.
Most people in the country have a strongsense ofnationalidentity.
My parents instilled a strongsense of responsibilityin me.
Clubs try to create asense of community.
Helmets can give cyclists afalse sense of security.
Hefeltan overwhelmingsenseof loss.
sense (that)…Ihad the sense thathe was worried about something.
Igot the sensethat she wasn't very pleased to see us.

definitions.definition: a normal state of mind; the ability to think clearly
examples: If she threatens to leave, it shouldbring him to his senses.
I waited for them tocome to their sensesand return.
No one in their right senses would give him the job!
(old-fashioned)Are youout of your senses? You'll be killed!

definitions.definition: to try and persuade somebody to stop behaving in a stupid way, sometimes using rough or violent methods
examples: Try and talk some sense into her before she makes the wrong decision.
Where would I be without you to knock some sense into my head?

definitions.definition: to have a meaning that you can easily understand
examples: This sentence doesn't make sense.

definitions.definition: to be a sensible thing to do
examples: It makes sense to buy the most up-to-date version.
There are strict medicals for pilots, whichmakes good sense.

definitions.definition: to be easy to understand or explain
examples: John wasn't making much sense on the phone.
Who would send me all these flowers? It makes no sense.
It allmade perfect senseto me.

definitions.definition: to understand something that is difficult or has no clear meaning
examples: I can’t make sense of that painting.

definitions.definition: to start to be sensible or reasonable
examples: I tried to make him see sense, but he just wouldn't listen.

definitions.definition: a feeling or understanding that an event is important or special
examples: Candles on the table gave the evening a sense of occasion.

definitions.definition: to start behaving in a crazy way

origin: late Middle English (as a noun in the sense ‘meaning’): from Latinsensus‘faculty of feeling, thought, meaning’, fromsentire‘feel’. The verb dates from the mid 16th cent.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is possible
examples: That may or may not be true.
He may have(= perhaps he has)missed his train.
Theymay wellwin.
There is a range of programs on the market which may be described as design aids.

definitions.definition: used to ask for or give permission
examples: May I come in?
You may come in if you wish.

definitions.definition: used when admitting that something is true before introducing another point, argument, etc.
examples: They may be well-meaning, but they are wrong.

definitions.definition: used as a polite way of making a comment, asking a question, etc.
examples: You look lovely, if I may say so.
May I ask why you took that decision?
If I may just add one thing…

definitions.definition: used to express wishes and hopes
examples: May she rest in peace.
Business has been thriving in the past year.Long may it continueto do so.

definitions.definition: used to say what the purpose of something is
examples: There is a need for more resources so that all children may have a decent education.

definitions.definition: despite that
examples: I know that he has tried hard; be that as it may, his work is just not good enough.

definitions.definition: used to say that something seems the best thing to do in the situation that you are in, although you may not really want to do it
examples: If no one else wants it, we might as well give it to him.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no real difference between two things that you might do
examples: If you are going to have sugar, you may as well have a bar of chocolate.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to a smaller degree; not so much
examples: less expensive/likely/intelligent
less often/enthusiastically
less than…I read much less now than I used to.
The receptionist was less than(= not at all)helpful.
She wasn'tany the lesshappy for(= she was perfectly happy)being on her own.
That this is a positive stereotype makes itno less astereotype, and therefore unacceptable.

definitions.definition: continuing to become smaller in amount
examples: She found the job less and less attractive.

definitions.definition: almost
examples: I've more or less finished the book.

definitions.definition: approximately
examples: She could earn $200 a night, more or less.

definitions.definition: and certainly not
examples: No explanation was offered, still less an apology.
He’s too shy to ask a stranger the time, much less speak to a room full of people.

origin: Old Englishlǣssa, of Germanic origin; related to Old Frisianlēssa, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekloisthos‘last’.

definitions.definition: of a higher standard or less poor quality; not as bad as something else
examples: We're hoping for better weather tomorrow.
Her work is gettingbetter and better.
He is in amuch bettermood than usual.
The meal couldn't have been better.
There's nothing better than a long soak in a hot bath.
If you can only exercise once a week, that'sbetter than nothing(= better than taking no exercise at all).

definitions.definition: more suitable or appropriate
examples: Can you think of a better word than ‘nice’?
It would be better for him to talk to his parents about his problems.
You'd be bettergoing by bus.

definitions.definition: more able; showing more skill
examples: She's far better at science than her brother.

definitions.definition: less ill or unhappy
examples: She's a lot better today.
His leg wasgetting better.
You'll feelall the better fora good night's sleep.

definitions.definition: fully recovered after an illness; in good health again
examples: Don't go back to work until you are better.

definitions.definition: most of something, especially a period of time; more than half of something
examples: He drank the best part of a bottle of Scotch waiting for her to get home.
The journey took her the better part of an hour.

definitions.definition: used to say that something should be as big, small, etc. as possible
examples: I love giving parties, the bigger the better.

definitions.definition: you should avoid danger and not take unnecessary risks
almost or just the same as; almost or just as bad as
examples: The path was no better than a sheep track.
Charles VII was a bad king and Charles VIII was no better.

definitions.definition: it is better to stop something bad from happening rather than try to deal with the problems after it has happened
examples: Prevention is better than cure, so start looking after your heart now.
Prevention is better than cure, so start taking care of yourself.

definitions.definition: used to give support to somebody who has been upset and is trying to become calmer
examples: Dry your eyes now. That's better.

definitions.definition: used to praise somebody who has made an effort to improve
examples: That's much better—you played the right notes this time.

definitions.definition: used to say that two people can achieve more than one person working alone

origin: Old Englishbetera(adjective), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchbeterand Germanbesser, also tobest.

definitions.definition: the state of health of somebody's mind
examples: Volunteering can also improve your mental health and help you live longer.
She called for more honest conversations about mental health.
Many of us struggle with our mental health.
It's important to tackle the causes of stress and poor mental health early on.
to havemental health issues/problems
There is often a stigma attached to mental health issues.

definitions.definition: the system for treating people with mental health problems
examples: The government has announced £600 million extra funding for mental health.
The demand for mental health services rose after the pandemic.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: used (to ask) if somebody is upset, unhappy, etc. or if there is a problem
examples: What's the matter?Is there something wrong?
Isanything the matter?
matter with somebody/somethingIssomething the matterwith Bob? He seems very down.
There's something the matter with my eyes.
‘We've bought a new TV.’ ‘What was the matter with the old one?’
What's the matter with youtoday(= why are you behaving like this)?
What on earth was the matter with her, she wondered. She’d come here to enjoy herself and here she was, stuck on her own in the hotel.

definitions.definition: a subject or situation that you must consider or deal with
examples: It's a private matter.
They had important matters to discuss.
She may need your help with some business matters.
financial/legal/political matters
They've agreed in theory, but now we need to discuss practical matters.
The matter should be referred to a senior manager.
Do I have any choice in the matter?
I always consulted him on matters of policy.
matter for somebodyIt's a matter for the police(= for them to deal with).
matter of something(formal)It was a matter of some concern to most of those present(= something they were worried about).
It should have been a simple matter to check.
It wasno easy mattergetting him to change his mind.
Let's get on with thematter in hand(= what we need to deal with now).
I wasn't prepared tolet the matter drop(= stop discussing it).
We have sent these concerns to Professor Tribe, and nowconsider the matter closed(= it is finished).
I did not feel that we had got tothe heart of the matter(= the most important part).
And that isthe crux of the matter(= the most important thing about the situation).
(ironic)And then there'sthe little matterof the fifty pounds you owe me.

definitions.definition: the present situation, or the situation that you are talking about
examples: Unfortunately, there is nothing we can do to improve matters.
I'd forgotten the keys, which didn'thelp matters.
And then,to make matters worse, his parents turned up.
I decided totake matters into my own hands(= deal with the situation myself).
Matters came to a head(= the situation became very difficult)with his resignation.

definitions.definition: a situation that involves something or depends on something
examples: matter of somethingLearning to drive is all a matter of coordination.
Some people prefer the older version to the new one. It'sa matter of taste.
She resigned overa matter of principle.
The government must deal with thisas a matter of urgency.
Justas a matter of interest(= because it is interesting, not because it is important), how much did you pay for it?
‘I think this is the best so far.’ ‘Well, that'sa matter of opinion(= other people may think differently).’
It's not pure fantasy ! It's a plainmatter of fact.
Getting the effect you want is a matter of trial and error.
matter of doing somethingThat's not a problem. It'ssimply a matter ofletting people know in time.
Planning a project is just a matter of working out the right order to do things in.

definitions.definition: physical substance that everything in the world is made of; not mind or spirit
examples: to study the properties of matter
This swirling mass of energy and matter is classed as a spiral galaxy.

definitions.definition: a substance or things of a particular sort
examples: Add plenty of organic matter to improve the soil.
elimination ofwaste matterfrom the body
She didn't approve of their choice ofreading matter.
There were small lumps of matter floating on the surface.

definitions.definition: used to add a comment on something that you have just said, usually adding something that you think the other person will be interested in
examples: It's a nice place. We've stayed there ourselves, as a matter of fact.

definitions.definition: used to disagree with something that somebody has just said
examples: ‘I suppose you'll be leaving soon, then?’ ‘No, as a matter of fact I'll be staying for another two years.’

definitions.definition: to be very different
examples: I know which area they live in, but whether I can find their house is a different matter.
That's a whole different matter.
I don't mind lizards, but snakes are a different matter.

definitions.definition: to be something serious that you should not joke about
used to emphasize a statement, especially one that is the opposite of what has just been mentioned
examples: A new car would be wonderful but the fact of the matter is that we can't afford one.

definitions.definition: used to add a comment on something that you have just said
examples: I didn't like it much. Nor did the kids, for that matter.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: (as) the usual and correct thing to do
examples: We always check people's addresses as a matter of course.

definitions.definition: only a few hours, minutes, etc.
examples: It was all over in a matter of minutes.
The bullet missed her by a matter of inches.

definitions.definition: used to describe a situation that is very important or serious
examples: You mustn't let anyone know— it's a matter of life and death.
These talks are a matter of life or death for the factory.
Delivering on time is a matter of life and death for a small company.

definitions.definition: something that has been recorded as being true
used to say that something is not important
examples: If that doesn't work out, no matter, I'll rent for another year or two.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is always true, whatever the situation is, or that somebody should certainly do something
examples: They don't last long no matter how careful you are.
Call me when you get there, no matter what the time is.

origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latinmateria‘timber, substance’, also ‘subject of discourse’, frommater‘mother’.

definitions.definition: used for making suggestions or as a polite way of telling people what you want them to do
examples: ‘Shall we check it again?’ ‘Yes, let's.’
let's do somethingLet's go to the beach.
Right, let's begin.
(formal)Let us review these points in more detail.
Let's not tell her what we did.
(British English)Don't let's tell her what we did.
Now let's not be silly about this.

definitions.definition: used for offering help to somebody
examples: Let mehelp you with that.
Here, let me do it.
Let us get those boxes down for you.
'Please, let me,' she said, opening the door.

definitions.definition: used for making requests or giving instructions
examples: Let me have your report by Friday.
Let me know(= tell me)how I can help.
Please let us know your decision as soon as possible.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody to do something or something to happen without trying to stop it
examples: let somebody/something do somethingLet them splash around in the pool for a while.
Don't let her upset you.
Let your body relax.
I'll let others decide whether the play was a success or not.
Will you let me finish?
let somebody/somethingHe'd eat chocolate all day long if I let him.

definitions.definition: to give somebody permission to do something
examples: let somebody/something do somethingThey won't let him leave the country.
In some countries they let you drive at 15.
They refused to let us cross the border.
let somebody/somethingI wanted to go but my parents wouldn't let me.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to go somewhere
examples: to let somebody into the house
This lets more water into the soil.
I'll give you a key so that you can let yourself in.
The hall will be able to let in more spectators.
Please let me past.
The cat wants to be let out.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are not afraid or worried about somebody doing something
examples: If he thinks he can cheat me, just let him try!

definitions.definition: used to express a strong wish for something to happen
examples: Let her come home safely!

definitions.definition: used to introduce what you are going to say or do
examples: Let me give you an example.
Let me just finish this and then I'll come.

definitions.definition: used to say that you are supposing something to be true when you calculate something
examples: Let line AB be equal to line CD.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody to use a house, room, etc. in return for regular payments
examples: I let the spare room.
They decided to let out the smaller offices at low rents.

definitions.definition: to stop trying to change the behaviour or state of somebody/something
examples: I know I'm being grumpy, but I'm really tired so just let me be.

definitions.definition: to mention something in a conversation, by accident or as if by accident
examples: She let fall a further heavy hint.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody to be free
examples: Will they let the hostages go?
He's finally been let go.

definitions.definition: to make somebody have to leave their job
examples: They're having to let 100 employees go because of falling profits.

definitions.definition: to stop holding somebody/something
examples: Don't let the rope go.
Don't let go of the rope.
Let go! You're hurting me!

definitions.definition: to give up an idea or an attitude, or control of something
examples: It's time to let the past go.
It's time to let go of the past.

definitions.definition: to stop taking care of a house, garden, etc.
examples: I'm afraid I've let the garden go this year.
He'd really let the house go since she'd died.

definitions.definition: to attack somebody physically or with words
to say or do no more about something
examples: I don't entirely agree, but I'll let it go at that.
I thought she was hinting at something, but I let it go.

definitions.definition: used when you are thinking or trying to remember something
examples: Now let me see—where did he say he lived?

definitions.definition: used when commenting on a situation to suggest that you could say something more or worse but prefer not to
examples: Let's just say he wasn't very happy about the decision(= he was very unhappy or angry about it).

definitions.definition: used when making a suggestion or giving an example
examples: I can let you have it for, well let's say £100.

definitions.definition: to behave in a relaxed way without worrying about what people think of your behaviour
examples: Come on, enjoy yourself—let yourself go!

definitions.definition: to stop being careful about how you look and dress, etc.
examples: He has let himself go since he lost his job.

definitions.definition: available for rent
examples: The website advertises flats to let in the local area.

origin: verbOld Englishlǣtan‘leave behind, leave out’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlatenand Germanlassen, also tolate.

definitions.definition: a larger number or amount of
examples: I want some more!
more somethingmore bread/cars
Only two more days to go!
people with more money than sense
more of something/somebodyI can't stand much more of this.
I hope we'll see more of you(= see you again or more often).
more than…She earns a lot more than I do.
There is room forno more thanthree cars.

definitions.definition: continuing to become larger in number or amount
examples: More and more people are banking online.
She spends more and more time alone in her room.

origin: Old Englishmāra, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchmeerand Germanmehr.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize something unexpected or surprising
examples: Shedidn't evencall to say she wasn't coming.
Henever evenopened the letter(= so he certainly didn't read it).
It wouldn't have been possible 30 years ago,maybe even20.
It was cold there even in summer(= so it must have been very cold in winter).
Even a child can understand it(= so adults certainly can).

definitions.definition: used when you are comparing things, to make the comparison stronger
examples: You know even less about it than I do.
She'seven moreintelligent than her sister.
The Second World War made thingseven worse.
We face an even greater challenge today.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a more exact description of somebody/something
examples: It's an unattractive building, ugly even.

definitions.definition: just at the same time as somebody does something or as something else happens
examples: Even as he shouted the warning the car skidded.

definitions.definition: despite the possibility that; no matter whether
examples: I'll get there, even if I have to walk.

definitions.definition: despite the fact that; even though
examples: He is a great leader, even if he has many enemies.

definitions.definition: despite what has/had happened
examples: I've shown him the photographs but even now he won't believe me.
Even then she would not admit her mistake.

definitions.definition: at this or that exact moment
examples: The troops are even now preparing to march into the city.

definitions.definition: despite that
examples: There are a lot of spelling mistakes; even so, it's quite a good essay.

definitions.definition: despite the fact that
examples: I like her, even though she can be annoying at times.

origin: Old Englishefen(adjective),efne(adverb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutcheven,effenand Germaneben.

definitions.definition: to put powder,lipstick, etc. on your/somebody’s face to make it more attractive or to prepare for an appearance in the theatre, on television, etc.
to form something
examples: Women make up 56 per cent of the student numbers.
be made up of somethingThe course is made up of 12 two-hour sessions.

definitions.definition: to put something together from several different things
to invent a story, etc., especially in order to trick or entertain somebody
examples: He made up some excuse about his daughter being sick.
I told the kids a story, making it up as I went along.
You made that up!

definitions.definition: to complete a number or an amount required
examples: We need one more person to make up a team.

definitions.definition: to replace something that has been lost; tocompensatefor something
examples: Can I leave early this afternoon and make up the time tomorrow?

definitions.definition: to prepare a medicine by mixing different things together
to prepare a bed for use; to create a temporary bed
examples: We made up the bed in the spare room.
They made up a bed for me on the sofa.

definitions.definition: to clean a hotel room and make the bed
examples: The maid asked if she could make up the room.
They had forgotten to make up the room.

definitions.definition: to end an argument with somebody and become friends again
examples: Why don't you twokiss and make up?
Has he made it up with her yet?
Have they made it up yet?

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a thing that is difficult to deal with or to understand
examples: big/serious/major problems
She has a lot ofhealth problems.
financial/social/technical problems
Let me know if youhave any problems.
The government mustaddress the problemof child poverty.
We cannottackle this problemeffectively on our own.
We aredealing witha seriousproblemhere.
Money isn't going tosolve the problem.
(especially North American English)tofix a problem
If he chooses Mary it's bound tocause problems.
topose/create a problem
Theproblemfirstarosein 2018.
problem with somethingThere is a problem with this argument.
problem of somethingthe problem of youth homelessness
problem of doing somethingMost studentsface the problemof funding themselves while they are studying.
problem for somebodyUnemployment is a veryreal problemfor graduates now.
It’s a nice table!The only problem is (that)it’s too big for our room.
Part of the problemis the shape of the room.
Stop worrying about their marriage—it isn't your problem.
There's no history of heart problems(= disease connected with the heart)in our family.
the magazine’sproblem page(= containing letters about readers’ problems and advice about how to solve them)

definitions.definition: a question that can be answered by using logical thought or mathematics
examples: mathematical problems
to find the answer to the problem
The teacher set us 50 problems to do.
I have five problems to do for homework.

definitions.definition: to disagree with or object to something/somebody
examples: I have no problem with you working at home tomorrow.
(informal)We are going to do this my way.Do you have a problem with that?(= showing that you are impatient with the person that you are speaking to)
Do you have a problem with her?

definitions.definition: used to show that you do not care about somebody else’s difficulties
examples: If they can't afford to go, that's not my problem.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are happy to help somebody or that something will be easy to do
examples: ‘Can I pay by credit card?’ ‘Yes, no problem.’

definitions.definition: used after somebody has thanked you or said they are sorry for something
examples: ‘Thanks for the ride.’ ‘No problem.’

definitions.definition: used to show that you think a person should deal with their own difficulties
examples: ‘My parents will be furious!’ ‘That’s your problem.’

definitions.definition: used to show that you think somebody is being unreasonable
examples: What's your problem?—I only asked if you could help me for ten minutes.

origin: late Middle English (originally denoting a riddle or a question for academic discussion): from Old Frenchprobleme, via Latin from Greekproblēma, fromproballein‘put forth’, frompro‘before’ +ballein‘to throw’.

definitions.definition: used to say that something cannot be avoided
examples: The number of places available is necessarily limited.
Servants necessarily had close contact with their employers.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is possibly true but not definitely or always true
examples: Biggest doesn't necessarily mean best.
It isn't necessarily true that sugar is bad for you.
The more expensive articles are not necessarily better.
‘We're going to lose.’ ‘Not necessarily.’
The views in this article do not necessarily reflect those of the college.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a feeling of being uncertain about something or not believing something
examples: a feeling of doubt and uncertainty
New evidence hascast doubt onthe guilt of the man jailed for the crime.
He was starting tohavesome seriousdoubts.
to have lingering/grave/nagging doubts
doubt about somethingThe articleraised doubtsabout how effective the new drug really was.
There is some doubt about the best way to do it.
doubt (that)…There is no doubtat all that we did the right thing.
There's little doubtin my mind that he's going to go right to the top.
doubt as to somethingSheleaves no doubtas to her own view of Picasso's work.
doubt as to who, what, etc…If you are in any doubt as to whether you should be doing these exercises, consult your doctor.
doubt over somethingMedical experts haveexpressed doubtover how the scientist died.
She knewwithout a shadow of a doubtthat he was lying to her.

definitions.definition: to be uncertain
examples: The success of the system is not in doubt.
The arrangements for the event still seemed to be in doubt.

definitions.definition: in a way that shows that something is completely certain
examples: The research showed beyond doubt that smoking contributes to heart disease.
(law)The prosecution was able to establishbeyond reasonable doubtthat the woman had been lying.
The evidence proves beyond doubt that he is innocent.

definitions.definition: to accept that somebody has told the truth or behaved well because you cannot prove that they have not
examples: She may have been lying, but I felt I had to give her the benefit of the doubt.

definitions.definition: to have reasons why you are not certain about whether something is good or whether something good will happen
examples: I've had my doubts about his work since he joined the firm.
It may be all right. Personally, I have my doubts.
They say they'll be here on time, but I have my doubts about that.

definitions.definition: used to give advice to somebody who cannot decide what to do
examples: If in doubt, wear black.
If in doubt, consult your doctor.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that something is likely
examples: No doubt she'll call us when she gets there.

definitions.definition: used when you are saying that something is certainly true
examples: He's made some great movies. There'sno doubt about it.

definitions.definition: to make something uncertain
examples: The proposed development has been thrown into doubt by the decision.

definitions.definition: used when you are giving your opinion and emphasizing the point that you are making

definitions.definition: for a long time

definitions.definition: confident that you know something or that you are right

definitions.definition: in order to help somebody

definitions.definition: in order to help somebody
examples: They campaigned on behalf of asylum seekers.

definitions.definition: an amount of time; the amount of time available to work, rest, etc.

definitions.definition: a period of time, either long or short, during which you do something or something happens

definitions.definition: an occasion when you do something or when something happens
examples: Every timeI hear that song I feel happy.

definitions.definition: an event or occasion that you experience in a particular way

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event

one of the fastest times ever

examples: This piece is in four-four time.
a slow waltz time

definitions.definition: the correct speed and rhythm of a piece of music
examples: in time to somethingTry and dance in time to the music(= with the same speed and rhythm).

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.

examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time

definitions.definition: many times; frequently

examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards

definitions.definition: almost always

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: to write or speak about something/somebody, especially without giving much information

theearth's surface/crust

definitions.definition: used to give a negative reply or statement

definitions.definition: a period of time when you are not at work or school

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you will/would never do something

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel or look older

definitions.definition: the desire for new sexual experience that is thought to be felt after seven years of marriage

examples: I've been going there every summer since the year dot.

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel or look younger
examples: That haircut takes years off her.

examples: Year by year their affection for each other grew stronger.

definitions.definition: every year

examples: Spending has increased year on year.
a year-on-year increase in spending

origin: Old Englishgē(a)r, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchjaarand GermanJahr, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekhōra‘season’.

definitions.definition: used withbe,doorhaveto form the negative of verbs; used to form the negative of modal verbs likecanormust

examples: He warned me not to be late.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that something belongs to or is connected with somebody

definitions.definition: used to say what normally happens in a particular situation
examples: In the ordinary way, she's not a nervous person.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.

definitions.definition: to some extent; not completely
examples: In a way it was one of our biggest mistakes.

definitions.definition: in a manner that is appropriate to or typical of a person or thing but that may seem unusual to other people
examples: I expect she does love you in her own way.
The building is very attractive in its own way.
He was attractive in his own way.

definitions.definition: stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: You'll have to move—you're in my way.
I left them alone, as I felt I was in the way.
There were several rocks in the way.

definitions.definition: used in questions and negative sentences to talk about the types of something that are available
examples: There isn't much in the way of entertainment in this place.

definitions.definition: to avoid somebody
examples: Let's keep out of her way while she's in such a bad mood.

definitions.definition: to be familiar with a place, subject, etc.
to make a lot of money easily and feel very pleased about it
to get yourself into or out of a situation by lying
to deliberately avoid seeing somebody/something
examples: Prison officers know what's going on, but look the other way.

definitions.definition: to become lost
examples: We lost our way in the dark.

definitions.definition: to forget or move away from the purpose or reason for something
examples: I feel that the project has lost its way.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to pass; to allow somebody/something to take the place of somebody/something
examples: Make way for the Lord Mayor!

definitions.definition: to move or get somewhere; to make progress
examples: Will you be able tomake your own wayto the airport(= get there without help, a lift, etc.)?
Is this your plan formaking your way in the world?

definitions.definition: to stop behaving badly
(to take/find) an acceptable course of action that avoids two extreme positions
used to show that you are certain about something
examples: It was the wrong decision—there are no two ways about it.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is no possibility that you will do something or that something will happen
examples: ‘Do you want to help?’ ‘No way!’
No way am I going to drive them there.
There's no way we could afford that sort of money.

definitions.definition: during the journey
examples: He stopped for breakfast on the way.
She grabbed her phone and bag on her way out.

definitions.definition: going or coming
examples: I'd better be on my way(= I must leave)soon.
The letter should be on its way to you.
Then he went off on his merry way.

definitions.definition: not yet born
examples: They've got three kids and one on the way.

definitions.definition: to make it possible for somebody to do something or for something to happen
examples: The agreement could open the way for the country to pay off its debts.

definitions.definition: in the opposite position, direction or order
examples: I think it should go on the other way round.

definitions.definition: the opposite situation
examples: I didn't leave you. It was the other way round(= you left me).

definitions.definition: in a safe place where somebody/something cannot be hurt or injured or do any damage to somebody/something
examples: She put the knife in a drawer, out of harm’s way.
I prefer the children to play in the garden where they’re out of harm’s way.

definitions.definition: no longer stopping somebody from moving or doing something
examples: I moved my legs out of the way so that she could get past.
I didn't say anything until Dad was out of the way.

definitions.definition: finished; dealt with
examples: Our region is poised for growth once the election is out of the way.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences to mean ‘unusual’
examples: She had obviously noticed nothing out of the way.

definitions.definition: not on the route that you planned to take
examples: I'd love a lift home—if it's not out of your way.

definitions.definition: a point at which two people or groups of people decide to separate
examples: These events led to a final parting of the ways.

definitions.definition: to create a situation in which somebody will be able to do something or something can happen
examples: This decision paved the way for changes in employment rights for women.

definitions.definition: to make enough money to pay what it costs to keep it going
examples: The bridge is still not paying its way.

definitions.definition: to pay for everything yourself without having to rely on anyone else’s money
to walk carefully, choosing the safest, driest, etc. place to put your feet
examples: She picked her way delicately over the rough ground.
We picked our way carefully over the jagged rocks.

definitions.definition: to make somebody annoyed or angry, often without intending to, by doing or saying something that offends them
examples: She tends to rub people up the wrong way.

definitions.definition: to realize or admit that you have done something wrong and decide to change your behaviour
examples: Will they realize the error of their ways before it is too late?

definitions.definition: to find that it is possible or convenient to do something
examples: Small builders cannot see their way clear to take on many trainees.

definitions.definition: to get an idea of what is likely to happen before deciding what to do
to do something first so that other people can follow
to make it easier for somebody/something to develop or make progress
examples: These negotiations are intended to smooth the path to a peace treaty.
We’re trying to smooth the way for women who want to resume their careers.

definitions.definition: to (not) prevent somebody from doing something
examples: If you believe you can make her happy, I won't stand in your way.
He wanted to go to university and would let nothing stand in his way.

definitions.definition: to bebisexual(= sexually attracted to both men and women)
to end a difficult situation by choosing the simplest solution even if it is not the best one
examples: He took the easy way out and didn’t go to the meeting.
She took the easy way out and paid someone to write the article for her.

definitions.definition: to be offended by a remark that was not intended to be offensive
examples: Don't take this the wrong way, but maybe you're trying a bit too hard.

definitions.definition: to make excuses and give reasons for not doing something; to manage to get yourself out of a difficult situation
examples: I managed to talk my way out of having to give a speech.

definitions.definition: that is the situation and we cannot change it, so we must accept it
there are many different ways to achieve something
in my opinion
having started
examples: Preparations are well under way for a week of special events in May.

definitions.definition: a plan of action that is likely to be successful
examples: Personal electronic health records are the way forward for effective healthcare in the future.

definitions.definition: something that allows you to join a group of people, an industry, etc. that it is difficult to join, or to understand something that it is difficult to understand
the typical pattern of behaviour of a person or group
examples: the American way of life
She loved the Spanish way of life and immediately felt at home there.
the beliefs and practices of the Hindu way of life

definitions.definition: the way that most people behave; the way that things happen, which you cannot change
examples: The rich and powerful make the decisions—that's the way of the world.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody else has either to agree with your opinion or to leave
the methods and materials available for doing something
examples: ways and means of raising money

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that you are pleased about something they have done
examples: Good work, guys! Way to go!

definitions.definition: the way to make somebody like or love you
examples: The way to a man's heart is through his stomach(= by giving him good food).

definitions.definition: if you really want to do something then you will find a way of doing it
a series of victories or successes
examples: He'll look to continue his winning ways with a victory in Mexico on 16 February.

definitions.definition: to do something from beginning to end, especially when it takes a lot of time or effort
examples: She worked her way through the pile of documents.

definitions.definition: to have a job or series of jobs while studying, travelling, etc. in order to pay for your education, etc.

examples: He worked his way up from messenger boy to account executive.

origin: Old Englishweg, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwegand GermanWeg, from a base meaning ‘move, carry’.

definitions.definition: one anotheris used when you are saying that each member of a group does something to or for the other people in the group
examples: We all try and help one another.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: either of the two halves of a surface, an object or an area that is divided by an imaginary central line

satellite links tothe other side of the world

a factory on the west side of town

people onboth sidesof the Atlantic

definitions.definition: one of the flat surfaces of something that is not the top or bottom, front or back

at the side (of something)The kitchen door is at theside of the house.
aside door/window/panel
a side wall/entrance

definitions.definition: a part near the edge of something; an area just beyond the edge of something

at the side of somethingA van was parked at the side of the road.

definitions.definition: either the right or left part of a person’s body, from thearmpit(= where the arm joins the body)to thehip(= where the leg joins the body)
examples: I've got a stitch in my side(= a sudden pain from running or laughing).

on your sideHe waslying on his side.

definitions.definition: either of two surfaces of something flat and thin, such as paper or cloth
examples: The upper side of the leaf was a much darker green.

on a/the side (of something)Write on one side of the paper only.
Fry the steaks for two minutes on each side.

examples: He told us not to write more than three sides.

definitions.definition: any of the flat surfaces of a solid object
examples: A cube has six sides.

definitions.definition: any of the lines that form a flat shape such as a square ortriangle
examples: a shape with five sides

definitions.definition: used in adjectives to state the number or type of sides
examples: a six-sided object
a glass-sided container

definitions.definition: a place or position very near to somebody/something

examples: Both sidesagreed to restore diplomatic relations.

on the… sideto be on thewinning/losing side

definitions.definition: one of the opinions, attitudes, or positions held by somebody in an argument, a business arrangement, etc.
examples: We heardboth sides of the argument.

on the… sideOnthe plus side, the film is beautifully shot and edited.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect of something, especially a situation or a person’s character
examples: These poems reveal her gentle side.

thedarker sideof human nature
a behind-the-scenes documentary which promises toshow a sideof him rarely seen in public

definitions.definition: a feeling that you are better than other people
examples: There wasno sideto him at all.

examples: As captain, Pryce is confident of leading his side to victory.

definitions.definition: the part of your family that people belong to who are related either to your mother or to your father
examples: a cousin on my father’s side(= a child of my father’s brother or sister)

definitions.definition: aside dish(= a small amount of food, for example a salad, served with the main course of a meal)
examples: Your dinner comes with a choice of two sides.

definitions.definition: one of the two halves of an animal that has been killed for meat
a television channel
examples: What's on the other side?

definitions.definition: to support and agree with somebody
examples: I'm definitely on your side in this.

definitions.definition: the boyfriend or girlfriend of somebody who is already married or in a steady sexual relationship with somebody else
examples: Is he your bit on the side?

definitions.definition: to choose between two possible choices
to show too much of a good quality in order to avoid showing too little
examples: I thought it was betterto err on the side of caution(= to be too careful rather than take a risk).

definitions.definition: moving to the left and then to the right and then back again
examples: He shook his head slowly from side to side.

definitions.definition: from or living in a poor area or part of town
to make somebody pleased with you/annoyed with you
to be in a bad mood for the whole day for no particular reason
said about people who never seem happy with what they have and always think they could be happier in a different place or situation
to have something as an advantage that will make it more likely that you will achieve something
examples: She may not win this year, but she does have youth on her side.

definitions.definition: to know where you can get an advantage for yourself
to be forced to change from feeling pleased or satisfied to feeling disappointed or annoyed
examples: He’ll be laughing on the other side of his face when he reads my letter.

definitions.definition: to fail to give your friends, family, etc. the help and support they expect, or to behave in a way that makes them disappointed
examples: You can always rely on Maggie—she’d never let the side down.

definitions.definition: to be cheerful or positive about a bad situation, for example by thinking only of the advantages and not the disadvantages

definitions.definition: to stay with somebody, especially in order to take care of them
in or from all directions; everywhere
examples: We realized we were surrounded on all sides.

definitions.definition: slightly too big, small, high, etc.
examples: These shoes are a little on the tight side.

definitions.definition: used to introduce the good points about somebody/something, especially after the bad points have been mentioned
examples: On the credit side, she’s always willing to work very late.

definitions.definition: on the woman’s side of the family
out of your way
examples: I left my bags on one side.

definitions.definition: to be dealt with later
examples: I put his complaint to one side until I had more time.

definitions.definition: younger or older than 40, 50, etc. years of age
being especially careful; taking no risks
examples: I took some extra cash just to be on the safe side.

definitions.definition: in addition to your main job
examples: a mechanic who buys and sells cars on the side

definitions.definition: secretly or illegally
examples: He's married but he has a girlfriend on the side.

definitions.definition: served at the same time as the main part of the meal, but on a separate plate
in trouble with the police
the aspect of a situation that is the opposite of or contrasts with the one you have been talking about
with the top part turned to the top; in the correct, normal position
examples: I dropped my toast, but luckily it fell right side up.

definitions.definition: close together and facing in the same direction
examples: There were two children ahead, walking side by side.

definitions.definition: together, without any difficulties
examples: We have been using both systems, side by side, for two years.

definitions.definition: used to refer to either of two opposite opinions on an issue
examples: The two countries are often on opposite sides of the fence when it comes to climate change.

definitions.definition: to laugh a lot at somebody/something
to express support for somebody in an argument
examples: She didn't think it was wise to take sides in their argument.

definitions.definition: to speak to somebody in private, especially in order to warn or tell them about something
before a particular time, event, age, etc.
examples: They aren't likely to arrive this side of midnight.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that repeatedly annoys somebody or stops them from doing something
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to talk about two ways of looking at the same situation

definitions.definition: on one occasion only; one time
examples: I've only been there once.
He cleans his caronce aweek.
She only sees her parentsonce everysix months.

Last year the region received rainonly once.
(informal)He only did itthe once.
Rome is a city you should visitat least oncein your life.

definitions.definition: at some time in the past
examples: I once met your mother.

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences and questions, and afterifto mean ‘ever’ or ‘at all’
examples: Henever onceoffered to help.

definitions.definition: used with a preposition or another adverb to mean‘as soon as somebody/something is/was’

definitions.definition: suddenly
examples: All at once she lost her temper.

definitions.definition: all together; at the same time
examples: I can't do everything all at once—you'll have to be patient.

definitions.definition: immediately; without delay
examples: Come here at once!

definitions.definition: at the same time
examples: Don't all speak at once!
I can't do two things at once.

definitions.definition: on this occasion (which is in contrast to what happens usually)
examples: Just for once he arrived on time.

definitions.definition: said by anauctioneerto show that an item has been sold

examples: Once again the train was late.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody cannot change
examples: Once an actor, always an actor.

definitions.definition: after an unpleasant experience you are careful to avoid something similar
now and for the last time; finally or completely
examples: We need to settle this once and for all.

definitions.definition: very rarely
occasionally
examples: Everybody makes a mistake once in a while.

definitions.definition: a few times
examples: I don't know her well, I've only met her once or twice.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody has done something wrong or stupid again, and this time they will suffer because of it
examples: You've tried that trick once too often.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

origin: Middle Englishones, genitive ofone. The spelling change in the 16th cent. was in order to retain the unvoiced sound of the final consonant.

definitions.definition: the solid surface of the earth
examples: on the groundI found her lying on the ground.
to the groundHefell to the ground, crying out in pain.

below (the) groundThe library has four levels below ground and seven above.

a ground and air attack
on the groundHe called for moretroops on the ground.

examples: The kids were playing on waste ground behind the school.

examples: fishing grounds
feeding grounds for birds

examples: fertile ground for planting crops

examples: the hospital grounds

definitions.definition: an area of interest, knowledge or ideas
examples: He managed tocover a lot of groundin a short talk.

ground for somethingParis in the 1920s wasfertile groundfor artistic experimentation.

definitions.definition: a good or true reason for saying, doing or believing something
examples: ground(s) for somethingYou have no grounds for complaint.
ground(s) for doing somethingWhat were his grounds for wanting a divorce?
ground(s) to do somethingThere arereasonable groundsto believe that a crime has been committed.

on… groundsHe retired early on health grounds.

definitions.definition: the small pieces of solid matter in a liquid that have fallen to the bottom

examples: pink roses on a white ground

examples: They broke ground for the new highway this month.

definitions.definition: to make a new discovery or do something that has not been done before
examples: Her architectural designs have broken new ground.

definitions.definition: to make a new discovery or do something that has not been done before
examples: Her architectural designs have broken new ground.

definitions.definition: to suddenly cause somebody’s idea or plan to fail by doing something to stop them from continuing with it
to fail to produce the result or the effect that you hope for; to have little success
examples: His charming smile fell on stony ground with her.

definitions.definition: completely, or including everything, starting with the most basic things
examples: We need to rebuild the system from the ground up.

definitions.definition: to become more powerful or successful
examples: Sterling continues to gain ground against the dollar.

definitions.definition: to gradually get closer to somebody/something that is moving or making progress in an activity
examples: The police car was gaining ground on the suspects.

examples: Without more money, the movie is unlikely to get off the ground.
to get a new company off the ground

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to have an advantage; to lose an advantage for yourself
examples: They are not prepared to give ground on tax cuts.

definitions.definition: to hide, especially to escape from somebody
to have a sensible and realistic attitude to life
examples: In spite of his overnight stardom he still manages to keep his feet on the ground.

definitions.definition: to start doing something and continue very quickly and successfully
to continue with your opinions or intentions when somebody is opposing you and wants you to change
examples: Don't let him persuade you—stand your ground.

definitions.definition: to face a situation and refuse to run away
examples: It is not easy to hold your ground in front of someone with a gun.

definitions.definition: completely, so that there is nothing left
examples: The hotelburned to the ground.

definitions.definition: used with a preposition or another adverb to mean‘as soon as somebody/something is/was’
examples: Once insidethe gate, Sam hurried up the path.

definitions.definition: suddenly
examples: All at once she lost her temper.

definitions.definition: all together; at the same time
examples: I can't do everything all at once—you'll have to be patient.

definitions.definition: immediately; without delay
examples: Come here at once!

definitions.definition: at the same time
examples: Don't all speak at once!
I can't do two things at once.

definitions.definition: on this occasion (which is in contrast to what happens usually)
examples: Just for once he arrived on time.
Can't you be nice to each other just this once?

definitions.definition: said by anauctioneerto show that an item has been sold
one more time; another time
examples: Once again the train was late.
Let me hear it just once more.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody cannot change
examples: Once an actor, always an actor.

definitions.definition: after an unpleasant experience you are careful to avoid something similar
now and for the last time; finally or completely
examples: We need to settle this once and for all.

definitions.definition: very rarely
occasionally
examples: Everybody makes a mistake once in a while.

definitions.definition: a few times
examples: I don't know her well, I've only met her once or twice.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody has done something wrong or stupid again, and this time they will suffer because of it
examples: You've tried that trick once too often.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

origin: Middle Englishones, genitive ofone. The spelling change in the 16th cent. was in order to retain the unvoiced sound of the final consonant.

definitions.definition: an area of sand or small stones (calledshingle), next to the sea or a lake
examples: on the beachtourists sunbathing on the beach
to the beachHe decided to go to the beach for a swim.
at the beachShe often spent summer days at the beach.
asandy beach
a pebble/shingle beach
a beach bar

origin: mid 16th cent. (denoting shingle on the seashore): perhaps related to Old Englishbæce,bece‘brook’ (an element that survives in place names such as Wisbechand Sandbach), assuming an intermediate sense ‘pebbly river valley’.

definitions.definition: disagreeing strongly with something and trying to stop it
examples: opposed to somethingHe wasstrongly opposedto modernism in art.

the party most openly opposed to military rule

definitions.definition: very different from something
examples: Our views arediametrically opposedon this issue.

opposed to somethingHis actions seemed directly opposed to the values of the company.

definitions.definition: used to make a contrast between two things
examples: 200 attended, as opposed to 300 the previous year.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: used to refer to people or things that are additional or different to people or things that have been mentioned or are known about

definitions.definition: used to refer to the second of two people or things
examples: My other sister is a doctor.

definitions.definition: used to refer to the people or things that remain in a group
examples: I'll wear my other shoes—these are dirty.
‘I like this one.’ ‘What aboutthe other ones?’

definitions.definition: used to refer to a place, direction, etc. that is the opposite to where you are, are going, etc.
examples: I work on theother sideof town.

definitions.definition: recently
examples: I saw Jack the other day.

definitions.definition: except
examples: I don't know any French people other than you.

definitions.definition: different or in a different way from; not
examples: I have never known him to behave other than selfishly.

origin: Old Englishōther, of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanander, from an Indo-European root meaning ‘different’.

definitions.definition: not unusual or different in any way
examples: an ordinary sort of day
in the ordinary course of events
ordinary peoplelike you and me

definitions.definition: having no unusual or interesting features
examples: The meal was very ordinary.

definitions.definition: used to say what normally happens in a particular situation
examples: In the ordinary way, she's not a nervous person.

definitions.definition: unusual or different
examples: I'm looking for something a little more out of the ordinary.

origin: late Middle English: the noun partly via Old French; the adjective from Latinordinarius‘orderly’ (reinforced by Frenchordinaire), fromordo,ordin-‘order’.

definitions.definition: used to say what is the right thing to do
examples: They ought to apologize.

definitions.definition: used to say what you expect or would like to happen
examples: Children ought to be able to read by the age of 7.

definitions.definition: used to say what you advise or recommend
examples: We ought to be leaving now.

definitions.definition: used to say what has probably happened or is probably true
examples: If he started out at nine, he ought to be here by now.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: the way things are organized or arranged; how often something happens
examples: We are in contact on aregular basis.
to be employed on a permanent/temporary/part-time basis

on a day-to-day/weekly/monthly basis

definitions.definition: the reason why people take a particular action
examples: on the basis of somethingShe was chosen for the job on the basis of her qualifications.

on the basis that…Some movies have been banned on the basis that they are too violent.

definitions.definition: the important facts, ideas or events that support something and that it can develop from
examples: The basis of a good marriage is trust.
basis for somethingThis article willform the basis forour discussion.
The initial talks were intended toprovide a basis fornegotiations.
as a/the basis for somethingThese results willserve as a basis formore detailed research.

basis in somethingThe theory seems to have no basis in fact.

origin: late 16th cent. (denoting a base or pedestal): via Latin from Greek, ‘stepping’. Compare with the nounbase.

definitions.definition: the number 1
examples: Do you want one or two?
There's only room for one person.
One more, please!
a one-bedroomed apartment
I'll see you at one(= one o'clock).

definitions.definition: used in formal language or for emphasis beforehundred,thousand, etc., or before a unit of measurement
examples: It cost one hundred and fifty pounds.

definitions.definition: used for emphasis to mean ‘a single’ or ‘just one’
examples: There's only one thing we can do.

definitions.definition: a person or thing, especially when they are part of a group
examples: One of my friends lives in Brighton.

definitions.definition: used for emphasis to mean ‘the only one’ or ‘the most important one’
examples: He's the one person I can trust.

definitions.definition: used when you are talking about a time in the past or the future, without actually saying which one
examples: I saw her one afternoon last week.

definitions.definition: the same
examples: They all went off in one direction.

definitions.definition: used for emphasis instead ofaoran
examples: That was one hell of a game!

definitions.definition: used with a person’s name to show that the speaker does not know the person
examples: He worked as an assistant to one Mr Ming.

definitions.definition: in agreement; all together
examples: We spoke as one on this matter.

definitions.definition: to feel that you completely agree with somebody/something, or that you are part of something
examples: a place where you can feel at one with nature

definitions.definition: a return to the situation you were in at the beginning of a project, task, etc., because you have made no real progress
examples: If this suggestion isn't accepted, we'll be back to square one.

definitions.definition: to be the only person to have a particular opinion or to vote a particular way
used to emphasize that a particular person does something and that you believe other people do too
examples: I, for one, would prefer to postpone the meeting.

definitions.definition: to understand or guess something immediately
examples: ‘Oh, so she’s his sister!’ ‘Got it in one!’

definitions.definition: to get an advantage over somebody/something
examples: I'm not going to let them get one over on me!

definitions.definition: to do something better than somebody else or than you have done before
examples: She did well this year and next year she hopes to go one better.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody/something has different roles, contains different things or is used for different purposes
examples: She's a mother and company director in one.
