definitions.definition: first one person or thing, and then another, and then another, up to any number or amount
examples: The bills kept coming in, one after another.

definitions.definition: everyone
examples: Happy New Year to one and all!

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that somebody is famous
examples: Here she is, the one and only Rihanna!

definitions.definition: used for emphasis to mean ‘the same’
examples: I never realized Ruth Rendell and Barbara Vine were one and the same(= the same person using two different names).

definitions.definition: separately and in order
examples: I went through the items on the list one by one.

definitions.definition: a few
examples: We've had one or two problems—nothing serious.

definitions.definition: having an advantage over somebody
used to say that all types of the things mentioned are very similar
examples: I don't like science fiction novels much. When you've read one, you've read them all.

definitions.definition: a very long time ago
examples: I've been going there every summer since the year one.

origin: Old Englishān, of Germanic origin; related to Dutcheenand Germanein, from an Indo-European root shared by Latinunus. The initialwsound developed before the 15th cent. and was occasionally represented in the spelling; it was not accepted into standard English until the late 17th cent.

definitions.definition: a piece of electrical equipment with a screen on which you can watch programmes with moving pictures and sounds

a television drama/documentary/interview/commercial

on the phoneI talk to my family on the phone.

toput the phone down

xylophone

examples: anglophone
francophone

to be using the phone
examples: He's been on the phone to Kate for more than an hour.

definitions.definition: to have a phone in your home or place of work
examples: They're not on the phone at the holiday cottage.

origin: nounsense 5 mid 19th cent.: from Greekphōnē‘sound, voice’.

definitions.definition: what is measured in minutes, hours, days, etc.

time for somethingIt's time for lunch.
time (that)…It's time the kids were in bed.

time for somethingDo you have time for a chat?

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event

one of the fastest times ever

in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.
If you didn't win a prize, better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.
to deliberately make a situation difficult and unpleasant for somebody
examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: used to introduce another possibility
examples: Is your sister older or younger than you?

definitions.definition: used in negative sentences when mentioning two or more things
examples: He can't read or write.

definitions.definition: used to warn or advise somebody that something bad could happen;otherwise

definitions.definition: used between two numbers to show approximately how many

definitions.definition: used to introduce a different or opposite idea
examples: He was lying—or was he?

definitions.definition: about; approximately

definitions.definition: used when you are not exactly sure about a person, thing or place
examples: He's a factory supervisor or something.

way (that)…It's not what you say—it's the way that you say it.

definitions.definition: a way of doing something or of treating people that is much stronger and less sensitive than it needs to be
examples: the heavy hand of management

definitions.definition: to give somebody support in a difficult situation
examples: Do you want me to come along and hold your hand?

definitions.definition: to admit that you have made a mistake or are responsible for something bad
examples: I have to hold my hands up and admit that some of the problems have been all my own fault.

definitions.definition: being taken care of or dealt with by somebody that you think you can rely on
examples: Can I leave these queries in your capable hands?
He retired feeling confident that his company was in safe hands.

definitions.definition: if you have time or moneyin hand, it is left and available to be used
examples: We managed to redecorate the house and still have some savings in hand.

definitions.definition: if you have a particular situationin hand, you are in control of it
examples: Don’t worry about the travel arrangements—everything is in hand.

definitions.definition: the job, question, etc.in handis the one that you are dealing with
examples: Please confine your comments to the topic in hand.

definitions.definition: if somebody works a week, month, etc.in hand, they are paid for the work a week, etc. after they have completed it
being taken care of or controlled by somebody
examples: The matter is now in the hands of my lawyer.
At that time, the castle was in enemy hands.

definitions.definition: being taken care of well by somebody
examples: I've left the kids in safe hands—with my parents.
Their problem was in the safe hands of the experts.

definitions.definition: if you use the wordsan iron fist/handwhen describing the way that somebody behaves, you mean that they treat people severely. This treatment may be hidden behind a kind appearance (thevelvet glove).
examples: They promised that the army would strike with an iron fist at any resistance.
The iron hand in the velvet glove approach seems to work best with this age group.

definitions.definition: used to say that you are too busy to do anything else
if two peoplejoin hands, they hold each other’s hands
to work together in doing something
examples: Education has been reluctant to join hands with business.

definitions.definition: to occasionally do something that you used to do a lot so that you do not lose your skill at it
examples: She retired last year but still teaches the odd class to keep her hand in.

definitions.definition: to be very familiar with somebody/something
examples: This is where I grew up. I know this area like the back of my hand.

definitions.definition: to catch somebody that you are annoyed with
examples: Wait till I get my hands on him!

definitions.definition: to find or get something
examples: I know their address is here somewhere, but I can't lay my hands on it right now.

definitions.definition: to spend all the money you earn on basic needs such as food without being able to save any money
to make/lose money very fast and in large quantities
used to say that a job is made easier if a lot of people help
to do no work
examples: She hasn't done a hand's turn all week.

definitions.definition: to do nothing to help somebody
examples: The children never lift a finger to help around the house.

definitions.definition: to hold out your hand for somebody to shake
no longer your responsibility
on both/all sides; in both/all directions
examples: Mist curled from the water on either hand.

definitions.definition: available, especially to help
examples: The emergency services were on hand with medical advice.

definitions.definition: if you have somebody/somethingon your hands, you are responsible for or have to deal with them/it
examples: Let me take care of the invitations—you've enough on your hands with the caterers.
They’ll have a fight on their hands if they want to close down the school.

definitions.definition: used to introduce different points of view, ideas, etc., especially when they are opposites
examples: On the one hand they'd love to have kids, but on the other, they don't want to give up their freedom.

definitions.definition: difficult or impossible to control
examples: Unemployment is getting out of hand.

definitions.definition: if you reject, etc. somethingout of hand, you do so immediately without thinking about it fully or listening to other people’s arguments
examples: All our suggestions were dismissed out of hand.

definitions.definition: no longer your responsibility
examples: I'm afraid the matter is now out of my hands.

definitions.definition: to fail to achieve success by judging your position to be stronger than it really is
a person who can do, or is doing, a job
examples: We need an extra pair of hands if we're going to finish on time.

definitions.definition: to do exactly what an enemy, opponent, etc. wants so that they gain the advantage in a particular situation
examples: If we get the police involved, we'll be playing right into the protesters' hands.

definitions.definition: to spend money or give it to somebody
examples: I've heard he doesn't like putting his hand in his pocket.

definitions.definition: easily controlled or influenced by another person
examples: She'll persuade him. He's like putty in her hands.

definitions.definition: to hit or threaten to hit somebody
a person that you can trust to do a job well
examples: Colleagues regard him as a safe pair of hands.

definitions.definition: by being told about something by somebody else who has seen it or heard about it, not by experiencing, seeing, etc. it yourself
examples: I'm fed up of hearing about these decisions third hand!

definitions.definition: to make your plans or intentions known
a way of voting for or against something in which people raise their hands to vote
examples: The vote was passed by a show of hands.
Let’s have a show of hands. Who’s in favour of the proposal?

definitions.definition: to stop yourself from doing something; to prevent you from doing something
examples: It was prudence, not logic, that stayed his hand.

definitions.definition: to make yourself do something that you are afraid of
examples: Taking her courage in both hands, she opened the door and walked in.

definitions.definition: to deal with somebody in a strict way in order to improve their behaviour
to deal with a particular situation yourself because you are not happy with the way that others are dealing with it
to do something illegal in order to punish somebody for doing something wrong, instead of letting the police deal with them
examples: After a series of burglaries in the area, the police are worried that residents might take the law into their own hands.
When police failed to arrest the suspect, local people took the law into their own hands and beat him up.

definitions.definition: to risk being killed
examples: You take your life in your hands just crossing the road here.

definitions.definition: to stop doing something or taking part in something, especially because you are not successful
to raise both hands in the air to show that you are feeling annoyed or upset about something
examples: We threw up our hands in despair at the scale of the problem.

definitions.definition: that you can reach or get easily
examples: I'm afraid I don't have the latest figures to hand.

definitions.definition: to do something such as an activity or a sport for the first time
to start doing something or be able to do something, especially when you do it well
examples: Jim can turn his hand to most jobs around the house.

definitions.definition: to take care of somebody’s needs so well that they do not have to do anything for themselves
examples: He seems to expect me to wait on him hand and foot.

definitions.definition: to refuse to be responsible for or involved with somebody/something
examples: When her son was arrested again she washed her hands of him.
I’ve washed my hands of the whole sordid business.

definitions.definition: to win something very easily
to press somebody’s hand very hard when you shake hands
to hold your hands together, andtwistand press them together in a way that shows you are anxious or upset, especially when you cannot change the situation

origin: Old Englishhand,hond, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhandand GermanHand.

definitions.definition: to mention or speak about somebody/something
examples: The victims were not referred to by name.

definitions.definition: to describe or be connected to somebody/something
examples: The star refers to items which are intended for the advanced learner.

definitions.definition: to look at something or ask a person for information
examples: You may refer to your notes if you want.
to refer to a dictionary

origin: N/A

examples: I don't find him funny at all. Quite the contrary.

examples: I've been there quite a few times.

examples: They drank quite a lot of wine.

definitions.definition: a large amount of something
examples: She hasn't been seen for quite some time.

definitions.definition: used to show that a person or thing is particularly impressive or unusual in some way

origin: Middle English: from the obsolete adjectivequite, variant ofquit.

time (that)…It's time the kids were in bed.

the violent times we live in(= the present period of history)

one of the fastest times ever

in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always

examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.

examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.

a very difficult or unpleasant job or time

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.

examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly

definitions.definition: many times; frequently

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens

used to say that something should have happened before now

that has ever existed

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something

examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: to be connected with something/somebody; to refer to something/somebody

definitions.definition: to be able to understand and have sympathy with somebody/something

definitions.definition: closely connected with the subject you are discussing or the situation you are in

definitions.definition: the fact of being valuable and useful to people in their lives and work

used to comment on something that was almost successful but in fact failed

origin: Old Englishfeorr, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchver, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritparaand Greekpera‘further’.

definitions.definition: to organize the contents of something; to tidy something

examples: Could you sort out the toys that can be thrown away?

definitions.definition: to deal with somebody’s/your own problems successfully

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to such a great degree

definitions.definition: likely to be affected by something, especially something bad

origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘(person) owing obedience’): from Old Frenchsuget, from Latinsubjectus‘brought under’, past participle ofsubicere, fromsub-‘under’ +jacere‘throw’. Senses relating to philosophy, logic, and grammar are derived ultimately from Aristotle's use ofto hupokeimenonmeaning ‘material from which things are made’ and ‘subject of attributes and predicates’.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the great degree of something
examples: This issue was of such importance that we could not afford to ignore it.
such a/an…Why are you in such a hurry?

definitions.definition: of the type already mentioned

examples: The new job is not a promotion as such, but it has good prospects.

definitions.definition: to be successful in life

The form with-dwas established in the 16th cent.sound somebody out/​sound something out.Middle English: from Old Englishgesund, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchgezondand Germangesund.verbsense 6 late Middle English: from Old Frenchsonder, based on Latinsub-‘below’ +unda‘wave’.

definitions.definition: confident that you know something or that you are right

definitions.definition: used to say ‘yes’ to a suggestion or request
examples: ‘Are you coming?’ ‘Sure thing.’

definitions.definition: used to admit that something is true
examples: He is intelligent, to be sure, but he's also very lazy.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchsur, from Latinsecurus‘free from care’.

definitions.definition: to become bigger or more important than something else; to replace something
examples: Try not to let negative thoughts take over.
It has been suggested that mammals took over from dinosaurs 65 million years ago.
In your teens, peer-group friendships may take over from parents as the major influence on you.

definitions.definition: to begin to have control of or responsibility for something, especially in place of somebody else
examples: Paul's daughter took over the job in 2017.
Gerald Ford took over the presidency after Nixon resigned.

definitions.definition: to gain control of a political party, a country, etc.
examples: The army is threatening to take over if civil unrest continues.

definitions.definition: to gain control of a business, a company, etc., especially by buying shares
examples: CBS Records was taken over by Sony.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to get up onto your feet
examples: The children stood up when the teacher walked into the room.

definitions.definition: to be on your feet
examples: There were no seats left so I had to stand up.
You'll look taller if youstand up straight.

definitions.definition: to deliberately not meet somebody you have arranged to meet, especially somebody you are having a romantic relationship with
examples: I've been stood up!

definitions.definition: to remain true, relevant or acceptable even when tested, examined closely, etc.
examples: His argument simply doesn't stand up to close scrutiny.
I'm afraid this document will never stand up in a court of law.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to achieve something that you have been trying to do or get; to have the result or effect that was intended
examples: Our plan succeeded.
Whether wesucceed or faildepends on ingenuity and luck.
succeed in doing somethingHe succeeded in getting a place at art school.
I tried to discuss it with her butonly succeededin making her angry(= I failed and did the opposite of what I intended).

definitions.definition: to be successful in your job, earning money, power, respect, etc.
examples: You will have to work hard if you are to succeed.
succeed in somethingShe doesn't have the ruthlessness required to succeed in business.
succeed as somethingHe had hoped to succeed as a violinist.

definitions.definition: to come next after somebody/something and take their/its place or position
examples: Who succeeded Kennedy as President?
Their early success was succeeded by a period of miserable failure.
Strands of DNA are reproduced throughsucceeding generations.

definitions.definition: to gain the right to a title, property, etc. when somebody dies
examples: She succeeded to the throne(= became queen)in 1558.
He had no right to succeed to the tenancy when his father died.

definitions.definition: when you are successful in one area of your life, it often leads to success in other areas

origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchsuccederor Latinsuccedere‘come close after’, fromsub-‘close to’ +cedere‘go’.

definitions.definition: to continue, especially starting after somebody/something else has finished
examples: The band's new album takes up where their last one left off.

definitions.definition: to fill or use an amount of space or time
examples: The table takes up too much room.
I won't take up any more of your time.
Her time is fully taken up with writing.

definitions.definition: to learn or start to do something, especially for pleasure
examples: They've taken up golf.
She has taken up(= started to learn to play)the oboe.

definitions.definition: to start or begin something such as a job
examples: He takes up his duties next week.

definitions.definition: to join in singing or saying something
examples: to take up the chorus
Their protests were later taken up by other groups.

definitions.definition: to continue something that somebody else has not finished, or that has not been mentioned for some time
examples: She took up the story where Tim had left off.
I'd like to take up the point you raised earlier.

definitions.definition: to move into a particular position
examples: I took up my position by the door.

definitions.definition: to accept something that is offered or available
examples: to take up a challenge
She took up his offer of a drink.

definitions.definition: to make something such as a piece of clothing shorter
examples: This skirt needs taking up.

definitions.definition: to be badly affected by a disease, pain, sad feelings, a lack of something, etc.
examples: I hate to see animals suffering.
The country hassufferedgreatlyat the hands ofits corrupt government.
suffer from somethingto suffer from a disorder/a disease/an illness/a condition
patients suffering from depression/cancer
Increasing numbers of children aresuffering frommental healthproblems.
He suffers from asthma.

definitions.definition: to experience something unpleasant, such as injury, defeat or loss
examples: tosuffer a stroke/heart attack
Victimssufferedsevereinjuriesin the accident.
The companysufferedhugelossesin the last financial year.
Cropssufferedseriousdamageas a result of the floods.
The partysuffered ahumiliatingdefeatin the general election.
His mother died young, and his sistersuffered the same fate(= his sister also died young).

definitions.definition: to become worse
examples: His school work is suffering because of family problems.

definitions.definition: to be very impatient with people that you think are stupid
examples: She was a forceful personality who didn't suffer fools gladly.

origin: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman Frenchsuffrir, from Latinsufferre, fromsub-‘from below’ +ferre‘to bear’.

definitions.definition: a thing that helps you to be better or more successful than other people
examples: adistinct/significant/huge advantage
Itgivesyouan unfair advantage(= something that benefits you, but not your opponents).
Shehad the advantage ofa good education.
You willbe at an advantage(= have an advantage)in the interview if you have thought about the questions in advance.
advantage over somebodyThe company was able to gain acompetitive advantageover its rivals by reducing costs.
Being tall gave him an advantage over the other players.

definitions.definition: a quality of something that makes it better or more useful
examples: Each of these systems has itsadvantages and disadvantages.
Continuous assessment offers clear advantages for teachers and students.
advantage of (doing) somethingA small carhas theaddedadvantage ofbeing cheaper to run.
advantage in (doing) somethingIs there any advantage in getting there early?
advantage to (doing) somethingThere are many advantages to online shopping.

definitions.definition: the first point scored after a score of 40–40
examples: Advantage Miss Stephens.

definitions.definition: to give you an advantage; to change a situation in a way that gives you an advantage
examples: It would be to your advantage to attend this meeting.
Eventually, the new regulations will work to our advantage.

definitions.definition: to make use of something well; to make use of an opportunity
examples: She took advantage of the children's absence to tidy their rooms.

I took complete advantage of the situation.

definitions.definition: to make use of somebody/something in a way that is unfair or dishonest
examples: He took advantage of my generosity(= for example, by taking more than I had intended to give).

definitions.definition: in a way that shows the best of somebody/something
examples: The photograph showed him to advantage.
May was the month to see the garden to best advantage.
The bright lighting showed the jewels to their best advantage.

definitions.definition: to use or change a bad situation so that it helps you
examples: Every magician has a trick that goes wrong, but you can turn this to your advantage by pretending it was deliberate and making the audience laugh.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchavantage, fromavant‘in front’, from late Latinabante‘in front’, fromab‘from’ +ante‘before’. The initiala-was mistakenly assimilated toad-in the 16th cent.

definitions.definition: an area of water that has been created for people to swim in
examples: anindoor/outdoor swimming pool
aheated swimming pool
anopen-air swimming pool

definitions.definition: the building that contains a publicswimming pool
examples: She trained five times a week at her local swimming pool.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to talk to somebody about something; to have a conversation with somebody
examples: speak to somebodyThe President refused to speak to reporters.
speak about somethingWe never spoke about her illness.

speak to somebody about something/somebodyI've spoken to the manager about it.
(especially North American English)speak with somebody (about something/somebody)Can I speak with you for a minute?
‘Do you know him?’ ‘Not to speak to.’(= I recognize him but do not really know him)
‘Can I speak to Susan?’ ‘Speaking.’(= at the beginning of a phone conversation)
I saw her in the street but we didn't speak.

definitions.definition: to use your voice to say something
examples: The illness left him unable to speak.

definitions.definition: to be able to use a particular language
examples: to speak several languages
to speak a little Urdu

definitions.definition: to use a particular language to express yourself
examples: speak somethingWhat language is it they're speaking?
speak in somethingWould you prefer it if we spoke in German?

definitions.definition: speaking the language mentioned
examples: French-speaking Canada
non-English-speaking students

definitions.definition: to mention or describe something/somebody
examples: She still speaks about him with great affection.
He wasspeaking publiclyfor the first time about the incident.

Her studentsspeakveryhighlyof her(= they say very good things about her).
Speaking of travelling,(= referring back to a subject just mentioned)are you going anywhere exciting this year?

definitions.definition: to make a speech to an audience
examples: Hundreds of people came to hear her speak.
+ adv./prep.I hatespeaking in public.
He wasinvited to speakat a conference.
to speak on the radio
speak (to somebody) (about something)Professor Todd spoke to the group about her research.
Shespoke in favour ofthe new tax.
He has a number ofspeaking engagementsthis week.

definitions.definition: to say or state something
examples: She was clearlyspeaking the truth.
He spoke the final words of the play.
They had the courage to speak the truth.

definitions.definition: what a person actually does means more than what they say they will do
to be willing to be polite or friendly towards somebody, especially after an argument
examples: She's not been on speaking terms with her uncle for years.
Are they speaking to each other again yet?

definitions.definition: it is not necessary to give any further explanation about something because the information that is available already proves that it is true
used to show that what you are saying is true in a general, etc. way
examples: Generally speaking, the more you pay, the more you get.
There are, broadly speaking, two ways of doing this.
Personally speaking, I've always preferred Italian food.

definitions.definition: if you think about it in a particular way; true in some but not all ways
examples: All these points of view are related, in a manner of speaking.

definitions.definition: such a small amount that it is not worth mentioning
examples: They've got no friends to speak of.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you are expressing something in an unusual or humorous way
examples: They were all very similar. All cut from the same cloth, so to speak.

definitions.definition: to be so easy to see and understand that you do not need to say anything else about it/them
examples: Her success speaks for itself.
Recent events speak for themselves.

definitions.definition: to express what you think or want yourself, rather than somebody else doing it for you
examples: I'm quite capable of speaking for myself, thank you!

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that a general statement they have just made is not true of you
examples: ‘We didn't play very well.’ ‘Speak for yourself!’(= I think that I played well.)

definitions.definition: to say or think bad things about somebody
examples: Don't speak ill of the dead.

definitions.definition: used to say that you are the type of person mentioned and are expressing your opinion from that point of view
examples: Speaking as a parent, I'm very concerned about standards in education.

definitions.definition: to say exactly what you think, in a very direct way
examples: She’s never hesitated about speaking her mind.
Everyone should have the right to speak their mind.

definitions.definition: people sayspeak/talk of the devilwhen somebody they have been talking about appears unexpectedly
examples: Well, speak of the devil—here's Alice now!

definitions.definition: to say something when you should not, for example because it is not the right time or you are not the right person to say it
to say something that you should not because it is the wrong situation or because it offends somebody
to be able to communicate easily with another person because you share similar opinions and experience
to tell you a lot about something/somebody, without the need for words
examples: His achievement speaks volumes for his determination.
What you wear speaks volumes about you.

definitions.definition: to say good or bad things about somebody
examples: She never speaks ill of anyone.

definitions.definition: if you are using words or rules in their exact or correct sense
examples: Strictly speaking, the book is not a novel, but a short story.
Using the word in that context is not, strictly speaking, correct.

origin: Old Englishsprecan, laterspecan, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchsprekenand Germansprechen.

definitions.definition: to begin to have a particular quality, appearance, etc.
examples: The chameleon can take on the colours of its background.

definitions.definition: to employ somebody
examples: to take on new staff

definitions.definition: to play against somebody in a game or contest; to fight against somebody

definitions.definition: to decide to do something; to agree to be responsible for something/somebody
examples: I can't take on any extra work.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody/something to enter
examples: The bus stopped to take on more passengers.

definitions.definition: an arrangement that somebody has with a bank, etc. to keep money there, take some out, etc.

account with somebody/somethingDo you have an account with us?
account at somethingHeopened an accountat a bank in Germany.

definitions.definition: an arrangement that somebody has with a company that allows them to use the internet, send and receive messages by email, social media, etc.

a first-hand/personal/first-person account
account of somethingCan yougiveusan accountof what happened?

definitions.definition: an explanation or a description of an idea, a theory or a process

definitions.definition: a written record of money that is owed to a business and of money that has been paid by it
examples: to do the accounts
to keep the accounts up to date
the accounts department

definitions.definition: an arrangement with a shop or business to pay bills for goods or services at a later time, for example in regular amounts every month
examples: Put it on my account please.

definitions.definition: a bill for goods or services provided over a period
examples: Departing guests shouldsettle their accountsat the office.

definitions.definition: a regular customer
examples: The agency has lost several of its most important accounts.

definitions.definition: a description of an event that gives you all the details in the order in which they happen
examples: He insisted on giving us a blow-by-blow account of what had happened.
She gave us a blow-by-blow account of the incident.

definitions.definition: according to what other people say
examples: I've never been there, but it's a lovely place, by all accounts.

definitions.definition: according to what you say yourself
examples: By his own account he had an unhappy childhood.

definitions.definition: to make somebody explain a mistake, etc. because they are responsible for it
to do something or perform well, especially in a contest
examples: The team gave a good account of themselves in the match.

definitions.definition: not important
examples: Emotional matters were of no account to them during the war.

definitions.definition: if you buy something or payon account, you pay nothing or only a small amount immediately and the rest later
examples: Call a cab on account.

definitions.definition: because of what you think somebody wants
examples: Please don't change your plans on my account.

definitions.definition: because of somebody/something
examples: She retired early on account of ill health.

definitions.definition: not for any reason
examples: On no account should the house be left unlocked.

definitions.definition: for yourself
examples: In 2012 Smith set up in business on his own account.

definitions.definition: because you want to and you have decided, not somebody else
examples: No one sent me. I am here on my own account.

definitions.definition: because of the particular thing that has been mentioned
examples: Weather conditions were poor, but he did not delay his departure on that account.

definitions.definition: to use something in a good or helpful way
examples: He turned his artistic talents to good account by becoming a sculptor.

definitions.definition: to hurt or punish somebody who has harmed or cheated you in the past
examples: ‘Who would do such a thing?’ ‘Maybe someone with an old score to settle.’

definitions.definition: to consider particular facts, circumstances, etc. when making a decision about something
examples: The company takes account of environmental issues wherever possible.

origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘counting’, ‘to count’): from Old Frenchacont(noun),aconter(verb), based onconter‘to count’.

definitions.definition: a period of 100 years

for centuriesThey have lived there for centuries.
over the centuriesTheir techniques have evolved over the centuries.

definitions.definition: to leave the ground and begin to fly
examples: The plane took off an hour late.

definitions.definition: to leave a place, especially in a hurry
examples: When he saw me coming he took off in the opposite direction.

definitions.definition: to become successful or popular very quickly or suddenly

definitions.definition: to copy somebody’s voice, actions or manner in a humorous way

examples: He was taken off after twenty minutes.

definitions.definition: to remove something, especially a piece of clothing from your/somebody’s body
examples: to take off your coat

examples: I've decided to take a few days off next week.

examples: The show was taken off because of poor audience figures.

definitions.definition: to remove some of somebody’s hair, part of somebody’s body, etc.

definitions.definition: to leave a place; to make somebody leave a place

examples: The officer leading the investigation has been taken off the case.

definitions.definition: to remove an amount of money or a number of marks, points, etc. in order to reduce the total
examples: The manager took $10 off the bill.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to begin doing or using something

start to do somethingIt started to rain.
I was starting to feel tired.

start doing somethingShe started laughing.

start on somethingIt's time you started on your homework.
start by doing somethingLet's start by reviewing what we did last week.

definitions.definition: to begin happening; to make something begin happening

start somethingWhostarted the fire?

start somebody/something doing somethingThe news started me thinking.

definitions.definition: when youstarta machine or a vehicle or itstarts, it begins to operate

definitions.definition: to begin to exist; to make something begin to exist

start upThere are a lot of small businesses starting up in that area.
start somethingShestarted a businesswhile she was still at school.

start something upHe decided to start up his own film festival.

definitions.definition: to begin from a particular place, amount or situation; to begin in a particular way

+ adj.The best professional musiciansstart young.

definitions.definition: to begin, or to begin something such as a career, in a particular way that changed later
examples: start as somethingShe started as a secretary but ended up running the department.

start out/offThe company started out with 30 employees.
start out/off as somethingI started off as a dishwasher making 25 cents an hour.

start something doing somethingShestarted her careerworking for the BBC.

definitions.definition: to begin a journey; to leave
examples: What time are we starting tomorrow?

definitions.definition: to begin to move in a particular direction
examples: I started after her(= began to follow her)to tell her the news.

definitions.definition: to move suddenly and quickly because you are surprised or afraid
examples: The sudden noise made her start.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody not to complain or be critical
examples: Don't start! I told you I'd be late.

definitions.definition: to make something start happening; to make sure that something continues to happen
to start a relationship well/badly
examples: I seem to have got off on the wrong foot with the new boss.

definitions.definition: to begin doing something
examples: It's nearly ten o'clock. Let's get started.
It's already late, so I think we should get started.

definitions.definition: to start a relationship well/badly
examples: I seem to have started off on the wrong foot with the new boss.

definitions.definition: to cause trouble
used when you are giving the first and most important reason for something
examples: To start with, it’s much too expensive…

definitions.definition: at the beginning
examples: The club had only six members to start with.

definitions.definition: used to blame somebody for beginning a fight or an argument

origin: Old Englishstyrtan‘to caper, leap’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchstorten‘push’ and Germanstürzen‘fall headlong, fling’. From the sense ‘sudden movement’ arose the sense ‘initiation of movement, setting out on a journey’ and hence ‘beginning of a process, etc.’.

definitions.definition: the true facts about something, rather than the things that have been invented or guessed
examples: Do you think she'stelling the truth?
We are determined to get at(= discover)the truth.
She had onlyspoken the truth.
So now youknow the truth.
I knew thetruthwouldcome outin the end.
the truth is (that)…The truth is that there are no easy answers.

Thesimple truthis that new roads just encourage more traffic.
the truth about somethingI don't think you are telling methe whole truthabout what happened.

The awful truth about his disappearance finally dawned on her.
the truth behind somethingOnly recently has the truth behind the killings emerged.
It’s thegospel truth!(= completely true)
I honestly don't know, and that's the truth.

definitions.definition: the quality or state of being based on fact
examples: there is no/some/much, etc. truth in somethingThere is no truth in the rumours.

definitions.definition: a fact that is believed by most people to be true
examples: universal truths

definitions.definition: if you say that somebody has beeneconomical with the truth, you mean that they left out some important facts, but you do not want to say that they were lying
to say something that is not completely true
examples: I wasn’t exactly lying when I said I hadn’t seen her—I was just bending the truth a little.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody the true facts about a situation, especially when these are not known by other people
examples: If the truth be known, I was afraid to tell anyone.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize the true facts about a situation
examples: She laughed and chatted but was, in truth, not having much fun.

definitions.definition: a time when somebody/something is tested, or when important decisions are made
examples: The moment of truth is when the trainee pilots take over the controls of the plane.

definitions.definition: used to say that a fact or comment is completely false
examples: She thinks I don’t like her but nothing could be further from the truth.

definitions.definition: used when admitting something
examples: To tell you the truth, I'll be glad to get home.

definitions.definition: used to say that things that actually happen are often more surprising than stories that are invented
used to say that people will find out the true facts about a situation even if you try to keep them secret

origin: Old Englishtrīewth,trēowth‘faithfulness, constancy’ (seetrue,-th).

definitions.definition: some but not all of a thing
examples: We spent part of the time in the museum.

Voters are givenonly part of the story(= only some of the information).
Part of mefeels sorry for him(= I feel a little sorry for him, but not very sorry).
We spent a good part of the day rehearsing.

definitions.definition: a section, piece or feature of something
examples: part of somethingThe mountains are covered with snow for alarge partof the year.
The early part of her life was spent in Paris.
the latter part of the 19th century
Making mistakes is anintegral partof the learning process.
We've done the difficult part of the job.
animportant/essential partof the project
Wool production was akey partof the economy.
in partsThe novel is good in parts.
The procedure can be divided into two parts.
The worst part was having to wait three hours in the rain.

definitions.definition: an element or member of something; a person or thing that helps to make up a whole or belongs in it
examples: Snacks can be part of a healthy eating plan.
You need to be able to workas part of a team.
Under the Saxons, Westmorlandformed partof the Kingdom of Northumberland.
Antibiotics havebecome partof our daily existence.

definitions.definition: a separate piece or area of a human or animal body or of a plant
examples: The cancer could spread to otherparts of the body.
It may one day be possible to grow newbody partsin the laboratory.
Different insects feed on different parts of the plant.

definitions.definition: a piece of a machine or structure
examples: The factory manufactures aircraft parts.
car/machine/engine parts
the working parts of the machinery
Where can I getspare partsfor my bike?

definitions.definition: an area or a region of the world, a country, a town, etc.
examples: The island is mostly flat, but there are some hilly parts.
part of somethingthe northern part of town
differentparts of the world/country
Which part of Japan do you come from?
Come and visit us if you're ever in our part of the world.

definitions.definition: a region or an area
examples: She's not from these parts.
He's just arrived back from foreign parts.

definitions.definition: a section of a book, television series, etc., especially one that is published or broadcast separately
examples: The documentary was screened in four parts.
Henry IV, Part II
The final part will be shown next Sunday evening.

definitions.definition: a role played by an actor in a play, film, etc.; the words spoken by an actor in a particular role
examples: Sheplayed the partof Juliet.
(figurative)He's alwaysplaying a part(= pretending to be something that he is not).
Have you learned your part yet?
part in somethingHe got a small part in a West End play.
in a partShe was very good in the part.

definitions.definition: the way in which somebody/something is involved in an action or situation
examples: Luckplayed abigpartin their success.
She plays an active part in local politics.
We allhave a part to playin the fight against crime.
Hehad no part inthe decision.
Iwant no part ofthis sordid business.

definitions.definition: music for a particular voice or instrument in a group singing or playing together
examples: the clarinet part

I can play the guitar part to that song.

definitions.definition: a unit of measurement that allows you to compare the different amounts of substances in something
examples: Add three parts wine to one part water.
fluoride levels of 0.2 parts per million

definitions.definition: a line on a person’s head where the hair is divided with acomb
to be better or more effective as a group than you would think just by looking at the individual members of the group
examples: The team is greater than the sum of its parts.

definitions.definition: most of something, especially a period of time; more than half of something
examples: He drank the best part of a bottle of Scotch waiting for her to get home.
The journey took her the better part of an hour.

definitions.definition: you should avoid danger and not take unnecessary risks
mostly; usually
examples: The contributors are, for the most part, professional scientists.

definitions.definition: used to express your own, his, their, etc. opinion or feelings about something
examples: For my part, I would reject all these arguments.

definitions.definition: to a great extent
examples: Their success is due in large part to their determination.

definitions.definition: partly; to some extent
examples: Her success was due in part to luck.
Your salary depends in part on your qualifications.

definitions.definition: to have an appearance or wear clothes suitable for a particular job, role or position
examples: He acts and dresses the part of a gentleman.
He was a pirate in the school play and certainly looked the part.

definitions.definition: a person with many skills
made or done by somebody
examples: It was an error on my part.

definitions.definition: a normal part of something
examples: Keeping the accounts is part and parcel of my job.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that you are so used to seeing that you no longer notice them
examples: I worked there so long that I became part of the furniture.

definitions.definition: to accept something slightly unpleasant without complaining or being offended
examples: He took the teasing in good part.

definitions.definition: to be involved in something
examples: to take part in a discussion/demonstration/fight/celebration
How many countries took part in the last Olympic Games?
They took little part in the discussion.

definitions.definition: to support somebody, for example in an argument
examples: His mother always takes his part.

origin: Old English (denoting a part of speech), from Latinpars,part-. The verb (originally in Middle English in the sense ‘divide into parts’) is from Old Frenchpartir, from Latinpartire,partiri‘divide, share’.

definitions.definition: to say things; to speak in order to give information or to express ideas or feelings
examples: Stop talking and listen!
Wetalked on the phonefor over an hour.
talk to somebodyWho were you talking to just now?
Ann and Joearen't talkingto each other right now(= they refuse to speak to each other because they have argued).
talk to yourselfWatch the scene where Don talks to himself in the mirror while shaving.
talk with somebodyWe looked around the school and talked with the principal.
talk about somebody/somethingTheytalk a lotabout the inspiration for their music.
When they get together, all they talk about is football.
What are you talking about?(= used when you are surprised, annoyed and/or worried by something that somebody has just said)
I don't know what you're talking about(= used to say that you did not do something that somebody has accused you of).
to talk openly/freely/publicly about something
talk to/with somebody about somebody/somethingShe started talking loudly to her friends about the film they'd just seen.
talk of doing somethingMary is talking of looking for another job.
talk of somethingWe often talked of the war.
talk yourself + adj.We talked ourselves hoarse, catching up on all the news.

definitions.definition: to discuss something, usually something serious or important
examples: This situation can't go on. We need to talk.
The two sides in the dispute are finally willing to talk.
talk to/with somebodyTalk with your doctor before radically changing your diet.
talk about somethingIs there anything you'd like to talk about?
talk to/with somebody about somethingWe want to talk to people about this issue.
talk somethingtotalk politics

definitions.definition: to say words in a language
examples: The baby is just starting to talk.
Alex can't talk yet—he's only just one year old.
talk in somethingWe couldn't understand them because they were talking in Chinese.
talk somethingAre they talking Swedish or Danish?

definitions.definition: to say things that are/are not sensible
examples: She talks a lot of sense.
(British English)You're talking rubbish!
See if you cantalk some sense into him(= persuade him to be sensible).

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an amount of money, how serious something is, etc.
examples: We're talking £500 for three hours' work.
Do you know what this will cost? We’re talking megabucks here.

definitions.definition: to talk about a person’s private life
examples: Don't phone me at work—people will talk.

definitions.definition: to give information to somebody, especially unwillingly
examples: The police questioned him but he refused to talk.

definitions.definition: to try and persuade somebody to stop behaving in a stupid way, sometimes using rough or violent methods
examples: Try and talk some sense into her before she makes the wrong decision.
Where would I be without you to knock some sense into my head?

definitions.definition: to have knowledge about something from your own experience
examples: I worked there for 20 years, so I know what I'm talking about.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that they should not criticize somebody else for something because they do the same things too
examples: ‘George is so careless with money.’ ‘Look who's talking!’

definitions.definition: people who have a lot of money have more power and influence than others
used when you like what somebody has suggested very much
people sayspeak/talk of the devilwhen somebody they have been talking about appears unexpectedly
examples: Well, speak of the devil—here's Alice now!

definitions.definition: to say something that you should not because it is the wrong situation or because it offends somebody
to be able to communicate easily with another person because you share similar opinions and experience
used to emphasize something
examples: Talk about mean! She didn't even buy me a card.

definitions.definition: to talk to somebody about sex in order to make them sexually excited
to talk in a way that sounds convincing, but may not be sincere
examples: The senior managers talk a good game about customer relations, but really they don't care about the customer.

definitions.definition: to talk too much, especially about things that are boring or not important
used when you are going to say more about a subject that has already been mentioned
examples: Talking of Sue, I met her new boyfriend last week.

definitions.definition: people saytalk/speak of the devilwhen somebody they have been talking about appears unexpectedly
examples: Well, talk of the devil—here's Alice now!

definitions.definition: to talk about your work with the people you work with, especially when you are also with other people who are not connected with or interested in it
examples: Whenever we meet up with Clive and Sue they always end up talking shop.

definitions.definition: to be able to talk in a confident way that makes people think you are good at what you do
examples: You can talk the talk, but can you walk the walk?(= can you act in a way that matches your words?)

definitions.definition: to say silly things while you are talking about a subject you do not understand
to tell people very strongly what you want
to talk about something seriously
to make excuses and give reasons for not doing something; to manage to get yourself out of a difficult situation
examples: I managed to talk my way out of having to give a speech.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that they should not criticize somebody else for something because they do the same things too
used to tell somebody that they should not criticize somebody else for something because they do the same things too

origin: Middle English: frequentative verb from the Germanic base oftaleortell.

definitions.definition: used for referring to a person or thing that is not near the speaker, or not as near to the speaker as another
examples: Who's that?
That's Peter over there.
(British English)Hello. Is that Jo?(= when speaking on the phone)InNorth American EnglishsayIs this Jo?
That's a nice dress.
Those look riper than these.

definitions.definition: used for referring to somebody/something that has already been mentioned, or is already known about
examples: What can I do about that?
Do you remember when we went to Norway? That was a good trip.
That's exactly what I think.

definitions.definition: used for referring to people or things of a particular type
examples: Those present were in favour of change.
There are those who say(= some people say)she should not have got the job.
Salaries are higher here than those in my country.

definitions.definition: used as a relative pronoun to introduce a part of a sentence which refers to the person, thing or time you have been talking about
examples: Where's the letter that came yesterday?
Who was it that won the US Open?
The watch (that) you gave me keeps perfect time.
The people (that) I spoke to were very helpful.
It’s the best novel (that) I’ve ever read.
We moved here the year (that) my mother died.

definitions.definition: and everything else connected with an activity, a situation, etc.
examples: Did you bring the contract and (all) that?

definitions.definition: used to say what something means or to give more information
examples: He's a local government administrator, that is to say a civil servant.
You'll find her very helpful—if she's not too busy, that is.

definitions.definition: used to say that somebody is right, or is doing something right
examples: No, the other one… that’s it.
That's it, carry on!

definitions.definition: used to say that something is finished, or that no more can be done
examples: That's it, the fire's out now.
That's it for now, but if I get any news I'll let you know.
A week to go, and that's it!

definitions.definition: used to say that you will not accept something any longer
examples: That's it, I've had enough!

definitions.definition: used to talk about the reason for something
examples: So that's it—the fuse had gone.
You don't love me any more, is that it?

definitions.definition: used to say that your decision cannot be changed
examples: Well I'm not going, and that's that.

origin: Old Englishthæt, nominative and accusative singular neuter ofse‘the’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchdatand Germandas.

definitions.definition: the process of caring for somebody/something and providing what they need for their health or protection
examples: Some people were badly injured and neededmedical care.
We aim to continually improve the quality ofpatient care.
The parents shared equally in the care of the children.
care for somebody/somethingThe companyprovides carefor older people living in their own homes.
with careWith proper care, the plants may last for fifty years.
skin/hair careproducts

definitions.definition: the fact of providing a home in an institution run by the local authority or with another family for children who cannot live with their parents
examples: in careMost children in care live with foster carers.
The two girls weretaken into careafter their parents were killed.
He had been in foster care since he was five.
Mentors support teenagers leaving care.

definitions.definition: attention or thought that you give to something that you are doing so that you will do it well and avoid mistakes or damage
examples: Great careis needed when choosing a used car.
with careShe chose her words with care.
Fragile—handle with care(= written on a container holding something that is easily broken or damaged)
Consumers must exercise care when buying medicines online.

definitions.definition: a feeling of worry; something that causes problems or worries
examples: I felt free from the cares of the day as soon as I left the building.
Sam looked as if hedidn't have a care in the world.

definitions.definition: used when writing to somebody at another person’s address
examples: Write to him care of his lawyer.

definitions.definition: being cared for by somebody
examples: The child was left in the care of friends.

definitions.definition: used when saying goodbye
examples: Bye! Take care!

definitions.definition: to be careful
examples: Take care (that) you don't spill any of it!
Care should be taken to close the lid securely.

definitions.definition: to care for somebody/something/yourself; to be careful about something
examples: Who's taking care of the children while you're away?

definitions.definition: to be responsible for or to deal with a situation or task
examples: Don't worry about the travel arrangements. They're all being taken care of.
