definitions.definition: receiving medical care from somebody
examples: He's under the care of Dr Parks.
refugees under the care of the relief agencies

origin: Old Englishcaru(noun),carian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Old High Germanchara‘grief, lament’,charon‘grieve’, and Old Norsekǫr‘sickbed’.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are grateful to somebody for something they have done
examples: thank you (for something)Thank you for your letter.
thank you (for doing something)Thank you very muchfor sending the photos.

definitions.definition: a polite way of accepting something that somebody has offered you
examples: ‘Would you like some help with that?’ ‘Oh, thank you.’

definitions.definition: a polite way of refusing something that somebody has offered you
examples: ‘Would you like some more cake?’ ‘No thank you.’

definitions.definition: used at the end of a sentence to tell somebody clearly that you do not need their help or advice
examples: ‘Shall I do that?’ ‘I can do it myself, thank you.’

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a thing that is known to be true, especially when it can be proved
examples: fact about somethingFirst, somebasic factsabout healthy eating.
fact (that)…Isn't it a fact that the firm is losing money?
It's awell-known factthat dogs have an acute sense of smell.
Iknow for a fact(= I am certain)that she's involved in something illegal.
The report is based onhard facts(= information that can be proved to be true).
Get your facts right(= make sure your information is correct)before you start making accusations.
It's about time you learnt toface (the) facts(= accepted the truth about the situation).
(informal)We didn't get on well, and that's a fact.
The judge instructed both lawyers to stick to the facts of the case.

definitions.definition: things that are true rather than things that have been invented
examples: The story is based on fact.
Is the accountfact or fiction?
This is simplyhistorical fact.
It's important to distinguish between fact and opinion.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a particular situation that exists
examples: the fact that…I could no longerignore the fact thathe was unhappy.
We don'thide the fact thatwe've made mistakes.
I did everything I could, and sheacknowledged the fact.
Students are already highly media-literate and the curriculum needs toreflect this fact.
The very factthat this happened more than once shows there is a problem.
despite the fact that…Despite the fact that she was wearing a seat belt, she was thrown sharply forward.
due to the fact that…Due to the fact that they did not read English, the prisoners were unaware of what they were signing.
apart from the fact that…She was happy apart from the fact that she could not return home.
in view of the fact that…Voluntary work was particularly important in view of the fact that women were often forced to give up paid work on marriage.
the fact of somethingWe want to celebrate thesimple factof their being here.
Themere factof being poor can put children at an educational disadvantage.
The fact remainsthat we are still two teachers short.

definitions.definition: after something has happened or been done, when it is too late to prevent it or change it
examples: On some vital decisions employees were only informed after the fact.

definitions.definition: used to add a comment on something that you have just said, usually adding something that you think the other person will be interested in
examples: It's a nice place. We've stayed there ourselves, as a matter of fact.

definitions.definition: used to disagree with something that somebody has just said
examples: ‘I suppose you'll be leaving soon, then?’ ‘No, as a matter of fact I'll be staying for another two years.’

definitions.definition: used to emphasize a statement, especially one that is the opposite of what has just been mentioned
examples: A new car would be wonderful but the fact of the matter is that we can't afford one.

definitions.definition: a situation that cannot be changed, especially one that is unpleasant
examples: Illness is just a fact of life.
It’s a fact of life that some people will always be racist.

definitions.definition: accurate and detailed information
examples: I've asked to see all the facts and figures before I make a decision.
All the facts and figures were presented at the meeting.

definitions.definition: the details about sex and about how babies are born, especially as told to children
it is not necessary to give any further explanation about something because the information that is available already proves that it is true
used to give extra details about something that has just been mentioned
examples: I used to live in France; in fact, not far from where you're going.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize a statement, especially one that is the opposite of what has just been mentioned
examples: I thought the work would be difficult. In actual fact, it's very easy.

definitions.definition: used to say what is true in a situation
examples: In point of fact, she is their adopted daughter.

definitions.definition: used to reply to a statement that you find interesting or surprising, or that you do not believe
examples: ‘She says I'm one of the best students she's ever taught.’ ‘Is that a fact?’

origin: late 15th cent.: from Latinfactum, neuter past participle offacere‘do’. The original sense was ‘an act’, later ‘a crime’, surviving in the phrasebefore (or after) the fact. The earliest of the current senses (‘truth, reality’) dates from the late 16th cent.

definitions.definition: a particular situation or a situation of a particular type
examples: in… casesIn some casespeople have had to wait several weeks for an appointment.
In most casesthe increases have been marginal.
In many casesit is the pressure of the job that causes managers to behave like this.
What should be donein such cases?
The substances causes nausea, sickness and,in extreme cases, death.
in cases of somethingThe company only dismisses its employees in cases of gross misconduct.
in the case of somethingIn the case of banks, the law can limit activities.
Sometimes compost isn't enough,in which caseartificial products may have to be used.
a case of somethingIt's aclassic case(= a very typical case)of bad planning.
This appears to be a case of mistaken identity.

definitions.definition: the true situation
examples: be the caseIf that is the case(= if the situation described is true), we need more staff.
it's the case that…It is simply not the case that prison conditions are improving.
It remains the case that not enough graduates are going into teaching.

definitions.definition: a situation that relates to a particular person or thing
examples: in somebody's caseI cannot make an exception in your case(= for you and not for others).
In your case, we are prepared to be lenient.
in this caseDon't underestimate the power of the pen, or in this case, the power of the keyboard.
In the present case, the facts are not disputed.
Every application will be decidedon a case-by-case basis(= each one will be considered separately).

definitions.definition: a matter that is being officially investigated, especially by the police
examples: They never solved the Jones murder case.
Police believe it is a simple case of theft.
Four officers areinvestigating the case.

definitions.definition: a question to be decided in court
examples: acourt case
acriminal/civil case
Thecase will be heardnext week.
The Supreme Court heard the case yesterday.
towin/lose a case
When does her case come before the court?

definitions.definition: a set of facts or arguments that support one side in a trial, a discussion, etc.
examples: Our lawyer didn't think wehad a case(= had enough good arguments to win in a court of law).
case for somethingthe case for the defence/prosecution
case against somebody/somethingThe case against her was very weak.
Let us considerthe case for and againstprivate education.
case for doing somethingThe reportmakes out astrongcase(= gives good arguments)for spending more money on hospitals.
He successfullyargued the casefor accepting the agreement.
case that…They try tomake the casethat this war is necessary.
You will each be given the change to state your case.

definitions.definition: a container or cover used to protect or store things; a container with its contents or the amount that it contains
examples: a jewellery case

case of somethinga case(= 12 bottles)of champagne

definitions.definition: a container with flat sides and a handle, used for carrying clothes, etc. when you are travelling
examples: Let me carry your case for you.

definitions.definition: the fact of somebody having a disease or an injury; a person who has a disease or an injury
examples: a severe case of food poisoning
Over 130 000casesof cholerawere reportedin 2016.
The most serious cases were treated at the scene of the accident.

definitions.definition: a person who needs, or is thought to need, special treatment or attention
examples: He's a hopeless case.
After the exams, the teacher gave all borderline cases a spoken test.

definitions.definition: the form of a noun, an adjective or a pronoun in some languages, that shows its relationship to another word
examples: the nominative/accusative/genitive case
Latin nouns have case, number and gender.
In Polish the verb ‘to be’ takes the instrumental case.

definitions.definition: used to say that one of two or more possibilities is true, but which one is true depends on the circumstances
examples: There may be an announcement about this tomorrow—or not, as the case may be.

definitions.definition: to criticize somebody all the time
examples: She's always on my case about cleaning my room.

definitions.definition: to be dealing with a particular matter, especially a criminal investigation
examples: We have two agents on the case.

definitions.definition: a clear example of the problem, situation, etc. that is being discussed
examples: Many professions feel they deserve higher pay, and nurses are a case in point.

definitions.definition: a situation in business, politics, etc. where there is a lot of competition and people are willing to harm each other in order to succeed
examples: I'm afraid in this line of work it's a case of dog eat dog.
We're operating in a dog-eat-dog world.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to stop criticizing you
whatever happens or may have happened
examples: There's no point complaining now—we're leaving tomorrow in any case.

definitions.definition: because of the possibility of something happening
examples: You'd better take the keys in case I'm out.
You probably won't need to call—but take my number, just in case.
In case(= if it is true that)you’re wondering why Jo’s here—let me explain…

definitions.definition: if something happens
examples: In case of fire, ring the alarm bell.

definitions.definition: if that happens or has happened; if that is the situation
examples: ‘I’ve made up my mind.’ ‘In that case, there’s no point discussing it.’

definitions.definition: used to say that you do not need to say any more about something because you think that you have proved your point
used by lawyers in court to say that they have finished presenting their case
examples: The prosecution rests its case.

origin: nounsenses 1 to 6 andnounsenses 9 to 11 Middle English: from Old Frenchcas, from Latincasus‘fall’, related tocadere‘to fall’; in sense (11) directly from Latin, translating Greekptōsis, literally ‘fall’.nounsenses 7 to 8 late Middle English: from Old Frenchcasse,chasse(moderncaisse‘trunk, chest’,châsse‘reliquary, frame’), from Latincapsa, related tocapere‘to hold’.

definitions.definition: that is/are going to be mentioned next
examples: Answer the following questions.
The following article was written in 2017.

definitions.definition: next in time
examples: the following afternoon/month/year/week
They arrived on Monday evening and we got there the following day.
In the following nights, Lucy slept poorly.

definitions.definition: a wind blowing in the same direction as a ship or other vehicle that helps it move faster
examples: With a generous following wind, we sped across the lake.
(figurative)With a bit of luck and a following wind, the project will be completed on schedule.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: words or actions that show that you are grateful to somebody for something
examples: thanks to somebody for somethingHow can I everexpressmythanksto you for all you've done?

thanks to somebodyWeextendourthanksto Mary, who is stepping down as chair after serving for three years.

special/heartfelt thanks

Thanks to everyone who has participated.
She murmured her thanks.
He took the book withouta word of thanks.

definitions.definition: despite somebody/something; with no help from somebody/something
examples: We managed to get it finished in the end—no thanks to him(= he didn't help).

definitions.definition: used to show that you are very grateful to somebody for something they have done
examples: Thanks a lot for all you've done.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are annoyed that somebody has done something because it causes trouble or difficulty for you
examples: ‘I'm afraid I've finished all the milk.’ ‘Well, thanks a lot!’

definitions.definition: used to say that something has happened because of somebody/something
examples: It was all a great success—thanks to a lot of hard work.

definitions.definition: to be likely to do something or to happen in a particular way because this is what often or usually happens

definitions.definition: to take a particular direction or often have a particular quality

definitions.definition: to serve customers in a store, bar, etc.

origin: senses 1 to 2 Middle English (in the sense ‘move or be inclined to move in a certain direction’): from Old Frenchtendre‘stretch, tend’, from Latintendere.senses 3 to 4 Middle English: shortening ofattend.

definitions.definition: to leave the ground and begin to fly

definitions.definition: to leave a place; to make somebody leave a place

definitions.definition: to remove an amount of money or a number of marks, points, etc. in order to reduce the total

origin: N/A

on a… dayOn a sunny day in June…

definitions.definition: the hours of the day when you are active, working, etc., not sleeping

examples: I have many happy memories from my student days.

examples: My dad's always talking about how great everything was back in the day.

examples: After forty years in politics I think it's time for me to call it a day(= to retire).

definitions.definition: to be successful against somebody/something

definitions.definition: easy to see or understand
each day repeatedly (used especially when something is boring or annoying)
examples: She hates doing the same work day after day.

definitions.definition: all the time; continuously
examples: The store is open day and night.

definitions.definition: all the time; a little at a time and gradually
examples: Day by day his condition improved.

a person or thing will not continue to live, exist or be successful for much longer
examples: His days as leader of the party are numbered.
Whatever the protests, the school’s days are numbered and it will be closed down.

definitions.definition: to spend the last part of your life in a particular state or place

definitions.definition: everyone has good luck or success at some point in their life

examples: From that day forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned

definitions.definition: to no longer be successful, powerful, etc.

examples: Our car has seen better days!

definitions.definition: (used when talking about somebody’s age) at least
examples: He must be 70 if he's a day!

definitions.definition: the whole length of the day

definitions.definition: all the time; continuously

definitions.definition: a person or thing that makes people excited for a short time but does not last very long

very obvious
used to say that a complicated task will take a long time and should not be rushed
the time when you are young and do not have much experience of life
to prevent failure or defeat, when this seems certain to happen

at an unknown time in the future

exactly
examples: It's been three years to the day since we met.

origin: Old Englishdæg, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchdagand GermanTag.

definitions.definition: a particular position, point or area

definitions.definition: having a great effect on people or things; of great value

origin: nounsenses 3 to 5 Old Englishræst,rest(noun),ræstan,restan(verb), of Germanic origin, from a root meaning ‘league’ or ‘mile’ (referring to a distance after which one rests).nounsenses 1 to 2 late Middle English: from Old Frenchreste(noun),rester(verb), from Latinrestare‘remain’, fromre-‘back’ +stare‘to stand’.

definitions.definition: used to show that something exists or happens

working; being used

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.
You'll get used to the work in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?
The ambulance got there just in time(= to save somebody's life).

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
Time has flown since the holiday began.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.
Time was when we never needed to lock our house at night.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: used with uncountable nouns, especially in negative sentences to mean ‘a large amount of something’, or after ‘how’ to ask about the amount of something. It is also used with ‘as’, ‘so’ and ‘too’.
examples: I don't have much money with me.
‘Got any money?’ ‘Not much.’
How muchwater do you need?
How much is it(= What does it cost)?
Takeas muchtimeasyou like.
There wasso muchtrafficthatwe were an hour late.
I've got fartoo much todo.
(formal)I lay awake for much of the night.
(formal)There was much discussion about the reasons for the failure.

definitions.definition: the same
examples: Please help me get this job—you know I would do as much for you.
‘Roger stole the money.’ ‘Ithought as much.’

definitions.definition: used to say that something is difficult to do
examples: No dessert for me, thanks. It was as much as I could do to finish the main course.

definitions.definition: not fair or not reasonable
examples: It's a bit much calling me at three in the morning.
The noise from next door is getting a bit much.

definitions.definition: to treat something/somebody as very important
examples: He always makes much of his humble origins.

definitions.definition: used to say that there is little difference between two things
examples: I won, but there wasn't much in it(= our scores were nearly the same).

definitions.definition: not a good…
examples: He's not much of a tennis player.

definitions.definition: used to introduce something positive or definite
examples: I'll say this much for him—he never leaves a piece of work unfinished.

definitions.definition: a situation or experience that is too difficult to deal with
examples: It all proved too much for him.

origin: Middle English: shortened frommuchel, from Old Englishmicel‘great, numerous, much’, of Germanic origin; from an Indo-European root shared by Greekmegas,megal-.

definitions.definition: used to express a doubt or choice between two possibilities
examples: He seemed undecidedwhetherto goorstay.
It remains to be seenwhether or notthis idea can be put into practice.
I asked himwhetherhe had done it all himselfor whethersomeone had helped him.
I'll see whether she's at home(= or not at home).
It's doubtful whether there'll be any seats left.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is true in either of two cases
examples: You are entitled to a free gift whether you accept our offer of insurance or not.
I'm goingwhetheryou like itor not.
Whether or notwe're successful, we can be sure that we did our best.

origin: Old Englishhwæther,hwether, of Germanic origin; related to Germanweder‘neither’.

definitions.definition: attention to or thought and care for somebody/something
examples: regard for somebody/somethingtohave scant/little/no regard for somebody/something
to pay/show little regard for other people’s property
Social services shouldpay proper regardto the needs of inner-city areas.
without regard to somebody/somethingHe was driving without regard to speed limits.

definitions.definition: respect for somebody/something
examples: Heheld her in high regard(= had a good opinion of her).
regard for somebody/somethingI had great regard for his abilities.
Children no longer have proper regard for their parents and teachers.

definitions.definition: used to send good wishes to somebody at the end of an email or letter, or when asking somebody to give your good wishes to another person who is not present
examples: Withbest/kind regards, Yours…
Give your brother my regards when you see him.

definitions.definition: to remember and think carefully about something
examples: It is always necessary to have regard to the terms of the contract.
New sites for waste disposal will be determined having regard to environmental matters.
When exercising its discretion, the court will have regard to all the circumstances.

definitions.definition: relating to somebody/something
examples: a country’s laws in regard to human rights
The company's position with regard to overtime is made clear in their contracts.
I am writing with regard to your recent order.

definitions.definition: relating to what has just been mentioned
examples: I have nothing further to say in this regard.

origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchregarder‘to watch’, fromre-‘back’ (also expressing intensive force) +garder‘to guard’.

definitions.definition: to stop sleeping
examples: Wake up! It's eight o'clock.

definitions.definition: to become more lively and interested
examples: Wake up and listen!

definitions.definition: to make somebody stop sleeping
examples: You'll wake up the whole neighbourhood with that noise!

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel more lively
examples: A cold shower will soon wake you up.
The class needs waking up.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a strong feeling of approval of somebody/something because of their good qualities or achievements
examples: I havethe utmost respectfor her and her work.
They feel a deep andmutual respectfor each other.
It was a noble effort thatdeserves respect.
She soonearned the respectof her colleagues.
You did a great job. Respect!(= used to praise somebody)

definitions.definition: polite behaviour towards or care for somebody/something that you think is important
examples: respect for somebody/somethingto show a lack of respect for authority
They have a deep respect for the natural world.
He has no respect for her feelings.
Everyone has a right to betreated with respect.
A two-minute silence was held as amark of respect.
out of respect (for somebody/something)Out of respect for my friend, I didn't say anything.

definitions.definition: a particular aspect or detail of something
examples: In this respectwe are very fortunate.
This has been a difficult yearin many respects.
There was one respect, however, in which they differed.

definitions.definition: about; relating to
examples: A writ was served on the firm in respect of their unpaid bill.

definitions.definition: in payment for something
examples: money received in respect of overtime worked

definitions.definition: to visit somebody or to send a message of good wishes as a sign of respect for them
examples: Many came topay their last respects(= by attending somebody's funeral).

definitions.definition: used when you are going to disagree, usually quite strongly, with somebody
examples: With all due respect, the figures simply do not support you on this.
With all due respect, I think you've misunderstood what he said.

definitions.definition: in connection with
examples: The two groups were similar with respect to income and status.

origin: late Middle English: from Latinrespectus, from the verbrespicere‘look back at, regard’, fromre-‘back’ +specere‘look at’.

definitions.definition: towards or in a higher position
examples: He jumped up from his chair.
Your mum said you wereup here(= upstairs).
The sun was already up(= had risen)when they set off.
They live up in the mountains.
It didn't take long to put the tent up.
You look nice with your hair up(= arranged on top of or at the back of your head).
Lay the cards face up(= facing upwards)on the table.
Up you come!(= said when lifting a child)
The car was up on blocks.
I was up on deck at first light.
Two men were up on the roof, repairing a leak.
I pinned the notice up on the wall.
She tries to stay under water but she floatsback up.

definitions.definition: to or at a higher level
examples: She turned the volume up.
Prices are still going up(= rising).
United were 3–1 up at half-time.
The wind is getting up(= blowing more strongly).
Sales arewell up onlast year.

definitions.definition: to the place where somebody/something is
examples: A car drove up and he got in.
She went straight up to the door and knocked loudly.

definitions.definition: out of bed
examples: I stayed up late(= did not go to bed until late)last night.
(British English)He'sup and aboutagain after his illness.

definitions.definition: to or at an important place, especially a large city
examples: We're going up to New York for the day.
(British English, formal)His son's up at Oxford(= Oxford University).

definitions.definition: to a place in the north of a country; further north than somewhere else
examples: They've moved up north.
We drove up to Inverness to see my father.
I'mup therealmost every other weekend
Up herein Liverpool things are different.

definitions.definition: completely
examples: We ate all the food up.
The stream has dried up.

definitions.definition: so as to be finished or closed
examples: I have some paperwork to finish up.
Do your coat up; it's cold.

definitions.definition: into pieces or parts
examples: She tore the paper up.
They've had the road up(= with the surface broken or removed)to lay some pipes.
How shall we divide up the work?

definitions.definition: so as to be formed or brought together
examples: The government agreed to set up a committee of inquiry.
She gathered up her belongings.

definitions.definition: finished; over
examples: Time's up. Stop writing and hand in your papers.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is happening, especially something unusual or unpleasant
examples: I could tellsomething was upby the looks on their faces.
What's up?(= What is the matter?)
What's up withhim? He looks furious.
Is anything up? You can tell me.

definitions.definition: about to happen, be discussed, etc.
examples: Next upin our top ten is a new release by Ariana Grande.
Up after the break we will be introducing our mystery guest!

definitions.definition: to be somebody’s duty or responsibility; to be for somebody to decide
examples: It's not up to you to tell me how to do my job.
Shall we eat out or stay in? It's up to you.

definitions.definition: to be of poor quality; to not be very good
examples: His work isn't up to much.

definitions.definition: facing problems or opposition
examples: Teachers are up against some major problems these days.
She's reallyup against it(= in a difficult situation).

definitions.definition: moving upwards and downwards
examples: The boat bobbed up and down on the water.

definitions.definition: in one direction and then in the opposite direction
examples: She was pacing up and down in front of her desk.

definitions.definition: sometimes good and sometimes bad
examples: My relationship with him was up and down.

definitions.definition: if you swearup and downthat something is true, you say that it is definitely true
working; being used
examples: By that time the new system should be up and running.

definitions.definition: appearing in front of somebody in authority for a judgement to be made about something that you have done
examples: He came up before the local magistrate for speeding.

definitions.definition: on offer for something
examples: The house is up for sale.

definitions.definition: being considered for something, especially as a candidate
examples: Two candidates are up for election.

definitions.definition: willing to take part in a particular activity
examples: We're going clubbing tonight. Are youup for it?

definitions.definition: among or almost the best, worst, most important, etc.
examples: It may not have been the worst week of my life but it's up there.
OK, it's not my absolute dream, but it's up there.
These people can’t live without the internet—it’s up there with air and water.

definitions.definition: as far as a particular number, level, etc.
examples: I can take up to four people(= but no more than four)in my car.
The temperature went up to 35°C.

definitions.definition: not further or later than something; until something
examples: Read up to page 100.
Up to now he's been very quiet.

definitions.definition: as high or as good as something
examples: Her latest book isn't up to her usual standard.

definitions.definition: physically or mentally capable of something
examples: He's not up to the job.
I don't feel up to going to work today.

definitions.definition: doing something, especially something bad
examples: What's she up to?
What've you been up to?
I'm sure he'sup to no good(= doing something bad).

origin: Old Englishup(p),uppe, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchopand Germanauf.

definitions.definition: to train the body by physical exercise
examples: I work out regularly to keep fit.

definitions.definition: to develop in a successful way
examples: My first job didn't work out.
Things have worked out quite well for us.

definitions.definition: if somethingworks outat something, you calculate that it will be a particular amount
examples: The restaurant bill worked out at £23 each.
This all works out to around $11 000.
+ adj.It'll work out cheaper to travel by bus.

definitions.definition: to understand somebody’s character
examples: I've never been able to work her out.

definitions.definition: to calculate something
examples: to work out the answer

definitions.definition: to find the answer to something
examples: to work out a problem
work out what, where, etc…Can you work out what these squiggles mean?
I couldn't work out where the music was coming from.

definitions.definition: to plan or think of something
examples: I've worked out a new way of doing it.

definitions.definition: to remove all the coal, minerals, etc. from a mine over a period of time
examples: a worked-out silver mine

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: thinking about unpleasant things that have happened or that might happen and therefore feeling unhappy and afraid
examples: Don't lookso worried!
worried about somebody/somethingI'm not worried about her—she can take care of herself.
Doctors are worried about the possible spread of the disease.
worried about doing somethingPeople arereally worriedabout losing their jobs.
worried for somebody/somethingHe is extremely worried for her safety.
I was worried for my family.
worried by somethingWe're not too worried by these results.
worried (that)…The police are worried that the man may be armed.
I was worried you wouldn't come.
Where have you been? I've beenworried sick(= extremely worried).
Try not toget worried.
His worried parents were waiting for him when he got home.
She gave me a worried look.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that you wereworriedbecause you had not understood what they had said correctly
examples: You had me worried for a moment—I thought you were going to resign!

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: used in questions to ask for particular information about somebody/something
examples: What is your name?
What(= what job)does he do?
What time is it?
What kind of music do you like?

definitions.definition: the thing or things that; whatever
examples: What you need is a good meal.
Nobody knows what will happen next.
I spent what little time I had with my family.

definitions.definition: used to say that you think that something is especially good, bad, etc.
examples: What awful weather!
What a beautiful house!

definitions.definition: and other things of the same type
examples: It's full of old toys, books and what not.

definitions.definition: despite any problems or difficulties you may have
examples: He promised to support her come what may.

definitions.definition: to be punished/punish somebody severely
examples: I'll give her what for if she does that again.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize your opinion
examples: Is he stupid or what?

definitions.definition: used when you are not sure about something
examples: I don't know if he's a teacher or what.
Are we going now or what?

definitions.definition: used to express surprise at what somebody has just said
examples: ‘He's getting married.’ ‘Say what?’

definitions.definition: used to show that you think something is not important, especially after somebody has criticized you for it
examples: ‘He's fifteen years younger than you!’ ‘So what?’
So what if nobody else agrees with me?

definitions.definition: used when you have not heard or have not understood something
examples: What? I can't hear you.

definitions.definition: used to show that you have heard somebody and to ask what they want
examples: ‘Mummy!’ ‘What?’ ‘I'm thirsty.’

definitions.definition: used to express surprise or anger
examples: ‘It will cost $500.’ ‘What?’
‘I asked her to marry me.’ ‘You what?’

definitions.definition: used to make a suggestion
examples: What about a trip to France?

definitions.definition: used to introduce somebody/something into the conversation
examples: What about you, Joe? Do you like football?

definitions.definition: used instead of a name that you cannot remember
examples: She's just gone out with old what-d'you-call-him.

definitions.definition: for what purpose or reason?
examples: What is this tool for?
What did you do that for(= why did you do that)?
‘I need to see a doctor.’ ‘What for?’

definitions.definition: what would happen if?
examples: What if the train is late?
What if she forgets to bring it?

definitions.definition: used when admitting that something is true, to ask why it should be considered important
examples: Yes, I wrote the article. What of it?

definitions.definition: used to suggest that something you have heard is a stupid idea or does not make sense
examples: They dropped their best player. What's all that about?

definitions.definition: (know) what things are useful, important, etc.
examples: She certainly knows what's what.

definitions.definition: used to ask why somebody is behaving in a strange way
examples: What's with you? You haven't said a word all morning.

definitions.definition: used to ask the reason for something
examples: What's with all this walking? Can't we take a cab?

definitions.definition: used to list the various reasons for something
examples: What with the cold weather and my bad leg, I haven't been out for weeks.

origin: Old Englishhwæt, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwatand Germanwas, from an Indo-European root shared by Latinquod.

definitions.definition: to try to persuade somebody to agree to something or to do something
examples: He hasn't said he'll do it yet, but I'm working on him.

definitions.definition: to try hard to improve or achieve something
examples: You need to work on your pronunciation a bit more.
‘Have you sorted out a babysitter yet?’ ‘No, butI'm working on it.’
We are working on plans for a new swimming pool.

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to train the body by physical exercise
examples: I work out regularly to keep fit.

definitions.definition: to develop in a successful way
examples: My first job didn't work out.
Things have worked out quite well for us.

definitions.definition: if somethingworks outat something, you calculate that it will be a particular amount
examples: The restaurant bill worked out at £23 each.
This all works out to around $11 000.
+ adj.It'll work out cheaper to travel by bus.

definitions.definition: to understand somebody’s character
examples: I've never been able to work her out.

definitions.definition: to calculate something
examples: to work out the answer

definitions.definition: to find the answer to something
examples: to work out a problem
work out what, where, etc…Can you work out what these squiggles mean?
I couldn't work out where the music was coming from.

definitions.definition: to plan or think of something
examples: I've worked out a new way of doing it.

definitions.definition: to remove all the coal, minerals, etc. from a mine over a period of time
examples: a worked-out silver mine

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: to do something that involves physical or mental effort, especially as part of a job
examples: I can't work if I'm cold.
The kids alwayswork hardat school.
work on somethingThe whole team is currentlyworking on the project.
He is working on a new novel.
She's outside, working on the car.
work at somethingI've been working at my assignment all day.
work with somebodyWork with a partner to solve the problem.
Wework closelywith clients to develop specific solutions.
+ nounDoctors oftenworkvery longhours.
He works shifts at a metal plant.

definitions.definition: to have a job
examples: Both my parents work.
to work in an office/a factory
work for somebody/somethingSheworks foran engineeringcompany.
My father wants me to work for him when I leave school.
work in somethingI've always worked in education.
He worked in the oil industry for twenty years.
work with somebody/somethingDo you enjoy working with children?
work as somethingMy son is working as a teacher.

definitions.definition: to make efforts to achieve something
examples: work for somethingShe dedicated her life to working for peace.
work to do somethingThe committee is working to get the prisoners freed.
work togetherWe have proved that different groups can work harmoniously together.
work together to do somethingThe police and the public need to work together to combat crime.

definitions.definition: to make yourself/somebody work, especially very hard
examples: She works herself too hard.
He says they're working him hard.

definitions.definition: to function; to operate
examples: The printer isn't working.
How does the deviceactually work?
Are they any closer to understanding how the brain works?
work by somethingIt works by electricity.
work by doing somethingThe filtration process works by physically removing the contaminants from the water.

definitions.definition: to make a machine, device, etc. operate
examples: Do you know how to work the coffee machine?
The machine is worked by wind power.

definitions.definition: to have the result or effect that you want
examples: The pills the doctor gave me aren't working.
My plan worked, and I got them to agree.
The system seemed towork well.
Everything worked very smoothly.
work on somebody/somethingHis charm doesn't work on me(= does not affect or impress me).

definitions.definition: to have a particular effect
examples: work against somebodyYour age can work against you in this job.
work in somebody’s favourSpeaking Italian should work in his favour.

definitions.definition: to manage or operate something to gain benefit from it
examples: towork the land(= grow crops on it, etc.)
Boys who didn't go to school worked the land with their fathers.
He works a large area(= selling a company's goods, etc.).
You have to learn how towork the systemif you want to succeed.
(figurative)She was a skilful speaker who knew how towork a crowd(= to excite them or make them feel something strongly).

definitions.definition: to make a material into a particular shape or form by pressing, stretching, hitting it, etc.
examples: work somethingto work clay
to work gold
work something into somethingto work the mixture into a paste

definitions.definition: to use a particular material to produce a picture or other item
examples: an artist working in oils
a craftsman working with wool

definitions.definition: to move violently
examples: He stared at me in horror, his mouth working.

definitions.definition: to move or pass to a particular place or state, usually gradually
examples: + adv./prep.It will take a while for the drug to work out of your system.

definitions.definition: to arrange something in a particular way, especially by being clever
examples: Can you work it so that we get free tickets?

origin: Old Englishweorc(noun),wyrcan(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwerkand GermanWerk, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekergon.

definitions.definition: to a place in the north of a country; further north than somewhere else
examples: They've moved up north.
We drove up to Inverness to see my father.
I'mup therealmost every other weekend
Up herein Liverpool things are different.

definitions.definition: completely
examples: We ate all the food up.
The stream has dried up.

definitions.definition: so as to be finished or closed
examples: I have some paperwork to finish up.
Do your coat up; it's cold.

definitions.definition: into pieces or parts
examples: She tore the paper up.
They've had the road up(= with the surface broken or removed)to lay some pipes.
How shall we divide up the work?

definitions.definition: so as to be formed or brought together
examples: The government agreed to set up a committee of inquiry.
She gathered up her belongings.

definitions.definition: finished; over
examples: Time's up. Stop writing and hand in your papers.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is happening, especially something unusual or unpleasant
examples: I could tellsomething was upby the looks on their faces.
What's up?(= What is the matter?)
What's up withhim? He looks furious.
Is anything up? You can tell me.

definitions.definition: about to happen, be discussed, etc.
examples: Next upin our top ten is a new release by Ariana Grande.
Up after the break we will be introducing our mystery guest!

definitions.definition: to be somebody’s duty or responsibility; to be for somebody to decide
examples: It's not up to you to tell me how to do my job.
Shall we eat out or stay in? It's up to you.

definitions.definition: to be of poor quality; to not be very good
examples: His work isn't up to much.

definitions.definition: facing problems or opposition
examples: Teachers are up against some major problems these days.
She's reallyup against it(= in a difficult situation).

definitions.definition: moving upwards and downwards
examples: The boat bobbed up and down on the water.

definitions.definition: in one direction and then in the opposite direction
examples: She was pacing up and down in front of her desk.

definitions.definition: sometimes good and sometimes bad
examples: My relationship with him was up and down.

definitions.definition: if you swearup and downthat something is true, you say that it is definitely true
working; being used
examples: By that time the new system should be up and running.

definitions.definition: appearing in front of somebody in authority for a judgement to be made about something that you have done
examples: He came up before the local magistrate for speeding.

definitions.definition: on offer for something
examples: The house is up for sale.

definitions.definition: being considered for something, especially as a candidate
examples: Two candidates are up for election.

definitions.definition: willing to take part in a particular activity
examples: We're going clubbing tonight. Are youup for it?

definitions.definition: among or almost the best, worst, most important, etc.
examples: It may not have been the worst week of my life but it's up there.
OK, it's not my absolute dream, but it's up there.
These people can’t live without the internet—it’s up there with air and water.

definitions.definition: as far as a particular number, level, etc.
examples: I can take up to four people(= but no more than four)in my car.
The temperature went up to 35°C.

definitions.definition: not further or later than something; until something
examples: Read up to page 100.
Up to now he's been very quiet.

definitions.definition: as high or as good as something
examples: Her latest book isn't up to her usual standard.

definitions.definition: physically or mentally capable of something
examples: He's not up to the job.
I don't feel up to going to work today.

definitions.definition: doing something, especially something bad
examples: What's she up to?
What've you been up to?
I'm sure he'sup to no good(= doing something bad).

origin: Old Englishup(p),uppe, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchopand Germanauf.

definitions.definition: towards or in a higher position
examples: He jumped up from his chair.
Your mum said you wereup here(= upstairs).
The sun was already up(= had risen)when they set off.
They live up in the mountains.
It didn't take long to put the tent up.
You look nice with your hair up(= arranged on top of or at the back of your head).
Lay the cards face up(= facing upwards)on the table.
Up you come!(= said when lifting a child)
The car was up on blocks.
I was up on deck at first light.
Two men were up on the roof, repairing a leak.
I pinned the notice up on the wall.
She tries to stay under water but she floatsback up.

definitions.definition: to or at a higher level
examples: She turned the volume up.
Prices are still going up(= rising).
United were 3–1 up at half-time.
The wind is getting up(= blowing more strongly).
Sales arewell up onlast year.

definitions.definition: to the place where somebody/something is
examples: A car drove up and he got in.
She went straight up to the door and knocked loudly.

definitions.definition: out of bed
examples: I stayed up late(= did not go to bed until late)last night.
(British English)He'sup and aboutagain after his illness.

definitions.definition: to or at an important place, especially a large city
examples: We're going up to New York for the day.
(British English, formal)His son's up at Oxford(= Oxford University).

We drove up to Inverness to see my father.

OK, it's not my absolute dream, but it's up there.

examples: I can take up to four people(= but no more than four)in my car.
The temperature went up to 35°C.

definitions.definition: not further or later than something; until something
examples: Read up to page 100.
Up to now he's been very quiet.

definitions.definition: as high or as good as something
examples: Her latest book isn't up to her usual standard.

definitions.definition: physically or mentally capable of something
examples: He's not up to the job.
I don't feel up to going to work today.

definitions.definition: doing something, especially something bad
examples: What's she up to?
What've you been up to?
I'm sure he'sup to no good(= doing something bad).

origin: Old Englishup(p),uppe, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchopand Germanauf.

definitions.definition: to stay where you are or delay doing something until somebody/something comes or something happens
examples: She rang the bell and waited.
The President agreed to speak to the waiting journalists.
Theywaited patientlywhile I got ready.
Iwaited and waited, but the bus didn't come.
wait + adv./prep.Have you beenwaiting long?
towait (for) hours/days/weeks/months/years
I’ve been waiting (for) twenty minutes.
I'llwait outsideuntil the meeting's over.
(especially North American English)I had towait in lineat the bank.
wait for somebody/somethingWait for me!
towait for a bus
I'm stillwaiting for the resultsof my blood test.
He smiled,waiting for an answerto his question.
wait for somebody/something to do somethingWe're waiting for the rain to stop before we go out.
wait to do somethingHurry up! We're waiting to go.
We'll have to wait until it stops raining.
wait your turnYou'll just have to wait your turn(= wait until your turn comes).

definitions.definition: to hope or watch for something to happen, especially for a long time
examples: wait for somethingLeeds United had waited for success for eighteen years.
This is just the opportunity I've been waiting for.
They are waiting for the right moment to make their move.
wait for somebody/something to do somethingHe's waiting for me to make a mistake.
wait to do somethingI've beenwaiting a long timeto say that to her.
wait your chanceI waited my chance and slipped out when no one was looking.
We simply cannot afford to wait any longer.
I waited with bated breath for what would happen next.

definitions.definition: to be ready for somebody to have or use
examples: Your car is waiting, sir.
When we arrived on the platform, there was a train waiting.
wait for somebodyThere's a letter waiting for you at home.
There was a nice surprise waiting for me when I got back.
wait to do somethingThe hotel had a taxi waiting to collect us.

definitions.definition: to be left to be dealt with at a later time because immediate action is not needed
examples: I've got some calls to make but theycan waituntil tomorrow.
I'm afraid thiscan't wait. It's very important.
We've just spent a fortune on a new roof, so the new car is going tohave to wait.

definitions.definition: a thing or person that is very likely to cause danger or a problem in the future because of the condition it is in or the way they behave
examples: Any one of these nuclear plants may be a disaster waiting to happen.

definitions.definition: ready to take over a particular job or be used in a particular situation when needed
examples: She was aware of a whole host of ambitious young managers waiting in the wings.
There's a wealth of talent waiting in the wings of British theatre.

definitions.definition: used when you are emphasizing that somebody is very excited about something or keen to do it
examples: The childrencan't wait forChristmas to come.
I can hardly wait to see him again.

definitions.definition: to make somebody have to wait or be delayed, especially because you arrive late
examples: I'm sorry to have kept you waiting.

definitions.definition: to wait for a situation to become clear or certain
examples: He waited for the dust to settle after the election before making any new decisions.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody that they must be patient and wait to find out about something later
examples: We'll just have to wait and see—there's nothing we can do at the moment.
a wait-and-see policy
‘Where are we going?’ ‘Wait and see!’

definitions.definition: to serve food to people, for example at a formal meal
used to say that you are about to tell somebody something that is surprising
examples: They're off on a trip, to—wait for it—the Maldives!

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody not to start doing something yet, but to wait until you tell them
to wait for a short time
examples: Can you wait a second while I make a call?
Hey! Wait a minute! I'll come with you!

definitions.definition: used when you have just noticed or remembered something, or had a sudden idea
examples: Wait a minute—this isn't the right key.

definitions.definition: to take care of somebody’s needs so well that they do not have to do anything for themselves
examples: He seems to expect me to wait on him hand and foot.

definitions.definition: to work serving food to people in a restaurant
used to show that you are very excited about telling or showing something to somebody
examples: Wait till you see what I've found!

definitions.definition: used to threaten to punish somebody or do something bad to them
examples: Wait till I get my hands on him!

definitions.definition: used to suggest that you should all start doing what you have been discussing
used to tell somebody to do something now rather than later
examples: If the car needs cleaning, what are you waiting for?

definitions.definition: used to emphasize a threat, warning or promise
examples: I'll be famous one day, just you wait!

origin: Middle English: from Old Northern Frenchwaitier, of Germanic origin; related to the verbwake. Early senses included ‘lie in wait (for)’, ‘observe carefully’, and ‘be watchful’.

definitions.definition: used in polite offers or invitations
examples: Would you likea sandwich?
Would you have dinner with me on Friday?

definitions.definition: used to say what you like, love, hate, etc.
examples: would like, love, hate, prefer, etc. somethingI'd love a coffee.
would like, etc. to do somethingI'd be only too glad to help.
would like, etc. somebody to do somethingI'd hate you to think I was criticizing you.
would rather do somethingI'd rather come with you.
would rather somebody did somethingI'd rather you came with us.

definitions.definition: used as the past form ofwillwhen reporting what somebody has said or thought
examples: He said he would be here at eight o'clock(= His words were: ‘I will be there at eight o'clock.’).
She asked if I would help.
They told me that they probably wouldn't come.

definitions.definition: used for talking about the result of an event that you imagine
examples: She'd look better with shorter hair.
If you went to see him, he would be delighted.
Hurry up! It would be a shame to miss the beginning of the play.
She'd be a fool to accept it(= if she accepted).

definitions.definition: used for describing a possible action or event that did not in fact happen, because something else did not happen first
examples: If I had seen the advertisement in time, I would have applied for the job.
They would never have met if she hadn't gone to Emma's party.

definitions.definition: used for saying why somebody does something
examples: so that somebody/something would do somethingShe burned the letters so that her husband would never read them.
in order that somebody/something would do somethingHe worked hard in order that he would pass all his exams.

definitions.definition: used for saying what you want to happen
examples: I wish that you'd be quiet for a minute.

definitions.definition: used to show that somebody/something was not willing or refused to do something
examples: She wouldn't change it, even though she knew it was wrong.
My car wouldn't start this morning.

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody politely to do something
examples: Would you mindleaving us alone for a few minutes?
Would you open the door for me, please?

definitions.definition: used to give opinions that you are not certain about
examples: I would imagine the job will take about two days.
I'd say he was about fifty.

definitions.definition: used to give advice
examples: I wouldn'thave any more to drink,if I were you.

definitions.definition: used for talking about things that often happened in the past
examples: When my parents were away, my grandmother would take care of me.
He'd always be the first to offer to help.

definitions.definition: used for talking about behaviour that you think is typical
examples: ‘She said it was your fault.’ ‘Well,she would say that,wouldn't she?She's never liked me.’

definitions.definition: used to express a strong wish
examples: Would that he had lived to see it.

origin: Old Englishwolde, past ofwyllan, of Germanic origin.

definitions.definition: to do something that involves physical or mental effort, especially as part of a job
examples: I can't work if I'm cold.
The kids alwayswork hardat school.
work on somethingThe whole team is currentlyworking on the project.
He is working on a new novel.
She's outside, working on the car.
work at somethingI've been working at my assignment all day.
work with somebodyWork with a partner to solve the problem.
Wework closelywith clients to develop specific solutions.
+ nounDoctors oftenworkvery longhours.
He works shifts at a metal plant.

definitions.definition: to have a job
examples: Both my parents work.
to work in an office/a factory
work for somebody/somethingSheworks foran engineeringcompany.
My father wants me to work for him when I leave school.
work in somethingI've always worked in education.
He worked in the oil industry for twenty years.
work with somebody/somethingDo you enjoy working with children?
work as somethingMy son is working as a teacher.

definitions.definition: to make efforts to achieve something
examples: work for somethingShe dedicated her life to working for peace.
work to do somethingThe committee is working to get the prisoners freed.
work togetherWe have proved that different groups can work harmoniously together.
work together to do somethingThe police and the public need to work together to combat crime.

definitions.definition: to make yourself/somebody work, especially very hard
examples: She works herself too hard.
He says they're working him hard.

definitions.definition: to function; to operate
examples: The printer isn't working.
How does the deviceactually work?
Are they any closer to understanding how the brain works?
work by somethingIt works by electricity.
work by doing somethingThe filtration process works by physically removing the contaminants from the water.

definitions.definition: to make a machine, device, etc. operate
examples: Do you know how to work the coffee machine?
The machine is worked by wind power.

definitions.definition: to have the result or effect that you want
examples: The pills the doctor gave me aren't working.
My plan worked, and I got them to agree.
The system seemed towork well.
Everything worked very smoothly.
work on somebody/somethingHis charm doesn't work on me(= does not affect or impress me).

definitions.definition: to have a particular effect
examples: work against somebodyYour age can work against you in this job.
work in somebody’s favourSpeaking Italian should work in his favour.

definitions.definition: to manage or operate something to gain benefit from it
examples: towork the land(= grow crops on it, etc.)
Boys who didn't go to school worked the land with their fathers.
He works a large area(= selling a company's goods, etc.).
You have to learn how towork the systemif you want to succeed.
(figurative)She was a skilful speaker who knew how towork a crowd(= to excite them or make them feel something strongly).

definitions.definition: to make a material into a particular shape or form by pressing, stretching, hitting it, etc.
examples: work somethingto work clay
to work gold
work something into somethingto work the mixture into a paste

definitions.definition: to use a particular material to produce a picture or other item
examples: an artist working in oils
a craftsman working with wool

definitions.definition: to move violently
examples: He stared at me in horror, his mouth working.

definitions.definition: to move or pass to a particular place or state, usually gradually
examples: + adv./prep.It will take a while for the drug to work out of your system.

definitions.definition: to arrange something in a particular way, especially by being clever
examples: Can you work it so that we get free tickets?

origin: Old Englishweorc(noun),wyrcan(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwerkand GermanWerk, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekergon.

definitions.definition: to move to or towards a person or place
examples: + adv./prep.He left and said he was never coming back.
He came into the room and shut the door.
She comes to work by bus.
My son iscoming homesoon.
Come here!
Come and see us soon!
Here comesJo!(= Jo is coming)
There's a storm coming.
come to do somethingThey're coming to stay for a week.

definitions.definition: to arrive at or reach a place
examples: come to…They continued until they came to a river.
She came to work wearing a very smart suit.
They came as far as the gate.
People came from all over the country.
Your breakfast is coming soon.
Have any letters come for me?
Help came at last.

definitions.definition: to arrive somewhere in order to do something or get something
examples: come for somethingI've come for my book.
come about somethingI've come about my book.
come to do somethingI've come to get my book.
come doing somethingHe came looking for me.

definitions.definition: to move or travel, especially with somebody else, to a particular place or in order to be present at an event
examples: I've only come for an hour.
Thanks for coming(= to my house, party, etc.).
come to do somethingTen thousand people came to hear him speak.
come to somethingAre you coming to the lecture?
come to something with somebodyAre you coming to the club with us tonight?
come with somebodyCome with me—I've got something to show you.
come doing somethingWhy don't you come skating tonight?

definitions.definition: to travel a particular distance
examples: We've come 50 miles this morning.
(figurative)The company hascome a long way(= made lot of progress)in the last 5 years.

definitions.definition: to move in a particular way or while doing something else
examples: come doing something + adv./prep.A car came flying round the corner.
A huge wave came crashing over the sea wall.
(figurative)Questions came rushing into her head.
come doing somethingDon't expect me tocome runningevery time you call.

definitions.definition: to happen
examples: The agreement came after several hours of negotiations.
The rains came too late to do any good.
Spring came late this year.
The time has come(= now is the moment)to act.
His first goal came in the 81st minute.
come as somethingHis resignationcame as no surprise.
Her deathcame as aterribleshockto us.
More details of the event arecoming soon.

definitions.definition: used in questions to talk about how or why something happened
examples: How did he come to break his leg?
How do you come to be so late?

definitions.definition: to have a particular position
examples: That comes a long way down my list of priorities.
Shecame second(= received the second highest score)in the exam.
His familycomes first(= is the most important thing in his life).

definitions.definition: used in many expressions to show that something has reached a particular state
examples: At last wintercame to an end.
Hecame to powerin 2019.
We need tocome to an agreementon this issue.
When will theycome to a decision?
our understanding of how the universecame into existence
The trees are coming into leaf.

definitions.definition: to be available or to exist in a particular way
examples: come in somethingThis dress comes in black and red.
come with somethingThe new edition of the game comes with a whole range of upgrades.
+ adj.(informal)New cars don'tcome cheap(= they are expensive).
This luxury villacomes complete withits own private swimming pool.

definitions.definition: to become
examples: + adj.The handle came loose.
The buttons had come undone.
Everything willcome rightin the end.
come to do somethingthe Nigerian film industry, which came to be known as Nollywood

definitions.definition: to reach a point where you realize, understand or believe something
examples: In time she came to love him.
She had come to see the problem in a new light.
I've come to expect this kind of behaviour from him.

definitions.definition: to have anorgasm
to be very clever, stupid, etc.
to play the part of a particular type of person; to behave in a particular way
examples: Don't come the innocent with me.

definitions.definition: used to ask somebody to repeat something
examples: ‘She's an entomologist.’ ‘Come again?’ ‘An entomologist—she studies insects.’

definitions.definition: to arrive and leave; to move freely
examples: They had a party next door—we heard peoplecoming and goingall night.

definitions.definition: to be present for a short time and then go away
examples: The pain in my leg comes and goes.

definitions.definition: to be easy, natural, etc. for somebody to do
examples: Acting comes naturally to her.

definitions.definition: to be unsuccessful; to have no successful result
examples: How sad that all his hard work should come to nothing.
Her plans didn't come to anything.

definitions.definition: used to introduce something extra that is connected with what has just been said
examples: I don't really trust him—or his wife, come to that.

definitions.definition: despite any problems or difficulties you may have
examples: He promised to support her come what may.

definitions.definition: used to say you do not understand how something can happen and would like an explanation
examples: ‘I think you owe me some money.’ ‘How come?’
If she spent five years in Paris, how come her French is so bad?

definitions.definition: to not be important or successful
in the future
examples: They may well regret the decisionin years to come.
This will be a problemfor some time to come(= for a period of time in the future).

definitions.definition: when it is a question of something
examples: When it comes to getting things done, he's useless.

definitions.definition: somebody’s ideas, beliefs, personality, etc. that makes them say what they have said
examples: I see where you're coming from(= I understand what you mean).

origin: Old Englishcuman, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchkomenand Germankommen.

definitions.definition: accepted or wanted somewhere

a welcome guest/visitor

definitions.definition: that you are pleased to have, receive, etc.

a welcome sight

definitions.definition: used as a polite reply when somebody thanks you for something

origin: Old Englishwilcuma‘a person whose coming is pleasing’,wilcumian(verb), fromwil-‘desire, pleasure’ +cuman‘come’. The first element was later changed towel-‘well’, influenced by Old Frenchbien venuor Old Norsevelkominn.

definitions.definition: used to mean ‘fairly’ or ‘to some degree’, often when you are disappointed, surprised or expressing slight criticism
examples: rather odd/strange/unusual
arather largesum of money
Arather smallnumber of people turned up.

I thought it was arather goodidea.

It was arather difficultquestion.
It wasrather adifficult question.

He was conscious that he was talkingrather toomuch.

definitions.definition: used with a verb to make a statement sound less strong
examples: I've alwaysrather likedCharlie.

definitions.definition: used to correct something you have said, or to give more accurate information

definitions.definition: used to introduce an idea that is different or opposite to the idea that you have stated previously

definitions.definition: instead of somebody/something
examples: I think I'll have a cold drink rather than coffee.

origin: Old Englishhrathor‘earlier, sooner’, comparative ofhræthe‘without delay’, fromhræth‘prompt’, of Germanic origin.

definitions.definition: to write something on paper, especially in order to remember or record it
examples: Write down the address before you forget it.
I'd better write this down, otherwise I'll forget it.

definitions.definition: to reduce the value ofassetswhen stating it in a company’s accounts

the written-down value of the stock

origin: N/A

definitions.definition: a person or thing that is not included in a general statement
examples: Most of the buildings in the town are modern, but the church is an exception.

Nobody had much money at the time and I wasno exception.

definitions.definition: a thing that does not follow a rule
examples: Good writing is unfortunatelythe exception rather than the rule(= it is unusual).

definitions.definition: people say that something isthe exception that proves the rulewhen they are stating something that seems to be different from the normal situation, but they mean that the normal situation remains true in general
examples: Most electronics companies have not done well this year, but ours is the exception that proves the rule.

definitions.definition: to allow somebody not to follow the usual rule on one occasion or for one particular reason
examples: Children are not usually allowed in, but I'm prepared to make an exception in this case.

definitions.definition: except; not including
examples: All his novels are set in Italy with the exception of his last.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that the statement you are making is always true and everyone or everything is included
examples: All students without exception must take the English examination.

origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinexceptio(n-), fromexcipere‘take out’, fromex-‘out of’ +capere‘take’.

next/last year

in the year…She was born in the year 1865.
in… yearsThe crowd was much smaller than inprevious years.

for yearsThey haven't seen each other foryears and years.

a very long time

definitions.definition: every year for many years

examples: Year by year their affection for each other grew stronger.

any particular year after the birth of Christ

a year-on-year increase in spending

origin: Old Englishgē(a)r, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchjaarand GermanJahr, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekhōra‘season’.

definitions.definition: the ability to breathe, grow, produce young, etc. that people, animals and plants have before they die and that objects do not have
examples: This could mean the difference betweenlife and death.

definitions.definition: the state of being alive as a human; an individual person’s existence

alife class(= one in which art students draw a naked man or woman)

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)

definitions.definition: be the most important person or thing to somebody

definitions.definition: to improve something by introducing new ideas and making people more interested in it

definitions.definition: an essential part of a person’s existence

definitions.definition: to make somebody/something more interesting or exciting
examples: The new teacher really brought French to life for us.

definitions.definition: to become more interesting, exciting or full of activity

definitions.definition: to start to act or move as if alive

definitions.definition: to die. People say‘depart this life’to avoid saying‘die’.

examples: He led his poor partner a dog's life.

definitions.definition: to spend the last part of your life in a particular state or place
examples: He ended his days in poverty.

definitions.definition: a situation that cannot be changed, especially one that is unpleasant

examples: A young cyclist is fighting for his life after the accident.

definitions.definition: as hard or as fast as possible
examples: She was holding on to the rope for dear life.
