examples: I cannot for the life of me imagine why they want to leave.

definitions.definition: to frighten somebody very much
examples: You scared the life out of me coming in so suddenly like that.

definitions.definition: having a lot of energy
examples: It's nice to see her so full of life after her illness.

definitions.definition: used to tell somebody to stop being boring and to do something more interesting
examples: Do us all a favour… get a life!

definitions.definition: to hold somebody/something very tightly because you are afraid
to be very lucky in dangerous situations
to enjoy yourself very much
feeling frightened that you might be killed
a method of helping somebody who has stopped breathing to breathe again by placing your mouth on theirs and forcing air into their lungs
used to show surprise at seeing somebody/something
examples: I hadn’t seen her for fifteen years and then there she was, (as) large as life.

definitions.definition: looking or behaving in a way that is more interesting or exciting than other people, and so is likely to attract attention
examples: He's a larger than life character.

definitions.definition: to die in order to save somebody/something
examples: They were prepared to lay down their lives for their country.

definitions.definition: to live a happy and comfortable life with no problems or responsibilities
examples: The two crooks had been living the life of Riley.
He planned to retire early and then lead the life of Riley.

definitions.definition: the chance to live or last longer, or with a better quality of life
examples: Since her hip operation she's had a new lease of life.

definitions.definition: the possibility or belief that people continue to exist in some form after they die
examples: Do you believe in life after death?

definitions.definition: the liveliest and funniest person at a party, etc.
used to say that there is a situation in which it is not thought to be important if people somewhere die or are treated badly
seeming to move or function by itself without a person touching or working it
used to say that it is not worth wasting time doing something that you dislike or that is not important
examples: Life's too short to sit around moping.

definitions.definition: the person somebody loves more than any other
to cause problems for somebody
examples: She does everything she can to make life difficult for him.

definitions.definition: to behave in a way that makes somebody else feel very unhappy
examples: My old boss used to make my life a misery.
His constant criticism made her life a misery.

definitions.definition: the man or woman that you are having a sexual or romantic relationship with
examples: Anna has a new man in her life.

definitions.definition: used to describe a situation that is very important or serious
examples: You mustn't let anyone know— it's a matter of life and death.
These talks are a matter of life or death for the factory.
Delivering on time is a matter of life and death for a small company.

definitions.definition: to be completely unable to do something
examples: He can't interview people to save his life.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will definitely not do something
to risk being killed or injured in order to do something
examples: She risked life and limb to save her children from the fire.

definitions.definition: a film, play or book that gives a very realistic view of ordinary life
examples: This drama provides a slice of life in 1950s Connecticut.

definitions.definition: to suddenly start working or doing something
examples: ‘Let's go!’ he said, springing into action.
The town springs to life(= becomes busy)during the carnival.

definitions.definition: a basic food, especially bread
to kill somebody
to kill yourself
examples: She took her own life.

definitions.definition: to risk being killed
examples: You take your life in your hands just crossing the road here.

definitions.definition: used when you are disappointed about something but know that you must accept it
examples: It’s a shame I can’t go on the trip, but that’s life.

definitions.definition: when you saythat’s the story of my lifeabout a bad experience you have had, you mean you have had many similar experiences
examples: Another missed opportunity—that’s the story of my life!
Out of work with no money—that's the story of my life.

definitions.definition: seeming real rather than invented
examples: I don’t think the characters are very true to life.

definitions.definition: having a range of different experiences makes life more interesting
a person’s job or position in society
examples: She has friends from all walks of life.

definitions.definition: the typical pattern of behaviour of a person or group
examples: the American way of life
She loved the Spanish way of life and immediately felt at home there.
the beliefs and practices of the Hindu way of life

definitions.definition: in a bad situation you must not give up hope because there is always a chance that it will improve
used to say that you are certain that something will happen
examples: You can bet your life that he'll be late.

origin: Old Englishlīf, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchlijf, GermanLeib‘body’, also tolive1.

definitions.definition: the period from 1 January to 31 December, that is 365 or 366 days, divided into 12 months
examples: Elections take place every year.
I lost my job earlierthis year.
next/last year
She diedthe following year.
Three million tourists visit Yellowstoneevery year.
in the year…She was born in the year 1865.
in… yearsThe crowd was much smaller than inprevious years.
Inthe past few years, she has become one of our top-selling authors.
The museum is openall year round(= during the whole year).

definitions.definition: a period of twelve months, measured from any particular time
examples: It's exactly a year since I started working here.
She gave up teaching threeyears ago.
They got married twoyears later.
The autobiography could only be published 100years afterhis death.
I hope to retireintwoyears' time.
Where do you think you will be fiveyears from now?
Seeds can live in the soilfor many years.
in the… year (of something)in the first year of their marriage
in… yearsProduction has declined inrecent years.
Hisearly yearswere spent in San Francisco.
We have high hopes for thecoming year(= the next twelve months).
the pre-war/war/post-war years(= the period before/during/after the war)
I have happy memories of my years in Poland(= the time I spent there).

definitions.definition: age; time of life
examples: He was 14years oldwhen it happened.
She looks young for her years.
They were both only 20years of age.
a twenty-year-old man
He died in his sixtieth year.
She's getting on in years(= is no longer young).

definitions.definition: a period of twelve months connected with a particular activity
examples: thetax/fiscal year
theschool year

definitions.definition: a level that you stay in for one year; a student at a particular level
examples: in year…We started German in year seven.
in somebody's yearShe was in my year at school.
The project is the work of a group of year-seven pupils.
The first years do French.

definitions.definition: a long time
examples: It's years since we last met.
for yearsThey haven't seen each other foryears and years.
in yearsThat's the best movie I've seen in years.
It's the first time we've met in years.
over the yearsWe've had a lot of fun over the years.

definitions.definition: at some time in the future
examples: There are certain to be more job losses further down the road.
Will this movie still be funny a few years down the road?

definitions.definition: the last years of somebody’s life
a very long time
examples: We've known each other for donkey's years.
I met him once, but that was donkey’s years ago.
This technology has been around for donkey's.

definitions.definition: a person or thing that people decide is the best in a particular field in a particular year
examples: He was chosen as TV personality of the year.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize that you will/would never do something
examples: I'd never have thought of that in a million years.

definitions.definition: used in polite expressions to describe somebody as ‘very old’
examples: He was a man of advanced years.
(humorous)Even at my advanced age I still know how to enjoy myself!

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel or look older
examples: The illness put years on him.
His wife's death has put years on him.

definitions.definition: the desire for new sexual experience that is thought to be felt after seven years of marriage
since a very long time ago
examples: I've been going there every summer since the year dot.

definitions.definition: to make somebody feel or look younger
examples: That haircut takes years off her.
Careful make-up and styling can take years off you.

definitions.definition: the time when a new century/year starts
examples: It was built at the turn of the century.
The team are unbeaten since the turn of the year.

definitions.definition: every year for many years
examples: People return year after year.

definitions.definition: as the years pass; each year
examples: Year by year their affection for each other grew stronger.

definitions.definition: every year
any particular year after the birth of Christ
each year, compared with the last year
examples: Spending has increased year on year.
a year-on-year increase in spending

definitions.definition: not right or correct
examples: I got all the answers wrong.
He was driving on thewrong sideof the road.
Sorry, I must have dialled the wrong number.
You're holding the camerathe wrong way up!
That picture isthe wrong way round.
I soon realised I'd taken awrong turn.
He got all his calculations wrong.
The information is justplain wrong.

definitions.definition: not right about something/somebody
examples: I think she lives at number 44, but I could be wrong.
wrong about something/somebodyYou were wrong about Tom; he's not married after all.
wrong to do somethingWe were wrong to assume that she'd agree.
She wouldprove him wrong(= prove that he was wrong)whatever happened.
(informal)Correct me if I'm wrong(= I may be wrong)but didn't you say you two knew each other?
(informal)If you think I'm happy, you'redead wrong.
(informal)You think you've beaten me butthat's where you're wrong.
(informal)‘I thought this might interest you because I know you like boxing.’ ‘You're not wrong(= you are absolutely right). I love boxing.’

definitions.definition: causing problems or difficulties; not as it should be
examples: Isanything wrong? You look worried.
‘What's wrong?’ ‘Oh, nothing.’
wrong with somebody/somethingThere'ssomething wrongwith the printer.
Ihave something wrong withmy foot.
The doctor could findnothing wrongwith him.

definitions.definition: not suitable, right or what you need
examples: wrong something for somethingHe's the wrong person for the job.
wrong for somethingShe's simply wrong for this job.
wrong something to doI realized that it was the wrong thing to say.
Most people think that the country is headingin the wrong direction.
It was his bad luck to bein the wrong place at the wrong time(= so that he got involved in trouble without intending to).
We don't want this documentfalling into the wrong hands.
She's all wrong for you.

definitions.definition: not morally right or honest
examples: This man hasdone nothing wrong.
wrong to do somethingIt is wrong to tell lies.
It'smorally wrongto kill somebody.
wrong of somebody (to do something)It was wrong of me to get so angry.
wrong with doing somethingWhat's wrong with eating meat?
wrong with somethingThere's nothing inherently wrong with this type of nostalgia.
wrong that…It is wrong that he should not be punished for what he did.

definitions.definition: to support somebody/something that is not successful
to have the wrong idea about how to get or achieve something
examples: You're barking up the wrong tree if you're expecting us to lend you any money.

definitions.definition: from or living in a poor area or part of town
to start a relationship well/badly
examples: I seem to have got off on the wrong foot with the new boss.

definitions.definition: to make somebody pleased with you/annoyed with you
to be in a bad mood for the whole day for no particular reason
to understand something in the wrong way
to do, say or write something that is suitable/not suitable for a particular occasion
examples: It is a bizarre tale and the author hits just the right note of horror and disbelief.
Unfortunately, the president struck the wrong note in his speech, ignoring the public mood.

definitions.definition: almost correct
examples: Your guess wasn't far out at all.
They weren't far out with their estimate of 100 000.
You're not far wrong when you say he's the richest guy in town.

definitions.definition: younger or older than 40, 50, etc. years of age
thinking or behaving in the right/wrong way
examples: We haven’t found a cure yet—but we are on the right track.
The new manager successfully got the team back onto the right track.
The police were on the wrong track when they treated the case as a revenge killing.

definitions.definition: in trouble with the police
to make somebody annoyed or angry, often without intending to, by doing or saying something that offends them
examples: She tends to rub people up the wrong way.

definitions.definition: to be offended by a remark that was not intended to be offensive
examples: Don't take this the wrong way, but maybe you're trying a bit too hard.

origin: late Old Englishwrang, from Old Norserangr‘awry, unjust’; related towring.

definitions.definition: to become aware of somebody/something by using your eyes
examples: see somebody/somethingShe looked for him but couldn't see him in the crowd.
I looked out of the window but saw nothing.
The opera was the place tosee and be seen(= by other important or fashionable people).
Iwould like to seesome proof of this statement.
The diversreported seeinga lot of sharks.
Herecalls seeinghis father sitting at the kitchen table.
I'venever seenanything like this!
see (that)…He could see (that) she had been crying.
see what, how, etc…Did you see what happened?
If you watch carefully, you’ll see how it is done.
see somebody/something + adj.I hate to see you unhappy.
see somebody/something doing somethingShe was seen running away from the scene of the crime.
see somebody/something do somethingI saw you put the key in your pocket.
somebody/something is seen to do somethingHe was seen to enter the building about the time the crime was committed.
(figurative)The government not only has to do something, it mustbe seen to be doing something(= people must be aware that it is doing something).
see + adv./prep.The walls were too high to see over.

definitions.definition: to have or use the power of sight
examples: She will never see again(= she has become blind).
On a clear day youcan seefor miles from here.
see to do somethingIt was getting dark and Icouldn't seeto read.
I could hardly see because of the smoke.

definitions.definition: to watch a game, television programme, performance, etc.
examples: see somethingDid you see that programme on Brazil last night?
In the evening we went to see a movie.
Fifty thousand people saw the match.
I finally got to see them in concert.
I reallywant to seethis film now.
This performance was very beautiful, like nothing I hadever seenbefore.
Iremember seeingthat movie when I was a kid.
see something on somethingIt's not the sort of thing you see on TV that often.
This film really deserves to be seen on a big screen.

definitions.definition: to look at something in order to find information; to go to somebody in order to ask for help or information
examples: see something/somebodySee page 158.
If you missed the session, see a member of staff for advice.
See Table 2 for details.
See alsoparagraphs 6.5 to 6.9 above.
see + adv./prep.See above/belowfor a breakdown of the costs.
See over(= the next page)for details.

definitions.definition: to be near and recognize somebody; to meet somebody by chance
examples: Guess who I saw at the party last night!
Aren't you pleased to see me?
It's so good to see you!

definitions.definition: to visit somebody/something
examples: Come and see us again soon.
Around 200  000 visitors each year come to see the summer palace.

definitions.definition: to have a meeting with somebody
examples: see somebody about somethingYou ought to see a doctor about that cough.
What is it you want to see me about?
see somebodyI can only see you for five minutes.

definitions.definition: to spend time with somebody
examples: Are you seeing anyone(= having a romantic relationship with anyone)?
They've beenseeing a lot of each other(= spending a lot of time together)recently.
Wehaven't seen much ofLiz lately.
He has changed since his family last saw him.

definitions.definition: to understand something
examples: ‘It opens like this.’ ‘Oh,I see.’
see somethingHe didn't see the joke.
I don't think shesaw the pointof the story.
He changed the way wesee the worldaround us.
tosee a/the needfor something
Isee no needto involve the law.
I can see both sides of the argument.
Theysaw nothing wrongwith having three cars.
Make Lydiasee reason(= be sensible), will you?
see (that)…Can’t you see(that) he’s taking advantage of you?
Idon't see that it matterswhat Josh thinks.
see what, why, etc…‘It's broken.’ ‘Oh yes,I see what you mean.’
‘Can we go swimming?’ ‘I don't see why not(= yes, you can).’
They will be able to see what really happened there.

definitions.definition: to have an opinion of something
examples: Isee thingsdifferently now.
Try to see things from her point of view.
The way I see it, you have three main problems.
As far as I can see(= in my opinion), you've done nothing wrong.
Lack of money is the main problem,as I see it(= in my opinion).
She is in love with him but Darrenseestheir relationshipin a different light(= has a different opinion).

definitions.definition: to consider something as a future possibility; to imagine somebody/something as something
examples: see somebody/something/yourself doing somethingI can't see her changing her mind.
I can't really see myself working in a lab all day.
see somebody/something/yourself as somethingHis colleagues see him as a future director.
She was quiet and withdrawn and never saw herself as a dancer.

definitions.definition: to find out something by looking, asking or waiting
examples: ‘Has the mail come yet?’ ‘I'll just go and see.’
‘Is he going to get better?’ ‘I don't know, we'll just have towait and see.’
We'll have a great time,you'll see.
see what, how, etc…Go and see what the kids are doing, will you?
We'll have tosee how it goes.
It will beinteresting to seewhat happens.
see (that)…I see (that) interest rates are going up again.
it is seen that…It can be seen thatcertain groups are more at risk than others.

definitions.definition: to find out or decide something by thinking or considering
examples: ‘Will you be able to help us?’ ‘I don't know, I'll have to see.’
‘Can I go to the party?’ ‘We'll see(= I'll decide later).’
see what, whether, etc…I'll see what I can doto help.

definitions.definition: to make sure that you do something or that something is done
examples: See that all the doors are locked before you leave.
Could you see that the kids are in bed by 8 o'clock?

definitions.definition: to experience or suffer something
examples: He has seen a great deal in his long life.
I hope I never live tosee the daywhen computers finally replace books.
It didn't surprise her—shehad seen it all before.

definitions.definition: to be the time when an event happens
examples: Next year sees the centenary of Saint-Saëns' death.
The twentieth centurysawdramaticchangesin most areas of life.

definitions.definition: to be the place where an event happens
examples: This stadium has seen many thrilling football games.

definitions.definition: to go with somebody to help or protect them
examples: I saw the children across(= helped them cross)the road.
May Isee you home(= go with you as far as your house)?
My secretary willsee you out(= show you the way out of the building).

definitions.definition: that can beseenclearly; in a way that can beseenclearly
used when you are thinking or trying to remember something
examples: Now let me see—how old is she now?

definitions.definition: to realize that there is going to be a problem before it happens
examples: We should have seen it coming. There was no way he could keep going under all that pressure.

definitions.definition: to realize that somebody/something is not as good, pleasant, etc. as they/it seem
to find out or look at something yourself in order to be sure that what somebody is saying is true
examples: If you don't believe me, go and see for yourself!

definitions.definition: because of the fact that…
examples: Seeing that he's been off sick all week he's unlikely to come.

definitions.definition: goodbye
examples: I'd better be going now. See you!

definitions.definition: used when you are explaining something
examples: You see, the thing is, we won't be finished before Friday.

origin: verbOld Englishsēon, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchzienand Germansehen, perhaps from an Indo-European root shared by Latinsequi‘follow’.

definitions.definition: what is measured in minutes, hours, days, etc.
examples: The world exists in time and space.
The changing seasons mark the passage of time.
in timeA visit to the museum will take youback in timeto the 1930s.
At this point in time, it seems unlikely that he will write any more books.
over timePerceptions change over time(= as time passes).
As time goes onI love this city more and more.
As time went bywe saw less and less of each other.
The main character is able to travel through time.

definitions.definition: the time shown on a clock in minutes and hours
examples: What time is it/What’s the time?
Do you have the time?
(British English)What time do you make it?
(North American English)What time do you have?
Let me just check the time.
The time is now half past ten.
(British English)Can shetell the timeyet(= say what time it is by looking at a clock)?
(North American English)Can shetell timeyet?
My watchkeeps perfect time(= always shows the correct time).
Look at the time! We'll be late.
This time tomorrowI'll be in Canada.

definitions.definition: the time measured in a particular part of the world
examples: 6 o’clock local time
We're two hours behind Central European Time.

definitions.definition: the time when something happens or when something should happen
examples: What time do you finish work?
The baby loves bath time.
A computer screen shows arrival and departure times.
time to do somethingI think it's time to go to bed.
This is hardly the time to discuss politics.
time for somethingIt's time for lunch.
time (that)…It's time the kids were in bed.
It's time that farmers listened to consumers.
by the time (that)…By the time you get there the meeting will be over.
at a… timeHave I called at a bad time? Shall I call back later?
We stock six different beers at any one time.
at the timeI didn't realize it at the time, but that was the best day of my life.
The promotion came at just theright timefor me.
The new product will launchwhen the time is right.
You'll feel differently about itwhen the time comes(= when it happens).
around the time of somethingSeveral articles were published around the time of his death in 2018.

definitions.definition: an amount of time; the amount of time available to work, rest, etc.
examples: I can't go with you—I don'thave time.
Hespendsa lot of histimeworking.
time for somethingDo you have time for a chat?
time to do somethingAllowplenty of timeto get to the airport.
I can probablymake the timeto see them.
Ittakes timeto make changes in the law.
We haveno time to lose(= we must hurry).
We got to the airport with time to spare.
She plans to study overseas for an extendedperiod of time.
What do you like to do in yourfree time?
She doesn’t have muchspare time.
Sorry, I can’t spare the time.
Don'twasteany moretime!
What awaste of time!
Time's running outfor them to complete the project.
I didn't finish the test—I ran out of time.
Time's up—have you worked out the answer yet?
He never takes anytime off(= time spent not working).
Jane's worked herefor some time(= for a fairly long period of time).
Do it now please—not in three hours' time(= three hours from now).
The journey time is two hours.
I don’t want to take up too much of your precious time.
What’s the hurry? We have all the time in the world.

definitions.definition: a period of time, either long or short, during which you do something or something happens
examples: His injuries willtake a long timeto heal.
It took them a long time to get home.
Did you have to wait a long time to see the doctor?
for a timeI lived in Egypt for a time.
I was only there fora short time.
The early morning is the besttime of day.
Her parents dieda long time ago.
At one time(= at a period of time in the past)Emily was my best friend.
Mr Curtis was the managerin my time(= when I was working there).

definitions.definition: a period of history connected with particular events or experiences in people’s lives
examples: at the time of somethingThe movie is set at the time of the Russian Revolution.
in… time(s)the politician who promised ‘peace in our time’
in ancient times
The Industrial Revolution took place in Victorian times.
the violent times we live in(= the present period of history)
in times of somethingin times of trouble
The business was successful, even during difficult times.
Times are hard for the unemployed.
Times have changedsince Grandma was young.

definitions.definition: an occasion when you do something or when something happens
examples: Every timeI hear that song I feel happy.
It doesn't matter if you don't win every time(= every time you play).
Next timeyou're here let's have lunch together.
He failed his driving test three times.
He's determined to passthis time.
When was thelast timeyou saw her?
I see her two or threetimes a week.
How many times(= how often)do I have to tell you not to do that?
(especially North American English)I rememberone time(= once)we had to abandon our car in the snow.
(formal)At no time didI give my consent to the plan.

definitions.definition: an event or occasion that you experience in a particular way
examples: Did youhave a good timein Spain?
They'rehaving a hard timedealing with her illness.
I had an awful time in the hospital.
Mike and I shared some really good times.

definitions.definition: how long somebody takes to run a race or complete an event
examples: The winner's time was 11.6 seconds.
She completed the 500 metresin record time(= faster than any previous runner).
one of the fastest times ever

definitions.definition: the number ofbeats(= units of rhythm)in abarof music
examples: This piece is in four-four time.
a slow waltz time
The conductorbeat timewith a baton.

definitions.definition: the correct speed and rhythm of a piece of music
examples: in time to somethingTry and dance in time to the music(= with the same speed and rhythm).
Clap your hands tokeep time(= sing or move with the correct speed and rhythm).
in/out of timeto play in/out of time(= follow/not follow the correct speed and rhythm)
He always plays in perfect time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something should have happened before now
if you do somethingagainst time, you do it as fast as you can because you do not have much time
examples: They're working against time to try and get people out of the rubble alive.

definitions.definition: earlier/later than was expected
examples: We finished 15 minutes ahead of time.
The plane took off an hour behind time.

definitions.definition: having advanced or new ideas that other people use or copy later
during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: The letter was in my pocket all the time(= while I was looking for it).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: She leaves the lights on all the time.

definitions.definition: always
examples: Our representatives are ready to help you at all times.
Hot water is available at all times.

definitions.definition: even when the circumstances are very good
examples: He's never very happy at the best of times—he'll be much worse now!

definitions.definition: at one time; together
examples: She was laughing and crying at the same time.

definitions.definition: used to introduce a fact that is different and must be considered
examples: You have to be firm, but at the same time you should try and be sympathetic.

definitions.definition: separately or in groups of two, three, etc. on each occasion
examples: We had to go and see the principal one at a time.
She ran up the stairs two at a time.
He surfs the internet for hours at a time.

definitions.definition: at the age you are (especially when you are not young)
examples: Eyesight doesn't get any better at my time of life.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: He can be really bad-tempered at times.
The rain will become heavy at times in the afternoon.
He can be rather moody at times.

definitions.definition: to mark or follow the rhythm of music, by waving a stick,tappingyour foot(= hitting it against the floor), etc.
examples: She beat time with her fingers.

definitions.definition: happening before you were born or can remember or before you lived, worked, etc. somewhere
examples: ‘Were you taught by Professor Pascal?’ ‘No, he was before my time.’

definitions.definition: before the usual time in somebody’s life when something happens
examples: She got old before her time.

definitions.definition: old-fashioned in your ideas, methods, etc.
to be able to take advantage of opportunities when they come
examples: His success was down to being in the right place at the right time.

definitions.definition: to still be alive after the time when you were expected to die
examples: He’s been living on borrowed time ever since his last heart attack.

definitions.definition: to be doing something that other people are likely to soon stop you from doing
examples: According to the latest opinion polls, the government is living on borrowed time.

definitions.definition: used to encourage somebody who has not been successful at something
examples: Never mind— better luck next time.
If you didn't win a prize, better luck next time.

definitions.definition: to wait for the right time to do something
examples: He decided to bide his time until he got an opportunity to talk to her alone.

definitions.definition: to do something in order to delay an event, a decision, etc.
examples: The negotiators kept the gunman talking to buy time for the hostages.

definitions.definition: to say or decide that it is time for something to finish
a very difficult or unpleasant job or time
examples: I've had a devil of a job finding you.

definitions.definition: to spend time in prison
whenever there is a choice
examples: I don't really like cities—give me the countryside every time.

definitions.definition: if you do somethingfor old times’ sake, you do it because it is connected with something good that happened to you in the past
for a short period of time but not permanently
examples: You can leave your suitcase here for the time being.

definitions.definition: beginning on that day; from that time
examples: From that time forth she gave me endless friendship and encouragement.

definitions.definition: occasionally but not regularly
examples: She has to work at weekends from time to time.

definitions.definition: to delay something so that you can have more time to make a decision, deal with a problem, etc.
to deliberately make a situation difficult and unpleasant for somebody
examples: They really gave me a hard time at the interview.

definitions.definition: used to say that you prefer a particular thing or person to the one that has just been mentioned
examples: We don't go out much. Give me a quiet night in front of the TV any day!

definitions.definition: to have no difficulties or problems
examples: He’s had an easy time of it since he married Lucy.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to like and be interested in somebody/something
examples: I've always had a lot of time for Mary.

definitions.definition: to dislike somebody/something
examples: I have no time for lazy people like Steve.

definitions.definition: to have many problems or difficulties to deal with; to not be successful
examples: He’s had a thin time of it since losing his job.

definitions.definition: to enjoy yourself very much
to have nothing to do or not be busy
to enjoy yourself very much; to have a very good time
examples: The kids had a whale of a time at the theme park.

definitions.definition: when enough time has passed
examples: It is possible that in the course of time a cure for cancer will be found.
In the course of time, I began to understand.

definitions.definition: when the time is appropriate, usually after a long period
examples: I have no doubt that she’ll tell us in the fullness of time.

definitions.definition: early; with enough time so that you are not in a hurry
examples: Get to the airport in good time.

definitions.definition: used to say that something will be done or will happen at the appropriate time and not before
examples: Be patient, Emily! All in good time.

definitions.definition: at the very last moment; just before something bad happens, so that you manage to avoid it, but only just
examples: They escaped from the smoke-filled house just in the nick of time.

definitions.definition: so soon or so quickly that it is surprising
examples: The kids will be leaving home in no time.

definitions.definition: a long time ago in the past
examples: In olden times people would gather in town squares to gossip and exchange stories.

definitions.definition: how and when you want to, even though this might annoy other people
examples: He always does the work, but in his own sweet time.
She just ignores all the criticism and continues in her own sweet way.

definitions.definition: when you are ready and not sooner
examples: Don't hassle him! He'll do it in his own good time.

definitions.definition: in your free time and not when you usually work or study
after a period of time when a situation has changed
examples: They learned to accept their stepmother in time.
You'll get used to the work in time.

definitions.definition: not late; with enough time to be able to do something
examples: Will we be in time for the six o'clock train?
The ambulance got there just in time(= to save somebody's life).

definitions.definition: used to say that you think somebody should do something soon
examples: It's about time you cleaned your room!

definitions.definition: used to say that something will definitely happen, although you are not sure when
examples: It's only a matter of time before they bring out their own version of the software.

definitions.definition: to change and develop your ideas, way of working, etc. so that you do what is modern and what is expected
examples: Many complained that the Royal Family had failed to move with the times.

definitions.definition: to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: used to say hello to somebody you have not seen for a long time
to complete a journey quickly
examples: We made excellent time and arrived in Spain in two days.

definitions.definition: to do something quickly or very often because you wish you had started doing it sooner
examples: I’ll have to work hard now to make up for lost time.

definitions.definition: many times; frequently
to pass the time while you wait for something more interesting
examples: I'm just marking time in this job—I'm hoping to get into journalism.

definitions.definition: to makemarchingmovements without moving forwards
on the next, first, etc. occasion that the same thing happens
examples: He repeated none of the errors he'd made first time round.
This time round it was not so easy.

definitions.definition: used to say that something is usually true or almost always happens
examples: Nine times out of ten she gives the right answer.

definitions.definition: almost always
used to say that something should have happened before now
to refuse to speak to somebody because you do not like or respect them
examples: Since the success of her novel, people shake her hand who once wouldn't have given her the time of day.

definitions.definition: now is the best time to do something, not in the future
that has ever existed
examples: Many rated him the best singer of all time.

definitions.definition: used, especially at the beginning of stories, to mean ‘a long time ago in the past’
examples: Once upon a time there was a beautiful princess.

definitions.definition: at the arranged or correct time; not late
examples: The train arrived right on time.

definitions.definition: to say hello to somebody and have a short conversation with them
to find or create reasons why something has to be delayed, often so you have longer to prepare for it
examples: His legal team, clearly playing for time, sought to have the case heard a month later.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you have to do something or finish something very fast before it is too late
examples: Getting food to the starving refugees is now a race against time.

definitions.definition: something that you feel shows what things are like now, especially how bad they are
examples: The increasing number of people owning guns is an alarming sign of the times.

definitions.definition: it is better to deal with something immediately because if you wait it may become worse or more difficult and cause extra work
to not think about what will happen in the future
examples: I don't know if he'll get better. We're just taking it one day at a time.

definitions.definition: to use as much time as you need without hurrying
examples: There's no rush—take your time.

definitions.definition: used to say you think somebody is late or is too slow in doing something
examples: You certainly took your time getting here!

definitions.definition: to spend some time away from your usual work or activity in order to rest or do something else instead
examples: She is taking time out from her music career for a year.

definitions.definition: the fact that something has not happened before does not mean that it will never happen
used when you have failed to do something twice and hope that you will succeed the third time
often; on many or all occasions
examples: You will get a perfect result time after time if you follow these instructions.
Time and again, the girls have shown how strong and determined they can be.

definitions.definition: one and a half times the usual rate of pay
examples: We get time and a half on Sundays.

definitions.definition: time seems to pass very quickly
examples: How time flies! I've got to go now.
Time has flown since the holiday began.

definitions.definition: time is valuable, and should not be wasted
used to say that somebody can wait for something to happen or can wait before doing something
used to say that something used to happen in the past
examples: Time was when you could go for a walk in the country and not see another person for miles.
Time was when we never needed to lock our house at night.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will have to wait for some time to find out the result of a situation
examples: Only time will tell if the treatment has been successful.

definitions.definition: during the whole of a particular period of time
examples: My keys were in my pocket the whole time(= while I was looking for them).

definitions.definition: very often; repeatedly
examples: He drums his fingers on the table the whole time—it's very annoying.

origin: Old Englishtīma, of Germanic origin; related totide, which it superseded in temporal senses. The earliest of the current verb senses (dating from late Middle English) is ‘do (something) at a particular moment’.

definitions.definition: 60 minutes; one of the 24 parts that a day is divided into
examples: Ispent an houron the phone.
It willtakeaboutan hourto get there.
It usually takes me two hours to walk there.
The interviewlasted half an hour.
It was a three-hour exam.
Most cats sleep 13–16hours a day.
Architects can expect to spend 40 hours a week in meetings.
for an hourI waited for a couple of hours and then I left.
in an hourHe'll be back in an hour.
In less thantwenty-four hoursit would be Christmas.
by the hourWe're paid by the hour.
per/an hourTop speed is 120 miles per hour.
The rate of pay is £10.50 an hour.
within the hourWe hope to be there within the hour(= in less than an hour).
York was within an hour's drive.
He crossed the line in a time of 2 hours and 40 minutes.
Chicago is two hours away(= it takes two hours to get there).
We're four hours ahead of New York(= referring to the time difference).
I sat there,hour after hour, staring at a blank computer screen.

definitions.definition: a period of about an hour, used for a particular purpose
examples: I spent mylunch hourshopping.

definitions.definition: a fixed period of time during which people work, an office is open, etc.
examples: Opening hoursare from 10 to 6 each day.
Most people in this kind of job tend to worklong hours.
during office, business, etc. hoursThe library is open during normalworking hours.
What are youroffice hours?
a hospital’svisiting hours
Britain'slicensing hours(= when pubs are allowed to open)used to be very restricted.
after hoursThis is the only place to get a drink after hours(= after the normal closing time for pubs).
out of hoursYou can contact me on this number out of hours(= when the office is closed).

definitions.definition: a long time
examples: Ittook hoursgetting there.
It took us hours to find out what was wrong.
‘How long did it last?’ ‘Oh,hours and hours.’
Where have you been? I've beenwaiting hours.
for hoursI've beenwaiting for hours.

definitions.definition: a particular point in time
examples: You can't turn him away at this hour of the night.
The hour had come for us to leave.

definitions.definition: the time when something important happens
examples: This was often thought of as the country'sfinest hour.
She thought herlast hourhad come.
Don't desert me in myhour of need.
She helped me in my hour of need.

definitions.definition: the time when it is exactly 1 o’clock, 2 o’clock, etc.
examples: The clockstruck the hour.
ten minutes past the hour
on the hourThere's a bus every hour on the hour

definitions.definition: used when giving the time according to the 24-hour clock, usually in military or other official language
examples: The first missile was launched at 2300 hours(= at 11 p.m.).This is pronounced ‘23 hundred hours’.

definitions.definition: any time, especially a time that is not usual or suitable
examples: He's started staying outtill all hours(= until very late at night).
She thinks she can call meat all hours of the day and night.

definitions.definition: at the last possible moment; just in time
very early, especially when this is annoying
examples: The job involved getting up at some unearthly hour to catch the first train.

definitions.definition: very early or very late and therefore annoying
examples: I apologize for calling you at this ungodly hour.

definitions.definition: the time when you have to do something difficult or unpleasant
examples: I’d better go and see the dentist—I can’t put off the evil hour any longer.
If you keep on borrowing, you are only postponing the evil day when you have to pay it all back.

definitions.definition: if you keep regular, strange, etc.hours, the times at which you do things (especially getting up or going to bed) are regular, strange, etc.
to spend time doing something that is not important while you are waiting for something else to happen
examples: We killed time playing cards.

definitions.definition: the period of time very early in the morning, soon after midnight
examples: We worked well into the small hours.
The fighting began in the early hours of Saturday morning.
The party continued well into the early hours.

origin: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman Frenchure, via Latin from Greekhōra‘season, hour’.
